### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
No specific mission statement or educational philosophy is detailed in the available content. The school operates as a public **Grundschule** (primary school) with a **sportbetontes Profil** (sports-focused profile), open all-day care (**Offene Ganztagbetreuung - OGB**), and cross-grade learning groups in the early school phase.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
The Hermann-Gmeiner-Schule is located in Berlin-Lichtenberg and serves as a public primary school (Schulnummer: 11G07). It features facilities including parking for severely disabled persons and wheelchair-accessible restrooms, with recent additions like an elevator ensuring full barrier-free access.[1][5] Outdoor areas include floor patterns suitable for hopping games and small-group instruction outside classrooms.[3] It participates in programs supported by JuLi gGmbH since September 2009.[4] Community feedback includes positive parent reviews, such as one from 2020 praising the school highly.[2] The school hosts an open house day listed in the Berlin education calendar.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum includes **English as the first foreign language**. Key programs encompass **open all-day care (OGB)**, **cross-grade learning groups in the school entry phase**, and a **sports-focused profile**. No further details on core subjects or specialized academic tracks are provided.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students benefit from sports-oriented activities, flexible outdoor learning spaces for small groups, and all-day care options. The barrier-free design supports inclusive access for all students.[1][3][5] No specific details on daily schedules, extracurriculars, or freizeitaktivitäten (leisure activities) are listed beyond the sports profile.[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No quantitative achievements, inspection results, exam outcomes, or performance metrics are available in the content. Qualitative feedback includes a strong parent endorsement from 2020: "Dickes großes Kompliment. Ich bin einfach beeindruckt und geplättet. Das übertrifft alle meine Erwartungen."[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public **tuition-free Grundschule**, admissions follow standard Berlin public school procedures, likely based on local residency. The school offers an open house day for prospective families, listed in the Berlin education calendar. Contact the school leadership for details: Tel. +49 30 5598291, Email: Schulleitung@gmeiner.schule.berlin.de.[1]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The 37. Schule (Grundschule) operates as a modern primary school emphasizing reliable half-day education with open all-day options, fostering academic skills, physical activity, creativity, and community engagement in a supportive environment.[4][2] It prioritizes reading and writing competence through integrated reading circles and methods like Kamishibai storytelling theater to promote literacy enjoyment and text comprehension.[7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Opened in August 2022 as a new building in northwestern Friedrichshain, this public primary school replaced or expanded prior facilities, accommodating up to 432 students on a compact site with an integrated three-field sports hall, outdoor sports areas, a school garden with three raised beds, naturally lit learning landscapes, artistic staircases, colorful corridors, WLAN, interactive whiteboards, and barrier-free access.[1][2][3][4] Located in Berlin's Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg district (Friedrichshain neighborhood), it serves 360 students in 16 year-group homogeneous classes (up to 26 children each) with 29 teachers, 15 educators, two social workers, administrative staff, and a caretaker.[1][4][5] The eFöB (supplementary care, formerly Hort) provides breakfast, relaxation, and small support groups.[4][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Grundschule** (primary school for grades 1-6), it follows Berlin's standard curriculum with English as the first foreign language, offering reliable half-day instruction from 08:20 (preparation from 07:30), extendable to full-day care from 06:00-18:00.[4][5] Programs include mathematics olympiads for grades 3-6, reading clubs with book ratings and certificates (silver for 2+ books, gold for 5+), floorball in PE and after-school (Tuesdays 14:30-15:30), and literacy promotion via reading circles and Kamishibai.[6][7] Sports integrate into daily lessons with hall access.[2][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in diverse hands-on activities: class trips like 3C to Gärten der Welt (gardens, water play), 2C to Hölzerner See (adventure program, swimming), 4A Berlin Rallye (history tours with presentations); events such as Halloween parties (crafting, dancing), Fasching (costume shows, stations), sports days (sprints, throws), cross-country runs, garden visits (herb harvesting, animal encounters), and workshops (art with Lebenshilfe).[Scraped Website Content] eFöB offers play, games, mini-golf, and sponsored materials; breakfast and flexible care support daily routines.[4][7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
**35 students** from grades 3-6 competed in the first round of the Mathematics Olympiad, tackling challenging tasks.[Scraped Website Content] In cross-country at Volkspark Friedrichshain, 22 participants secured a **2nd place**, two **3rd places**, and positions 5th-7th, 12th, and two 13ths among top 15.[Scraped Website Content] Floorball training prepares for state finals; strong community support via Förderverein funds eFöB equipment.[Scraped Website Content][6] High enrollment near capacity reflects demand.[3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free Grundschule** (school number 02G37), admission follows Berlin's standard public primary school process based on residence in the catchment area (Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg).[1][5] Contact: sekretariat@02g37.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 290255911; open secretariat hours Mon-Thu 07:00-14:00, Fri 07:00-13:00.[4][5][8] Reliable half-day with optional extended care.[4]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The 37. Schule (Grundschule) emphasizes creating an inspiring learning environment built on a diverse community, joy in learning, sustainability, and safety, where every student feels secure and supported while collaborating toward shared goals.[7][1] It operates as an innovative **Compartment-Schule**, designed for modern pedagogical needs with architecture supporting individual learning processes, thematic compartments in a large school setting, flexible year-group-spanning instruction, holistic movement-oriented learning, and inclusive education.[7][1][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
This public primary school in Berlin-Marzahn-Hellersdorf (Mahlsdorf district) at Elsenstr. 7-9, 12623 Berlin, is a newly built four-track (vierzügige) open all-day school (Ganztagsschule) planned for around 570-600 students to relieve nearby schools.[1][2][3] Operations began in August 2024 with three classes at an interim site (Louis-Lewin-Straße), moved to the new building by summer 2025, and were officially opened on October 10, 2025.[1][2][5] The modular Compartment design features individual learning houses (Lernhäuser) with classrooms, group spaces, movement areas, retreats around a central forum, plus shared facilities like a cafeteria (Mensa), library, and two-story three-field gym (Dreifeldturnhalle); built in 18 months for ~74 million EUR with barrier-free access for inclusivity.[1][2][3][5] The school is still expanding stepwise to full capacity by 2029/2030, with outdoor areas completing in 2025.[5] Community focus includes protection from stress/violence, relaxation spaces, participation, democracy education, sustainability, movement, and media literacy.[1][7]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum aligns with the Compartment concept for flexible, inclusive, movement-oriented learning in thematic units across year groups, supported by specialized rooms and all-day structure.[1][7] Programs include English as the first foreign language (noted in related profiles), floorball as part of sports education and extracurriculars via Floorballverband Berlin-Brandenburg, and projects like circus training (acrobatics, trapeze, magic, trampoline).[4][6] Extracurriculars emphasize eFöB (erweiterte Förderung des Bildungsauftrags) with interest groups, outings, and holiday trips focused on inclusion and school priorities.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Daily schedule: Early care (6:00-7:30) for calm arrival/play; core instruction/learning (8:00-13:30) with projects, group work, movement breaks, and communal lunch; afternoon eFöB (13:30-16:00) for self-directed creativity, projects, play, research; late care (16:00-18:00) for relaxed wind-down in small groups.[7] Experiences promote cooperative learning, individual pacing, relaxation/protection from overload, and events like costume shows, game stations, and circus projects.[6][7] Floorball training occurs Tuesdays (14:30-15:30) and in holidays.[6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
As a new school (opened 2025), specific academic outcomes like exam results are not yet detailed in available content.[1][2] Achievements include rapid construction under Berlin's school building initiative, official opening attended by high-level officials (e.g., Senator Katharina Günther-Wünsch), successful start with expanding enrollment, and active programs like floorball training for state finals and circus project shows.[2][3][6]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
No specific admissions process detailed; as a public tuition-free Grundschule, enrollment follows Berlin public school procedures for local residents, with stepwise expansion from 3 classes in 2024 to full four-track capacity (~576 places) by 2029/2030.[1][2][5]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The 38. Grundschule aims to create a vibrant learning environment that provides students with a loving and secure start to school life.[1][2] It emphasizes collaborative shaping of education in a supportive atmosphere, as the school is newly founded and still developing alongside its website.[1][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
The 38. Grundschule is a newly established public primary school (Grundschule) in Berlin's Marzahn-Hellersdorf district, opening on August 1, 2025, alongside the 39. Grundschule.[1][2][3] Its permanent address is Bruno-Baum-Str. 72, 12685 Berlin (Marzahn), but until the new wooden modular building (with sports hall, capacity for 468 students, costing 43 million euros) is completed around the 2026/27 school year, classes are held as guests at the 39. Grundschule, Marzahner Chaussee 165, 12681 Berlin.[1][2][3][4] The school addresses growing demand in the expanding district, using sustainable Holzmodulbauweise (wooden modular construction) with a compartment principle for flexible learning spaces combining open areas, team rooms, and group spaces.[4][8] Leadership includes Michel Stark (acting principal) and Dirk Seidler (Ganztag leadership).[2][3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
No specific curriculum details are available on the website, as the school is under construction and its homepage is evolving.[1][2] Official records indicate English and French as first foreign languages.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Information on student experiences is limited due to the school's new status; it focuses on fostering a lively, secure environment but provides no detailed programs yet.[1][2] Temporary operations at the host site ensure continuity until the permanent facility opens.[1][3]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
As a newly founded school (opening 2025), no achievements or outcomes are reported.[1][3] The project contributes to Berlin's school construction initiative for additional places in growing areas.[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
The school invites families to get to know it and mentions an "Anmelden" (enroll) option on the website, with a Tag der offenen Tür (open house day) listed in the official calendar.[1][2] It is currently recruiting engaged teachers.[1] Enrollment follows standard public school procedures in Berlin; contact via email (10G38@10G38.schule.berlin.de or 10G38@diebildungspartner.de) or phone (+49 30 22009886).[2][3]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's motto is **"Hier lernen wir gerne"** (Here we like to learn), emphasizing a welcoming learning environment that prepares children for life through core subjects like German and math, alongside healthy nutrition, physical activity, cultural diversity, and social interaction.[2][9] It prioritizes basic competencies in **reading, writing, and arithmetic** to ensure a successful life for all students, with individualized support so every child learns at their own pace.[3] The philosophy supports inclusive, modern education in open, bright spaces aligned with current educational research, fostering both promotion and challenge.[1][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Opened in **March 2024** as part of Berlin's school construction initiative, the school moved into its new modular building in **August 2023**, designed on the **Compartmentprinzip** (compartment concept) for flexible learning landscapes accommodating up to **432 students**.[1][3] Located in Berlin-Lichtenberg (Alt-Hohenschönhausen), it serves grades 1-3, two Willkommensklassen (welcome classes for newcomers), and two special education small classes for children with intellectual development focus, led by **Schulleiterin Giorgina Kazungu-Haß** and a team of about **50 pedagogues**, including 22 classroom teachers as of 2024/2025.[3][5][7] Facilities include digital equipment, specialized rooms for art, music, science, teaching kitchen, library, **3-field sports hall** (accessible outside school hours), movement/therapy room, barrier-free access, outdoor areas with running track, soccer/basketball field, playgrounds, green classroom, school garden, raised beds, and table tennis.[1][3] It partners with SOCIUS for all-day care and benefits from an active parent community.[2][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **public Grundschule (primary school)**, it follows Berlin's standard curriculum with emphasis on core skills, offering **English** or **French** as first foreign languages.[3][5] Key programs include **Kulturtag** (Culture Day every Wednesday), merging music and art into project-based learning with exhibitions, theater, and choir; **Bärenstarke Grundschulkinder** (strong primary school children program) for physical development; and **English for Kids** (4 extra hours weekly beyond regular timetable).[3][4] Additional focuses: native-speaker English in development, inclusive education, and all-day offerings.[2][3] It operates as an **offene Ganztagsschule** (open all-day school).[4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in hands-on, joyful activities like author visits (e.g., Andreas Hüging's Straßentiger performance), Nikolausfeier with school song premiere and concerts, school choir, theater, and cultural projects.[9] Weekly Kulturtag unites the school; enrollment events and open doors provide family involvement; discipline shone during a 2024 fire incident.[9] The environment promotes social skills, cultural exposure, and individualized pacing in a growing community.[3][9]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Students developed the school leitbild (mission statement) via a conference on children's rights, creating a weighted "leitbildwolke" (mission cloud).[9] Successful events include 2024 Nikolausfeier with original school song debut, Straßentiger show, and smooth 2024/2025 start with new first graders.[9] No formal test results or outcomes detailed; focus is on community building in a new school.[5][9]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Enrollment documents available at https://grundschule-schleizer-strasse.de/infos-fuer-sorgeberechtigte/ under "Unterlagen zur Schulanmeldung"; personal appointments sent by post.[9] Open door days (e.g., 20.09.2024, planned for 2025) offer tours and presentations.[9] Welcomes new first graders; as a public school, admission follows Berlin regulations for local residents.[5][9]

The 39. Schule operates as a public primary school (Grundschule) in Berlin's Marzahn-Hellersdorf district, currently hosting the 38. The schools prioritize a lively school life with modern equipment, open culture, team spirit, and space for teacher ideas in building the institution from the ground up.[1][3] Temporary operations at the 39. # 40. Grundschule - School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The 40. Grundschule is a **public, tuition-free elementary school** founded in the 2023/24 school year that positions itself as "a school of diversity."[5] The school's pedagogical approach is grounded in **Early Excellence, the Berlin Model, and age-mixed learning (JüL)** in the first three grades, combined with differentiated instruction.[1][3]

The school emphasizes **individualized learning** to optimally support each child's development.[1] Its educational philosophy values the cultural, social, and subjective heterogeneity of its student body, viewing age-mixed instruction as more inclusive than homogeneous grouping and as a means to strengthen social competencies through peer learning, cooperation, and role modeling.[5] The school's pedagogical concept is based on **open teaching forms and reform-pedagogical approaches**, utilizing recurring elements such as morning rituals, weekly plans, and learning pathways.[5]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

The 40. Grundschule opened its doors in the 2023/24 school year as a newly established school.[1][3] The school has experienced rapid growth: it began with four classes and 70 students in 2023/24, expanded to six classes with 110 students in 2024/25, and currently serves 175 students across nine classes.[5] The school is projected to eventually become a two-stream (zweizügige) elementary school.

**Location and Facilities:** The school is located at Ella-Barowsky-Straße 62-63 in the Schöneberg district (Tempelhof-Schöneberg borough) of Berlin.[2][4] The newly constructed school building is situated in the picturesque Ceciliengärten, a quiet residential area.[1][3] The facility features modern classrooms and spacious common areas designed to create an atmosphere where each child feels safe and supported.[1][3] The school was described as offering "modern standard" with new, clean facilities.[6]

**Community Composition:** The school community is highly diverse, with **ten different languages spoken** among students and staff.[5] The school's staff includes a team of 25 educators across nine classes.[5] This linguistic and cultural diversity reflects the school's commitment to inclusive education and multicultural learning.

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Class Structure:** The school organizes students into age-mixed groups in the lower grades:
- **Grades 1-3:** Seven planned age-mixed groups (currently operating with Erdmännchen, Biber, Drachen, and Otter classes combining grades 1-3)
- **Grades 4-6:** Currently one age-mixed group (Dschungel class); from grade 4 onward, the school plans to transition to grade-level homogeneous classes[5]

The school has chosen age-mixed instruction in the lower three grades because it is more inclusive than homogeneous grouping, allows flexible duration of stay, enables differentiated learning offerings, and supports varied learning paces.[5]

**Language Support:** Students who are new to the school and learning German as a second language receive intensive language instruction in separate groups, with the goal of gradually integrating them into regular classes.[5]

**Teaching Methodology:** The curriculum integrates:
- Structured daily routines combining collective learning phases and individual rest periods with diverse free-work materials[1][3]
- Open teaching forms and reform-pedagogical approaches[5]
- Recurring elements including morning rituals, counting machines (Zählmaschine), weekly plans, and learning pathways[5]
- Differentiated instruction tailored to individual student needs[1]

**All-Day Care:** The school operates as an **open-form all-day school** (Ganztagsschule in offener Form) that provides not only regular instruction but also comprehensive afternoon care, including lunch after school and during school holidays.[3]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

The school creates a structured school day that balances **collective learning phases with individual recovery periods**, supported by diverse free-work materials.[1][3] The age-mixed learning environment fosters social competencies through role-switching between helping and receiving help, cooperation, mutual support, and peer modeling.[5]

The school's holistic approach to teaching and care enables children to recognize and develop their personal strengths.[3] Staff from the Pestalozzi-Fröbel-Haus (PFH), which has cooperated with the school since 2023, provide support not only during instruction but also actively enrich and support afternoon leisure time.[3]

**School Culture:** The school emphasizes respectful and friendly treatment of others, mindful movement through hallways, and appropriate use of materials and spaces. The school restricts personal mobile devices (phones, smartwatches, tablets) during the school day and on school grounds.[5]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The search results do not contain specific information about standardized test scores, academic achievements, or measurable learning outcomes for the 40. Grundschule. As a newly established school (opened 2023/24), comprehensive outcome data may not yet be available.

## 6. ADMISSIONS

The search results do not provide specific information about the admissions process, application deadlines, or enrollment requirements for the 40. Grundschule. As a public school in Berlin, it likely follows standard Berlin public school enrollment procedures, but specific details are not available in the provided sources.

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The 40. Schule (10G40) is a **Montessori-oriented primary school (Grundschule)** in Berlin's Marzahn-Hellersdorf district, emphasizing a new learning culture centered on **movement, creativity, language, and communal growth**. Its philosophy promotes open learning, mixed-age learning groups, individual self-directed holistic development, and inclusion, aiming to foster curiosity, responsibility, creativity, independence, and social accountability so every child can unfold their potential regardless of background, talent, or pace.[1][7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded as a new public school, operations began in the **2023/24 school year** with three to four 1st-grade classes (initially 70 children, growing to 110 by 2024/25), planning to expand into a two-track Grundschule; key milestones include the first parent evening in June 2025, official start on August 1, enrollment on September 13, and early projects like autumn nature walks.[1][8] The diverse community speaks ten languages and includes various nationalities, with a strong focus on inclusion and collaboration among children, staff, and parents.[1][8] Currently at a temporary site (**Dankratweg 19, 12623 Berlin**), the permanent address is **Haltoner Str. 21, 12683 Berlin (Biesdorf)**; the new building follows the innovative **Compartment principle**—modular wooden construction creating flexible "small school" units with classrooms, group rooms, and forums for modern, inclusive all-day learning, designed for sustainability and quick completion.[1][6][7]

### 3. CURRICURICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum integrates **Montessori methods** with foci on **art and sports**, alongside open learning, year-spanning groups (Jahrgangsübergreifendes Lernen or JüL), individualized differentiated instruction, Early Excellence, and the Berlin Model for the first three grades.[1][2] It features structured daily routines with communal learning phases, individual recovery, free work materials, and holistic support in movement, language, and creativity; supplementary care (eFöB) provides before/after-school and holiday support emphasizing individual needs, play, creativity, rituals, and parent-teacher exchange.[1][2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in self-paced, competency-level learning in flexible environments promoting **inclusion, social responsibility, and personal growth**, with activities like nature projects (e.g., collecting chestnuts and acorns, leaf studies), music integrations (e.g., enrollment ceremony with Viva Musica), and access to a donated piano for early music exposure.[1] All-day offerings include meals, homework support, play, friendships, and clubs in music, art, and sports; the upcoming Compartment design supports varied formats from group work to individual phases in bright, modern spaces with retreat areas.[1][2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
As a school in founding (started 2023/24), specific outcomes are emerging; achievements include rapid team growth, successful first enrollment and projects, a free piano from the Carl Bechstein Stiftung to promote early keyboard playing, and positive parent feedback at info evenings.[1] No formal test results or long-term metrics are detailed yet.[7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Admissions target local families in Marzahn-Hellersdorf (Biesdorf area), with info evenings for incoming 1st graders (e.g., post-autumn break for 2026/27); the school is growing class-by-class, prioritizing inclusive enrollment for diverse children.[1][3][7]

# Adam-Ries-Schule (Grundschule) - School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Adam-Ries-Schule is **an open, child-friendly, performance-promoting and performance-demanding primary school**[5] that prioritizes the development of the whole child. The school's core philosophy centers on providing children with reliability, security, care, and challenge[5]. 

The institution emphasizes **reading and language development as primary educational focuses**[4]. Key values include discipline, order, participation, and homework completion, with particular emphasis on democratic and collegial interaction among students[5]. The school aims to equip students with solid, applicable foundational knowledge for successful transition to secondary school[5].

Social learning is integrated into the regular curriculum, with **"EUROPA - Bildung" (European Education) offered from Grade 4 onwards** as part of the school's profile development[1]. The school prioritizes conflict resolution skills, teaching students core principles: "I listen to you," "I don't laugh at you," "I don't hurt you," and "I help you"[5].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Size:** The Adam-Ries-Schule is a **small, manageable three-stream primary school** located in the Gensinger Kiez neighborhood with approximately **550 students**[4]. The school is situated at Alt-Friedrichsfelde 66, 10315 Berlin, in the Friedrichsfelde district of Lichtenberg[1][3].

**Leadership:** The school is directed by Dr. Sergej Afonin[1].

**Facilities and Equipment:** The school provides[1]:
- Library
- Computer room
- Teaching kitchen
- Relaxation room
- Wheelchair-accessible restrooms

**Community Partnerships:** The school operates in cooperation with other educational institutions in the district[1].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Language Offerings:** Students have access to **English and French as first foreign languages**[1].

**Special Programs and Profiles:**
- **Information Technology Profile**[1]
- **Bilingual German-Turkish Education and Literacy**[1]
- **European Education (Europabildung)** from Grade 4 with instruction in the respective national languages across six countries[1]
- **School Entry Phase (SAPH)** with both **grade-level and mixed-age learning groups**[1]

**Support Services:**
- Learning groups for newcomers without German language skills[1]
- Special education small classes with autism support focus[1]
- Inclusive education for students with learning difficulties and high-ability students[5]
- Support for students with reading difficulties, spelling challenges, and language problems[5]
- Social, health, and motor skill development programs[5]

**Extracurricular Activities:** The school offers working groups in judo, football, ceramics, and a buddy project[1].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**All-Day Care (OGB):** The school provides **comprehensive open all-day care (Offene Ganztagbetreuung) operating from 6:00 AM to 6:00 PM on all school and holiday days with no closures**[2]. The program follows the motto "Playing – Learning – Trying – Finding Friends – Developing Talents"[2].

Care is organized in **six mixed-age groups of 20 children each** (Grades 1-4), designed on a family-support principle to maximize individual attention while fostering independence[2]. Educators help children organize engaging group activities, complete homework increasingly independently, try new experiences, and develop friendships[2]. During holidays, groups undertake excursions, attend events, go swimming, and explore new playgrounds[2].

**Classroom Structure:** The school entry phase includes both grade-level and mixed-age learning groups, allowing flexibility in how students are grouped[1].

**Student Governance:** Class councils and student parliament are permanent features of the school community[1].

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Adolf-Glaßbrenner-Grundschule aims to enable learning in community, fostering children's experience of their own capabilities and joy in learning, while ensuring they feel comfortable, get along well, and enjoy attending school.[Main Page] High expectations are placed on both students and educators.[Main Page] The school emphasizes **light and air for the free word**, quoting Adolf Glaßbrenner, promoting truth, community welfare, and open expression.[Main Page] As an open all-day school (offene Ganztagsschule), it integrates instructional and extracurricular areas through multiprofessional teams and external partners for holistic, all-day learning.[1][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Kreuzberg (Hagelberger Str. 34, 10965 Berlin), this public primary school (Grundschule) serves approximately **430-450 students** in 18 classes, with **38 teachers** and **22 educators** (including volunteers).[1][3][5] Named after poet Adolf Glaßbrenner (1810-1876), it operates in the Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg district as a reliable half-day school (Verlässliche Halbtagsgrundschule) with free core hours from 7:45-13:35 (extendable to 15:30), and full open all-day care from 6:00-18:00 via partnership with Diakonisches Werk Berlin-Stadtmitte e.V. since 2006; about **330-350 children** use after-school care.[3][5][6] Facilities include a **Lesekeller** (central learning hub for language, reading, and community events since 2008), library, computer room, pottery workshop, school kitchen, school garden, climbing frame, and student club.[1][2][5] Community ties emphasize neighborhood (Kiez) integration, with partners like Kitas, Schülerladen, and cultural institutions; it's a Kulturagenten-Schule since 2020, described as **approachable, action-oriented, participatory, and neighborhood-connected**.[4][2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Core instruction runs 8:00-13:35 daily, with early support from 7:30 for grades 4+ and afternoon blocks (14:00-15:30) for grades 5-6.[3] Grades 1-3 use six cross-grade groups (JüL) and three grade-specific groups (JabL); grades 4-6 are homogeneous with fixed class teachers and specialists.[3] English is the first foreign language; additional offerings include cross-grade learning groups and open all-day care (OGB).[2][3] Cultural education integrates theater, art, and literature projects 1-2 times yearly, using school and external sites like museums.[4] The Lesekeller focuses on language competency for social participation.[1][5]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in diverse activities: student newspaper **Brennglas** (weekly for grades 4+, Tuesdays 14:00-15:30, covering school news, environment, fun, knowledge).[About Page] Extracurriculars (AGs) include homework help, soccer (including girls'), ballet, running, athletics, theater, nature/gardening, table tennis, acrobatics, rugby.[2] Class blogs highlight projects like audioplays, Erich Kästner books, farewell events.[Main Page] Events include reading competitions, fairy tale hours, flea markets, climbing adventures, inventions topics, magical December, transitions to secondary school.[Main Page][About Page] Freizeitbereich supports play, crafting, gardening, informal learning, inclusion.[5][6] About 350 of 450 students participate, with dedicated educators per class through grade 4.[5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Recent highlights include a new climbing frame, Vorlese-Wettbewerb 2025, student newspaper issues on adventures and inventions, online prizes in competitions.[Main Page][About Page] Positive parent evaluation in 2023 (42% response rate).[6] Strong all-day integration praised for multiprofessional teamwork and Lesekeller impact on language.[1] Cultural projects networked with Jewish Museum and Technikmuseum.[4] Prepares for secondary transition with info sessions and tests (e.g., comparative math/German for grade 5).[Main Page]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school**, enrollment follows Berlin's standard primary school process (Schulanmeldung).[2][7] Freizeitbereich requires a youth office certificate for contracts beyond free core hours (7:30-13:30); all grades 1-3 attend Kinderhaus, grades 4-6 use Haus B.[3][6] Contact: Tel +49 30 50587531, email Adolf-Glassbrenner-Schule@web.de, website for Anmeldung im Freizeitbereich.[2][3]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Albert-Gutzmann-Schule embodies the motto **"Alle gemeinsam stark"** (All together strong), emphasizing a calm, motivating school climate that promotes acceptance, tolerance, and consideration to enable all students to achieve maximum knowledge, linguistic, and social competencies.[7][9] As a **Grundschule** (primary school) and **Förderzentrum** with a special education focus on **language** (Förderschwerpunkt Sprache), it supports children with identified language needs alongside mainstream students in an inclusive environment.[1][2][9] The school, named after Albert Gutzmann—a pioneer in speech therapy who screened 155,000 Berlin schoolchildren in 1886 and identified 1,550 with speech impairments—prioritizes linguistic development, health-oriented profiles, and holistic growth through sport- and culture-focused programs.[9][10]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded as a "Kiez-Grundschule" (neighborhood primary school) in the 2006/07 school year, the school serves approximately **570 children** primarily from the local Gesundbrunnen area in Berlin-Mitte, fostering a community-oriented environment at Pankstr. 70, 13357 Berlin (with some references to Orthstraße 1).[5][7][1][2][4] It combines a Grundschule with a Förderzentrum featuring a small secondary section (classes 7-10), offering open all-day care (Offener Ganztag) in partnership with ALBA BERLIN for sports and cultural activities.[1][7] Key facilities include supportive infrastructure noted in the 2025 school inspection for equipment and profile, with ongoing website restructuring to align with updated school programs.[7][8] Leadership is provided by Schulleiter **Herr U. Meinecke**, supported by konrektors, sekretariat staff, and specialists in EFöB (ergänzende Förderung und Betreuung), school social work, and sports coordination.[7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum follows Berlin's **Rahmenlehrplan** for primary education, with **English** as the first foreign language from early stages, building vocabulary on themes like colors, numbers, animals, weather, and hobbies, expanding through grades 3-6.[1][3][4][6] Grades 1-2 form the **Schulanfangsphase (Saph)** with joint year-group learning, transitioning to stable groups in grades 3-6 that include writing, grammar, swimming lessons (class 3 highlight), and **GeWi** (Gesellschaftswissenschaften: history, geography, politics) introduced in 2017/18 for grades 5-6, emphasizing reading comprehension, judgment, and media literacy on topics like nutrition, water, democracy, and urban diversity.[3] Special features include health-focused profiles, integration classes (open), lerngroups for new arrivals without German proficiency, and inclusive support via Förderunterricht, special educators, and school assistants.[1][3][4][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience a **sport- and culture-emphasized open all-day program** (freiwilliges Ganztagsangebot) led by ALBA BERLIN coaches and educators, integrating sports, culture, and after-school clubs like Hort and Schülerclub with diverse activities.[3][7] Inclusive practices support children with diagnosed needs through additional tutoring, year-spanning groups, school social work, and partnerships in sports, museum pedagogy, music/theater, and local "Kiez" collaborations (e.g., four nearby Kitas, Polizeiabschnitt 36, Charité Logopädie).[3][4][7] Recent events highlight active engagement: victories in Max-Schmeling-Halle tournaments, Olympia-Dialog participation, ALBA youth club ferien programs, and free swimming courses.[7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The 2025 Schulinspektion report evaluates strengths in **school framework (equipment/profile)**, **teaching quality (methods, differentiation, activation)**, **organization/leadership**, **performance assessment/inclusion**, and **school culture/parent cooperation**, identifying development areas with recommendations.[8] Achievements include student successes like 1st place for girls' teams in Max-Schmeling-Halle (Jan 2026), Olympia-Dialog press conference attendance, and steering group workshops; the school enables middle school qualifications/expanded vocational readiness in its Förderzentrum secondary track.[1][7] Partnerships enhance outcomes, with ALBA BERLIN's renowned youth work supporting daily school life.[7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free Grundschule** (öffentlich), admissions target local neighborhood children, with key information for parents on enrollment, instruction, school careers, and parent association available via the website's parent section, contacts, current year updates, and downloads.[7][4] Beratungsstelle for language-impaired children and lerngroups for non-German speakers facilitate access; contact Schulleiter Herr U. Meinecke at 030 46 90 59 60 or sekretariat@gutzmannschule.de for inquiries.[1][4][7]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Alfred-Brehm-Grundschule emphasizes respectful and friendly interactions, lifelong learning through values, skills, and competencies, and a balance of giving and receiving without tolerance for violence.[2][6] It values individual differences, promotes independent and cooperative learning, allows errors for development, fosters creativity, and integrates movement and health education daily.[2][6] Inspections highlight strengths in individualized support via differentiation and cooperative learning, a learning-conducive climate, and holistic child development.[3][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded with groundwork in September 1953, Richtfest on December 19, 1953, move-in on January 15, 1955, named Alfred-Brehm-Grundschule on September 12, 1956, and gym inaugurated in June 1963.[2][6] Located in Tegel-Süd (Reinickendorf district), Berlin, at Ascheberger Weg 8A, 13507 Berlin, in the Waldidyll settlement amid forests and lakes.[1][2][6] Serves 415 students with 30-35 teachers, 15 educators, and 2 school social workers; community includes parent association (Förderverein) with annual fee of 15 EUR.[1][2][6] Facilities include library, computer room, gym, outdoor sports area, playgrounds, recent extension with canteen/mensa (3.7 million EUR, opened post-construction delays), and open all-day care from 6:00-18:00 for 250 students by Stiftung Pro Gemeinsinn.[1][2][5] Accessibility features: wheelchair-accessible WC.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Operates as a reliable half-day school with open all-day care (OGB/EFöB), offering year-group learning groups in early phases, reading competency focus, interdisciplinary teaching, and conflict mediators.[1][2] Daily schedule: 8:05-15:05 across 8 periods with breaks; includes language-building, independent/problem-oriented learning, cooperative forms, and life-world relevance.[2][4] Extracurriculars (AGs): band gymnastics, soccer, health sports, self-defense, swimming, school garden, crafts, circus, computers, animals.[1] Partnerships: local Kitas, libraries, media centers, youth facilities.[1] Inspections note improved teaching quality above Berlin average, with development needs in internal curriculum and concepts for high-achievers.[3][4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience a friendly, disruption-free environment with high satisfaction, individualized support, cooperative and self-directed tasks (e.g., worksheets with choices), social work, and youth offers.[2][3][4] About 230-250 use all-day care with leisure/developmental activities; ~1/3 non-German native speakers, 25 with support status, all exempt from learning material fees.[4] Emphasizes self-reliance, creativity, health/movement, and safe school routes via Schulwegplan.[2][6] Staff collaboration enhances whole-child focus.[3][4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Inspections (2013, 2017-18) praise learning climate, teacher-educator teamwork, language measures, individualization; teaching quality exceeds Berlin/Grundschule averages with gains since prior review.[3][4] High satisfaction among all stakeholders; strengths in inner differentiation, school leadership.[3][4] No specific test results or recent outcomes detailed; events like school festival (canceled 2024 due to storm).[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Public Grundschule with open enrollment; school tours on September 26, 2025 (16:00-18:00), registration via email or phone (030-4322408).[2] Contact: sekretariat@12G14.schule.berlin.de.[2][7] Parent association membership optional at 15 EUR/year.[2]

# Alternativschule Berlin – Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Alternativschule Berlin is a **state-accredited comprehensive school** (Gemeinschaftsschule) in private ownership operating under the principle of "frei lernen, frei leben" (learn freely, live freely).[1] The school's foundational philosophy centers on **respect for free will** and the conviction that **learning occurs through relationships**.[1]

The school implements a progressive pedagogical approach that fundamentally differs from traditional grading systems. Students in grades 1-9 receive **no numerical grades**, with grades for the mid-year report in grade 10 negotiated with subject specialists.[1] Rather than traditional homework, the school employs **individual learning agreements** (Lernverabredungen) and structured study time, emphasizing self-directed learning and personal responsibility.

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Structure:**
Alternativschule Berlin is located at **Bondickstraße 8/9, 13469 Berlin** (Lichtenberg district).[2][3] The school currently serves **140 students** across grades 1-13.[1] The secondary level (Sekundarstufe 2) was established in August 2020 with 8 students in grade 11, representing the school's expansion into upper secondary education.[1]

**Facilities and Daily Operations:**
The school operates as a **full-day institution** from 8:30 AM to 5:00 PM, Monday through Friday.[1] The campus includes specialized learning spaces and creative workshops:
- Pottery workshop (Töpferei)
- Construction workshop (Bauwerkstatt)
- Art studio (Atelier)
- Sewing workshop (Nähwerkstatt)
- Media workshop (Medienwerkstatt)[1]

The school emphasizes **free play**, particularly for primary students, with a substantial portion of the daily schedule dedicated to unstructured play time.[1]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Structure and Pathways:**
The school serves students from **grades 1-13**, offering pathways to multiple German qualifications:
- **Mittlerer Schulabschluss (MSA)** – the most common exit qualification at grade 10[1]
- **Abitur** – available through the secondary level (grades 11-13)[1]

**Learning Organization:**

*Primary Level (Grades 1-6):*
- Individual learning agreements within weekly plans in dedicated learning spaces
- No traditional homework assignments
- Emphasis on play-based and experiential learning[1]

*Secondary Level 1 (Grades 7-10):*
- Students create **individualized quarterly schedules** (approximately 3-month cycles) selecting from available courses[1]
- Courses are differentiated into **two performance levels**: Grundkurs (foundational) and Aufbaukurs (advanced)[1]
- "Bridge courses" (Brückenkurse) supplement weekly plans and individual practice time
- Dedicated study time (Studienzeit) for independent learning from grade 7 onward[1]

**Student Participation:**
The school incorporates **democratic governance** through weekly school assemblies where students can raise topics and participate in school decision-making.[1]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

The school documents diverse experiential learning activities:

**Field Experiences:**
- Grade 8 "Challenge Journey" (Herausforderungsfahrt) – residential trips to natural settings, including recent excursions to the North Sea peninsula of Nordstrand
- Grade 7 environmental work projects – harvest activities on farms and apple plantations
- Primary school trips to outdoor education centers (e.g., Eichhof)[1]

**Cultural and Educational Outings:**
- Museum visits (e.g., Spectrum technology museum)
- International exchanges – documented exchange program with Aljarafe School in Seville, Spain (as of November 2023)[1]

**Community Events:**
- Winter Festival (Winterfest) with baking workshops
- Summer Festival with creative activities
- Monthly school café (Schulcafé) for families and students
- Contribution weeks (Beitragswochen) where secondary students engage in service projects[1]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The search results do not contain specific information about awards, rankings, certifications, or documented student achievement metrics for Alternativschule Berlin. The school's emphasis on alternative assessment methods (absence of grades through grade 9) means traditional performance data is not publicly available in the provided sources.

## 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES

**Enrollment Process:**
The school accepts applications for new students, with specific application deadlines announced annually. For the 2026/27 school year, applications for grade 1 were accepted from **August 1 – October 31, 2025**.[1] The school requires prospective families to review the school's educational concept before applying and indicates selective admissions ("we no longer accept applications" once capacity is reached).[1]

**Fee Information:**
**No tuition or fee information is available in the provided search results or website content.** The scraped website content explicitly notes "Fee/Tuition Pages Found (0 pages)" and "No PDF content extracted." While the search results confirm that Alternativschule Berlin is a **private school (Schule in freier Trägerschaft)** that is fee-based,[3] the specific amounts, payment structures, and fee categories are not disclosed in the accessible portions of the school's website or the provided documentation.

To obtain complete fee information, direct contact with the school is necessary via:
- **Address:** Bondickstraße 8/9, 13469 Berlin

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Alt-Lankwitzer Grundschule emphasizes **inclusive education** for all children regardless of intellectual or physical limitations, fostering **value appreciation, tolerance, and respect** through joint upbringing of disabled and non-disabled students from grades 1-6.[1][2][4] Its school motto, "Es ist normal, verschieden zu sein" (It is normal to be different), underscores diversity, with regular school assemblies where students actively shape community values.[2][9] Key focuses include social learning, media competence, and developmental support tailored to each child's abilities.[1][3][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in **1877**, the school is the oldest in Lankwitz, originally in a half-timbered house before moving to its current site; a "red schoolhouse" was added in 1900, forming its core, and it is a **listed historical monument**.[1][4] It serves ~**420 students** with **32 teachers** and **14 childcare educators**, located in Berlin-Steglitz-Zehlendorf at Schulstr. 17-21, 12247 Berlin.[1][3][4] Facilities include 18 classrooms, music room, natural sciences room, computer room (with 12 workstations), and specialized spaces; it operates as an open all-day school with afternoon care and meals when needed.[1][2] Community involvement features an active parents' association, Förderverein, and partnerships like music school collaborations.[2][3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **three-track primary school (Grundschule, grades 1-6)**, it implements **cross-grade learning** for grades 1-3 to build confidence, with inclusive models, jahrgangsübergreifende groups, and support for reading/writing difficulties.[1][2][3] Programs highlight **digital competence** (robotics, programming, explanatory videos, "Digitale Schule" award 2023), **MINT-friendly** (math/science, awarded 2022), foreign languages (English/French from grade 3/4), and environmental education (six-time "Umweltschule in Europa").[1][3][5] Extracurriculars include AGs like STS (Singen-Tanzen-Spielen, 20 years), hockey, sheep care, badminton, and music courses (guitar, recorder).[2][3][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in hands-on projects like **robotics (tinkering, building, programming)**, creating explanatory videos on nutrition, 100-day festivals, lantern parades, and paper-making for the 125th anniversary.[scraped] Events include winter holidays, carnival, conferences, Lichterfest, and integrations like Konfliktlotsen (conflict mediation).[1][scraped] Inclusive practices ensure individualized goals within mixed classes, with JÜL (likely Jungunterrichtler or similar early support), Haus der kleinen Forscher, and sports events promoting social competence.[3][scraped]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Celebrated its **125th anniversary** with creative projects; **20 years of STS-AG**.[scraped][7] Awards: **Digitale Schule** (only Berlin primary, 2023), **MINT-freundliche Schule** (3rd time, 2022), **Umweltschule in Europa** (6th time).[1][5] Hosts international delegations on social learning; participates in district sports, Christmas singing, and charity runs; 125 new first-graders welcomed in 2025.[3][5][7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free Grundschule**, enrollment follows Berlin public school procedures (Schulprogramm and Schulanmeldung available on site); contact Schulleitung for details (Angela Efinger or Kristin Schulz).[3][4][scraped] Serves local Lankwitz area with inclusive intake for ~420 students.[1][4]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Alt-Schmargendorf-Grundschule emphasizes a holistic approach encapsulated in its motto "Das Ganze ist mehr als die Summe" (The whole is more than the sum of its parts), fostering values like mutual respect, shared understanding of core principles among staff, and high identification with the school by educators.[2][4][5] It promotes a **sportbetontes Profil** (sports-focused profile) alongside social learning, democracy education, and health initiatives, with school inspection highlighting strong leadership, child-centered after-school offers, and a culture of appreciating student achievements.[1][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in the quiet, older part of Schmargendorf in Berlin's **Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf** district at Reichenhaller Str. 8, 14199 Berlin, the school building dates back to around 1900 (originally the 13th Volksschule), with a 1928-1930 extension in New Objectivity style; it adopted its current name in 1966 and celebrated its 50th name anniversary in 2015.[1][2][6][7] Facilities include **two gymnasiums**, a new playground course on the schoolyard, and a large yard with play equipment; it partners with local sports clubs for activities.[1][2][6] Community involvement features strong parent associations (Eltern, Förderverein), social work (Schulsozialarbeit), family programs (FiSch, SiS), and historical projects like studying Dahlem's St. Annen Church frescoes.[3][5] Leadership includes current head Sabine Sülflow (since 2016), following a line of rectors since 1945.[1][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a public **Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6), it offers standard Berlin curriculum with English as the first foreign language, open all-day care (OGB), year-group learning groups in early phases, and a sports profile.[1] Key programs include **Humanistischer Lebenskundeunterricht** (secular humanistic life skills based on scientific insights into nature and society), evangelical and Catholic religious education, reading initiatives (Leseinsel & Leseprofis), beginner learning support (Lernanfänger), and concepts like social learning curriculum, child protection, active breaks, democracy education, and mobility training.[1][4][5] After-school care ("Ergänzende Betreuung" or Hort) runs 6:00-7:30 AM, until 6:00 PM, and holidays, with homework, play, crafts, warm meals, and clubs.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in diverse, active experiences emphasizing sports and creativity: after-school clubs (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) in football, swimming, tennis, basketball, hockey, handball, gymnastics, athletics, art, street dance, literature, French, yoga, judo, computing (from grade 2), and ski trips to Austria/Seefeld.[1][2] Activities include project weeks (e.g., children's rights, games, green oasis, art, team-building), class trips (e.g., to St. Peter-Ording), tournaments (football girls' Herbstturnier, Ball-über-die-Schnur), ski weeks, writing workshops, book events, discos, festivals (Sommerfest, Nikolaus, Sankt Martin), and excursions (e.g., Luther traces, traffic safety with ADACUS).[5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
School inspections praise broad movement and health promotion, mutual respect among staff and educators, recognized leadership, goal-oriented actions, and interest-aligned after-school programs; it was among Berlin's first sports-focused primary schools.[4][7] Student successes feature in events like football girls at Sparki-Cup, chess tournaments (Schachmatt), writing competitions, and cultural projects (e.g., 2016-2017 Denkmal-Aktiv on medieval church art presented at Open Monument Day).[3][5] Positive inspection notes include shared values and high staff-school identification.[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public tuition-free school, admissions follow Berlin's standard process (Schulanmeldung) with open house days (Tag der offenen Tür, e.g., 2023, 2024) and enrollment info available.[1][5] It welcomes new students with events like Einschulung 2025, emphasizing a magical start quoted from Hermann Hesse.[5]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Amtsfeld-Schule operates as an **offene Ganztagsschule** (open all-day school), emphasizing extended support and care for students, including **erweiterte Förderung und Betreuung (eFöB)**, equivalent to after-school care ("Hort"), for nearly half of its students.[1][4] It focuses on individualized learning support, social-pedagogical guidance in individual or group settings, and strong parental partnerships, particularly through projects addressing educational, social, and personal challenges for students learning German as a second language (about 20% of pupils).[1][2] Collaboration with special educators, integration staff, teachers, and external organizations like JAO gGmbH underscores a holistic approach to keeping students in the educational process.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin's **Treptow-Köpenick** district, specifically the Köpenick borough's Allende-Viertel, the school serves the Allende I residential area, Müggelheimer Straße, and areas around the old town.[1][3] It comprises a main building for grades 3-6 at **Pablo-Neruda-Str. 8, 12559 Berlin**, and a separate branch for grades 1-2 at **Salvador-Allende-Str. 93**.[1][2] The 2025/26 school year reports **597 students** across **25 classes**, supported by **48 teachers**, **18 educators**, and **8 additional staff**.[4] Community ties include a parents' association (Förderverein) and partnerships like JAO gGmbH (since 2018) and SPI program (since August 2020).[1][5] Facilities support all-day operations, including a school club and garden.[2]

### 3. CURRICURICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6), the curriculum follows Berlin's standard framework with **English** as the first foreign language.[2][4] Key offerings include **Lerngruppen** for reading and writing difficulties, year-specific or cross-grade learning groups in the early phase, and open all-day care (OGB).[2] School hours for 2025/26 are **07:50 to 13:40** (end of 6th period).[4] Extracurricular **Arbeitsgemeinschaften (AGs)** cover diverse activities: Qi Gong, writing, ball games, cooking/baking, visual arts, games, guitar, math, soccer, peer mediation, reading club, performing arts, global children/geography, computers, school garden, choir, sketches, judo, English, crafting.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students benefit from **open all-day school** structure with extended care for **294 children** in eFöB/hort programs.[4] Lunch is managed via online registration with **Vielfalt Menü GmbH**, including options for **free school meals** ordered through the district.[4] Holiday care, such as during Winterferien (e.g., Feb 2-6, 2026, tel. 030 84598896), ensures continuity.[4] Support programs like JAO target individual needs, small-group instruction (10 hours/week with special educators), and family counseling, especially for ~20% learning German as a second language.[1] Enrollment for 2026 beginners opens Oct 6-17, 2025.[4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific academic outcomes, test results, or inspection reports are detailed in available content, though the school participates in Berlin's quality frameworks (e.g., Schulinspektion, Prüfungsergebnisse noted but not quantified).[2] Employee reviews on kununu average **4.0/5** with 100% recommendation rate from 2 reports.[10] Ongoing projects (JAO since 2018, SPI since 2020) indicate sustained external support for integration and development.[1][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Admissions follow Berlin public school catchment areas (Einzugsbereich: Allende I, Müggelheimer Straße, Altstadt area).[1] Beginner enrollment (Lernanfänger:innen 2026) is Oct 6-17, 2025, with events like Bio-Brotbox on Oct 13.[4] Contact: Tel. 030/6540192 (main), 6544126 (grades 1/2 branch); Email: sekretariat@amtsfeld.schule.berlin.de.[4][7] As a tuition-free public school (**öffentlich**), no fees apply; free meals available via district process.[4]

# Andersen-Grundschule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Andersen-Grundschule is committed to fostering **self-directed learning** while supporting the individual strengths of each student.[2] The school's core philosophy centers on creating a **learning oasis** where children feel protected and secure, emphasizing that "learning with all senses" is fundamental to their educational approach.[2]

The school's foundational pillars are **colorful diversity, performance orientation, and cooperative collaboration**.[2] Central to this mission is the cultivation of **democratic thinking and action**, with students regularly participating in class councils, student councils, and student juries to develop civic responsibility from an early age.[1] The school explicitly values **tolerance, courtesy, and respect** as essential to democratic practice.[2]

As a **neighborhood school (Kiezschule)**, the Andersen-Grundschule is deeply embedded in the Wedding district community, maintaining close connections with local residents and incorporating community engagement into numerous projects.[2]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Historical Background**

The Andersen-Grundschule was established on April 10, 1899, as the "4. Gemeindeschule Pankow" (4th Municipal School of Pankow), initially serving 358 students under five teachers.[1] The three-story brick building was constructed in 1898-1899 according to designs by Haikradt and is now a protected historical monument characterized by late classical architecture with symmetrical facades, rounded arch portals, and segmental arch windows.[1][7] The school received its current name on September 13, 1954, by Senate resolution.[1] Extensions were added in 1972-1973, and the gymnasium was renovated in 2009.[1]

**Community Composition**

The school serves a highly diverse student population, with approximately **80-90% of students having non-German native languages**.[6] The school community includes children from **40 different nations** representing various religious backgrounds, with particularly high representation from Muslim families and Christian-Orthodox families from the Serbian region.[6] Many students are from recently arrived refugee families experiencing formal schooling for the first time, alongside families long-established in the Wedding neighborhood.[6]

**Facilities and Resources**

The school operates as an **open all-day school (Ganztagsschule in offener Form)**[1] and includes a **library and reading oasis** where students can develop a love of reading.[8] The school has invested in a kitchen for cooking projects with students and features a computer room with iPads and numerous learning apps to develop digital competency.[8] The school also maintains a gymnasium and outdoor schoolyard that was renovated and redesigned.[1]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Chess Profile (Begaschule Schach)**

The Andersen-Grundschule is designated as a **chess gifted school (Begaschule Schach)**.[3] All students in grades 1 and 2 learn chess fundamentals as part of regular classroom instruction.[3] Beginning in grade 2, students demonstrating exceptional ability are selected for additional talent groups. High-performing students in these groups are admitted to the "Gifted Program" (Begabten Programm) and receive training through the end of their school career for participation in internal and external tournaments.[3] For chess enthusiasts not admitted to the gifted course, a weekly chess working group (AG) is available.[3] The gifted chess courses, held Thursdays from 1:45 PM to 3:45 PM, also welcome students from other schools who demonstrate outstanding chess performance.[3]

**Language and Communication**

English is the primary foreign language offered.[4] The school implements **WUV (Wahlunterrichtsverpflichtung)** as "School of Healthy Poets and Active Thinkers," promoting student development through language instruction with specialized German profile options including theater, poetry slam, poetry and reading workshops, creative writing workshops, and computer projects.[6]

**Democratic Learning**

**Class council (Klassenrat)** is established as a formal subject beginning in grade 3, where students sit in circles without teacher direction to address problems collaboratively, learn protocol writing, and develop problem-solving skills.[6]

**Creative and Enrichment Programs**

The school emphasizes creative learning through theater projects, choir, regular craft days, and writing workshops.[1][8] The school offers **project-based learning and working groups (AGs)** tailored to student interests, including both practical and artistic options.[6] The school aims to provide each child with an AG matching their interests.[6]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Pedagogical Approach**

The school strengthens **self-directed learning** and fosters individual student strengths through personalized support in each child's personal development and interests.[2] The school provides **digital and analog learning opportunities**, combining traditional reading with modern technology through iPads, computer rooms, and educational apps.[8]

**After-School Care**

The school offers **open all-day care (OGB)** with **hortmodule options** where students can play, craft, make music, complete homework, and participate in sports under the guidance of the school's pedagogical team.[8]

**Support Services**

The school employs a **multiprofessional team** of dedicated teachers, educators, school social workers, and external staff.[2] The school operates a **school social work team** supporting students and families in daily school life.[8] The school also features a **school station** providing additional student support.[4]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The search results do not provide specific information about standardized test scores, graduation rates, or quantifiable academic outcomes. The school's achievements are primarily documented through its recognition as a **chess gifted school** and its emphasis on fostering democratic citizenship, creative expression, and inclusive education for a highly diverse student population.[1][3]

## 6. ADMISSIONS

The search results do not contain specific information about admissions procedures, application deadlines, or enrollment requirements. As a public school in Berlin, the Andersen-Grundschule operates under standard Berlin public school enrollment policies, though specific details are not provided in the available sources.

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Anna-Lindh-Schule emphasizes inclusive education with a focus on **Hochbegabung (gifted education)** as a core profile, alongside reading promotion, developmental therapy/pedagogy (ETEP), and violence prevention.[1][4] It operates as an open all-day school (offener Ganztag) with a multiprofessional staff of 120 educators, supporting a diverse student body where 75% have non-German backgrounds, 50% live on transfer benefits, and 10% require special pedagogical support.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin's Wedding district (previously associated with Parkviertel in Mitte), the school serves approximately **750 students**, making it one of Berlin's largest primary schools (Grundschule).[1][2] The historic building is under monument protection with ongoing renovation needs; it has relocated to a new, modern facility at Reinickendorfer Straße 60, 13347 Berlin, effective February 10, 2025, as part of Berlin's school construction initiative.[1][3][5] The community includes partnerships like BrotZeit, Seniorpartners in School, and the Gesellschaft für interkulturelles Zusammenleben (GIZ).[1] After-school care (Hort/SPB) is available.[3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum features **English** instruction and a school experiment since 2020 for cognitively gifted students (about 10% of pupils).[1][4] It participates in Berlin's bonus program and maintains an open profile for gifted education.[1][4] The school also provides support for intellectual development (Förderschwerpunkt - Geistige Entwicklung).[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience an open all-day structure with multiprofessional support, including 25% of staff in lateral or cross-entry positions and training programs.[1] Enrichment comes from external cooperations, developmental pedagogy, reading initiatives, and violence prevention, tailored to a highly diverse population.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school is recognized as a **Schule besonderer Prägung Hochbegabung** (school with special emphasis on giftedness) and participates in Berlin's quality initiatives like SchuMaS.[1][4] The new building has been positively received for its learning-friendly environment.[5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public Grundschule, admission follows Berlin's standard public school enrollment process (Schulanmeldung); no specific barriers noted beyond general district procedures.[1][2] Open profile for gifted focus.[4]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Annedore-Leber-Grundschule positions itself as **"eine Schule für alle"** (a school for all), welcoming children with diverse backgrounds and needs while emphasizing individual strengths, personality development, mutual respect, tolerance, and conflict resolution.[1][4][5] It operates as an **inclusive Schwerpunktschule** (inclusive focus school) since August 2019, certified **BegaKursSchule** for gifted programs, and **MINT-freundliche Schule** (STEM-friendly school) since 2014, integrating open learning concepts, mathematics across the day, and participation in educational policy programs like "digitale Drehtür" since 2025.[1][4][7] The school's protective concept (Schutzkonzept) is recognized by the KMK, with structured conflict management, preventive measures, and cooperation with external partners.[1][4][8]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Built in 1955, the school includes a main building with annex, gym, cafeteria (Mensa), and four separate structures housing a theater, school social work office, and FILL learning therapy room; a filial branch at Alt-Lichtenrade 113 is wheelchair-accessible.[2][6] Named after Annedore Leber (born 1904 in Berlin-Wilmersdorf), it serves the Lichtenrade neighborhood ("Wir im Kiez") with 650 students, fostering community through events like Fasching, Lichtermarkt, Adventsbazar, and collaborations with Tannenhof e.V., Jugendamt, and partners like Musikschule Leo Kestenberg.[1][2][5][9] Staff for 2025/26 includes 72 teachers (9 in training), 33 educators, school leadership (Schulleiterin Tamara Adamzik, deputies), plus social workers, therapists, and AG leaders.[1][2][7]

### 3. CURRICURICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an **offene Ganztagsschule** (open all-day school), the curriculum emphasizes holistic learning across subjects like mathematics (via Themenkisten for problem-solving, arguing, and hands-on exploration at varied levels), language, inclusion, and development pedagogy, with English as the first foreign language.[1][2][4] Programs include **Begabtenkurse** (gifted courses since 2003: Math/Deutsch Plus, Caliope programming, art, puppets, chess, workshops) and **Förderkurse** (support for weaker students: temporary groups in "Eulenstunden" for math, German as second language, physio-motorics, concentration, reading/writing).[1][4] Additional focuses: **Inklusion** (Geistige/Körperliche Entwicklung, TaLi-Klassen for emotional-social needs, PSE model), MINT/STEM, media concept, double-class standards, and cultural/political education.[2][3][4][5]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in **offene Ganztagbetreuung** (OGB) with integrated elements like AGs (Arbeitsgemeinschaften: theater, photography/WUV, circus projects 2017/2025, juggling, researcher workshops), Hort (after-school care), Schulanfang (school entry with year-group learning groups), exchanges (e.g., Russia), outings, and roles like Konfliktlots*innen, Verkehrshelfer*innen, Klassensprecher*innen, Toiletten-Buddy from grades 1-6.[1][2][4][5] Daily life features praxis-oriented, varied methods (open tasks, group work, movement games, neighborhood ties), Vorlesewettbewerb (reading competition), sports like mini-marathon, and events (Fasching, Frühlingsfest); special needs supported via Sonderpädagogische Kleinklassen.[1][2][4][8]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The 2019 Schulinspektionsbericht praises qualities in student support, diverse offerings, methodical variety, social competence training, and structures for school development involving students/parents; areas for development include systematizing programmatic work.[5] Recognitions: KMK appreciation of Schutzkonzept, BegaKursSchule award (2019), MINT-friendly title (2014), SPI program participation since 2011 (extra inclusive staffing since 2018).[3][4][8] Successful events include Vorlesewettbewerb, Lichtermarkt, Adventsbazar, circus films, and student feedback highlights engaging, non-boring lessons.[1][8]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public Grundschule** (tuition-free), admissions follow Berlin's standard Schulanmeldung process via eGovernment services; contact Schulleitung@algs.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 902778243 for Meldung an die Schule (enrollment notifications), with support for Schulanfang phase and Oberschulanmeldungen (upper school registrations, e.g., March 5, 2026).[2][8]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Anne-Frank-Grundschule emphasizes three core pillars: Anne Frank (commemorating her legacy through events like the annual Gedenktag with a birthday table, Gedenklauf, and programs), **Soziales Lernen** (social learning via daily mediation, conflict resolution, student participation, and "Multi-Kulti" special multicultural lessons), and **Sportbetonung** (sports emphasis with a profiled program).[1][3][5][8] It operates as an open all-day school (offener Ganztagsbetrieb) fostering inclusive, communal learning in a diverse environment, including support for newcomers without German proficiency and children of traveling families like circus performers.[1][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1882 as the "128. Gemeindeschule für Mädchen," the school was renamed after Anne Frank in 1971 and relocated in 2000 to a renovated former freight depot (Güterschuppen Spreebord) in Berlin-Moabit along the Spree, neighboring the Federal President's residence; expansions added a sports hall and kindergarten between 1997-1999.[3][4][5] Leadership includes recent directors like Petra Kleiber (2014-2023) and current Heike Irogue.[1][5] The community-oriented facility offers wheelchair accessibility (parking, elevator, WC), computer rooms with internet, class PCs/printers, a school garden, library, and Schuloase after-school team; it supports a diverse Moabit population with Schulsozialarbeit and partnerships.[1][3][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6), it features English as the first foreign language, sports- and theater-focused profiles, learning groups for non-German speakers, and flexible school entry optimization (FLEX, Zuckertütenwoche).[1] Methods include project/station/partner/group work, analytic-synthetic reading, and Freshmethode; daily schedule runs 8:00-15:25 with up to 8 periods.[1][2][5] Extracurriculars (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) cover swimming, football, fitness, badminton, dragon boating, table tennis (grades 4-6), music theater, buddy projects, ceramics, gardening, art, cinema, and experiments.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in all-day care (8:00-13:50 required, extended via contract), annual activities like festivals (Fasching, Weihnachtsbasar), competitions, heat-shortened days, and events such as readings, Bildungsmesse for grade 6 parents, and Girls’ & Boys’ Day with record participation in Mitte district.[1][3][8] Inclusivity includes joint learning, foster bands, small-group support for learning/emotional needs, class councils, and special programs like Anne Frank events and police drawing contests.[2][8]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Recent successes include bronze at the 2026 Berlin Rapid Chess Championship (class IV, grades up to 6), winning police teddy bears in a drawing contest (3rd graders from 70 entries), and #1 in Mitte for Girls’ & Boys’ Day participation (2025).[1][8] The school maintains a sportbetontes Profil, theater focus, and transitions after 4th or 6th grade, with a 2023/24 school inspection noted.[3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Enrollment info references "Schulanmeldung Schuljahr 2026"; open house/events like Tag der offenen Tür (e.g., 16.01.2025) offer tours and virtual info; supports neuzugänge without German via lerngruppen.[1][3][7] As a public school, admission follows Berlin district procedures.
