description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Grundschule am Birkenhain operates under the guiding motto **"Gemeinsam leben - lernen - stark sein"** (Together live - learn - be strong), emphasizing an open, diverse, and engaged school community that fosters individual child development within a strong collective.[5][1] The philosophy prioritizes students' real-life contexts, social environments, and individuality, integrating them mindfully into school life while promoting social learning, cultural diversity as an opportunity, and democratic values through participation.[5][1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
The school has a long history, blending tradition with modern pedagogical concepts in Berlin-Spandau's Wilhelmstadt district, serving a **multicultural and vibrant community** of approximately 477 students (as of September 2024).[1][2][5] Leadership includes principal **Kevin Kirchner** (or Herr Kirchner), deputy principal Frau Dormanns, administrative head Frau Hein, secretary Frau Hönighaus, and coordinating educator Frau Möller.[1][3] Facilities support an **offene Ganztagbetreuung (OGB)** all-day program with on-site Hortbetreuung (after-school care), Mensa (cafeteria), Elterncafé (parents' café), computer room, and spaces for activities; it features modern IT equipment and hosts events like the school hort and mensa.[1][2][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a public **Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6), the curriculum includes mandatory English from early grades, Jahrgangsbezogenes Lernen (year-group based learning) from grade 3, and focuses on individualized support through social learning, reading promotion, temporary learning groups in math and language, and media education.[1][2][5] The comprehensive all-day concept harmonizes instruction, care, and leisure, with offerings like **KulturPiloten** cultural projects, woodworking workshop, career projects, sponsor runs, tournaments, Bundesjugendspiele, and digital competency training.[5][2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in a **multicultural environment** with participation via Klassenrat (class council), Schülerparlament (student parliament), and Elterncafé, alongside extracurriculars (AGs) in the eFöB after-school program such as **Mentale Stärke** (mental strength), **Faust-Los** (non-contact self-defense), **Ninja Parcour** (obstacle course), **Sport**, **Tech4Girls**, and others like relaxation, school newspaper, music, theater, circus, football, basketball, keyboard, and school band.[5][2][1] After-school options emphasize creativity, movement, team spirit, and skills like self-assertion and digital tech for grades 2-4 and 4-6.[5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific academic outcomes, test results, or awards are detailed in the sources; recent events include a donation handover to Ronald McDonald Haus (January 2026), Christmas bazaar invitation (December 2025), and school tours for new students (June 2024).[5] The school actively recruits school psychologists, educators, and volunteers for a freiwilliges Jahr (voluntary year), indicating community engagement and growth focus.[5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public Grundschule**, admissions follow Berlin's standard public school enrollment process via eGovernment/Schulanmeldung, with no fees mentioned; parents can visit during **Elternsprechzeiten** (Mon-Fri 8-9 AM, Tue 1:30-2:30 PM).[5][2] Secretariat hours are Mon-Wed 8 AM-1:30 PM, Thu 8 AM-1 PM, Fri 8 AM-12 PM; contact via phone (030-3519360) or email (info@grundschule-am-birkenhain.de).[5][1]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school positions itself as a vibrant learning and living space where values of **respect, diversity, and responsibility** are actively lived, fostering an environment of confidence and new opportunities for the 2025/26 school year.[8] It emphasizes handlungsorientierter (action-oriented) and project-based learning to develop competencies in subject knowledge, methods, social skills, and practical application across disciplines.[4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Construction began in 1988 for a polytechnic school under DDR plans, but political changes in 1989-1990 led to its opening as a primary school in 1991/92, with the name "Grundschule am Brandenburger Tor" assigned in 1994.[1] The modern building features a fully renovated roof (2001), a multimedia center (2000), a "Kompetenzzentrum Technik" for technical-handicraft education (2003), expanded play and group spaces (2005/06), well-equipped courtyards with play fields, climbing walls, sand pits, and colorful surfaces (completed 2007), and a recent photovoltaic system producing 86,000 kWh annually.[1][2][6] Accessibility includes parking for disabled persons, wheelchair elevators, and restrooms.[3] The school is publicly owned, located in Berlin-Mitte at Wilhelmstraße 52, 10117 Berlin, with leadership by Principal Katrin Jüdes.[1][3][5] Community ties include partnerships like Staatliche Europa-Schule Berlin (SESB) with Russian as partner language since 2004.[1][3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6), the curriculum prioritizes action-oriented math with material-based methods for numeracy, geometry, and stochastics; natural sciences as a main subject; society studies as a main subject with historical sources and presentations (in Russian for SESB classes); project-based German; English as first foreign language; informatics in grades 5/6; and SESB program with Russian instruction.[3][4] Specialized facilities support wood/metal workshops, pottery, painting, electronics, and a computer lab.[3] Extracurricular Arbeitsgemeinschaften (AGs) via Technische Jugendfreizeit- u. Bildungsgesellschaft include basketball, football, martial arts, theater, creative arts, music, and technical projects.[3][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in full-day options like gebundener Ganztagbetrieb (GGB) and offene Ganztagbetreuung (OGB), open learning groups in early phases, social-pedagogical after-school care (since 2005, for grades 1-6), and AGs on Mondays/Wednesdays focusing on practical skills, personality development, and age-specific activities.[3][7] Math olympiad participation with certificate notation is available, alongside networked themes emphasizing handlungskompetenzen (action competencies).[4] The school welcomes a diverse community near landmarks like Brandenburger Tor, with good public transport access.[5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Limited specific outcomes are detailed; students can qualify for Berlin math olympiad victories with progress noted on reports.[4] The school has an inspection report available and offers professional development in management.[3][9] Facilities expansions and SESB participation highlight ongoing improvements.[1][3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public **tuition-free Grundschule**, admissions follow Berlin public school processes; contact sekretariat@gsabt.schule.berlin.de or (030) 20 62 94-30 for enrollment, with after-school care spots available via application for grades 1-6.[1][3][7] Entry before 8:10 AM via Wilhelmstraße/Wilhelmplatz; afterward, use video intercom.[5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Established as a public primary school in Berlin's Spandau/Staaken district, the school celebrated its **50th anniversary** with projects like a jubilee film and dance performance.[1][2][5] It serves approximately **421 students** (as of 27.09.2024) in a **three-track (dreizügige)** structure on campus with on-site daycare (Hortbetreuung).[1][2] Community engagement includes parent-led events such as summer/winter festivals via the Förderverein and parent representatives; school social work by Frau Hanisch and Frau Friese strengthens local networks.[1][2][5][7] Facilities support **sport-focused profile** from grade 3, reliable half-day school (VHG), and open all-day operations (OGB) with ergänzende Förderung und Betreuung (eFöB).[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum features **JÜL (jahrgangsübergreifendes Lernen)** in grades 1-3 for differentiated, inclusive instruction oriented to individual starting points.[1][2][5] A **sportbetontes Profil** begins in grade 3.[1] Core school hours run 7:30-13:30, with detailed schedule including movement breaks (e.g., 9:40-9:55), blocks up to 15:05 for extended days, and meals/pauses for grades 4-6.[2] **Free lunch** is available upon registration; all-day care extends to 6:00-18:00 for families needing more support.[5]

Students experience project-based activities like circus weeks, excursions (e.g., Polar Experience exhibit, library visits, traffic school), and events such as Schulfasching (carnival).[1][5] Daily routines include reliable supervision, social learning emphasis, and support from school social workers for personal/school issues.[1][2][5][7] Extracurriculars feature parental involvement in festivals and a sport profile enhancing physical education.[1][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school marks its **50-year milestone** with cultural outputs like a jubilee film and dance show, highlighting community and artistic achievements.[5] It maintains steady enrollment of around 400-421 students and operates as a reliable public institution with inclusive programs, though specific academic metrics are not detailed in available content.[1][2] Note: Some website data is marked as outdated and pending updates.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Information focuses on school start for 2026/27, covering enrollment, expectations, and procedures via the website's "Start in die Schule" section.[5] As a **public tuition-free Grundschule**, admission follows Berlin public school processes; contact sekretariat@brandwerder.de or +49 30 36709520 for details.[3][4] Upcoming first school day is 05.01.2026.[5]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school promotes holistic development of students, fostering an ecological mindset in a life-relevant way, while emphasizing respect, tolerance, helpfulness, and honesty to create a positive atmosphere.[1][4] It aims to build responsibility, punctuality, independence, and rule awareness through tools like conflict mediators, class rules, and service duties; instruction motivates self-directed learning via differentiated methods, interdisciplinary projects, and continuous reflection.[1][4][7] The school serves as a welcoming space with rituals, collaborations with external partners, health promotion, media literacy, and smooth transitions to kindergartens and secondary schools.[1][4][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin's Marzahn-Mitte district near Bürgerpark Marzahn, the public primary school (Grundschule, school number 10G09) serves about 750 students from 31 classes, reflecting cultural diversity with children from Germany and many other countries.[1][5] Enrollment grew significantly from 307 in 2010 to 474 in 2015, prompting a 2015 modular extension building (MEB) costing 4.1 million EUR, fully operational by 2017 alongside renovations of the original DDR-era structure.[2] Staff includes 75 teachers, 16 educators, 3 integration educators, 2 special educators, and 2 social workers; facilities feature a school kitchen, woodworking shop, kiln, snoezelen room, movement room, school-neighborhood center, garden, sports field, laptops, and tablets.[1][2][5] It's an open all-day school (offene Ganztagsschule).[1]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Core instruction builds basic competencies with method training, differentiation, and interdisciplinary projects; from grade 3, support classes occur in small groups or workshops, shifting to subject-specific teaching by grades 5-6 with class-crossing projects.[4][5][7] English is the first foreign language; offerings include social/method competencies (1 hour/week in grades 5/6), diverse support (1-5 hours/week in grades 1-4), environmental education in the school garden, school social work, and language development via reading mentors.[5] Changes include eliminating sub-grades in German for grades 3-6 starting mid-year 2025/2026.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in extracurricular activities like schoolyard buddies, judo, health sports, drumming, sports carousel, ceramics, and woodworking; partnerships provide art workshops at Jugendkunstschule “Lin Jaldati” and external learning sites with specialists.[1][5][6] Events foster community, such as Christmas singing with crafts and food stations, and competitions like ISTAF Schools Cup sprints/staffels.[1][6] Upcoming 2026 events: report card day (Jan 30), carnival (Feb 17), study day closure (Mar 9), math day (Mar 19), parent-teacher conferences (May 5), bridge day closure (May 15), Pentecost Tuesday closure (May 26), and summer holiday care (Aug 3-21).[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Students from grades 3-4 achieved 4th place in ISTAF Schools Cup 50m sprints and relays among Berlin schools, qualifying for the Uber Arena finale.[1][6][8] Participation in external competitions like Street Soccer and Känguru math contest highlights athletic and academic engagement.[5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a tuition-free public Grundschule, admission follows Berlin's standard local enrollment process for children aged 6-10 (grades 1-4, extending to 6 in some cases); contact sekretariat@buergerpark.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 9309864 for details.[1][5][9] Principal: Dirk Montag.[5]

Grundschule am Buschgraben emphasizes a structured environment providing orientation through rules and rituals, focusing on values like politeness and non-violent conflict resolution, with strong transparency between school and parents via parent-teacher days, utilization of parental skills, and joint events.[1] The school's three core pillars are **Bewegung** (movement), **Partizipation** (participation), and **Medienbildung** (media education), alongside additional profiles in health-oriented education, nutrition, and student involvement.[1][6][7] It promotes integration, including Willkommensklassen (welcome classes) for children from refugee families since 2015, with patenklassen (sponsor classes) to foster language learning and prevent exclusion.[1]

Built in **1972** as a masonry structure with steel concrete ceilings, separate aula (auditorium), and mensa (cafeteria), the school is located in the green south of Zehlendorf near a nature reserve, between Museumsdorf Düppel and Kleinmachnow; it has been renovated and energy-efficiently upgraded with a colorful facade.[1][7][8] It serves approximately **245 students** with **24 teachers** and **17 educators** in a **gebundener Ganztagbetrieb** (bound all-day operation) from 7:30 AM to 4:00 PM, not bound by Berlin's residential principle, allowing applications from any location.[1][6][7] Community features include student assemblies for information sharing and honoring achievements, a Förderverein (support association) for projects, Schulstation Regenbogen for pause activities, anti-violence training, social learning, and a Theater-AG since 2011; it also integrates with local kindergartens and eco-gardens.[1][5][7] Accessibility includes rollstuhlgerechtes WC (wheelchair-accessible toilet) and ramp access.[6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Grundschule** (primary school, Schulnummer 06G10) in Berlin's Steglitz-Zehlendorf district (Zehlendorf ortsteil), it offers standard curriculum with **English** as the first foreign language, jahrgangsbezogenen Lerngruppen (year-group learning groups) in the early phase, and gebundener Ganztagbetrieb.[3][6] Programs highlight **Bewegung** (movement), **Ernährung** (nutrition), **Beteiligung** (participation), gesundheitsbetontes Profil (health-focused profile), and Medienbildung; extracurriculars include theater via Schulstation Regenbogen and potential eco-garden cooperation.[1][5][6][7] Welcome classes support refugee integration with German language focus and mainstreaming into patenklassen.[1]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Grundschule am Campus Hedwig, operated by SozDia Stiftung Berlin, emphasizes **democracy, diversity, inclusion, and sustainability** as core values rooted in a social-diakonic Christian tradition. The school aims to be a "school for all" welcoming children from diverse social backgrounds, religions, and life situations, with an evangelical profile that offers Christian religious experiences without proselytizing.[1][2] It promotes individual potential through self-directed learning in mixed-age groups, democratic participation, community engagement, and preparation for self-determined lives in society.[1][2] The philosophy integrates the Situationsansatz (situation-based planning from children's interests) and aligns with Berlin's Bildungsprogramm for inclusive education.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded by SozDia Stiftung as an integrated part of the **Campus Hedwig** in Berlin-Hohenschönhausen (Lichtenberg district), the school is in its fifth year of operation as of recent updates.[5] It currently operates from an **interim location** at Möllendorffstr. 33, 10367 Berlin, with a planned permanent building in Hedwigstraße starting Q1 2026.[3][5] The campus includes neighboring SozDia facilities like Kita Hedwig, Stadtteilzentrum Hedwig, and FLEXible Erziehungshilfen, fostering a "Bildungshaus" model for family-school-Kita cooperation.[1][2][8] Community ties emphasize local Kiez (neighborhood) integration, with open ganztag (all-day) offerings and events like Sprechzeiten (office hours).[2][3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **private Grundschule (primary school, grades 1-4 or 1-6 in Berlin context)**, it is a genehmigte Ersatzschule (approved substitute school) with **offene Ganztagbetreuung (OGB, open all-day care)** and English as the first foreign language.[4] The curriculum features **altersgemischte Lerngruppen (mixed-age learning groups)**, self-shaped learning environments, and the subject "Engagement und Gemeinschaft" for civic exploration.[2] It supports inclusive, democratic education without specified graduation pathways (as primary level), focusing on transition from Kita to school.[1][2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in **democratic participation** (e.g., shaping school environment, city exploration, personal projects), nature and resource-aware activities, and community networking via Campus Hedwig.[1][2] Extracurriculars include ganztag care linking to MiniClub, with recent news of 14 new students joining diverse activities.[7] Broader SozDia offerings (e.g., sports, cultural events via Stadtteilzentrum) enhance experiences, though school-specific details are limited.[8]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or student outcomes (e.g., Prüfungsergebnisse) are detailed in the content. Certifications noted are organizational, such as SozDia Kitas' 100% bio-certified meals (DE-BE-039-07546-B, valid to 2026), but not school-specific.[PDF: Bio-Zertifikat]. The school is officially listed by Berlin's Senatsverwaltung as a private Grundschule with OGB.[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
**Enrollment process**: Parents submit an interest form or contact directly; non-binding until a Betreuungsvertrag (care contract) is signed. Sprechzeiten available (e.g., Mon/Wed 8-10 AM at school, Wed/Thu afternoons at MiniClub). Contact: grundschule.hedwig@sozdia.de, Schulleitung Gérard Leitz (030 55 15 25 83 / 0176 60 34 75 34).[2][3] No specific requirements listed beyond openness to all families.

**Fees**: A **sozialverträgliches Schulgeld (socially compatible school fee)** is required due to partial state funding for "freie Trägerschaft" (independent sponsorship), necessitating parental contributions alongside SozDia funds to ensure accessibility across social milieus.[1] No exact EUR amounts, ranges, or structures (e.g., income-based, monthly) are specified on school pages. Related Kita documents mention a **23 EUR monthly parents' contribution for Mittagessen (lunch)**, waivable via berlinpass-BuT for eligible families (e.g., Bürgergeld recipients).[PDF: berlinpass-BuT]. Bio-meals are provided without extra Kita costs.[1][PDF: Bio-Zertifikat].

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's motto is **"Lernen mit Erfolg"** (Learning with Success), emphasizing the development of personal, social, academic, and methodological competencies through individual support and achievement.[Main Page][About Page] Core principles include mutual respect, recognition of others' achievements, handling criticism constructively, zero tolerance for violence or exclusion, and community involvement of students, staff, and parents.[About Page] The school fosters a supportive environment where all members—"Wir sind die Schule"—prioritize respectful interaction, conflict resolution, and parental engagement in learning processes.[About Page]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Lichtenrade amid single-family homes, the school features a large campus with a traffic yard for bike training, sports field, climbing playground (Brückenhof), leisure building, and renovated gym hall with energy-efficient upgrades and views of the sports field.[1][2][3][6] Recent additions include a new cafeteria and care rooms.[3] The facility is wheelchair-accessible (ground level or ramp) and includes a green classroom and school garden.[2] Staff comprises approximately **40 teachers** and **20 educators** for all-day care managed by Nachbarschaftsheim Schöneberg.[2][7] Community events involve a parents' association (Förderverein) funding projects via markets and sales.[Main Page] Leadership: Principal Axel Schmidt.[2][9]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a public **Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6), it follows Berlin's Rahmenlehrplan, offering English or French as the first foreign language, open all-day care (OGB), and year-group learning groups in the early phase.[2][Curriculum Page] Additional programs include school social work, conflict mediators, PC courses, and working groups (AGs) in table tennis, soccer, choir, homepage design, French, and chess.[2][1] Temporary homogeneous learning groups prepare grade 6 students for secondary school transitions, especially in math.[4] All-day concept covers mornings, afternoons, and vacations with child-led activities, outings, and creative/sports options.[1][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in hands-on activities like Bundesjugendspiele (federal youth games), reading competitions, Christmas singing with choir, and community events such as Lichtermarkt cookie sales and nativity displays.[Main Page] Daily routines emphasize respect (no cell phones/smartwatches on premises), weather-adaptive attendance, and structured schedules (e.g., first day 8:00-11:30, optional care to 13:30).[Main Page][4] All-day care promotes creativity, sports, and new trials in a diverse team environment focused on individual needs and parent partnerships.[1] Extracurriculars and ferienbetreuung (vacation care) prioritize child interests with Berlin-wide excursions.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Recent highlights include a grade 6b winner in the reading competition, successful Bundesjugendspiele with strong student performances, and enthusiastic participation in Christmas events and markets benefiting school projects.[Main Page] The renovated gym meets modern standards cost-effectively by reusing elements.[6] No specific exam results or quantitative outcomes detailed; school emphasizes individual successes and community integration.[About Page][2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school**, admission follows Berlin public enrollment processes for local residents (Lichtenrade, Tempelhof-Schöneberg district).[School Information][2][5] New students (Lernanfänger) have dedicated onboarding like Einschulungsfeier (enrollment ceremony) and parents' evenings.[Main Page][4] First school day typically features shortened hours with optional all-day care; parents report absences via email.[Main Page] Open house events listed in Berlin calendars.[2]

# Grundschule am Eichenwald - Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

**Grundschule am Eichenwald** operates as a **public, tuition-free elementary school** in Berlin-Spandau with a clear commitment to fostering self-directed learning and social development.[3][6]

The school's core mission emphasizes **tolerance, mutual respect, and trust** as foundational values.[6] The educational philosophy centers on creating a learning culture that supports **self-determined and self-responsible learning** while recognizing individual strengths and abilities of each child.[6] The school adopts a collaborative approach to education through the "L – E – S – E" model (Lehrer*innen - Erzieher*innen - Schüler*innen - Eltern), positioning educators as reliable advisors and companions in children's learning processes.[6]

A distinctive feature is the school's identity as a **"Reading School"** ("Lesende Schule"), with intensive reading promotion integrated across all subjects through a comprehensive reading curriculum.[1][3] The school also holds official status as a **Berlin Climate School** since 2016/2017, actively promoting environmental responsibility.[3]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Setting**

The school is situated in a quiet, family-home-dominated residential area near the Spandauer Forst (Spandau Forest) in the Hakenfelde district of Berlin-Spandau.[8] The location between the state forest and the Havel River provides a green, nature-oriented environment.[3]

**Student and Staff Population**

The school serves approximately **450 students** across grades 1-6, taught by **35 teachers**.[3] The student population is characterized by educationally-oriented families living within the school's catchment area, with parents demonstrating high engagement in school matters.[8]

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school features modern facilities including:[2][7]
- Four action rooms (Aktionsräume)
- A dedicated homework room
- School library (Schüler*innenbibliothek)
- Computer lab
- Gymnasium (accessible three times weekly for after-school care)
- Cafeteria (Mensa)
- Extensive outdoor grounds with climbing equipment and school football pitch
- Accessible design with ramps and barrier-free connections

Recent renovations included restructured administrative spaces, an enlarged teacher's room, and a new two-story connecting corridor with sustainable wood construction linking the administration building to the main school building.[9]

**Community Engagement**

The school maintains an active parent association (Förderkreis) that supports school development and enrichment initiatives.[3][6] The school also collaborates with external partners including kindergartens, secondary schools, sports clubs, and youth services.[6]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Core Academic Structure**

The school operates as an **open all-day school** (Offene Ganztagsgrundschule - OGB) with **age-cohort classes** (Jahrgangsbezogene Klassen - JabL) beginning in grade 3.[1]

**Language Programs**

Students learn **English as the primary foreign language**, with **French** also offered.[7] Additional language options including Latin, French, and Spanish are available as specialized subjects or working groups (AGs) from grade 5 onwards.[1]

**Specialized Programs and Initiatives**

- **Reading Curriculum**: Comprehensive reading promotion across all subjects with an annual reading project week[3]
- **Climate Education**: Official participation in Berlin's Climate Schools program with integrated environmental responsibility initiatives[3]
- **Social-Emotional Learning**: Conflict resolution program featuring student "Konfliktlotsen" (conflict mediators)[7]
- **Student Publications**: School newspaper "Eichenwaldpost"[7]

**Working Groups and Enrichment**

Students can participate in diverse after-school activities including music, football, theater, relaxation, acrobatics, and crafts.[2]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Pedagogical Approach**

The school emphasizes **individualized learning processes** with differentiated support and challenge for each student.[6][8] Teachers and educators work collaboratively to provide comprehensive support, with educators accompanying classroom instruction on an hourly basis.[2]

**All-Day Care Structure**

The **open all-day program** (eFöB - ergänzende Förderung und Betreuung) is operated by the **AWO Kreisverband Berlin-Spandau** (Workers' Welfare Association).[1][2] Care is provided by state-certified educators and integration specialists who support children's development toward independence and responsibility.[2]

**School Climate**

The school cultivates a positive, child-centered school climate with emphasis on creating a welcoming environment where all community members feel comfortable.[8] Student testimonials highlight friendly teachers, strong peer relationships, and enjoyable outdoor activities during breaks.[3]

- **Positive school climate** oriented toward children's wellbeing

The school holds official designation as a **Berlin Climate School** and participates in the **"Begabtenförderung" (Gifted Promotion) School Network** of the West Region (Spandau).[7]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Grundschule am Hollerbusch emphasizes **respect, tolerance, and participation** as core values, prioritizing the general well-being, joy of life, physical fitness, and social contacts of all students.[2][5] As a **bound full-day school (gebundene Ganztagsschule)** in the FlexGanztag trial and a Bonus- and Startchancen-Schule since 2024/25, it focuses on health education, integration of students with special needs, and a friendly, motivating atmosphere where students enjoy learning.[1][2][3][5] The school integrates health projects, movement breaks, and biorhythm-oriented scheduling to reduce stress and promote holistic development.[1][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in **1991** as a school with an **integration focus**, it serves approximately **800 students** (previously noted as 540) in Berlin's Marzahn-Hellersdorf district, Hellersdorf area, near Gärten der Welt (U-Bahn Line 5).[1][2][3][4] Located at Erich-Kästner-Str. 64, 12619 Berlin, it operates across **two school buildings** with a spacious outdoor area, including accessibility features like wheelchair ramps, elevators, and toilets.[2][5] The community includes motivated staff (36 teachers, 27 educators previously), partnerships with tjfbg gGmbH, and a parent-led Förderverein with ~100 members supporting events and improvements.[2][3][6] Leadership transitioned from Karin Ronneberger (since 1990) to Heike Tietze.[3][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum features **English as the first foreign language** from grades 1-2 (introductory), with project-based and differentiated courses in grades 3-6, including theater workshops, A/B/förder courses in German, English, and math to prepare for gymnasium.[1][5][7] It includes **health-focused projects** (e.g., nutrition in grade 1, addiction prevention in grades 5-6), psychomotorics, dynamic sitting, and annual themes like violence prevention.[1][3] Digital tools like Anton app track learning progress, and year-group learning groups support early phases.[5][7] The school exceeds Berlin averages in performance comparisons despite 60% of students from socially disadvantaged families.[3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students benefit from **free full-day care** from 7:30-16:00, with mandatory sessions until 14:30 including lessons, support, learning time, and lunch; optional FlexModul afterward upon semester registration.[2] Traditions foster engagement: school year start with methods week, peer-led enrollment, AGs (extracurriculars), recitation/reading/kangaroo competitions, team trips, festivals, Christmas market, sports day.[2] Integration support via Schulstation for needs like hearing impairments, psychomotorics room, and inclusive classes in an accessible neighborhood.[1][5] Recent events include school garden award, AG descriptions, and holiday plans.[4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school holds a **strong reputation**, consistently **above Berlin Grundschule averages** in performance, with positive inspections noting friendly, motivated students and rare conflicts.[1][3] Achievements include **1st place for school garden**, health education standouts, and successful integration since 1991.[1][4] It attracts full enrollment without recruitment issues and partners with community initiatives like wirlegendieplattehoch.de.[3][4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public Grundschule**, admission follows Berlin's standard process for children in the catchment area (Hellersdorf, Marzahn-Hellersdorf), with enrollment involving data processing for assessments, reports, and support needs; no fees apply.[5][7][8] The school handles full classes easily due to its reputation.[3]

# Grundschule am Insulaner - School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Grundschule am Insulaner is a **public primary school with a music-focused profile** located in the Steglitz-Zehlendorf district of Berlin[2]. The school's educational philosophy emphasizes developing students' individual artistic and creative abilities through a holistic, project-based learning approach[3]. The school prioritizes fostering social competencies including responsibility, empathy, and cooperation within a climate of mutual respect and trust[3].

The school pursues a **sustainable and comprehensive learning approach** that integrates musical, sporting, artistic, and environmental activities[3]. Students are guided to take responsibility for themselves, their peers, and the environment[3]. The school views sports as a fixed component of its educational program[3].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Development**

Grundschule am Insulaner was **opened on March 28, 1962** after approximately two years of construction[1]. The school buildings were designed by architects Völker & Grosse and were constructed to address overcrowding at the nearby 5. Grundschule Berlin[1]. In **1987, the school underwent a curriculum restructuring to establish its music-focused emphasis**[1].

**Current Size and Staffing**

The school serves approximately **413 students with 41 teaching staff members**[2]. It operates as a **full-day primary school (Ganztagsgrundschule)** with supplementary care for students[2].

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school campus includes[1]:
- Four original school buildings
- A small cafeteria
- A sports field
- A school garden
- A wetland biotope (Feuchtbiotop)
- A dedicated after-school care building (Hort-Haus), completed in summer 2008[1]

The school underwent a **schoolyard renovation in 2007**, and the campus features distinctive colorful building designs as part of its renewal efforts[1].

**Community and Support Services**

The school employs a school social worker from tandem BTL who serves as a direct contact for students and families facing school or home-related challenges[4]. The social work program focuses on prevention, mediation, inclusion, counseling, and crisis intervention[4].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Music-Focused Profile**

Grundschule am Insulaner is **one of 15 music-focused primary schools in Berlin**[2]. The music emphasis is mandatory and structured by grade level[5]:

| Grade Level | Music Curriculum |
|---|---|
| Grade 1 | Musical early childhood education (Musikalische Früherziehung) |
| Grades 2-3 | Recorder instruction in small groups, singing-playing-dancing, choir, music theater, rhythmic movement education, instrument switching opportunities |
| Grades 4-6 | Alto recorder, violin, cello, flute, guitar, clarinet, saxophone, piano, orchestra, band, choir, Orff instruments |

Beginning in **Grade 2, students participate in one hour per week of group instrumental instruction**, and from Grade 2 onward, participation in a music working group is mandatory[5]. The school organizes music performances and projects both within and outside the school[5].

**Foreign Languages**

The school offers **English and French as foreign languages beginning in Grade 3**[1]. The school organizes **field trips to France and England** as part of language learning[6].

**Science and Technology**

The school implements a **science and technology program (TuWas!)** designed to develop students' interest in natural sciences and technology through hands-on, inquiry-based learning[2]. Students engage in independent exploration, observation, and documentation using experiment kits compiled by the Free University of Berlin[2]. The program aims to foster teamwork, language development, and scientific methodology[2].

**Teaching Structure**

In **Grades 5 and 6, subject-matter specialists primarily teach the curriculum**[3]. Specialist teachers instruct music and sports from Grade 1 onward, and foreign language specialists teach from Grade 3[3].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**All-Day Care and Meal Services**

The school provides **supplementary care from 1:30 PM to 4:00 PM with no need to demonstrate need**[2]. Additional care is available before regular school hours and after 4:00 PM for families with documented need[2]. **All students receive free lunch in the school cafeteria**, with two meal options available daily that students choose themselves[2].

**Extracurricular Activities**

The school organizes **project-oriented musical, sporting, artistic, and other activities** across class and subject boundaries[3]. Traditional activities include a **joint singing event before Christmas** held in the gymnasium for Grades 1-3 and Grades 4-6[9]. The school participates in the **LifeTeachUs partner program**, enriching daily school life through real-world knowledge and experience from parents, siblings, and grandparents[8].

**School Traditions**

Recent community events include a **sponsorship run with a school zone demonstration** (September 2025) and a **neighborhood flea market** (September 2025)[8]. The school organizes **summer camp trips** and **winter holiday programs** through its after-school care program (Happylaner)[8].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The search results do not provide specific information about standardized test results, academic rankings, or measurable student achievement outcomes. The school's primary documented achievement is its **established reputation as one of Berlin's 15 music-focused primary schools**, with a structured instrumental music program that has been in place since 1987[2][5].

## 6. ADMISSIONS

The search results do not contain specific information about admissions procedures, enrollment requirements, application deadlines, or selection criteria. As a public school in Berlin, Grundschule am Insulaner follows standard Berlin public school enrollment procedures, but these details are not provided in the available sources.

- **Email:** sekretariat@06g17.schule.berlin.de[6]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Grundschule am Karpfenteich is a performance-oriented public primary school emphasizing natural sciences (**naturwissenschaftlichem Schwerpunkt**) and early English instruction (**Frühenglisch**) from the school entry phase, preparing most students after 6th grade to transition to gymnasiums.[1][2][3][6] It promotes "Bildung heute - für die Welt von morgen" (Education today - for the world of tomorrow), integrating computer education, language emphasis, environmental education, and a mathematically-natural scientific profile.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Lichterfelde (Steglitz-Zehlendorf district), the school serves the local community with wheelchair-accessible facilities including a rollstuhlgerechtes WC and ramp access.[1][4] Leadership includes Rektorin Frau Susanne Möllenberg, Konrektorin N.N., extended leadership (Frau Birkner, Frau Rex, Frau Vomberg), Sekretariat Frau Rowek (Mo-Fr 07:30-14:30), Hausmeister Herr Mielke, and an on-site Kinderhaus am Karpfenteich for extended care of ~250 students in grades 1-6.[1][5][6][7] Contact: Hildburghauser Str. 135-145, 12209 Berlin; Tel. 030 7200 699-0; Email info@karpfenteich.schule.berlin.de.[1][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum features a **mathematisch-naturwissenschaftliches Profil**, early English as the first foreign language, open all-day care (**Offene Ganztagbetreuung - OGB**), cross-grade learning groups in the introductory phase (**Schulanfangsphase mit jahrgangsübergreifenden Lerngruppen**), language-focused (**sprachbetontes Profil**) and environmentally focused (**umweltbetontes Profil**) programs, plus environmental education and computer training.[1][6] It offers extended support through the on-site Kinderhaus.[7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in performance-oriented learning with a focus on sciences, early English, and all-day programs fostering cross-grade collaboration and environmental awareness; absence plans are shared via BID-Messenger from 20.11.2024, with daily updates.[6] Illness reports are handled via answering machine (07:00-08:00) or email; the school supports inclusive access for wheelchair users.[1][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Most students transition to gymnasiums after 6th grade, reflecting its leistungsorientierte (performance-oriented) approach; official profiles note exam results and management quality, though specific data is not detailed here.[1][3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public **Grundschule** (tuition-free), admissions follow Berlin's standard public school enrollment process via eGovernment/Schulanmeldung; contact the school or Senatsverwaltung for details.[1][6]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Grundschule am Kollwitzplatz positions itself as an **open all-day school** welcoming all children, emphasizing engagement, openness, and individualized learning to create a vibrant place for daily education and life.[1][3][4] It fosters holistic development of self-responsible, confident, and independent personalities through differentiated instruction adapted to each child's developmental stage, promotion of individual strengths and interests, social competencies, modern teaching methods, and openness to diverse cultures and challenges.[1][3] The school views its grounds as a socio-ecological living space and prioritizes comprehensive support, including suitable interventions for all children.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Built between 1972-1974 as a new school in Knaackstraße, it was named Grundschule am Kollwitzplatz on June 11, 1998, and has undergone significant developments including a new gym cornerstone in 1999 (topped out 2002), intercultural projects (1999-2003), pilot school program (2000-2003), courtyard redesign (2003-2004), extensive renovations (2003-2005), Horthaus handover (2007), Hortgarten opening (2008), and ALBA Youth Center in the old gym (2008).[5] Located at **Knaackstr. 67, 10435 Berlin (Prenzlauer Berg)**, it is publicly owned by Bezirksamt Pankow, led by Nils Meyer (previously Fr. Hartig), with 491 students, 21 classes, 27 teachers, 2 school assistants, 1 trainee, and 6 extended leadership members (as of 2017); Hort serves 391 children with 19 educators in 18 group rooms (17 workshops), a dining room, and kitchen on-site.[1][2][6] The community includes diverse backgrounds with learning groups for new arrivals without German skills, and partnerships like ALBA Berlin.[2][3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Core operations follow a reliable half-day schedule from **7:30 to 13:30**, extendable via on-site Schulhort with over 10 workshops for crafts, building, drawing, theater, pottery, meditation, and gymnastics.[3][4] Instruction features mixed-age classes (1-2) with experienced teams using modern didactics and methods tailored to individual paces; upper classes emphasize subject-specific teaching.[3] Profiles from grade 3 include **sportbetonte Klassen** (extra 2 weekly sports hours with ALBA coaches, plus year-round activities fostering coordination, conditioning, cognition, teamwork, and confidence) and **Schulversuch Französisch**; other offerings: English/French as first foreign languages, choir, two dance AGs, chess AG.[1][3][6][8] Additional structures: binnendifferenzierung in grades 5/6, student-led class councils, mediators, elective IgEL courses, Reggio-inspired workshop principle in Hort.[1][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in individualized, versatile learning in a diverse environment with half-day reliability, optional Hort for afternoon activities, sport-focused classes, language experiments, and AGs like dance and chess.[3][4] Extracurriculars include podcasts ("Kolle-Cast" on school meals, Bundesliga; episodes Nov 2025-Jan 2026), midday meals, open group work, and inter-year learning groups in early phases.[1][2][10] Recent challenges like teacher shortages and support needs post-COVID/Ukraine prompted proposals for shorter 40-minute lessons and later starts (pending approval).[9]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific academic outcomes or awards are detailed, but achievements include sustained profiles in sports (since 2009) and French (since 2022/23), infrastructure upgrades (e.g., gym, Horthaus, gardens), pilot projects, and active student media like podcasts.[3][5][8] Enrollment stability at ~491 students (2017) with comprehensive staffing supports ongoing operations.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free Grundschule**, admission follows Berlin's standard process with an enrollment period for 2025 noted and "Tag der offenen Tür 2025" events; first school day for 2024/25 newcomers was Sept 13, 2024, in the gym.[1][10] Learning groups support non-German speakers; sport class and French available from grade 3.[2][6][8]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Grundschule am Königsgraben emphasizes holistic child development through key focuses including the promotion of reading competence, social learning with training and deployment of **Streitschlichter** (conflict mediators), IT-supported learning to prepare for digitalization, development of work techniques and methodological competence, and integration of dance across all grade levels.[1][2] The school integrates evangelischer and katholischer Religionsunterricht alongside Lebenskundeunterricht, and received special funding from the Berlin Senate for its curriculum under the e-education Berlin Masterplan.[2] Social learning is prioritized in daily school life, with open educational offers, pedagogical workshops, and jahrgangsübergreifendes Lernen (cross-grade learning) in grades 1-2.[2][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

# Grundschule am Ritterfeld - School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The **Grundschule am Ritterfeld** is a public elementary school located in Berlin-Kladow, Spandau, dedicated to providing comprehensive education within a spacious, green campus environment.[1] The school emphasizes interdisciplinary learning and cultural engagement, as evidenced by projects spanning climate education, arts, and media literacy.[5] The institution prioritizes inclusive education and supports students with varying learning needs through specialized programs including gifted student promotion (Hochbegabtenförderung).[2]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Historical Background**

The school was **founded in 1958** and has evolved significantly over decades.[1] At its peak, the institution served approximately 1,000 students with seven parallel classes. Between 1985 and 1995, major infrastructure expansion occurred, including construction of a triple gymnasium, media center with music room, computer lab, student library, and auditorium.[4]

**Campus and Facilities**

The school occupies a **spacious, green campus** at Schallweg 31-45, 14089 Berlin (Kladow district).[1][2] The facility includes multiple classrooms, specialized subject rooms, and recreational areas. The school is **wheelchair accessible** with accessible elevators and restrooms, and provides parking for people with disabilities.[2] On-site services include a cafeteria (LUNA) with published meal plans and a Hort/VHG (after-school care program) offering afternoon supervision and diverse programming.[1]

**Leadership and Staff**

The school is led by **Schulleiter Sven Gehrmann**, with administrative support from additional leadership staff.[3]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Language Instruction**

The school offers **English as the primary foreign language** beginning in Grade 1, with **French available as a second foreign language** from Grade 1 onwards.[2]

**Specialized Programs**

- **Gifted student support (Bega-Kurse):** Programs for children and youth with special talents across all ability areas for advanced learning[5]
- **Bläserklassen (Wind band classes)** in grades 5-6[2]
- **Schulanfangsphase (School entry phase)** with age-grouped learning classes[2]

**Extracurricular Activities**

The school offers **working groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften)** including hockey, judo, and girls' football.[2] Recent projects demonstrate commitment to interdisciplinary learning, including climate-focused religious education, street art projects, and film production collaborations with external media organizations.[5]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

Students engage in **project-based learning** across multiple disciplines. Recent initiatives include:

- Climate education integrated with religious studies (Grade 6)[5]
- Art projects featuring street art techniques (Grade 5)[5]
- Film production and media literacy programs in partnership with the Berlin Children and Youth Film Association[5]
- Environmental stewardship through tree-planting initiatives for first-grade classes[5]

The school maintains an **open all-day care model (Offene Ganztagbetreuung)** providing structured afternoon programming.[2]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The school received the **Schulsportpreis (School Sports Award) in 2023**, recognizing sustained excellence in sports education over many years.[5] This award reflects the school's commitment to physical education and athletic development across the student body.

## 6. ADMISSIONS

As a **public school in Berlin**, the Grundschule am Ritterfeld operates under standard Berlin school enrollment procedures. The school is **four-form capable** (vierzügig konzipiert), though grades 5-6 operate as two-form due to the affiliated gymnasium's selective intake.[4] Interested families should contact the school directly for current enrollment information and procedures.

**Contact Information**

- **Phone:** +49 30 36500100
- **Email:** sekretariat@05g19.schule.berlin.de
- **Website:** ritterfeldschule.de
- **School Code:** 05G19

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Grundschule am Rüdesheimer Platz is a **Schwerpunktschule for Inklusion**, emphasizing inclusive education where children with and without disabilities learn together, fostering mutual support, respect, and individual development.[1][2][5][6][8][9] Its philosophy prioritizes qualified, experienced staff providing personalized support, open learning forms, social skills development, and comprehensive care beyond classroom instruction, as outlined in the school program.[5][6][8]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1956 as a pavilion school in Berlin's Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf district (Wilmersdorf locality), the school expanded with a barrier-free main building from 1995-1999, supporting inclusive and open learning.[3][5][7][9] Facilities include a spacious gym, Eberhard-Alexander-Burgh-Bibliothek, Haus in Johannisberger Straße for after-school care (Hort), extensive outdoor areas with playgrounds and a sports field, wheelchair-accessible features like elevators and ramps, and a canteen for midday meals.[2][5][6][7] It serves around 350 students in an open all-day structure, one of 27 primary schools in the district, with strong community engagement via events like flea markets and open days.[5][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a public **Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-4/6), it offers standard Berlin curriculum with **English** from grade 3 as the foreign language.[2][5] Key focuses include **inclusive education** for cognitive, physical, and motor development needs; year-spanning learning groups (1-3); elective programs in music-aesthetics, ecology, technology-science, computers, and French (grades 5-6); and project weeks.[2][3][5][9] All-day care runs reliably from 7:30-13:40, extendable to 16:00 or 18:00 for eligible students.[5]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students benefit from motivating instruction, inclusive environments promoting respect and peer support, conflict mediation training, and diverse activities like art calendars, poster contests (e.g., "Mobility of the Future"), graffiti workshops, philosophy projects, and temporary play streets.[5][6][9] The school provides online illness reporting, updated school rules (June 2025), and family resources like "What if...?" FAQs.[5] Extracurriculars include sports, library access, and all-day programs used by ~350 students.[5][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Recognized since 2004 as an integration focus school and 2011 regional inclusion model, it excels in social competence, inclusive practices, and broad stakeholder engagement.[6] Recent successes include students' posters in exhibitions, project week presentations, Berlin finals achievements, and renovated schoolyard reopening.[5][9] High demand from local and surrounding districts for its special needs support.[6]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public Grundschule**, admission follows Berlin's local enrollment based on residence, with priority for inclusive needs.[1][2] No registration required for open house events (e.g., early October); parents receive class codes for services like illness reporting.[5] Updated school rules require parental acknowledgment form at school year start.[5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Marzahn-Hellersdorf (Hellersdorf area) at Nossener Str. 85, 12627 Berlin, the public primary school serves approximately 500 students from a socially burdened catchment area with many families reliant on transfer benefits.[3][7] The school comprises a main building, secondary building, gym, and soccer field, adjacent to the Schleipfuhl wetland used as a learning site; it faced years of renovations, temporary relocations, and container use but gained a new wooden modular extension building (Holz-MEB) in 2024 via Berlin's school construction initiative, featuring bright classrooms, green roof, and facilities for all-day care and inclusive education.[1][2][3][4] Leadership is under Hendrik Zeidler.[1][7]

The school has demonstrated resilience by maintaining education in its catchment area despite multi-year renovations and temporary facilities, earning praise for exemplary efforts under challenging conditions.[1][2] Recent highlights include the 2024 opening of the Holz-MEB as part of Berlin's school offensive and community events like the 2025 Lesefest and art exhibitions.[1][2][4][8] No specific academic outcome data like test results is detailed in available sources.[7]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Grundschule am Tegelschen Ort operates under the motto **"Lernen. Erleben. Wachsen."** (Learn. Experience. Grow.), emphasizing a familial, nature-oriented elementary school in the tradition of Wilhelm von Humboldt, located near where he grew up.[1][5] The school fosters cognitive development ("Kopf" - head), practical skills ("Hand" - hand), and moral values ("Herz" - heart), combining classical education with modern approaches to cultivate curiosity, competence, and an ethical compass.[1][2] Parents, many alumni themselves, value this blend, supported by features like the school dog Milo for emotional support.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Established in **1895**, the school moved to its current building constructed in **1954** and expanded in **1994** (previously in Luisenstraße); it is largely single-story and barrier-free.[1][5] Serving **400 students** in **6 year-group homogeneous classes** taught on a **triple-track system** (dreizügig), it is located in Berlin-Reinickendorf (Tegel) at Gerlindeweg 11-23, 13505 Berlin, near the Havel River and Tegeler Forst.[1][5][8] The **2.8-hectare campus** includes two playgrounds (sport-oriented and pine-forest themed), climbing playground, football pitch, table tennis, streetball court, two gymnasiums, school garden, "green classroom," insect hotel, and occasional wildlife like foxes and wild boars.[1][5][7] Facilities feature age-appropriate furniture, smartboards, Wi-Fi, music room, computer room (11 workstations plus classroom islands), science room, library, teaching kitchen, multi-purpose room with stage, and wheelchair-accessible WC.[1][5] The team includes over **25 teachers** led by Dr. David Bordiehn, 14 educators for after-school care (EFöB/"Hort"), administration, janitorial staff, and school social workers.[1][5][6] A parents' association (Förderverein) supports events and projects like the green classroom.[1][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum promotes self-directed practice of competencies and cultural techniques alongside cognitive and moral development, with modern technology integration.[1] Key offerings include **English and French** as first foreign languages, open all-day care (OGB), and year-group learning groups in early phases.[5] Specialized programs feature gifted education ("Begabtenförderung") across five areas via "Begabtes Berlin," "Digitale Drehtür," "MINTmachtage" (STEM days with ZDF coverage), and external experts; the school participates in "Leistung macht Schule.".[2] Extracurriculars (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) cover flute, choir, computers, technology/crafts, football, swimming, pottery, chess, theater, French, English, school band, and gardening.[5][7] Nature-integrated learning uses the school garden and green classroom.[1][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in a nature-rich environment with direct classroom access to gardens and courtyards for younger classes, promoting play, movement, and hands-on learning.[1][5] Daily life includes pet therapy with Milo, wildlife encounters, and inclusive support from social workers and educators addressing learning hurdles.[1][6] Experiences blend academic rigor with exploration, such as STEM experiments, digital platforms, sailing initiatives, and community events like summer ferienfests.[2][7] After-school care (EFöB) extends learning with 14 educators, emphasizing well-rounded growth in a familial setting.[1][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific test results or outcomes are detailed, but achievements include expanded gifted programs funded by "Begabtes Berlin," participation in national initiatives like "Leistung macht Schule" and "Kinder forschen" (featured on ZDF), and external expert involvement.[2] The school trains pedagogical trainees successfully and maintains strong parent/alumni engagement via the Förderverein.[1][6][7] It offers barrier-free access and diverse extracurriculars, positioning it as a "segel-freundliche Schule" (sailing-friendly).[5][7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public Grundschule (school number 12G18), admission prioritizes the catchment area, but gifted students from outside may enroll after aptitude assessment.[2][5] Contact via email (12G18@12G18.schule.berlin.de or grundschule-tegelort@t-online.de), phone (+49 30 4311786), or fax (+49 30 4310058).[4][5] Open all-day and inclusive programs support broad access.[5]

# Grundschule am Teutoburger Platz - School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The **Grundschule am Teutoburger Platz** is dedicated to fostering successful learning for every child through a child-centered approach that emphasizes active engagement in the learning process[3]. The school's core philosophy centers on three key principles: creating a friendly and calm atmosphere where children develop curiosity and joy in learning through differentiated instruction and targeted support[3]; building a community grounded in tolerance and mutual respect where all members can voice opinions and contribute to school life[3]; and promoting environmental responsibility by teaching sustainability, resource conservation, and digital literacy[3].

The school views parents and community partners as essential collaborators in each child's educational journey[3].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Infrastructure**

The school was established in 1979 and underwent comprehensive renovation from 2010 to 2012, with total project costs of **6.017 million EUR** (including 1.750 million EUR from the Stadtumbau Ost program and EU EFRE funds)[5]. The renovation addressed critical infrastructure needs including fire safety, window and door replacement, and accessibility improvements, transforming the facility into a modern, well-equipped learning environment[5]. The school officially reopened in October 2012[5].

**Location and Community**

Located in the Prenzlauer Berg district of Pankow, Berlin, at Templiner Str. 1-3, 10119 Berlin[1], the school serves a catchment area characterized by education-oriented families[3]. The community is increasingly diverse, with a growing proportion of families of non-German origin[3].

**Facilities and Capacity**

The school accommodates approximately 480 students across six grade levels with three to four parallel classes[3]. The facility includes modern, well-equipped classrooms and recreational spaces[3]. Six classrooms serve dual purposes for both instruction and afternoon care, while eight additional "Horträume" (care rooms) provide leisure and recreational activities[4]. The school features wheelchair-accessible facilities and includes specialized spaces such as a school garden[2][5].

**Staffing**

The school employs 33 teachers who provide specialized instruction and 16 educators and caregivers who manage afternoon care and support services[3].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Core Subjects**

The curriculum encompasses the following subject areas: German, Mathematics, Natural Sciences, Music, Sport, Art, Religion, and Lebenskunde (Life Studies)[3]. The school offers specialized course options for grades 5 and 6[3].

**Language Education**

English is the primary foreign language offered[1].

**Special Programs and Support**

The school provides several specialized offerings:

- **Schulanfangsphase (School Entry Phase)**: Age-homogeneous learning groups with dedicated educator support for grades 1-2[3][4]
- **Welcome Classes**: Two dedicated classes for newly arrived students without German language skills[1][3]
- **Learning Support**: LRS (reading/writing disorder) and dyscalculia assistance[1]
- **Psychomotor Programs**: Physical development and coordination support[1]
- **Conflict Resolution**: Mediation and peer conflict resolution training[1]
- **Inclusion Services**: Comprehensive support for students with diverse learning needs[2]

**Instructional Approach**

Teaching methodology emphasizes learning techniques that develop student independence and active engagement with content[4]. Classrooms are thoughtfully designed with distinct learning areas (reading corners, building areas, material centers) and teachers clearly communicate learning objectives to students[4].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**All-Day Care Structure**

The school operates as a **Ganztagsschule in offener Form** (open-form all-day school) with afternoon care and enrichment activities[2]. The afternoon program includes supervised care and recreational opportunities[3].

**Extracurricular Activities**

The school offers diverse AG (working group) programs including:

- Pirate Sports[7]
- Cooking AG[7]
- Theater projects (TUSCH program)[6]
- Summer concerts[6]
- Student-led budget initiatives (Schüler*innenHaushalt)[6]

**Special Features**

- **Sports-Focused Profile**: The school emphasizes physical education and athletic development[2]
- **School Garden**: An active gardening program integrated into the curriculum[3][7]
- **Arts Integration**: Student artwork displayed in school galleries; theatrical and musical performances[6]
- **Virtual Campus Tour**: Online exploration of school facilities available[6]

**Afternoon Care (Hort)**

The school's hort program provides comprehensive afternoon supervision and enrichment, managed by trained educators[3]. Contact: 030 40 500 501[6].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Inspection Findings**

According to the 2016 school inspection report, the school's key strengths include:

- Strong promotion of social competencies among students[4]
- Effective teaching methodology that develops student learning techniques[4]
- Close collaboration between teaching staff and afternoon care teams[4]
- Child-centered classroom climate with clear communication of learning goals[4]
- Welcoming and well-organized physical environment[4]

**Community Engagement**

The **Förderverein** (support association) actively enhances school offerings through parent and community involvement, funding initiatives including hort program expansion, school beautification projects, financial support for student trips, school garden development, and playground maintenance[7].

## 6. ADMISSIONS

As a **public school** (öffentlich), the Grundschule am Teutoburger Platz operates under Berlin's public education system. Specific admissions procedures follow standard Berlin district enrollment processes through the Pankow district office. The school welcomes students of all backgrounds and provides specialized support for newly arrived students through dedicated welcome classes[3].

**Contact Information**

- **School Phone**: +49 30 44358050[1]
- **School Fax**: +49 30 44358051[1]
- **Email**: 03G16@03G16.schule.berlin.de[1]
- **Hort Phone**: 030 40 500 501[6]
- **Website**: schule-am-teute.de[1]
- **School Leadership**: Katrin Kosmata (Principal)[1]

Established in 1935 in Berlin-Reinickendorf's Lübars district, the school has evolved its teaching methods while maintaining strong teacher, educator, parent, and staff engagement; today, it serves 370 students in 15 classes across two buildings.[1][3] Facilities include chalk-free classrooms equipped with Activboards, specialized rooms for music, art, natural sciences, and computers (with 20 workstations), a small gym, sports field, school garden, after-school play area, water play area, library, learning path in a wooded area, and a bee museum; the campus is wheelchair-accessible with ramps and adapted restrooms.[1][2][3] Community ties are strong, with students mostly from educationally interested families in the village-like area, some from nearby Märkisches Viertel, and support from a parent-led Förderkreis for non-publicly funded projects.[1][2][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As an open all-day school (offene Ganztagsschule), it offers English as the first foreign language from early grades, profile electives (Wahlpflichtunterricht) for 5th and 6th graders (one weekly hour, switchable half-yearly), flute lessons in Schulanfangsphase, reading and language promotion, conflict mediation training, and diverse methods with year-group or cross-grade learning groups in early phases.[2][3] After-school care (Hort/eFöB) serves ~190 children with 15 educators, alongside working groups like football, young beekeepers, electrotechnics, Spanish (free), and paid options such as English language school, instrumental music, and computer courses.[1][3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in traditions like pre-Christmas crafting days, lantern runs, Christmas concerts, reading competitions (from early years to 6th grade), art workshops at the Atrium, and sports events; 6th graders take a 12-day England trip for language and culture immersion, while 5th graders visit Spreewald, Karl’s Erdbeerhof, or a lignite mine, with early-phase groups on end-of-year excursions.[4] Extracurriculars include school garden projects, joint events like sports competitions, foreign language contests, reading nights, and access to playgrounds, climbing areas, and creative water play.[1][2][4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTSTANDING OUTCOMES
The school has achieved Berlin championship in the Drumbo-Cup and regularly participates in district and city-wide sports; it maintains a violence-free environment with active student representation, conflict mediators, and communal events fostering identity.[2][4] Recognized as one of Berlin's few chalk-free schools with modern Activboards throughout.[1][3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public Grundschule (tuition-free), admissions follow Berlin's standard neighborhood-based enrollment for primary school ages (typically ages 6-10, classes 1-6); contact the secretariat for details, with open all-day care available.[3][7] No specific application processes or fees are mentioned beyond public system norms.[1]

# Grundschule am Weinmeisterhorn School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Grundschule am Weinmeisterhorn understands itself as a **place of life and learning** where the goal is to provide all students with optimal conditions for learning in an appropriate atmosphere.[1] The school emphasizes that it is important for all participants in school life to identify with the school and experience it as a place of learning and security.[5]

The school's educational approach is built on two essential pillars: **social competence and learning competence**.[5] The school aims to support and challenge children, placing their needs at the center within an atmosphere of trust, respectful interaction, and mutual appreciation.[5] To meet the different learning prerequisites of children, the school provides targeted methods and differentiated offerings.[1]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Community Context**

The Grundschule am Weinmeisterhorn is located in the quiet southern Wilhelmstadt district of Spandau, near the Rieselfelder (sewage treatment fields).[5] The school's name has historical roots: there were vineyards in Spandau maintained by wine masters (Weinmeister), and "Horn" refers to a peninsula on the Havel River.[3] The school celebrated its **50-year anniversary on June 24, 2017**.[8]

**Student Population and Capacity**

The school serves approximately **418 students** as of September 27, 2024.[2] Students come from various areas including Amalienhof, Wilhelmstadt, Kladow, Gatow, and surrounding regions.[6]

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school is **partially barrier-free**.[2] In September 2018, renovation work began on the former caretaker's residence to expand school capacity.[7] According to school leadership, the after-school care facility (Hort) has been operating at full capacity, with the Konrektorin noting in 2017 that it was "bursting at the seams" and requesting a larger building.[3]

**Community Partnerships**

Jugendwohnen im Kiez has provided school social work services at the school since the 2020/2021 school year.[1] The school cooperates with Havel-Kids Kinderbetreuung gGmbH for after-school care (eFöB) on the school grounds.[2]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Language Education**

The school offers **mandatory English instruction beginning in grade 3**, with early English instruction available from grade 1.[2]

**Learning Structure**

The school uses **year-level-based learning (Jahrgangsbezogenes Lernen - JabL)** in the school entry phase.[2]

**Extracurricular Activities and Offerings**

The school provides various activities and support programs including:[2]

- Learning time and homework supervision
- Hockey
- Chess
- Hip-Hop
- Dance
- Football

**School Format**

The Grundschule am Weinmeisterhorn is an **open all-day primary school (Offene Ganztagsgrundschule - OGB)**,[2] offering extended care options beyond traditional half-day instruction.

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

The school actively engages students in community and environmental learning. Recent activities documented on the school website include participation in the 51st Forest Run (Waldlauf) of Spandau schools and kite-flying activities with paired classes and families.[5] The school also conducts student budget elections, allowing children to participate in democratic decision-making about school resources.[5]

The school uses **iServ as a digital exchange platform** for communication and learning, implemented since the pandemic period.[5]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Grundschule am Weißen See prepares all students equally for a successful future by professionally imparting subject knowledge and strengthening social competencies with engagement and passion.[4] It operates as a reliable half-day primary school with an open all-day care offer, emphasizing social learning, support for those involved in the educational process, collaboration with mediators, and prevention programs for addiction and violence.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
This public primary school (Grundschule) is located in Berlin's Pankow district, Weißensee neighborhood, currently operating from Falkenberger Str. 31, 13088 Berlin, in a temporary building due to ongoing renovation of its listed historic main building at Amalienstraße 6, 13086 Berlin.[1][3][5][6] The original Backsteinbau school complex, designed by architect Reinhold Mittmann in the New Objectivity style, opened in 1931 as Weißensee's first secular co-educational school; it was repurposed during WWII, renamed multiple times under GDR rule (e.g., 7. Polytechnische Oberschule Weißensee "Waldemar Schmidt"), and became the Grundschule am Weißen See in 1994.[5] It serves around 424-431 students[1][3] in the Südliches Weißensee area, owned by Bezirksamt Pankow, led by Beatrice Meier-Bartsch.[2][6] Facilities include a protected ensemble with aula, two-story gym with rooftop garden, teacher housing, and school garden; current capacity is 2.5 Züge (class units), planned to expand to 4.[3][5] Community partnerships include djo-Bildungswerk for youth social work and cooperations with nearby schools (03K08, 03P11, 03Y14).[1][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The school follows Berlin's primary curriculum as a **Ganztagsschule in offener Form** (open all-day school), featuring a Schulanfangsphase (early primary phase) with year-group-specific learning groups and English as the first foreign language from grades 1-2 (one hour weekly).[2][6] Extracurricular activities (AG-Angebote) include sports (Fit am Ball, Handball, Judo, Ballschule), arts (Therapeutischer Tanz, Kindertanz, Tanzkurs, Malen, Arbeiten mit Alabaster, Keramik), music (Keyboard, Gitarre, Flöte), and Schulgarten; it holds "Gesunde Schule" certification and offers midday meals.[2][6] Open Ganztagsbetreuung (all-day care) is available.[6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience a supportive environment with social learning in classes and small groups, counseling, and prevention initiatives.[1] The open all-day format provides flexible care, midday provisioning, and diverse electives fostering physical, artistic, and musical skills alongside core academics and early English.[2][6] Team structures support fixed and project-based collaboration among staff.[7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Limited outcome data is available; the school maintains steady enrollment of 424-431 students and is expanding capacity amid renovation.[1][3] It has established team structures, partnerships for social work, and certifications like "Gesunde Schule."[1][6]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public tuition-free Grundschule in Berlin, admission follows local district procedures for primary school enrollment, typically based on residential address in the catchment area (Südliches Weißensee, Pankow).[3] Contact: Tel. +49 30 9269462, Fax +49 30 34353387, Email: 03G17@03G17.schule.berlin.de or schule.am.weissen.see.cids@t-online.de.[2][6]
