### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Jewish International School - Masorti-Grundschule integrates modern pedagogy with Jewish tradition and values, emphasizing Judaism as an inclusive, egalitarian, and open experience structured around the Jewish calendar, Tora, Halacha, and the seven Masorti principles that blend tradition with contemporary life.[1][2] It fosters a living encounter between Jewish past and future, promoting diversity, respect, project-based learning, responsibility, and lifelong friendships in a family-like community.[1][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 2018, the school operates as a state-recognized independent school ("staatlich anerkannte Schule in freier Trägerschaft") and open all-day school ("offene Ganztagsschule") with after-school programs and daycare until 5 p.m., including vacations.[1][5] Located at Roscherstr. 6, 10629 Berlin, it currently enrolls about 80 students (aiming for 120) and is part of Masorti Deutschland, affiliated with the global Masorti movement (Conservative Judaism), the Jewish Community of Berlin, and the Oranienburger Straße Synagogue.[1][2][3] Facilities include integration with a beautiful garden, own kitchen for vegetarian organic meals (shared with Kita), and use of Berlin's museums and forests as extended classrooms; it emphasizes a warm, international, diverse community welcoming varied cultural and Jewish backgrounds.[1][2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The school serves grades 1-6 (Grundschule), following the Berlin Rahmenplan curriculum with Hebrew as a core component in classroom and daily routines, alongside math, reading, writing, science, project-based and formal learning.[1][2] It operates as an all-day school with structured Jewish calendar integration; no secondary levels, Abitur, MSA, or BBR pathways mentioned.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in project-based activities like writing, rehearsing, and performing plays on Jewish holidays or other topics, collaborative work promoting respect and skills, and excursions to Berlin's museums and forests.[1] After-school programs until 5 p.m. include daycare during vacations; the school fosters friendships and community ties, with vegetarian organic meals and Hebrew/English-German language exposure via integrated Kita.[1][2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No awards, rankings, certifications, or specific student outcomes mentioned beyond state accreditation as an independent all-day school and current enrollment growth from founding in 2018.[1][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
The school has openings for Grundschule enrollment; it welcomes diverse Jewish families seeking an inclusive environment, with a dedicated international educator team.[1][2] No enrollment process details provided. ### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Joan-Miró-Grundschule operates as a public primary school (Grundschule) in Berlin's Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf district, featuring two branches under one roof: a standard German-language Regelschule and the bilingual Staatliche Europa-Schule Berlin (SESB) in German-Spanish.[1][3][4] Its philosophy emphasizes bilingual education, cultural tolerance, and preparation for life in a united Europe, with instruction in SESB delivered equally in German and Spanish by native-speaking teachers to achieve high proficiency in both languages alongside the German and Spanish curricula.[2][4] The school prioritizes art (inspired by painter Joan Miró), media literacy for creative and responsible use, sports for physical and social development, coding, health culture over violence prevention, and child protection through complaint management.[3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Built in 1899-1900 as the 19th and 20. Gemeindeschule Charlottenburg, the four-story main building in Academic Historicism style has housed various institutions, including a gymnasium attended by Walter Benjamin, a WWII lazaret, and other schools before becoming the Joan-Miró-Grundschule in 1969; the SESB branch started in 1994.[4][6] Located centrally at Bleibtreustr. 43, 10623 Berlin (City-West, Charlottenburg), it serves a diverse community: the Regelschule draws from the local area (nearly 70% non-German heritage language), while SESB attracts students citywide; in 2017/18, it had 735 students (495 non-German heritage speakers), 62 teachers, and 30 educators (20% male).[5][1] Facilities include a main building with grade-level classrooms, a side building for cross-grade learning (grades 1-3), specialized rooms for sciences, music, art, religion, life skills, teaching kitchen, aula, group rooms, multipurpose spaces for film/theater/dance, workshop, 2 PC rooms, library, 3 gyms, and three schoolyards with climbing, table tennis, basketball, soccer, 50m track, jump pit, trees, and green areas.[1][5] Accessible via S-Bahn (Savignyplatz), U-Bahn (Uhlandstraße), and buses (M19, M29, M49, X34).[5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The school offers a Regelschule following the standard German primary curriculum with English as the first foreign language, and the bilingual SESB aligning with both German and Spanish educational standards, including year-crossing groups in early phases and full-day operations.[1][2][4] Key focuses include art (KidS project), coding (state-funded), media education, sports, and projects like computer anatomy, vertical farming, and European competitions; after-school activities (AGs) cover reading, puzzles, games, crafts (stitching, skateboarding, hip-hop), sports (soccer, basketball), and cooking.[1][3] It provides bound full-day care (GGB), open full-day options, library access, and computer corners in classrooms.[1][2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in a supportive learning climate with cross-grade and international elements, including poetry weeks, school newspapers (Morgenstern), art projects (Henry Moore, self-portraits), concerts, and competitions; SESB fosters bilingual immersion and cultural understanding.[2][3] Extracurriculars promote health, fairness, creativity, and responsibility via sports, media projects, and AGs; the school emphasizes a vibrant learning environment with international orientation.[2][3] Inspections note strengths in learning conditions, teaching climate, and staff cooperation (rated B/C).[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Inspections highlight a learning-conducive climate, effective implementation of development goals, and potential for diverse, international school life; qualities like language promotion, learning conditions, and teaching structure rated B/C.[2] Participation in European student competitions, Arte projects, and ongoing news like poetry and farming initiatives reflect active engagement; nearly 70% non-German speakers achieve bilingual proficiency in SESB.[2][3][4] As Berlin's largest Grundschule in its district, it maintains strong enrollment and staff support.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Public school with enrollment for 2025/2026 and 2026/2027 at local Grundschule (Oct 7-18, 2024 for 2025/2026), plus SESB-specific application there and in-person at Joan-Miró-Grundschule; info evenings and documents available.[3][7][8] Contact: 030/887165910, 030/887165929, mail@joan-miro-grundschule.de.[1]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Johanna-Gerdes-Grundschule emphasizes learning with heart, combining over 100 years of tradition with personal closeness in a small private primary school where children feel secure and grow individually. Founded on reform pedagogy principles, it prioritizes individual support in small classes to optimally foster each child, offering an open, non-denominational environment welcoming diverse cultures, religions, ethnicities, and backgrounds.[Main Page][About Page]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Established in 1912 by pedagogue Johanna Gerdes based on reform pedagogy, the state-recognized private primary school is located near Mexikoplatz in Berlin-Schlachtensee (Steglitz-Zehlendorf district) at Salzachstr. 4, 14163 Berlin. It serves about 125 students across two school buildings with average class sizes of 18 children, ensuring individualized instruction. As an open all-day school, it includes reliable half-day care and supplementary support (Schulhort).[About Page][School Information][1][3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
This Grundschule covers grades 1-6 (Jahrgangstufen 1-6), with English taught from grade 1 and swimming from grade 2. It features a Schulanfangsphase with year-group learning groups and operates as a state-recognized Ersatzschule (substitute school).[About Page][3][1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
The school offers an open all-day structure with morning lessons, reliable half-day care (07:30-13:30, even during canceled classes), and optional Schulhort modules for extended care (e.g., early morning 06:00-07:30, afternoons 13:30-18:00, including holidays). Extracurriculars include Arbeitsgemeinschaften (e.g., swimming, dancing, natural sciences, chess, games, student newspaper), meals (Speisepläne), events like Laternenfest, and staff dinners prepared by students. School life highlights include workshops on safe media use.[Main Page][Fee/Tuition Pages][PDF: Hortanmeldeformular][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or outcome data (e.g., exam results, university placements) are mentioned in the provided content.

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
**Enrollment Process**: For grade 1 (2027/2028), add children to the interest list; selection starts about one year prior (October) with parent interviews, prioritizing siblings but evaluating individually—not first-come, first-served. Use the Anmeldeformular. For mid-year transfers (Quereinstieg), submit form with latest report card; if space available, parents meet class teacher, child hospitiates, and decision is joint (child, parents, teacher, principal). Otherwise, placed on waitlist.[Admissions Page]

**Fees** (all in EUR, from multiple sources; note slight date variations):
- **Monthly tuition (Schulgeld)**: 315.00 (classes 1-6) + 15.00 Vereinsbeitrag = **330.00 total** (effective 01.08.2024).[PDF: Beiträge][Admissions Page]
- **Sibling reductions**: 2nd child: 3/4 rate; 3rd: 1/2; further: 1/4 (if justified by income/wealth, with proof). Vereinsbeitrag only for one child. If two prior siblings attended ≥8 years combined, 3rd+ at 3/4 rate.[PDF: Beiträge]
- **Income-based reductions**: Possible if annual gross income ≤29,420 (for guardians), no relevant assets; annual application with proof (e.g., tax returns). May waive transport costs and 150.00 Vertragspauschale.[Admissions Page]
- **One-time fees at enrollment**: Kaution/deposit 945.00; Vertragspauschale 150.00 (waivable on low-income application).[PDF: Beiträge]
- **After-school care (Hort/Schulhort)**: Free for grades 1-3; income-based from grade 4 (apply via Berlin Service Portal to Jugendamt for Hortgutschein). Modules: e.g., Modul 1 (early morning), up to full-day (06:00-18:00).[PDF: Hortanmeldeformular][PDF: Hortgutschein beantragen]
- **Other**: Transport costs (pauschal per half-year, by class); Förderverein annual 60.00 (separate); older doc mentions 5.00/month per child/family.[PDF: Beiträge][PDF: Beitragsordnung des Fördervereins]
- School year: 01.08–31.07; payments from August.

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Johannes-Tews-Grundschule positions itself as a **"Schule für alle"** (school for all), inspired by its namesake Johannes Tews, emphasizing high educational and upbringing standards while fostering an inclusive environment for every child.[1][2][10] It prioritizes respectful interactions, diverse educational and leisure offerings, individual recognition of each child's unique abilities, and development of competencies, values, judgment, independence, and responsibility.[1][10] As an inclusive Berlin primary school, it welcomes children with special needs, particularly focusing on hearing-impaired students with specialized expertise, equipped classrooms, and supportive conditions across all grades, though full accessibility is limited due to missing barrier-free features.[1][10]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Built in **1927**, the school serves approximately **461-465 students** in grades 1-6, organized into 3-4 classes per year group, with about 280 participating in extended care.[1][4] A team of **over 50 staff** (33 teachers, 16 educators, administrative personnel, and caretaker) collaborates closely with parents, a Förderverein (parent support association since 1971 with over 70 members), and partners like Stadtteilzentrum Steglitz e.V.[1][2][4][6] Located in Berlin-Steglitz-Zehlendorf's Nikolassee district at Wasgenstr. 50, 14129 Berlin (main site), with branches at Tewsstr. 16A and Beskidenstraße 7, it features a scenic setting amid green spaces near Schlachtensee, multiple buildings (Haupthaus, Mensa, Neubau, Altbau, Villa), playgrounds, and recent expansions.[1][3][10] It operates as a public **Ganztagsschule** (all-day school) with open all-day care (OGB).[3]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The school follows Berlin's standard primary school curriculum as a reliable half-day school with mandatory lessons per the Grundschulverordnung, including year-group-specific classes and a Schulanfangsphase (grades 1-2 as a unit lasting 1-3 years based on performance).[4] It offers English as the first foreign language, open all-day care, year-group learning groups in early phases, and extended support for diverse needs in inclusive settings valuing student variety and potential.[3][4] Extracurricular elements include extensive AG-Angebote (activity groups), competitions, events, and advocacy for Berlin's 6-year primary model, proving benefits for grades 5-6 without early gymnasium transfer.[2][3][4] Free midday meals are ensured for all up to 13:30, with adjusted schedules.[scraped]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience care from **7:30 to 13:30** daily (independent of care contracts), with lessons starting at 7:40: 1st (7:40-8:25), 2nd (8:30-9:15), 3rd (9:20-10:05), short break, 4th (10:25-11:10), 5th (11:15-12:00), 30-min yard break with meals for grades 5-6, 6th (12:30-13:15), optional 7th (13:20-14:05), 8th (14:05-14:50).[scraped] Lessons adapt to weather (e.g., outdoors on hot days) but continue fully with class-based supervision until end time; no short days planned.[scraped] Daily life includes current events, substitute plans, concepts, reports, parent involvement, and a happy atmosphere with friends, values education, and special focus on hearing support.[1][2][10] Activities encompass school community events, Fasching celebrations, probe lessons, and partnerships.[scraped]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Content highlights ongoing efforts toward a "good school" via team-parent partnerships, but specific achievements like competition wins or test results are not detailed.[2] It notes successful schedule adjustments post-2019 trial for earlier meals without extending days, supporting more children (9 classes currently).[scraped] Participation in programs like SPI since 08.2021 via Stadtteilzentrum Steglitz, extensive AGs, events, and Förderverein-backed enhancements indicate active school life.[2][5][6] No quantitative outcomes (e.g., Prüfungsergebnisse) are provided in sources.[3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free Grundschule**, admission follows Berlin's standard public school enrollment process (Schulanmeldung), open to all local children with inclusive practices.[3][scraped] Trial lessons for 6th-grade prospects and open house days (Tag der offenen Tür) are offered; contact via sekretariat@jtg-berlin.de or +49 30 8032072 for inquiries.[2][3][scraped] Parents are invited to explore via website, staff contacts, and events; no specific barriers noted beyond general inclusivity limits.[2]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Johann-Georg-Elser-Schule emphasizes **individualized student support** beyond the standard Berlin curriculum framework, with personalized weekly task folders allowing advanced students extra challenges and others targeted practice to avoid boredom or overload.[1][5][6] Key priorities include creating a safe, nurturing environment like a "second home," fostering strong personalities through parental collaboration, promoting peaceful social behavior, and integrating full-day care with school for holistic development across diverse faiths.[3][5] The approach rejects frontal teaching in favor of self-paced learning in mixed-age groups (grades 1-3 and 4-6), with no homework as class time suffices for practice.[1][6][7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in the **2002/03 school year** by Gisela Klatt and her husband Ingo (school leader and supporting educator), the school started with 12 students and grew to over 50 (expandable to 70), operated by the Neuzeitlich-Christliche Initiative e.V. (NCI), a nonprofit founded in 1989 for child care.[1][5][7] Located in an Altbau (old building) at **Mahlower Str. 23-24, 12049 Berlin-Neukölln**, with classrooms in the front ground floor (grades 1-3) and back building (grades 4-6), it features home-like rooms with carpets, shelves, and group tables; accessibility includes a wheelchair lift and WC.[1][4][6] It serves a community-oriented setting with close parent involvement via representatives, caring for children of various beliefs.[5]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **state-approved private replacement primary school (Grundschule, grades 1-6)** with **teilgebundener Ganztagsbetrieb** (partially binding all-day operation), it follows the Berlin Rahmenplan but extends it through individualized learning in two mixed-age groups.[1][3][4][5] English instruction starts in grades 1-2 via songs and games, advancing to writing in grades 3-4; strong emphasis on sports exceeds public school standards.[1][3][6] Affiliated with an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (integrated secondary school)** for post-primary pathways.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
**Full-day care** integrates school with afternoon Hort (after-school care) offering free-time activities, workshops, sports, outdoor play, music (singing, dancing, instruments), and movement programs from toddler age, prioritizing physical, mental, and social development in a friendly, non-authoritarian environment.[1][3][5] Sports and outdoor activities are central, with daily free play and child gymnastics.[3]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or outcome data (e.g., exam results) mentioned in available content; growth from 12 to 110 students noted, with recent addition of a Realschulklasse.[4][7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Johann-Peter-Hebel-Grundschule emphasizes individualized learning paths, project-based work, and fostering curiosity and joy in learning to accommodate diverse student backgrounds and paces.[2][4] It promotes mutual support among older and younger students through mottos like "Zeig mir mal, wie du das machst!" and prioritizes comprehensive personal development in a reliable half-day structure with open all-day options.[1][2][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Originally opened in 1906 as the II. Humanistische Gymnasium and inaugurated in its current building in 1910 by architect Otto Herrnring, the school transitioned to a Grundschule and has grown with expansions in 2011 and 2024/25 to support increasing enrollment.[1][4][5] Located centrally in Berlin-Wilmersdorf (Emser Str. 50, near U-Bahns Hohenzollernplatz and Fehrbelliner Platz), it serves about 540 students aged 6-12 from diverse nations in 23 regular classes plus three Willkommensklassen for those with limited German skills, supported by 65 educators including teachers, childcare workers, and social workers.[3][4] Facilities include original 1910 building with 15 classrooms, specialist rooms (Nawi, art, music, sports, computer lab with 24 stations), 2011 extension with SAPH classrooms, info lab, cafeteria, after-school rooms, and 2024 extension with additional classrooms, art/Nawi rooms, and workspaces; the large, greened schoolyard supports sports.[4][5]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Schwerpunktschule Französisch**, the school offers French or English as the first foreign language from grade 3, with grade 6 students eligible for DELF Prim A1 certification via extracurricular activities.[1][2][3] It uses jahrgangsübergreifendes Lernen (JüL) in eight mixed groups for grades 1/2 (transitioning to four grade-homogeneous groups per grade from 2026/27 in SAPH), and four homogeneous groups from grade 3; emphasizes research-based learning in grades 5/6 via district projects on philosophizing and inquiry.[1][2][4] General projects include Aktion Toter Winkel, France mobile, literature festival, method training, cyberbullying education, and patron projects; competitions like The Big Challenge and Spelling Bee.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience reliable half-day care from 07:30 to 13:30/14:15 (up to 14:30 max), even during cancellations, in well-equipped group rooms, with open all-day options including extracurricular activities (AGs) in sports (basketball, football, fencing, badminton, table tennis), arts (dance, guitar, choir, painting, theater), sciences, and more to build skills, social competencies, and friendships across ages.[3][6][7] Diverse offerings like student newspaper, chess, and nature experiments provide non-graded interest pursuit; multiprofessional teams ensure support in mornings and afternoons.[4][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific achievement metrics like test scores or awards are detailed in available content, though the school highlights sustained growth to 540 students, recent expansions, and high engagement in AGs and competitions.[2][4][6] It maintains reliable operations as a Verlässliche Halbtagsschule since 2005/06 and supports integration via Willkommensklassen.[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Online registration for first-graders (Schulanfänger) is available; immediate online absence reporting is required. As a public Grundschule, admission follows Berlin's standard public school processes via berlin.de services.[3]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Johann-Strauss-Grundschule emphasizes developing competencies for independent living, self-reliance, and responsible future orientation through structured learning, rituals, and a creative-musical environment.[3][5] It prioritizes musical education as a core profile, fostering creativity, social acceptance of differences, and a positive school climate via programs like music emphasis, school social work, and emotional-social competence promotion (e.g., SPI program).[2][5][6][8]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in a quiet residential area of Biesdorf-Nord (Berlin-Marzahn-Hellersdorf), the school serves around 450-650 students with under 5% non-German native speakers and is fully barrier-free with ramps and elevators.[4][5][7] Named Johann-Strauss-Grundschule since 1995, it marked 30 years of music emphasis in 2025; the building supports inclusive participation, including for students with physical-motor needs.[7][8] Community features include Schulsozialarbeit for conflict mediation and social learning, Buddy-Programm, and partnerships like JAO gGmbH and Schlaufuchs for after-school support.[1][5][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
This six-year, district-open Grundschule offers a music-emphasized profile with free instrumental (e.g., piano, guitar, violin, recorder) and ensemble lessons (e.g., choir, orchestra, drums, theater, dance, sound technology) starting after early musical education in the flexible entry phase.[2][3][5][8] Structure includes year-crossing groups in early years (1st/2nd as "stars/moons"), core lessons in mixed-age home groups for structure, and year-level courses for basics; open all-day operation (OGB) from grade 3 in homogeneous groups.[2][3][5] Additional focuses: English as first foreign language, individualized learning, language/cultural/political education, and promotion for diverse needs.[2][3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience structured daily routines with rituals, open Lernstudio for supported self-study (no prior registration needed, documented via email), and holiday programs like Winterferienbetreuung with meals and activities until 14:30.[1][3] Extracurriculars include Schulweg-Challenge for eco-mobility and traffic safety, Ferien-Camps ("Fit für die Oberschule" for grades 5-6 focusing on math/German/English), music events (e.g., Adventszauber, 6K United choir), and pause/afternoon offers promoting play, health, and community.[1][5] Positive atmosphere noted with disciplined, confident behavior.[7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Inspections highlight a pleasant learning atmosphere, active student engagement, and strengths in communication/problem-solving promotion, though recommend more focus on reading/methods competence.[7] Music profile provides free specialized training among Berlin's 17 such Grundschulen; participates in SPI for emotional-social development since 2021.[6][8] Strong inclusivity for diverse learners, with temporary groups and high individualization.[5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public Grundschule (tuition-free), admissions follow Berlin's standard Schulanmeldung process via bezirksoffene structure; contact sekretariat for details (Mo-Th 7:30-15:00, Fr 7:30-14:00, prior appointment preferred).[2][3] Open to local district with flexible entry phase.[3]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Jüdische Traditionsschule combines secular and Jewish education, fostering natural curiosity, joy in learning, and a strong Jewish foundation while aligning with Berlin's state curriculum frameworks. Its philosophy emphasizes small classes, individualized support, multilingual communication (German, Hebrew, English from Grade 1), personal development into confident individuals with societal responsibility, care, compassion, tolerance, and an international community atmosphere.[1][4] The school promotes lifelong learning, personality strengthening, and competencies like factual, methodological, social, and personal skills through structured, stimulating environments.[3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Part of the Pears Jüdische Campus opened on June 25, 2023, in Berlin-Wilmersdorf, this is the largest Jewish institution for education, culture, and sports in Berlin since the Shoah, initiated by Chabad Berlin on an 8,000 sqm site including a Kita, Grundschule, Gymnasium, art studios, music studios, a 100-seat cinema, and sports/event hall.[5] Located at Westfälische Str. 15, 10709 Berlin, it serves an international community of students from around the world with about 60 teachers and educators; leadership includes Schulleitung Heike Michalak and Schulrabbiner David Gewirtz.[4][7] Facilities support computer-aided instruction, state sports halls/swimming pools, kosher meals, smartboards, and online teaching.[2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a state-approved Ersatzschule (substitute school), it follows Berlin Senate frameworks for Grundschule (primary) and Gymnasium (grammar school), offering equivalent qualifications to public schools including G8 Abitur after eight years.[1][4] Focuses on small, mixed-age groups from age 5, with emphasis on languages: English from Grade 1 (playful, integrated themes), Hebrew (as profile subject with increased hours, also for Religion/Judaistik), German; trilingual instruction starts early.[1][4] Includes Jewish life, festivals, customs integrated into secular subjects; general education covers self-perception, community living, nature, space, history, technology, media.[3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Daily care from 7:30 to 15:40 includes small learning groups, extracurricular activities, movement-based education in state sports facilities to build independence/creativity, creative arts under professional guidance, and bus service.[4][6] Early English via life-world themes (colors, school items, body parts) fosters communication; strong Jewish identity, kosher meals, and international tolerance promote holistic growth.[2][4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or student outcome statistics (e.g., Abitur rates) are mentioned in the content. The school is state-accredited, offers scholarships and stipends, and aligns with public school standards for high-level education leading to Abitur.[1][4]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Judith-Kerr-Grundschule is a **state-run Europa-Schule** emphasizing bilingual education in **German and French**, fostering a multicultural environment that promotes language immersion and cultural exchange between German and French-speaking communities.[1][2][5] As a **gebundene Ganztagsgrundschule** (bound all-day primary school) under Berlin's "Berlin macht ganztags Schule" initiative, it integrates instruction and extracurricular activities into a unified pedagogical approach, with mandatory participation for all students on 4 days from 8:00 to 16:00, extending care potentially from 6:00 to 18:00 or during holidays under certain conditions.[1][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Named after Jewish author Judith Kerr, the school operates from a **historic building** constructed in 1900 (Historismus style, protected as a landmark, expanded 1907-1908) in Berlin-Schmargendorf (Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf district).[2][4][5] The parent-led **Förderverein**, founded in 1992, supports the school's francophone focus by employing 7-8 native French-speaking educators for all-day care and cultural projects, drawing students from francophone households across Berlin without a strict catchment area.[2][3] Leadership includes principal **Bettina Knust-Marquardt**, deputy **Christelle Heycke-Lautissier**, ganztag coordinator **Silke Trutz**, administrative head **Frau Haack**, secretary **Tracy Bediako** (on maternity leave, covered by Frau Zaremba), and caretaker **Markus Hoffmann**.[1] Contact: Friedrichshaller Str. 13, 14199 Berlin; Tel: (030) 897 99 40; Email: verwaltung@04G27.schule.berlin.de.[1][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Primarily German-taught with **French immersion** from grade 1 in two levels (**Muttersprache** for fluent speakers, **Partnersprache** for learners), delivered through games, activities, and native speakers rather than systematic lessons in early years; English as first foreign language.[1][2][5] Offers **gebundener Ganztagbetrieb** (bound all-day program), small French library, and extracurriculars like chess, streetdance, basketball.[5] Partnerships include Institut français Berlin, local music school, and international ties (e.g., Nancy pedagogy school, African music/ballet).[5] As a **Staatliche Europa-Schule Berlin (SESB)**, it prepares for bilingual certifications up to Abi-Bac (German Abitur + French Baccalauréat).[2][8]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience **integrated all-day learning** combining academics with play, sports, and language immersion, supported by native French educators in ganztag care.[1][3][5] Activities include sponsor runs, cross-country finals (e.g., 2025/2026 Bezirksentscheid), and cultural events like "Rallye-Lecture" with French Gymnasium partners.[1][9] Meals provided; strike contingencies offer shortened classes with optional notbetreuung.[1] Förderverein enhances francophone projects; post-16:00 or holiday care requires eFöB contract application.[1][3]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Recent highlights include successful **Sponsorenlauf** (sponsor run) and qualification for **Crosslauf Bezirksentscheid Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf 2025/2026**.[1] As Berlin's unique SESB primary, it provides balanced bilingual support via parent-funded staff, promoting multicultural cohesion.[3][8] No specific exam results or outcomes detailed in sources.[5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Open to students from francophone households across Berlin districts (no explicit catchment); registration provides info on applications, including eFöB for extended care.[1][2][3] Tag der offenen Tür (open day) listed in school calendar.[5]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Karl-Friedrich-Friesen-Grundschule emphasizes providing parents with insights into school life, answering key questions for new families on topics like child care duration, parental involvement, information sources, and lunch provisions, while fostering active parental participation in school activities.[6] The school prioritizes collaboration with all parents to support child well-being and school improvement.[1][6] It operates as an **offene Ganztagsschule** (open all-day school) with a focus on **"Bewegte Schule"** (active/movement-oriented school), promoting physical activity through movement offers and extracurriculars under the motto "Bewegte Schule - ein Tor ins Leben" (Active School - A Gateway to Life).[1][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Marzahn (Marzahn-Hellersdorf district), a residential area with 1980s Plattenbau (prefabricated concrete) housing, the school serves over 500 students, primarily from the surrounding neighborhood, described as socially challenged.[1][3] The main building was constructed alongside nearby housing, underwent energy-efficient renovation and expansion in 2014/15, with a schoolyard redesigned by students in 2016; additional features include a natural playground (former kindergarten garden with hills, trees, balance courses, climbing equipment, and ball fields), pavilions for community use, a running track, small sports fields, a school garden, and access to a district gym.[1][3][4] Enrollment has risen sharply since 2011 (414 students in 2016).[3] School social work has been offered since 2021/22 to support personal development, social skills, crisis management, and family-school coordination.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Instruction occurs in year-group-based classes, with the early school phase (Schulanfangsphase) using year-specific learning groups.[1][2][4] Programs include English as the first foreign language, open all-day care (Offene Ganztagbetreuung/OGB), inclusion experiments (participating in INKA1 model since 2011/12, integrating 23 children as of inspection report), and a sports-focused profile ("Bewegte Grundschule").[2][4] Afternoon offerings feature movement activities, working groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften), and partnerships with ALBA Berlin (basketball) and Circus Cabuwazi.[1] A 2016 inspection noted strengths in social learning offers but development needs in lesson planning, cooperative methods, and school-wide structures.[4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
The school day runs from 7:45 to 14:30 across 7 periods, with breaks: 1st (7:45-8:30), 2nd (8:40-9:25, break), 3rd (9:45-10:30), 4th (10:40-11:25, break), 5th (11:50-12:35), 6th (12:45-13:30), 7th (13:45-14:30).[6] All-day care is available openly via Hort (after-school care).[1][2][6] Experiences emphasize a pleasant atmosphere with child-friendly classrooms, though past inspections highlighted teacher-centered lessons with limited cooperative learning.[4] Extracurriculars promote movement and social skills; school social work provides daily support (Mon-Fri 8:00-14:00).[1][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Strengths include diverse social learning programs and facilities like the renovated building and active schoolyard.[4] The school participates in inclusion models and sports initiatives, with growing enrollment reflecting community demand.[3] A 2016 inspection identified development needs in school structures, process documentation, and instruction quality, recommending collaboration with oversight for improvement.[4] No recent achievement data or test results are detailed in available content.

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public Grundschule** (tuition-free), admission follows Berlin's standard compulsory schooling process for local residents aged 6-10/13.[1][2] New parents are directed to website resources for initial questions; open house days (Tag der offenen Tür) are announced in the district calendar.[2][6] Contact school leadership for appointments.[6][7]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Karlsgarten-Schule is a public primary school (Grundschule) emphasizing inclusivity, cultural diversity, and multilingualism, welcoming every child regardless of social, ethnic, cultural, or religious background.[5] As an **Inklusionsschule**, it promotes joint learning for all students, using year-spanning learning groups (JÜL) to foster collaboration, responsibility, and individual strengths in a supportive environment.[5][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
The school is located at Karlsgartenstr. 7, 12049 Berlin (Neukölln), adjacent to Volkspark Hasenheide, offering a green, quiet setting without traffic noise, with a landscaped schoolyard.[5] The site has a 400-year history as a hunting ground, gymnastics site, excursion venue, artists' training area (including the "Karlskiste"), and post-WWII school construction site after demolishing the Etablissement Karlsgarten.[1][2] A permanent historical exhibition, created by students in 1999-2000 and updated 2018-2023, features 30 large panels, photos, films, and time-witness interviews, with public openings like September 13, 2025.[1][2][3] The community reflects northern Neukölln's diversity with ~450 students from many nations; facilities include a library, cafeteria, computer room, wheelchair-accessible elevator and WC, and extended care until 18:00.[5][6] Extracurriculars cover crafts, student newspaper, computer, dance, theater, circus, sports (hockey, volleyball, basketball, table tennis), and gender-specific groups.[6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum features **year-spanning learning groups (1-3)**, support for new arrivals without German knowledge, open all-day care (OGB), and an inclusive school experiment.[6] English is taught as the first foreign language; additional programs include SINUS for math/science efficiency and a school start phase with mixed-age groups.[6] The teilgebundene Ganztagsschule provides instruction until 13:30 and hort (after-school care) until 18:00 daily.[5]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in collaborative, inclusive learning in a diverse, socially focused neighborhood, with activities like circus AG (historical collaborations with artists), student newspaper "Karlchen" (featuring time-witness interviews), and international projects (e.g., with Oakthorpe Primary School, London, on Berlin landmarks).[1][2][6][7] A 2011 documentation highlights dynamic teaching, sensory exercises, conflict resolution training, and pride in the renovated schoolyard for play and movement.[8] Cultural exhibits and events like "48h Neukölln" and "Goldener Floh" award enrich experiences.[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school maintains a historical exhibition writing its own history since 1999, with student-led expansions, public viewings, and awards like "Goldener Flohs."[2] Participation in cultural projects, such as the 2018 Museum Neukölln exhibit "Neukölln macht Schule 1968-2018," and international exchanges demonstrate community impact.[7][9] As a hospitationsschule with SINUS involvement, it supports inclusive, efficient STEM education.[6]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
No specific admissions details are available; as a public Grundschule, enrollment follows Berlin's standard local assignment process for primary schools, open to all eligible children in the district.[6]

Key focuses include health-promoting life and learning, literacy development, method learning, independence, creativity, and social competence, with strong parent involvement and transition support from kindergarten.[2][3][4]

Located in a quiet, green area of southern Lichtenberg, Berlin (Lisztstr. 6, 10318), the school serves over 680 students with about 74 educators and recently renovated facilities including modern classrooms, science/art/music rooms, library, computer rooms, student kitchen, movement room (opened Dec 2025), green classroom, playgrounds, sports areas (running track, long jump, football goals, basketball, volleyball), climbing equipment, and nearby swimming hall.[1][2][4] It features open all-day care (OGB) from 6:00-18:00, health-focused profile, wheelchair accessibility, and community events like ski camps, Christmas singing, lantern parades, and parent associations.[1][2][5]

# Karl-Weise-Schule (Grundschule) - School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Karl-Weise-Schule is guided by six core principles that define its educational approach[2][3]:

- **Values education and care** as a precious good
- **Promotes democratic processes** in school life
- **Prioritizes relationships** as the foundation for learning and development
- **Celebrates cultural and personal diversity** as a strength
- **Functions as a life space** (Lebensort) for students
- **Fosters environmental and nature awareness**

The school's educational philosophy centers on enabling access to education for all children and developing them into independent young people[2]. Teachers and educators employ diverse learning and teaching methods to meet individual student needs[2].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Basic Information**
Karl-Weise-Schule is a **public primary school** (Grundschule) located in Berlin's Neukölln district[1]. The school serves approximately **350 students** from highly diverse backgrounds[2].

**Community Composition**
The school is notably international, with students speaking different languages at home and families originating from various countries[2]. This multicultural environment is viewed as both an enriching opportunity and an educational challenge[2].

**School Structure and Hours**
The school operates as a **bound all-day school** (gebundene Ganztagsschule), with students spending their day between 8:00 and 16:00 (4:00 PM)[2]. The daily schedule features well-structured rhythms that alternate between learning and play phases[2].

**Facilities and Resources**
The school includes the following facilities[1]:
- Library
- Computer lab
- Teaching kitchen
- Psychomotor room
- Accessible restroom facilities (at branch location: Lichtenrader Str. 4a)

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Language Education**
English is offered as the first foreign language[1].

**Educational Profiles and Special Programs**
The school emphasizes multiple educational focuses[1][2]:
- **Health-focused profile** (gesundheitsbetontes Profil)
- **Theater-focused profile** (theaterbetontes Profil)
- **Nature-based learning** through the "Schule im Wald" (Forest School) project

**Organizational Structure**
The school features[1]:
- Cross-grade learning groups for grades 1-3
- School entry phase with cross-grade learning groups
- School station (Schulstation) for student support

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Forest School Program**
One of the school's primary pedagogical focuses is the monthly "Schule im Wald" (Forest School) program[2]. Eight classes participate in regular forest visits to Grünau, where students learn continuously about the forest across different seasons, engage in physical movement, and experience nature-based learning[2].

**Extracurricular Activities**
The school offers diverse working groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) including[1]:
- Basketball and Fußball (soccer)
- Theater and dance
- Art and creative design (Kunst und Künstler, Kreatives Gestalten, Basteln mit Perlen)
- Music with Orff instruments
- Cooking and baking
- Yoga for children
- Movement games
- Online newspaper
- Culture and research activities
- Buddy project participation

**Special Programs**
The school provides a school station (Schulstation) offering additional student support services[1].

As a public school in Berlin, Karl-Weise-Schule follows standard Berlin school registration procedures[1][4][6]. Specific admission requirements and registration details are available through the school's website and the Berlin education authority's school registration guide.

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Kastanienbaum-Grundschule emphasizes making the **art of learning** experiential through a broad curriculum with focuses on **language, art, music, natural sciences, movement**, and innovative learning forms to prepare students for a changing world.[Main Page][4][8] The school's philosophy views education as developing character, self-determination, lifelong learning, and curiosity, beyond mere knowledge acquisition, fostering personal growth in an inclusive environment where children from diverse nations and cultures learn together.[Curriculum Page][Main Page][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded on **31 May 1834** as "Communal-Armenschule des 8. Bezirks" in Linienstraße 162, the school moved to its current **Gipsstraße 23A, 10119 Berlin-Mitte** location in 1874 on a former gypsum factory site, designed by architect Hermann Blankenstein; the building is under **monument protection**.[6][1] It serves ~**420 students** across **18 classes** (grades 1-6), with ~**35 teachers** and **18 educators** for supplementary support, in a culturally diverse community recognized locally for high satisfaction.[Main Page][3][1][2] Facilities include a **mensa** for healthy meals, lockers for rent, open all-day care (OGB), and spaces for exhibitions and forums; strong parent involvement occurs via Gesamtelternvertretung (GEV), Förderverein, events, and committees.[Main Page][1][2][4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMICS PROGRAMS
As a **public Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6), the curriculum promotes inclusive, heterogeneous learning with **English** as the first foreign language from early grades, optional AGs in Spanish, French, and Turkish, and emphases on **reading, arts, music, natural sciences, and movement**.[1][7][Curriculum Page] Extracurricular **Arbeitsgemeinschaften (AGs)** include judo, basketball, handball, choir, musical early education, chess, fitness, dance, research club, nature crafts, theater, and more; year-group mixed classes in early phases, with projects in art, music, sports, and sciences.[1][2][Main Page] The 2021 Schulprogramm (approved 16 Feb 2021) guides individualized, student-centered instruction.[Curriculum Page][9]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in a vibrant all-day schedule with **Ferienbetreuung** (holiday care) during breaks like Sommerferien (24 Jul–5 Sep 2025), Herbstferien, Weihnachtsferien, etc., and events such as Einschulung (13 Sep 2025), Weihnachtsmarkt, Fasching, Frühlingskonzert, Sportfeste, Sommerfest, and outings like Friedrichstadt-Palast or "6K United!".[Main Page][4] **Lernentwicklungsgespräche** (learning development talks) occur regularly per grade; student participation via **Schüler:innenvertretung (SV)** from grade 3, with elected representatives shaping school decisions; inclusive practices support diverse learners, with school social workers for challenges and email sick reporting (ganztag@kb.schule.berlin.de).[Main Page][3][4] Daily structure includes meals, AGs, and presentations in forums.[2][4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Inspections highlight strengths in **community recognition, steady development despite past leadership challenges, diverse extracurriculars, and engaged parents**; good classroom climate with structured, student-oriented lessons and high learning motivation.[2] Offers **offene Ganztagbetreuung**, jahrgangsübergreifende groups in early phase, and competitions like Känguru-Wettbewerb; school song "Wenn im Herbst Kastanien fallen" (since 2007) reflects student heritage.[Main Page][1][2] No specific test outcomes detailed, but positive inspection on teaching atmosphere and projects.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
**Public tuition-free** school with **Schulanmeldung** by appointment (6–17 Oct 2025); **Einschulung** on 13 Sep 2025 and **Tag der offenen Tür** (1 Oct 2025).[Main Page][1] Serves Berlin-Mitte catchment area near U-Bahn Weinmeisterstraße; contact Sekretariat at +49 30 275816330 or sekretariat@kb.schule.berlin.de.[1][Main Page]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Katharina-Heinroth-Grundschule emphasizes cultural and linguistic diversity, housing two branches under one roof: the **Regelschule** (a neighborhood German-language primary school) and the **Staatliche Europa-Schule Berlin Deutsch/Polnisch (SESB)** (a bilingual encounter school in German-Polish).[1][2] It promotes self-determined learning in a supportive environment where "lust and leistung" (enjoyment and achievement) go together, fostering courageous, engaged individuals through social-pedagogical support and overcoming disadvantages.[1][4] The bilingual branch highlights cultural exchange between German and Polish traditions, with cooperation in Germany and abroad.[2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in March 2000 by merging the Michael-Grzimek- and Paul-Eipper-Grundschulen, the school occupies a historic four-story twin-wing building constructed 1905-1907 by Otto Herrnring for the former Goetheschule, featuring early modern design with late Gothic and Renaissance elements; it is a protected monument.[3] Serving approximately **520 children from 20 nations**, it is organized in four parallel classes per year group with three welcome classes for non-German speakers.[1] The community includes students from the local area (Regelschule) and across Berlin (Europa-Schule), supported by a school principal, deputy principals, 50 teachers, two special educators, and 22 childcare workers.[1][7] Facilities include a virtual school tour, adventure playground "Spirale," side building "Horthaus," and spaces for after-school care.[3][7][8]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Both branches follow Berlin's framework curricula, with year-group-based classes starting at 8:00 AM; English is introduced playfully as the first foreign language from grades 1-2.[2] The **Regelschule** is an open all-day school with free care until 13:30, offering welcome classes for language preparation.[2] The **Europa-Schule** is a bound all-day school (mandatory 8:00-16:00, Fridays earlier), bilingual in German-Polish: literacy starts in mother tongue, with Polish subjects incorporating Polish curricula; subjects are divided (e.g., Math/Sports in German, Social Studies in Polish), plus electives like Music/Art in either language.[2] Extracurriculars include AGs (working groups) in art, basketball (multiple ALBA league and Berlin champions), nature exploration, judo, music, and student newspaper.[6][8]

| Branch | Key Features | Languages | Daily Structure |
|--------|--------------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Regelschule** | Neighborhood focus, open all-day | German (English intro) | 8:00 AM start; care to 13:30 free, optional paid before/after |
| **Europa-Schule** | Bilingual encounter, citywide intake | German-Polish (English) | Bound 8:00-16:00; optional care 6:00-8:00/16:00-18:00 |

[1][2][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience diverse, inclusive environments with project weeks in the first school week, cultural exchanges (e.g., dance trips to Krakau), sports successes, and social work offering confidential counseling, group activities, and parental support.[2][4][6] Regelschule pupils enjoy local Kiez community ties and language integration; Europa-Schule students learn in mixed German-Polish mother-tongue groups with native teachers emphasizing cultural heritage.[2] All-day structures blend lessons, breaks, and leisure; AGs provide artistic, athletic, and outdoor activities.[2][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school earned the "Schule ohne Rassismus – Schule mit Courage" plaque on January 22, 2014.[3] Basketball AG achievements include multiple ALBA-Grundschulliga wins and Berlin championships in 3x3 streetball for girls.[6] It supports seamless transitions via welcome classes and individualized integration.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
**Regelschule**: Open to local neighborhood children; non-German speakers enter welcome classes for preparation before regular class integration.[2] **Europa-Schule**: Special pre-enrollment procedure assesses mother tongue (German/Polish) proficiency for group assignment; side-entry possible if places available.[2] Both branches are year-group organized; contact sekretariat@katharina-heinroth-grundschule.de or +49 30 8904395111.[7][9]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Käthe-Kollwitz-Grundschule emphasizes a welcoming school community with a focus on musical education, as evidenced by its recertification as a **Musikalische Grundschule** on September 10, 2024.[1][6] The school's philosophy highlights unique profiles, including music integration, while providing comprehensive information on its programs and history to support community engagement.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1912 as a Gemeindeschule designed by Friedrich Eckler, the school celebrated its 100th anniversary in 2012 with a project week on "School 100 Years Ago."[5] It suffered damage during World War II, including destruction of the southern wing on December 29, 1943, and a partial roof fire, with a temporary roof still in place today.[5] Located in Berlin's Tempelhof-Schöneberg district (Lichtenrade neighborhood), the main address is Mellener Str. 38-42, 12307 Berlin, with a branch at Rehagener Str. 38.[1][2][4] Facilities include a main building from 1912 (recently renovated for modern pedagogy, energy-efficient facade, barrier-free access, and BNB Silver certification target), two sports halls under general renovation, a multi-purpose canteen/mensa in the basement, pavilions for classrooms, and a video school tour recorded during COVID.[1][4][5][7] Leadership is under Sandra Grosser; contact: +49 30 902778241, sekretariat@kollwitz-gs.schule.berlin.de.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a public **Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6), it offers English as the first foreign language, open all-day care (Offene Ganztagbetreuung - OGB), year-group learning groups in early phases, and a school station (Schulstation).[1] Key programs include the **Musikalische Grundschule** profile.[1][6] Related sources note differentiated instruction (e.g., double staffing in grades 1-2, stations learning, project-based learning), reading programs, and collaboration with kindergartens and secondary schools for smooth transitions.[3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in artistic projects like Vincent van Gogh's "Starry Night" reproductions (grade 6a), Hundertwasser-inspired fantasy houses (grade 2c), and 2025 project days on "Peace and Friendship" (grades 5a/5b).[1][6] Participation in the Federal President's History Competition 2022/23 was successful.[1] Experiences include video tours, regular art displays, and community actions promoting peace.[1][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Recent achievements include recertification as a **Musikalische Grundschule** on September 10, 2024, and strong performance in the 2022/23 Geschichtswettbewerb des Bundespräsidenten.[1][6] The school maintains inspection reports and quality profiles, with offerings like OGB and early-phase support.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public tuition-free Grundschule, admissions follow Berlin's standard Schulanmeldung process; the 2025 enrollment is scheduled for September 13, 2025, with invitations sent separately.[1][9] Inquiries are welcomed via email or phone.[1][6]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Käthe-Kruse-Grundschule aims to be **a school for all**, emphasizing independent thinking, social responsibility, and personal development in a welcoming, family-like atmosphere where children and staff feel comfortable.[1][2][3] Key focuses include systematic reading promotion through the school library and "Lesebuddys" (older students reading to younger ones), digital education in all-day programs, prevention in child and youth protection, and a culture of appreciation via displays of student work and awards like the Fairness-Pokal.[1][3] The school promotes handlungsorientiert (action-oriented) learning, cooperative methods such as partner and group work, and social learning through "Buddys" for reading, order, comfort, and sports.[1][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Built in **1904** as a three-story red brick building in Wilhelminian style, the school was renamed after doll-maker Käthe Kruse in **1990** and serves **415 students** in nearly three-track classes (almost durchgängig dreizügig).[1][2] It fosters a small, familial community with **34-36 teachers**, **15-17 educators**, and one social worker, supporting quick integration especially for first- and second-graders in mixed-age and same-year classes.[2][8] Facilities include a main building at Tietzenweg 108, a new two-story extension in Söhtstraße 8 (700 sqm with 8 rooms, educator room, tech room, and accessible WC for 200 children), child-friendly courtyards with climbing nets and boulders, a "Ruheflur" quiet zone for early years (shoe-free), school library since 2007 (over 7,000 media), mensa, gym, music room, and workshop.[1][2][7] Community involvement features parent support via Förderverein, social work, and programs like "Eigenständig werden" for conflict resolution.[3][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **public Grundschule (primary school, grades 1-4)**, the curriculum integrates **English as first foreign language**, open all-day care (OGB), year-specific and cross-year learning groups in early phases, and development priorities like digital education and reading.[4][3] Reading is central, with weekly library hours for all grades, open pauses (Mon-Thu), and integration into lessons; the library offers 7,000+ media, magazines (e.g., Geolino), and extension via parent donations or 40€ annual subscriptions.[Main Page][Curriculum Page] Music programs include 2 choirs, school orchestra, instruments (flute, guitar), concerts, and Leo-Borchard-Musikschule cooperation; after-school AGs cover football, basketball, art, reading, chess, early English, tinkering, dance, yoga, and more.[2][4][7] Instruction occurs in a respectful, varied atmosphere with excursions, project days, and expert visits.[3][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience a **lernförderliche (learning-supportive) atmosphere** with respectful interactions, child-friendly room designs, and diverse methods like group work utilizing all school spaces.[3] Daily schedule: 7:30-14:30 (up to 7th hour), with breaks (20min after 2nd, 35min after 4th); reliable half-day to 13:30 as Verlässliche Halbtaggrundschule (VHG), extended via eFöB (8 rooms for open work) using mensa, gym, etc.[Main Page][7] Extracurriculars emphasize social skills via Buddys, prevention, and AGs; library borrowing (3 weeks, extendable) fosters independent reading choice.[1][Curriculum Page][7] Excursions, class trips, and cooperative learning promote autonomy and joy in subjects like reading, which "opens new worlds."[3][Curriculum Page]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The 2024 school inspection highlights **positive teaching profiles** with frequent criteria for effective learning, a current school program (updated from 2018), and progress in digital and reading focuses.[3] 2014 inspection praised social learning via Buddys and facilities; the school maintains a familial environment aiding quick adaptation, with prüfungsergebnisse (exam results) available via official portrait.[1][3][4] Ongoing programs like LHS Lebenshilfe since 08.2021 and "Eigenständig werden" support inclusion and conflict skills; no specific outcome metrics detailed beyond qualitative inspections.[3][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public Grundschule**, admission follows Berlin public school processes (local catchment, no fees mentioned); contact sekretariat@kaethe-kruse-grundschule.de or +49 30 84416950 (Mon-Fri 8-13).[Main Page][4] Open house (Tag der offenen Tür) listed in Berlin school calendar; early years supported by mixed classes and Ruheflur.[2][4] Parents assist with school routes; inquire via leadership (Frau Willer) or eFöB (Frau May, +49 30 84419750).[Main Page][10]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Katholische Schule Herz Jesu is a Catholic primary school emphasizing the motto "Miteinander leben, Miteinander lernen, Miteinander glauben - mit dem Herzen sehen" (Living together, learning together, believing together - seeing with the heart).[1][6] Its philosophy is rooted in a Christian worldview, promoting holistic education that integrates faith experiences with knowledge acquisition, fostering personal responsibility, social learning, respect, and values-based action.[PDF Schulprogramm] The school aims to nurture joy in learning, individual talents, and community cooperation among students, parents, teachers, and staff, while addressing development focuses like social behavior, school pastoral care, independent learning, and a supportive learning atmosphere.[PDF Schulprogramm][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1945 by the Sacré-Coeur Sisters (Herz Jesu order, established 1800 by Madeleine Sophie Barat) post-WWII with Allied approval, the school moved to Insterburgallee 8-10 in 1949 and received state recognition as a six-year primary and lower secondary school in 1956; it became a pure primary school after 1976 and transitioned to Erzbistum Berlin sponsorship in 1970.[1][3][PDF Schulprogramm] Located in Berlin-Charlottenburg's Neu-Westend (Bezirk Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf), it serves ~350 students from 19 nations, primarily from Charlottenburg, Spandau, and surroundings, in a green, accessible area.[PDF Schulprogramm][2] Facilities include a renovated building (2006-2009 expansion) with 14 classrooms, specialized rooms for art, computers, music, sciences, library, gym, stage, gymnastics room, quiet room, counseling space, cafeteria with kitchen, and outdoor areas like soccer field, playground, and rope course; the on-site Hort (after-school care) uses additional buildings including a former convent.[PDF Schulprogramm][1] Staff includes head Petra Buttenberg, 25 teachers, 10 childcare workers (as of 2022/2023), secretary, janitor, cook, and confectioner; parents actively support via Freundeskreis.[PDF Schulprogramm][Main Page]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
This state-recognized private **Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6) offers a reliable half-day program from 7:30-13:35, with optional extension to 15:10.[Curriculum Page][PDF Schulprogramm][5] Schedule: 0th hour (7:30-7:55, early support), six core hours (8:00-13:35), optional 7th/8th.[Curriculum Page] Languages: English and Latin as first foreign languages.[5] Features project-based teaching, team preparation, iPad carts, modern classrooms, extracurricular learning trips, AGs (clubs), foster classes, and integration of Hort educators in early school phase; emphasizes Christian values, social-emotional learning, and self-responsible habits.[PDF Schulprogramm] No secondary levels or specific graduation paths like Abitur/MSA mentioned.

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
After school (post-13:35), students transition independently to 10 Hort groups (218 children, themed names like "Räuberhöhle"), doing homework with support, outdoor play, creative activities, and group vesper (15:15-15:45, no pickups); late service until 18:00, including grades 4-6.[Hort Pages][PDF Schulprogramm] Extracurriculars include religious children's week, musisches projects, sports days, excursions, bike tests, school festivals, internal/external AGs, class trips, and außerschulische learning sites; strong school-Hort cooperation with fixed group leaders promoting autonomy and community.[PDF Schulprogramm][Hort Pages] Facilities support arts, sports, music; parents/ Freundeskreis aid events.[PDF Schulprogramm]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or test outcomes detailed in content; strengths noted internally include low absenteeism, structured organization, diverse methods, good facilities, low conflicts, and positive climate via Erziehungsvereinbarung (parent-school agreement).[PDF Schulprogramm] State-recognized Ersatzschule with offene Ganztagbetreuung (OGB).[5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
**Enrollment**: For grade 1 (e.g., 2025/26), submit online form from September 9, 2025, call secretariat (030/33007010) for Kennlernnachmittag (get-to-know afternoon); bring birth certificate copy, baptismal certificate (if applicable), child photo, child's drawing "My Family and I", slippers for gym.[7] Hort: Contact secretary Frau Liebisch with Bezirksamt needs certificate (noting waiver info); forms available on-site or via public primary school; income-based per TKBG law + meals.[Hort Anmeldung Pages] Secretariat hours: 7:30-9:00, 12:00-14:00; illness reports from 8:00.[All Pages]  
**Fees**: No specific EUR amounts listed for tuition, registration, materials, or meals. School tuition not mentioned (typical for state-recognized Catholic primaries under Erzbistum).[9] **Hort** (13:35-18:00, optional, grades 1-6): Income-based via TKBG (Tageskostenbeteiligungsgesetz) + Essensgeld; free school lunch for grades 1-6 via Berlin program.[Hort Anmeldung][Mittagessen Page] No base/standard rates, ranges, or scholarships specified.

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Katholische Schule Salvator is a **christlich geprägte** (Christian-oriented) state-recognized private school emphasizing a holistic educational approach where school serves as a space for learning, working, praying, and celebrating. It promotes individual development based on Christian human values, fostering personal potential while ensuring students feel accepted, respected, and valued.[1][4][5][6] Rooted in the salvatorian tradition, it aims to nurture socially responsible individuals in a supportive community, open to talents and failures alike.[5][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded on February 8, 1947, as a girls' Gymnasium by Sr. Luminosa Wimmer, the school began operations in November 1947 with 70 students, mostly taught by nuns; it expanded to all grade levels by 1949, added a Realschulzweig in 1953, and received state-recognized private school status in 1955.[3][6] A Grundschule was added in 1964 (inaugurated by Archbishop Alfred Bengsch), with a branch in Märkisches Viertel opening in 1971; boys were admitted from 1965, and ownership transferred to the Erzbistum Berlin in 1970.[3][6][7] Today, it serves about 700 students in the Oberschule (Integrierte Sekundarschule and Gymnasium) plus Grundschule students, with ~70 teachers; located idyllically near Tegeler Fließ in Berlin-Waidmannslust.[2][6] Facilities include two sports halls (large and small), a small sports field, beach volleyball court, and a 1989-built science building with labs for biology, physics, and chemistry.[3]

### 3. CURRICRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The school combines an independent **Grundschule** (grades 1-6, main site and St. Martin branch in Märkisches Viertel) with an Oberschule featuring a **dreizügiges Gymnasium** (one Zug with musical focus) and **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)**, enabling seamless transition from grades 1-13 and potential switches between branches.[1][2][6] Offers **G8 Abitur** (after 8 years), Catholic Religion as compulsory to grade 11 (Abitur exam option), Leistungskurse in history, politics, geography, religion in Oberstufe, and links between German and performing arts.[1] Languages: S1 English/French, S2 Spanish; comprehensive career orientation from grades 7-10 including Jobdetektive, Werkstatt-Tage, internships, and Schülerfirma.[1][2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include strong music programs (e.g., Big Band CD in 2005, musical Gymnasium Zug), performing arts integrated with German, regular geography competitions, and past student exchanges with Lycée Notre Dame de Garaison (France, 1986-1998).[1][3] Sports facilities support activities like beach volleyball; career prep features workshops, forest seminars, and job training.[1] Daily schedule starts at 8:00 AM with 30-minute lessons and two breaks.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
First Abitur in 1954 (9 girls); state-recognized Ersatzschule status; ongoing participation in geography competitions; historical expansions and approvals (e.g., full grades by 1949, private status 1955).[1][3] Serves as genehmigte Ersatzschule with Grundschulteil, gymnasiale Oberstufe, and Zweiter Bildungsweg options; no recent awards or rankings specified.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
As a state-recognized private **Ersatzschule**, admissions likely follow standard Berlin processes (e.g., Schulanmeldung via berlin.de), with seamless internal transitions from Grundschule; leadership: Ralf Malz, Thomas Pritsch; contact via phone/email.[1][2][4] **No fee or tuition information** (Schulgeld, Beiträge, etc.) found in provided content, including no mentions of monthly/annual fees, registration, meals, after-school care, materials, scholarships, or income-based structures.[1-9]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's mission is rooted in a **christian human image**, emphasizing respect for others, individual responsibility for the community, and the holistic development of students. It promotes an educational partnership between students, parents, and teachers, using differentiated and individualized teaching methods to foster diverse talents. Modern teaching approaches, with a focus on **media education**, are integral, alongside mutual support within classes through self-created rules that respect individuals and enhance learning atmosphere. Catholic religious education is a core subject (2 hours weekly), supplemented by daily morning prayers, school/class services, and pastoral elements tied to the church year.[1][2][8]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in **1963** as a branch of St. Ludwig School in a temporary barrack in Berlin-Marienfelde, the **Katholische Schule Sankt Alfons** is a state-recognized primary school (Grundschule) owned by the Erzbistum Berlin. Located at **Tennstedter Str. 1, 12249 Berlin (Marienfelde)**, it serves grades 1-6 with approximately **312 students** in a two-track structure. The community includes Schulleiterin **Katinka Stemmler**, secretary Beata Janulewicz, and partnerships like HISA gGmbH for after-school care. Facilities include library, computer room, art room, science room (NaWi), multi-purpose room, canteen (Mensa), playground, and wheelchair-accessible areas; a virtual 360° tour highlights these.[2][4][7][10] Public transport access via buses M11, M82, X11, M77, 112.[2]

### 3. CURRICURICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **verlässliche Halbtagsgrundschule (reliable half-day primary school)** (BSN 07P04), it covers **grades 1-6** with standard Berlin curriculum, including **English** as the first foreign language from grade 1. Emphasis on individualized learning in year-group-based groups during the school entry phase, media education, and religious instruction. No secondary levels or specific graduation paths like Abitur mentioned; it functions as a state-recognized substitute school.[2][6][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
The school offers a full-day structure with **eFöB (ergänzende Förderung und Betreuung, or Hort/after-school care)** modules: early morning (6:00-7:30), afternoon (13:30-16:00 or 16:00-18:00), and ferien (holiday) care (7:30-13:30). From August 1, 2025, eFöB is managed directly by Erzbistum Berlin, requiring Jugendamt approval and contracts. Extracurriculars include counseling, school pastoral care, special education, religious trips, social projects, conflict mediators, and student guides. Sports like swimming competitions, arts, music, theater, fitness, and health are promoted. Events include open day (Tag der offenen Tür 2025), carnival, and religious holidays.[1][2][4][8]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or test results are detailed in the content. It notes state recognition and reliable half-day status, with ongoing development toward inclusive structures via cooperation with Förderzentrum St. Hildegard.[2][5][7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Admissions require Jugendamt bewilligungsschreiben (approval letter/voucher) for eFöB contracts with Erzbistum Berlin; forms via Senatsverwaltung website. Submit bewilligungsschreiben, Hortvertrag (double copy), Hortkonzept, Benutzungs- und Beitragsregelung, and SEPA mandate to school office or Erzbistum Berlin (Niederwallstr. 8–9, 10117 Berlin). No core tuition (Schulgeld) amounts specified; as a private Catholic Grundschule, regular instruction appears free or state-subsidized, with fees only for optional eFöB/Hort. **Benutzungs- und Beitragsregelung Hort Berlin** (PDF) details contributions, but no extractable EUR amounts; fees likely income-based via Jugendamt voucher. Open day for visits.[1][2][8]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Katholische Schule Sankt Franziskus (KSSF) is a Catholic school under the sponsorship of the Erzbistum Berlin, emphasizing Christian values such as mutual help, friendly interaction, community responsibility, and regular spiritual practices like morning school prayers and Gottesdienste.[1][3][4] The educational approach integrates Montessori pedagogy in the Grundschule (grades 1-6), featuring Freiarbeit where children choose activities in prepared environments focusing on sensory training, language, math, cosmic education, and self-controlled learning from concrete to abstract concepts.[3][8] It promotes self-organized learning, values-based education for youth, differentiated instruction (e.g., Grundniveau and Erweiterungsniveau in core subjects), and holistic development including handlungskompetenz and sachkompetenz.[1][7][8]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1889 as a Catholic higher girls' school in Berlin-Schöneberg, the school was taken over by Franziskanerinnen in 1918, closed by Nazis in 1940, and reorganized post-WWII; it became co-educational in 1967, adopted its current form in 1970 under the Erzbistum Berlin, integrated branches in 2004, and reopened gymnasiale Oberstufe in 2008.[4] Located at Hohenstaufenstr. 1-2, 10781 Berlin (Schöneberg, Tempelhof-Schöneberg district), it features a listed main building from 1959, Schulhof, offene Kapelle for pastoral work, Schulgarten in Gleisdreieckpark, and facilities like a Holzwerkstatt and Textilwerkstatt.[1][2][3][4][7] The community includes ~310 Grundschule students, dynamic staff, and partners like Förderverein; it currently cannot accept new applications due to building renovations.[Admissions Page]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
KSSF offers Grundschule (grades 1-6, ~310 students, halbtags 7:30-13:40 with Montessori Freiarbeit), Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS, grades 7-10 with binnendifferenzierung and Leistungsniveaus in sciences), and gymnasiale Oberstufe leading to Abitur (with LK options in Math, Biology, Chemistry).[1][2][3][4][5] Daily schedule: 8:05-18:05 (45-min lessons).[1] Languages: English (S1, 5 hours in bilingual track grades 7-10), second foreign language French/Latin/Spanish from grade 7 (or Spanish from 11).[1][5] Special elements: JÜL-Klassen (grades 1-3), SaPh with Montessori training sought for staff.[3][10][Curriculum Page]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars emphasize arts, sports, culture, and projects: Adventskonzert with Oberschule, Patronatstag Gottesdienst, sport Wahlpflichtkurse (excursions), community games in Gleisdreieckpark, music projects with Rias-Kammerchor and UdK performance, Schulchor at Berliner Schulchorwettbewerb, upcycling workshops (Bauchtasche), goldsmith visits (Kunst WPK), Känguru-Mathematikwettbewerb, history excursions (AlliiertenMuseum, Marienfelde), museum trips (Barberini Potsdam), AI storytelling (bilingual 9a), VR sexuality education, Afro-Germans walking tour, Cajon-building, Schulgarten project.[Main Page][7] After-school via partners KiKO e.V. (155 children, 13:40-18:00, homework, meals, arts/sport, ferienbetreuung from 7:30, only 8 closure days) and KIEZBÜNDNIS Schülerläden (small groups 16-22, Hausaufgaben, Ausflüge, integration).[Fee/Tuition Pages]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Participation in competitions: 31st Känguru-Mathematikwettbewerb (880,000 students nationwide), Berliner Schulchorwettbewerb (FEZ Berlin), Jugend forscht via Forscher AG.[Main Page][1] First Abitur in 2011 after Oberstufe reopening; state-recognized Ersatzschule with ganztags offerings.[4][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Admissions require an Aufnahmeantrag flyer (available via Förderverein at www.foerderverein-kssf.de); currently no applications accepted due to Schulsanierung.[Admissions Page] A Schulvertrag exists (with Kündigungsformular referencing Schulgeldstelle), implying tuition, but no specific EUR amounts are stated on the site or in provided content.[PDF: Kündigungsformular] After-school care (KiKO/KIEZBÜNDNIS) requires Betreuungsbescheid from school/youth office; costs are income-assessed by the office based on contract and Einkommensunterlagen, yielding a Kostenbescheid—no fixed EUR figures listed (biological meals from Luna GmbH mentioned, but no price).[Fee/Tuition Pages] No registration, material, meal, or other fees specified in EUR.
