description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Katholische Schule Sankt Ludwig operates under a **Christian human image** emphasizing respect for others and individual responsibility within the community, forming the foundation of its educational and upbringing mission.[1][2] It fosters an **Erziehungspartnerschaft** (partnership in education) among students, parents, and teachers, using differentiated and individualized teaching methods to support diverse talents in a supportive classroom community with mutual aid and agreed class rules.[2] Catholic religious education is a core subject with two weekly hours, complemented by daily morning prayers, school and class services, and pastoral elements tied to the church year, making faith experiential.[2] Key focuses include social learning, community building, values education, project-based interdisciplinary learning, sustainability education, sustainable digitalization, and school pastoral offerings.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in **1904** in Berlin-Wilmersdorf, the school is owned by the **Erzbistum Berlin** (Catholic Archdiocese of Berlin) and closely integrated with the Pfarrei St. Ludwig parish both spatially and pastorally.[1][5] It serves **256 students** (as of 2025 inspection), including 169 Catholics, 16 Evangelicals, 21 Orthodox, 4 Muslims, and others, with 4 children receiving special needs support from a cooperating center; families often span generations, drawing from Wilmersdorf and beyond (e.g., Tempelhof-Schöneberg, Mitte).[1] The community values its sheltered atmosphere and transitions to nearby renowned secondary schools like Katholische Schule Liebfrauen or Canisius-Kolleg.[1] Facilities include limited outdoor space due to urban integration in a residential row house at **Düsseldorfer Str. 13, 10719 Berlin**; specialized rooms like learning corners, woodworking/ton workshop, specialist classrooms, division rooms, modern sports hall with stage, "Ludwigskrug" cafeteria, school library for full classes, laptop carts, and iPad kits per floor; a virtual tour highlights music room, gym, courtyards, and more.[1][10] It holds **GKSI certification** from the Gemeinsame Katholische Schulinspektion.[4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
This **zweizügige Grundschule** (two-track primary school) serves **grades 1-6** (grades 1-5 double-track, grade 6 single-track currently) as a state-recognized Ersatzschule (substitute school) with **Offenes Ganztagsbetrieb** (open all-day program).[1][2][7] It follows state frameworks with modern methods, including **English as first foreign language**, year-group learning groups in early phases, and Catholic religious instruction as a regular subject.[2][7] Emphasis on project-oriented, interdisciplinary learning; digital tools like WLAN, tablets; expert courses in grade 6.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in **diverse extracurriculars** including Arbeitsgemeinschaften (clubs), fencing, sponsor runs, harvest thanksgiving services and appeals, class trips (e.g., 5b excursion), and sports hall activities with theater stage.[1][4] After-school care (Hort Sankt Ludwig, run by the parish) integrates with school, open 6:00-7:30 and 13:30-18:00, with consistent caregivers across three sites for ~220 children by grade level; educators join morning classes for rhythm.[Scraped Hort page] Facilities support play (toy lending for recesses), library reading, workshops, and pastoral events tied to the church year.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school underwent **GKSI 2.0 inspection** (March 31-April 4, 2025) by qualified external inspectors, evaluating quality across 7 criteria (e.g., educational mission, curriculum, teaching, pastoral care, participation, development, leadership) via 22 lesson observations (94.7% of teachers), interviews with leadership, 9 teachers/hort staff/social worker, 11 students, 13 parents, and 2 staff, plus school tour; it received a detailed report with self-assessment comparison for development impulses (full profile in PDF).[PDF: KSSL_Qualitaetseinschaetzung_20250306.pdf] It maintains a strong reputation for transitions to elite secondaries and multi-generational attendance.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Admissions for 2027/2028 target children born **October 1, 2020-September 30, 2021**; process via online form (with emailed copy) or PDF by **October 1, 2026**, followed by interviews requiring birth certificate copy, baptism certificate (if applicable), vaccination book, and family/child drawing.[Admissions page] Decisions by **November 2025**; forms include Anmeldebogen and Erzbistum application.[Admissions page] **No specific EUR fee amounts** found in provided content; "Informationen zum monatlichen Schulgeld" links to a page/PDF not extracted here, indicating monthly school fees (Schulgeld) exist but details unavailable; as a private Catholic Ersatzschule, fees likely apply per Erzbistum norms (covering pedagogy, materials, facilities).[Admissions page][9] Hort (after-school) mentioned without fees; no registration, materials, meals, or income-based details specified.[Scraped Hort page]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The **Katholische Schule Sankt Marien (Grundschule)** emphasizes a holistic education rooted in **Christian values**, fostering community, trust, respect, and individual child development at their own pace. The school positions itself as more than a learning place—a space for children to experience community, unfold talents, and grow in a secure atmosphere prioritizing well-being, academic skills, and social competencies, with the child at the center.[1][7] Principal Ricarda Pagel highlights an engaged team of teachers, pedagogical staff, and volunteers delivering varied, inspiring instruction within a reliable Catholic framework providing orientation and support.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
No specific founding history is detailed in the content. The school is part of the **Katholisches Erzbistum Berlin** network, operating as a state-recognized substitute private Catholic primary school (Grundschule) in Berlin-Gropiusstadt, Neukölln district.[1][3][7] Located at **Hugo-Heimann-Str. 2, 12353 Berlin**, it serves grades 1-6 and offers **offene Ganztagbetreuung (OGB)** (open all-day care).[3][7] Facilities include a 360° virtual tour; related secondary campus at Donaustr. 58 mentions cafeteria, computer rooms, laptops, and whiteboards, but primary-specific details are absent.[2][6] Community links include Schulhort (after-school care), Förderverein (parent support association), and Bildungsspender (donation platform).[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Grundschule (primary school, grades 1-6)**, the curriculum focuses on holistic development with **English as the first foreign language** and schulanfangsphase (early primary phase) using year-group learning groups.[7] It is a state-recognized Ersatzschule with open all-day offerings, but no detailed subjects, special programs, or graduation pathways (e.g., Abitur) are specified for the primary level; the school feeds into the affiliated integrated secondary school and gymnasium on the same campus, offering profiles like art/theater, MINT (math/informatics/science/technology), and languages (English, French, Polish, Latin, Spanish).[2][4][6][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
The school promotes varied instruction, projects, and joint events in a child-centered environment, with recent classroom updates featuring new furniture and materials for inviting learning spaces.[1][9] Extracurriculars are not detailed for primary, but the broader Sankt Marien campus offers working groups in choir, chess, guitar, sports (rugby, volleyball, basketball, football, strength training), and Catholic youth activities; secondary includes language trips and competitions.[2][4] Primary emphasizes community events and Catholic values integration.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No awards, rankings, certifications, test results, or specific outcomes are mentioned in the provided content.[1][7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Admission details are not specified; the site invites visits and contact for inquiries, with a Tag der offenen Tür (open day) listed in Berlin's school calendar.[1][7] **No tuition or fee information** (e.g., Schulgeld, Beiträge, Gebühren) or EUR amounts appear in the scraped pages, fee-related pages, or PDFs; Hausordnung PDFs are noted but contain no extracted fee content.[1] As a private Catholic Ersatzschule under the Erzbistum, fees may apply but are undisclosed here—contact the school (Tel: +49 30 6052032, sekretariat@kssm-gs.schulerzbistum.de) for details.[7][8]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's mission centers on fostering holistic personality development based on a **christian human image**, emphasizing individual promotion according to each child's abilities, joy in learning, and self-achieved performance. It provides a supportive environment for religious life within a Catholic-Christian value framework, including Catholic religious education as a regular subject with 2 weekly hours per class, shared church year celebrations, and integration into the pastoral space of Friedrichshain-Lichtenberg. The educational approach prioritizes differentiated and individualized teaching methods, aesthetic-musical education (sinnliche, bildnerische, klangliche experiences), creativity, conflict resolution through non-violent culture and mediator training, and values like mutual respect and responsibility.[1][2][4][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in **1995** as a single-track primary school under the sponsorship of the Erzbistum Berlin, initiated by engaged parents in Berlin-Lichtenberg; it is one of 13 such schools run by the archdiocese and led by Rektorin **Kathrin Henkel**. The community includes 156 students, teachers, parents, and the local Catholic parish St. Mauritius, fostering joint religious events like church year festivals and school services. Facilities include a school playground with a Spielehaus equipped for sports and games, open all-day operation (Mo-Fr 7:30-13:35 Uhr), supplementary after-school care via Catholic Kita St. Mauritius (at Schulze-Boysen-Str. 26, Mo-Fr 7:00-17:00 Uhr, capacity 60 children) and MiniClub Farbklecks (at John-Sieg-Str. 1-3, Mo-Fr 6:00-18:00 Uhr), free lunches for hort children (planned extension to all in 2019/2020), and a 360° panorama tour showing foyer, hort rooms, and church.[1][2][5][6][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Serves **grades 1-6** (einzügig/single-track) as a state-recognized reliable half-day primary school with open all-day operation, following Berlin's educational program and the *Grundlagenpapier für die Katholischen Schulen im Erzbistum Berlin* (1999). Core elements include English as the first foreign language, year-group learning groups in the early phase, computer courses (2 hours for grades 5-6), Catholic religious instruction, and modern methods for comprehensive knowledge, skills, and values to enable independent learning and societal engagement. No secondary levels or specific graduation pathways like Abitur/MSA mentioned; focuses on primary education preparation.[1][2][4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars emphasize arts and music: school choir ("Mauritiusmeisen" grades 1-2, Vorchor grade 3, main choir grades 4-6) led by Bettina Hüffner, Kinderopernhaus cooperation with Caritas/Staatsoper from grade 3. Sports and activities include AGs (e.g., pottery, soccer, chess, reading for grades 2-4), Capoeira, Coolnesstraining, computer for grades 5-6; class trips (every 2 years to Zinnowitz/St. Otto for religious orientation, play, nature; also grades 3/6); "Schnuppertag" at St. Marien for grade 5; conflict mediation training; active breaks with sports equipment; homework and AGs in hort.[1][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or outcome data (e.g., exam results) mentioned in the content. It holds state recognition as an Ersatzschule with open ganztagsbetreuung and is part of the Erzbistum Berlin's network of 26 Catholic schools emphasizing Christian profile and inclusion.[4][8]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Contact sekretariat@st-mauritius-grundschule.de or phone 030/55151821 (Mo-Th 7:30-13:30, Fr 7:30-11:30) for inquiries; "FREIE SCHULPLÄTZE" (free places) highlighted, with a parental info section on **Schulgeld** (school fees) mentioned but no specific amounts or process details provided. After-school care via partner Kitas (no fees listed); **free midday meals** for hort children, planned for all students. No enrollment requirements or EUR amounts extracted for tuition, registration, materials, or other fees.[5]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Katholische Schule Sankt Paulus is a Catholic primary school emphasizing **Glaube (Faith)**, **Hoffnung (Hope)**, and **Liebe (Love)** as core principles. It fosters a supportive environment for children of diverse backgrounds and talents, integrating Christian values like neighborly love, respect, and tolerance alongside academic instruction. The school builds confidence in a challenging world through caritative activities and promotes community via festivals, sports, parent cafés, excursions, and worship services. Guided by the Gospel, it prioritizes personal development, responsibility, and a vibrant school culture through schulpastoral (school pastoral care), including regular Gottesdienste, meditations, and cooperation with the local St. Paulus parish.[1][4][Schulprogramm März 2025 PDF]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded on April 10, 1964, the school opened its building in 1965 next to St. Paulus Church in Berlin-Moabit, on former orphanage land, under the Erzbischöflichen Ordinariates Berlin. It transitioned to an open all-day school (verlässliche Halbtagsgrundschule with ergänzende Förderung und Betreuung, EFöB) in 2005/2006, with the "Arche Noah" after-school program taken over by the diocese in 2008. Current enrollment is approximately **370-380 students**, with ~70% non-German native speakers and high diversity (62-70% migration background). Staff includes **25-28 teachers**, **14-17 educators**, 2 social workers, kitchen staff, a janitor, and volunteers. Leadership: Schulleiterin **Agnes Prüfer**, Konrektorin **Stephanie Utz**. Facilities support half-day core instruction (7:30-13:30), early care from 6:00, homework supervision, and extended care to 18:00, with play areas upgraded in 2012.[1][2][Schulprogramm März 2025 PDF][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Grundschule (grades 1-6)**, the curriculum follows Berlin's Rahmenlehrpläne, with Catholic Religion as a core subject and **English** from grade 3 (fachlich in grades 5-6). Grades 1-4 feature vorfachlicher Unterricht (integrated Deutsch, Math, Sachkunde, English, Music, Art, Religion, 3x PE weekly, swimming in grade 3). Grades 5-6 differentiate Deutsch, Math, English in smaller groups; Sachkunde splits into Naturwissenschaften and Gesellschaftswissenschaften. Methods include Freiarbeit, Wochenplanarbeit, Stationstraining, Projektarbeit, vernetzter Unterricht, Morgenkreis, and Reflexionsrunde. Grade 6 offers **Expertenkurse** (e.g., Deutsch, Latein, Kunst, Naturwissenschaften) as Wahlpflichtkurse to prepare for Oberschule. No secondary levels or specific graduation pathways (Abitur/MSA/BBR) mentioned, as it is primary only.[1][2][Schulprogramm März 2025 PDF]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars emphasize community and faith: regular Schulgottesdienste, Sternsinger, Advent meditations, Kirchenjahr decorations, Hausaufgabenbetreuung in Hort "Arche Noah" (modules: 6:00-7:30 early care, 13:30-18:00 afternoon with homework 13:30-14:30, play, etc.). Activities include festivals, sports competitions, Eltern-Cafés, Ausflüge, Pfadfinderschaft, Malteserhilfsdienst, Chor, Instrumentalmusik, Fußball, Handball, Schach, Schülerzeitung, Töpfern, Tanz, Theater. Recent events: SpoSpiTo-Bewegungs-Pass (health/climate project), Jugend forscht (science AG in Biology/Chemistry), Vorlesewettbewerb, Schülervollversammlung, creative Garderobenschränke. Conflict resolution via "Medis" (student mediators).[1][2][About Page Berichte][Schulprogramm März 2025 PDF]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or certifications are detailed. Participation in external programs like Jugend forscht 2025 (6 groups in regional competition), SpoSpiTo-Bewegungs-Pass 2025, and Vorlesewettbewerb 2023 highlights engagement. The school notes strong community events (e.g., Sternsinger-Gottesdienst) and smooth transitions to all-day model.[About Page Berichte][Schulprogramm März 2025 PDF]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Admissions for Grundschule involve contacting the Sekretariat (Tel: 030-3954196, sekretariat@kssp.schulerzbistum.de) for tours or info; Hort/EFöB enrollment via application there, per bewilligter Betreuungsvertrag and Berlin Senate guidelines. No specific requirements listed. **No tuition or fee amounts in EUR** found across website, Hort pages, or PDFs; core half-day instruction (7:30-13:30) is state-recognized private with no Schulgeld mentioned. EFöB/Hort ("Arche Noah", ~300 children) is ergänzende Betreuung, likely subsidized (references Berlin Senate site), with modules but no costs specified. Open to all confessions, diverse backgrounds.[Hort-EFöB Pages][Anmeldung im Hort][Ergänzende Betreuung][Schulprogramm März 2025 PDF][1]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Katholische Schule Sankt Ursula emphasizes a holistic, personal, and social education rooted in Christian values, fostering personal faith, lifelong learning, and community.[1][2][4] The school's philosophy centers on "Das Kind in unserer Mitte" (the child at our center), promoting learning through relationships, personality development, faith strengthening, and community building in an open all-day program.[6] It aims for recognition-oriented education partnerships that build self-confidence, self-respect, and individual strengths via methods like morning circles, free work, networked lessons, and conflict resolution through "Konfliktlotsen."[2][4][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded on April 29, 1854, by Ursulinen sisters, it is Berlin's oldest Catholic school, originally a girls' gymnasium, now a state-recognized private primary school (Grundschule) under the Archdiocese of Berlin (Erzbistum Berlin).[2][6] Located at Kleinaustr. 4, 14169 Berlin (Zehlendorf/Steglitz-Zehlendorf), it serves ~314 students with leadership by Birgitta Wiese and Cordula Horstmann-Nieder.[1][5][7] Facilities include a central aula, room of silence, gym, library, secretariat, canteen, community kitchen, 16 classrooms, PC room, spacious hallways with glass architecture, schoolyard, and eFöB (after-school care) areas at Gartenstr. 22 for grades 1-2 and on-site for grades 3+.[6][7] The campus supports accessibility with parking for disabled, wheelchair elevator, and accessible WC.[7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Offers grades 1-6 as a state-recognized Ersatzschule (substitute school) with open all-day operation (offener Ganztagsbetrieb), year-group classes, individual support, media curriculum, interactive learning platform, school library, and competitions in sports, music, math, reading, languages.[4][5][7] English as first foreign language starts in grade 3; emphasizes head-heart-hand learning, religious education (e.g., Monday assemblies, school services), and student participation via class councils, kidsmeetings, student parliament.[2][5] No secondary levels or specific graduation paths like Abitur mentioned; focuses on primary education.[7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Extracurriculars include working groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) in pottery, choir, instrumental music, juggling, soccer, dance, French, chess, computer, yoga, art, photography, theater, hockey, sailing, languages.[2][4][5][8] After-school (eFöB) offers play, creativity, outings, gardening, baking, yoga, during holidays (e.g., Easter, winter, autumn with crafts, nature walks, cinema).[main scraped content] Additional experiences: project weeks, class trips, excursions, homework supervision, paten classes (buddy system).[5] Meals follow DGE standards, free for Berlin primary students with allergy accommodations.[main scraped content]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or outcome statistics (e.g., test results) detailed in content; noted for historical significance as Berlin's oldest Catholic school and emphasis on values-based education open to all confessions.[2][5] Participation in competitions across subjects.[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Admissions via online application form; welcomes all interested families with info on school site and parents' letters; eFöB requires district needs certificate (Bedarfsbescheid) and application to coordinator Sabine Grolewsky.[6][main scraped content] No specific EUR tuition amounts found for core schooling; as a state-recognized private Catholic primary under Erzbistum Berlin, likely subsidized with minimal or no standard tuition (common for such Grundschulen).[10]  
**Fee/Tuition Details**:  
- School tuition: No amounts specified; not mentioned as Schulgeld or Beitrag.  
- Meals: Free (kostenbeteiligungsfrei) for grades 1-6 per Berlin policy, aligned with DGE standards.[main scraped content]  
- After-school care (eFöB/Hort): Costs per TKBG (Tagesbetreuungskostenbeteiligungsgesetz), income-based; requires district approval; no fixed EUR listed.[main scraped content]

description:
# Kiefholz-Grundschule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Kiefholz-Grundschule prioritizes the **safety, well-being, and holistic development** of its students within a supportive school community[5]. The school emphasizes **movement-based learning environments**, healthy lifestyles, and inclusive education practices. The institution is committed to fostering **tolerance, non-violent interaction, and mutual trust** among students[4], while integrating inclusive education principles into its physical infrastructure and pedagogical approach[7].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Community Context**

The Kiefholz-Grundschule is located in **Alt-Treptow, Treptow-Köpenick district**, in the heart of Berlin[2][5]. The neighborhood is characterized by historic buildings, new residential developments, the Treptower Park, the Spree River, and a vibrant shopping street[5].

**School Status and Hours**

Since 2014, the school operates as an **eigenständige verlässliche Halbtagsgrundschule** (independent reliable half-day primary school), providing care for all children **from 7:30 AM to 1:30 PM**[5]. Extended afternoon care through ergänzende Förderung und Betreuung (eFöB—supplementary support and care) is available on a partially fee-based basis[5].

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

Between 2016-2018, the school underwent comprehensive renovation and expansion, including a new floor level that created additional specialized and classroom spaces[2]. Current facilities include:

- **Specialized Learning Spaces**: Science laboratory for experiments, art room with wood, clay, and other materials[2]
- **Dining and Social Areas**: Large cafeteria hosting school meals, games club, readings, and performances; equipped teaching kitchen for cooking and baking activities[2]
- **Leisure Facilities**: Six thematic functional rooms including construction, creative, role-play, and relaxation spaces, plus reading and games areas[2][4]
- **Movement and Recreation**: Two courtyards with climbing and play equipment, sports field with integrated soccer field and basketball hoops[4]
- **Library and Support Spaces**: School library on the second floor; "island room" on ground floor for work with students requiring integration support[4]
- **Classroom Design**: Large rooms for grades 1-3 with learning corners including reading areas, computer stations, conference tables, and self-checking stations[2]

The school features **movement-promoting furniture** and displays throughout[2]. A recent playground enhancement project integrated a colorful play surface with hopscotch fields, game circles, and footprints, designed around existing trees to maintain the natural environment[1].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Class Organization**

Students in grades 1-3 typically learn in **age-mixed (JüL) classes**, while grades 4-6 are organized into **age-homogeneous year groups**[5]. Each class is staffed by a teaching team consisting of one teacher and one educator who provides classroom support during morning instruction[8].

**Instructional Approach**

The teaching team collaborates to plan and organize instruction, with educators bringing their individual strengths and interests to the curriculum[8]. The school emphasizes **collaborative learning through projects, excursions, class trips, and integrated activities**[8].

**Project-Based Learning Examples**

The curriculum incorporates thematic projects such as:
- Potato festival
- Air-themed project
- Theater and museum visits
- Visits to science centers (JUKUZ), planetariums, and archaeological museums[8][10]
- Youth circus (CABUWAZI) program in the fourth school year[2]

**Support and Enrichment**

The school operates a **support concept** (Förderkonzept) and offers **school-based activity groups (Schul-AGs)**[9]. Social work services are integrated into the school[9].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Afternoon Care Program**

The eFöB (supplementary support and care) operates across floors 1-4 of the school building, utilizing six group rooms and classroom spaces after instruction ends[4]. The program emphasizes:

- **Self-directed learning**: Children manage attendance through a name-labeled clip system to visualize their location, promoting independence while keeping parents informed[4]
- **Healthy lifestyle practices**: Emphasis on nutrition, adequate movement, and daily outdoor time using the school's generous outdoor facilities[4]
- **Cooperative responsibility**: Shared cleanup after use of materials and equipment, fostering collective responsibility[4]
- **Physical activity**: Access to small equipment including scooters, balls, and jump ropes[4]

**Outdoor Learning**

The school utilizes the **Treptower Park** as an extension of its learning environment[2]. The playground features climbing opportunities, play equipment, and the newly installed play surface designed to encourage diverse play activities[1][4].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The search results do not contain specific information about standardized test scores, academic rankings, or measurable student outcomes. The school's documented achievements focus on **infrastructure improvements** (2016-2018 renovation and expansion) and **environmental enhancements** (playground redesign with integrated play surfaces)[1][2][3].

The search results do not provide detailed admissions procedures, application deadlines, or enrollment requirements. The school website indicates an "Anmeldung Schule" (school registration) section[9], but specific admissions information is not included in the available content.

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Kiekemal-Schule emphasizes a child-oriented, respectful, and community-focused environment where students, teachers, educators, staff, parents, and guests feel welcome, learn successfully, and experience joy.[1][4][7] Its philosophy promotes curiosity-driven learning ("Kiek mal rin" – look inside), well-being, mutual respect, open communication, and fun, encapsulated in the motto of creating a place to feel good, get along, help each other, and grow.[4][7] The school fosters an inclusive, supportive atmosphere with values like community living and holistic development.[1][2][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1935/36 as the 10. Volksschule at Erich-Baron-Weg 106-118 (now a private school), the building was expanded in 1936/37 and modernized in 1968.[3] It evolved through name changes: 10. Grundschule (1945), 10. Oberschule (1959), 10. Polytechnische Oberschule "Helene Weigel" (1978 with grades 1-10), and became the 32. Grundschule (Kiekemal-Schule) in 1990, serving grades 1-6 per Berlin's system.[1][3] Located in Berlin-Mahlsdorf (Marzahn-Hellersdorf district), a family-friendly, nature-rich area, the main site is at Hultschiner Damm 219, 12623 Berlin, with a branch (Filiale) at Bütower Str. 16 hosting grades 1-2 temporarily (113 new first-graders in 2023/24).[1][2][3][9] Facilities include accessibility features (parking for disabled, wheelchair elevator, WC, ramps), a main building, branch, sports, creative, and musical areas.[1][7] Community involvement features a Schulförderverein (parent association), TMFV (dance/music club), social pedagogical services, school social work, and integration support.[1][5][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a public Grundschule (grades 1-6), it follows Berlin's curriculum with English as the first foreign language.[1] Programs include Hochbegabtenförderung (gifted education), Offene Ganztagsbetreuung (OGB) from 6:00-18:00, year-group learning groups in early phases, and an inclusion experiment.[1][2] Certified as a "Musikalische Grundschule" (musical primary school) with TMFV for dance, singing, and instruments; INKA-Schule for integration.[1] Emphasizes exploratory learning, social-pedagogical support, and cultural activities like talent shows.[1][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in a full-day program (6:00-18:00) promoting discovery, play, research in a natural setting, with sports, creativity, music, and community events (e.g., Christmas singing, talent shows, newsletters).[2][7] Social services provide counseling, integration (contact: Frau Bischoff et al.), and well-being support via school social workers Katrin Matthes and Saskia Kilian.[1][5] Experiences highlight respect, fun, meals via "Sunshine" caterer, and extracurriculars like TMFV.[2][5][7] Recent activities include "Unterricht aus dem Eis" (lessons from ice) and Weihnachtsbrief.[7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Certified as "Musikalische Grundschule" and INKA-Schule; participates in inclusion trials.[1] Positive inspection notes its quiet Mahlsdorf location supporting conditions for learning.[9] Hosts events like Talenteschau 2026 and welcomes large cohorts (113 first-graders in 2023/24).[3][7] No specific test results or outcomes detailed in available content.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public tuition-free Grundschule, admission follows Berlin's local enrollment for grades 1-6.[1] Contact Sekretariat at +49 (0)30 56 59 78 40 or sekretariat@kiekemal.schule.berlin.de for inquiries; integration via integration-kiekemal@tjfbg.de.[1][7] Open Ganztagbetreuung available; website provides term dates and updates.[2][7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
The Kiepert-Grundschule, named in the 1960s, was originally housed in three sequential school buildings on a narrow plot dating back to around 1850, with expansions in 1905 and 1910; these were demolished by 1973 after the school moved to a new complex in the 1970s.[1] The site later accommodated the Solling-Oberschule, built in the mid-1970s on former farmland with dual-use design for potential hospital conversion.[1] Current facilities at Prechtlstr. 21-23, 12277 Berlin (Marienfelde), include wheelchair accessibility (ground level or ramp), a sports hall with covered walkway, and spaces formerly used for pre-classes.[1][2][3] Community photos from 2006-2007 depict winter scenes, playground features like hopping stones, and local events such as class visits to a nearby fire station.[3]

### 3. CURRICUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The school follows Berlin's standard **Grundschule** (primary school) curriculum, with English as the first foreign language from grade 1 and additional profile classes in French and cooking.[2] Offerings include learning groups for new arrivals without German proficiency, year-specific and cross-year early-phase groups, open all-day care (OGB), and working groups (AGs) in English, chemistry (from grade 1), Spanish, soccer, dance, instrumental music, art, flute, aerobics, and more.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students participate in extracurriculars like an Evangelical-Catholic morning meetup, England language trips, and diverse AGs emphasizing languages, sports, arts, and sciences.[2] Facilities support active learning with oriented classrooms toward green areas, playgrounds, and sports halls.[3]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific achievements, exam results, or outcomes are detailed beyond general mentions of exam results availability and school quality indicators on official portals.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public **Grundschule**, admission follows Berlin's standard public primary school process, with support for non-German speakers via dedicated learning groups; contact via phone (+49 30 902774100), fax (+49 30 02774101), or email (07G28@07G28.schule.berlin.de).[2]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Klax International School focuses on modern, project-oriented, and self-organized learning to develop 21st-century key competencies, emphasizing art and digital skills to prepare students for future challenges.[1][2] The school's **Klax Pedagogy** prioritizes individual strengths, innovative methods, short instruction phases alternating with self-directed learning paths, learning stations, and viewing mistakes as growth opportunities; teachers act as learning guides rather than traditional instructors.[2][3] It promotes self-organized, creative, digital, and project-based education, fostering responsible, socially competent personalities in an international, professional, and cosmopolitan environment.[1][2][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 2000, the school became a **Gemeinschaftsschule** (comprehensive school) in 2020 and serves approximately 550 students from grade 1 to Abitur, with about 50 learning guides (specialist teachers, educators, and social pedagogues).[2][5] Located in Berlin-Pankow above a shopping center at Neumannstr. 13A, 13189 Berlin, it features three redesigned schoolyards on rooftops, a dedicated school library, and a Makerspace for technical equipment, crafts, and creativity.[1][4][5] The community emphasizes small class sizes (max 22 students per class, two classes per grade), age-mixed learning families, daily routines with morning/closing circles and three shared meals, and an international outlook with cultural diversity.[2][3][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a state-approved **Gemeinschaftsschule** with **gymnasiale Oberstufe**, it covers **Grundstufe** (primary, grades 1-4, bilingual German/English), **Mittelstufe** (middle school, bilingual), and **gymnasiale Oberstufe** (upper secondary leading to Abitur).[1][2] The bilingual track integrates English/German instruction; curriculum follows Berlin's Framework Curriculum, including project work (3-4 weeks/year), art trips, digital skills via MS Office 365 Teams, and preparation for nationwide exams (Abitur, MSA recognized by Berlin Senate).[1][2][3][5] Focus areas include art (extra lessons in primary) and digitalization; all graduations enable higher qualifications nationwide.[2][5]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in a full-day program with ganztags (all-day) structure, project weeks, art and study orientation, and extracurriculars like sports festivals, theater projects ("Who Am I?", "Wildlife"), graffiti clubs, maker messes/robo competitions, and school firms ("2 Life Plastic").[1] International exchanges via Erasmus+ include trips to Italy, Finland, Greece (Thessaloniki), Denmark, and partnerships (eTwinning, Niftarlake College); sustainability AG "Our Happy Future" organizes cleanups, World Cleanup Day, and conferences.[1] Other activities: Zukunftstag (career day), Vorlesetag (reading day), Herbstmarkt, Schüler-Mauerlauf (running event), ARD-Radionacht, and guest speakers (e.g., journalists, authors).[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
- **MINT-freundliche Schule** (STEM-friendly school) award.[1]
- eTwinning-Siegel for sustainability project.[1]
- Förderpreis "Praktisches Lernen" for school firm "2 Life Plastic"; Sonderpreis Dieter Schwarz Stiftung.[1]
- "School without Racism – School with Courage"; "Excellent Digital School" (Berlin); Comenius EduMedia Award 2022; nominated German School Prize 2021 final.[2]
- 1st place JUNIOR Landeswettbewerb Berlin 2024; 2nd place architecture competition.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Quereinstieg (mid-year entry) is possible; events like Tag der offenen Tür (open days, e.g., 24.01.2026, 27.09.2025) and Zukunftstag (20.03.2026) support inquiries.[1] No specific enrollment process details provided. **No tuition or fee amounts in EUR** (e.g., Schulgeld, Monatsbeitrag, Aufnahmegebühr) found across main pages, 24 fee/tagged pages, or PDFs; no income-based, meal, material, or after-school fees mentioned.[1][2]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Klecks-Grundschule emphasizes a school environment rooted in **mutual respect, reliability, openness, and tolerance**. It prioritizes **high-quality instruction** and a **demanding leisure program**, supported by local partners, parents, and a sponsoring association.[6][5] Since the 2021/22 school year, it operates as an **open all-day school** (Ganztagsschule) offering diverse activities inside and outside regular lessons.[5][1][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Pankow's Tiroler Viertel, a mixed residential area of old and new buildings, the school serves approximately **540 students**.[2] It is publicly owned by Bezirksamt Pankow, led by principal **Anna Paulus**, with over **35 teachers** and **20 educators**, plus support staff including school social workers and psychologists.[1][3][4][7] Facilities support all-day care, with a virtual tour and school brochure available online; a new school profile focuses on **"Movement and Relaxation"**, highlighted by a student art contest.[6][8] Community involvement includes partnerships from the neighborhood (Kiez), parents, and a Förderverein.[6]

### 3. CURRICURICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Grundschule (primary school)**, it features **English as the first foreign language**, year-group-based learning groups in the early phase (Schulanfangsphase), and project-based learning with public presentations.[1][3][5] Classes typically stay with the same lead teacher for two years, with changes after grades 2 and 4 for optimal expertise matching; annual project weeks and school-wide themes integrate learning across subjects.[5] Additional offerings include after-school programs (AG-Angebote), midday meals, and school station support.[1][3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students benefit from **all-day opening hours**: Monday-Thursday 7:00-16:00, Friday 7:00-15:30, with no care during specified holidays like summer breaks (e.g., 11-15 August 2025, 27-31 July 2026) and teacher days.[Scraped Main Page][Scraped About Page] Experiences emphasize participation in school decisions, joint celebrations, and afternoon leisure activities, fostering community and belonging.[5][6] School social work operates specific hours (e.g., Mon 8:45-16:00, Fri 8:45-15:30), and integration support is available.[7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific test results, awards, or outcome metrics are detailed in the sources. The school highlights student-led initiatives like the art contest for its "Movement and Relaxation" profile and ongoing support from social workers since 2021/22.[2][8]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
New first-graders (Erstklässler:innen) can be cared for starting **1 August** of the calendar year; an **Open Day** is scheduled for 30 September 2025 (10:00-15:00), with a virtual tour film as an alternative.[6][Scraped Main Page] A **school brochure (Schul-Steckbrief)** is available in the "About the School" section.[6]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Kolibri-Grundschule emphasizes inclusive, peaceful learning for all students, guaranteeing equal participation and respecting each child's individuality from the school entry phase through 6th grade.[5][7] The school promotes a violence-free environment, social competence, and collaborative efforts among all involved groups to foster successful learning tailored to students' abilities.[1][5][7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Established in a building originally constructed in 1997 as a cooperative primary school with a support center, Kolibri-Grundschule moved into the facility in 2011.[3] It is the largest primary school in Berlin's Marzahn-Hellersdorf district, operating across three locations with approximately 900 students in 38 regular and small classes.[1] Facilities include barrier-free access (wheelchair-accessible entrance, WC, elevator, and parking), a large school garden, gymnasium complex, homework room, school club, and a family center (FaZKo) offering open consultations, parent café, creative meetups, and garden activities for families.[1][2][4] The family center operates Monday-Wednesday 8-17 Uhr, Thursday 10-16 Uhr, Friday 8-15:30 Uhr, and supports social learning through programs like Sozialer Gruppenarbeit (SGA).[1][4] Leadership is provided by Anke Peters.[2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum features a **sport-emphasized** and **language-emphasized** profile, with English as the first foreign language.[1][2] It includes participation in the INKA school experiment for inclusive education, ongoing temporary learning groups (TLGs) for diverse support needs, reading-writing support groups, and year-specific or cross-year learning groups in the early school phase.[1][2] Additional offerings encompass open all-day care (OGB), social group work, and working groups in apparatus gymnastics, athletics, dancing, and woodworking.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in hands-on projects like traffic safety ("Toter Winkel"), sports qualifications for ISTAF competitions, and garden activities.[4][9] Social learning focuses on conflict resolution, solidarity, and violence prevention, with improved playground behavior and peer inspiration noted through programs like ProRespekt.[7] All-day options include meals, homework support, and extracurriculars promoting peaceful coexistence.[2][7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school supports around 120 new students annually during enrollment events and has qualified teams for prestigious sports events like ISTAF.[1][6][9] Inclusive practices from INKA have become integral, enhancing integration and social competencies, with visible improvements in individual and group behavior on playgrounds and in classes.[1][7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public Grundschule, admissions follow Berlin's standard catchment-area process for local residents, with enrollment events like the September 13 festivities for new first-graders.[6] Open house days are listed in the Berlin education calendar; contact the school for details (Tel: +49 30 9989232, email: 10G22@10G22.schule.berlin.de).[2]

# Kolumbus-Grundschule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Kolumbus-Grundschule is committed to awakening and fostering the spirit of discovery in its students, inspired by its namesake Christopher Columbus.[6] The school's pedagogical approach emphasizes **independent learning** as a central focus, with the goal of enabling all children to make a successful transition to secondary school with a solid foundation in basic education.[4]

The school is dedicated to creating an inclusive learning environment that serves all children equitably, regardless of gender or migration background.[4] The educational philosophy integrates cross-curricular and interdisciplinary support for students, including reading instruction, language development, violence prevention, and student participation.[6]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Historical Background**

The Kolumbus-Grundschule was **built in 1970** and designed by architect Sergius Ruegenberg, who was influenced by Hans Sharoun's organic architecture principles.[2][3][4] The main building complex was completed between 1968 and 1970.[2] The school is located in the **Reinickendorf district** in southeast Berlin, near the Klemkepark, in a quiet, green setting.[4] The building is protected as an architectural monument (Baudenkmal).[1]

**Architectural Features**

The school's distinctive **hexagonal design** creates a unique stepped building structure that maximizes natural light across all floors.[1][2] The hexagonal classrooms are arranged in a honeycomb-like pattern, grouping classes by grade level in two- to three-story interconnected structures.[1] The main building is surrounded by rolling green spaces, with large south-facing windows in the classrooms.[1]

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school campus includes:
- Specialized rooms for music, art, and natural sciences[1]
- A multipurpose room and student library ("Leseclub")[1]
- Two playground areas with wooden climbing structures, slides, four table tennis tables, and a fenced soccer field[1]
- A small sports facility with two running tracks and a long jump pit[1]
- A 13m × 26m sports hall in one of the extension buildings[1]
- A former kindergarten facility (integrated since 2005) used for supplementary support and care programs[1]
- A mobile extension building with twelve classrooms (erected in 2006/2007)[1]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

The school implements **cross-curricular and interdisciplinary instruction** that supports diverse student learning needs.[6] Specific curriculum areas include:
- Reading and literacy instruction
- Language development and support
- Violence prevention programs
- Student participation initiatives[6]

The school practices **mixed-age learning** (jahrgangsübergreifendes Lernen) for grades one through three.[2]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**School Structure and Care**

The Kolumbus-Grundschule operates as an **open all-day school (Offene Ganztagsgrundschule - OGS)**, providing reliable supervision from 7:30 AM to 1:30 PM.[4] All children have access to a daily warm lunch in the school's cafeteria upon request.[4]

**Support Programs**

Since 2011, the school has offered a welcome class (Willkommensklasse) for newly arrived students.[1] The school collaborates with various external partners and programs to support student development, including:
- Children for a Better World e.V.
- Create Your Life - SETHASA (student support)
- eEducation Berlin Masterplan
- Federal program "Menschen stärken Menschen"
- Student Budget program (Schüler*innen Haushalt)
- Youth social work programs[6]

**Student Projects and Achievements**

In 2024, student projects at the school won recognition in the Children's Jury competition, with two projects ("Our Courtyard Should Be More Beautiful" and "The Colorful Construction Wagon") receiving a combined grant of €1,400.[6]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The school underwent an official inspection by the Berlin School Inspection Authority (Schulinspektion), with a report documented in the Berlin Digital Library.[8] The school has established itself as a recognized educational institution in the Reinickendorf district, combining innovative pedagogical approaches with distinctive architectural design that supports learning.

## 6. ADMISSIONS

As a public primary school (Grundschule) in Berlin, the Kolumbus-Grundschule follows standard Berlin enrollment procedures for primary education. Specific admissions details are not provided in the available search results. Interested families should contact the school directly or consult the Berlin education authority (Senatsverwaltung für Bildung) for current enrollment information.

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Königin-Luise-Stiftung's mission centers on the leitbild **"Selbst sein - Miteinander - Weiterkommen"** (Be yourself - Together - Progress), emphasizing individuality, respectful social interaction, and personal development through competence-building, self-directed learning, and real-world relevance.[Main Page][About Page] It promotes traditional values like respect, tolerance, and responsibility alongside modern education, fostering a community where children, youth, and adults learn together in a supportive environment that values diversity, conflict resolution, and sustainability.[About Page][PDF: Schulordnung der KLS_2025.pdf]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1811 as a "living memorial" to Queen Luise, influenced by reformers like Delbrück and Humboldt, the foundation has evolved from a girls' institute into a modern educational campus combining preserved traditions with contemporary needs.[About Page][4] The campus at **Podbielskiallee 78, 14195 Berlin** (opposite the Botanical Garden) integrates schools, boarding, sports fields, playgrounds, and a park for holistic living and learning.[Main Page][About Page] It serves ~950 students, 33 boarding places, and ~170 staff, with a family-like atmosphere across grade-homogeneous classes, Montessori options, and small groups.[Main Page][Gymnasium Page]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The foundation offers a **Grundschule** (grades 1-4/6, ~300 students) with year-group homogeneous classes or mixed-age Montessori; an **Integrierte Sekundarschule (ISS)** (secondary, unique in Berlin for enabling all qualifications including MSA); and a **Gymnasium** (~470 students, grades 5-13, class sizes 20-24, 100% Abitur pass rate).[Main Page][Gymnasium Page][1][2] Curriculum follows Berlin standards with enhancements: ITG and Medienkunde as subjects, social practicum (class 8), career practicum (class 9), Wahlpflichtkurse (e.g., third languages like Italian, arts, IT), free textbooks/ebooks in Sek I, modern tech (interactive boards, laptops, WLAN).[Gymnasium Page][3] Languages include English (intensified, Cambridge certs), French (DELF), Latin, Spanish (DELE); upper secondary offers performance courses in German, History, Politics.[3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students benefit from small classes, open all-day programs, homework supervision, inclusion support, and differentiated learning (e.g., extra Math/English hours, reading training, presentation skills in ISS).[ISS Angebote Page][Gymnasium Page] Extracurriculars include UNESCO projects (peace, environment), sustainability initiatives, bio-certified cafeteria with DGE standards, seasonal/regional meals, free special diets, and feedback options.[Main Page] Boarding (Jugendhilfe-Internat, up to 33 places for ages 11-18 in family-like groups) emphasizes social skills, therapy support, and integration with school.[Main Page][4][6] Sports, arts, music, theater, and creative activities are available; events like Infotag (24.01.2026), Lichtermarkt, Weihnachtsparty.[Main Page][4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Certified as a **UNESCO-Projektschule** for peace, environment, sustainability, and equity projects, and **Internationale Nachhaltigkeitsschule - Umweltschule in Europa** for development-focused initiatives.[Main Page][5] Gymnasium reports **100% Abitur pass rate**; digital school ranking place 27.[Gymnasium Page][3] Strong focus on individualized support yields high engagement, with small classes and modern facilities aiding outcomes.[Gymnasium Page]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Admissions involve an online form selecting Grundschule, ISS, Gymnasium, or Internat; contact specific secretariats (e.g., Cornelia Otto for Grundschule: +49 30 84181440).[Admissions Page] Applications accepted anytime, including mid-year for vacancies; regular intake for grades 1 and 5 in Grundschule.[9] **Fees (Schulgeld)** are income-dependent, tiered for fairness, paid in 12 equal monthly rates; reduced for siblings (2nd/3rd+ child); annual reset, must reapply by July for reductions (otherwise max rate applies); starts next month after application with proofs (e.g., social aid qualifies for tier 1); no retroactive.[Fee Page] No specific EUR amounts listed; contacts: Michelle Zurek (+49 30 84181312), Daniel Russau (+49 30 84181321).[Fee Page] Meals free of extra charge for allergies/diabetes; no other fees detailed.[Main Page]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Konkordia-Grundschule operates under the motto **"Schule als Lebenswelt"** (School as a Living World), emphasizing a holistic approach where school extends beyond academics to foster comprehensive child development, social skills, and lifelong learning.[5][7] Core values include individual support tailored to each child's developmental stage, respectful interactions, mutual tolerance across 29 nationalities, non-violent communication, conflict resolution, and ethical foundations like respect, solidarity, and empathy.[2][3][4] The philosophy prioritizes reading promotion across all subjects, cooperative learning, and preparing students for independent action through fachliche (subject-specific), methodological, social, and personal competencies.[2][3][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded on **January 4, 1922**, in Berlin-Wilhelmstadt (originally at Konkordiastr.), the school moved to its current building at Elsflether Weg 26 in 1965, accommodating 436 children initially.[7] It serves a diverse community in Berlin-Spandau with **474 students** (243 boys, 231 girls) across **18 classes** from 29 nationalities in a socially varied catchment area.[2][3][7] Staff includes **44 teachers**, **14 educators**, 1 secretary, 1 janitor, 2 volunteer mediators, 5 reading sponsors, 1 school assistant, and a city library employee.[7] Facilities support open all-day care (OGB), a computer room, and expanded extracurriculars; a 2018 inspection praised the welcoming atmosphere and parental satisfaction.[1][6][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum features **English from Grade 1** and external subject differentiation (Deutsch, Mathematik, Englisch) in Grades 5-6 with 5 weekly hours in two-teacher, two-room, two-level groups focusing on shared goals with varied strategies.[2][3] Reading is a central focus integrated across subjects, with flexible differentiation for high-achievers (3-6 students per group) and support for weaker learners.[2] Lower grades (1-4) emphasize interdisciplinary, student-oriented lessons incorporating children's ideas; all-day offerings include homework circles (Mon-Thu) and clubs like football, judo, rowing, dance, drumming, computer, rhythm, choir, orchestra, theater, recorder, French, gardening, and games/athletics.[1][5][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students benefit from **all-day care (eFöB)** from **6:00 to 18:00**, homework assistance, and a new schedule from September 2, 2024: 7 periods (8:00-14:50) with three yard breaks (20-30 min).[1][5] Experiences promote social cohesion between age groups, self-responsibility, and a positive climate, with strong individual support in lower grades and cooperative tasks; upper-grade differentiation partially meets self-directed learning goals.[4][6] Extracurriculars and events (e.g., library visits, traffic school, Easter festival, gardening) enhance engagement; the school seeks volunteer reading mentors.[5][7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
A **2018 inspection** highlighted high parental satisfaction, diverse extracurriculars (sports to arts), welcoming environment, and effective lower-grade teaching with student input integration.[6] The school maintains an extensive internal curriculum, open Ganztagbetreuung, year-group learning groups in early phases, and first foreign language (English); it fosters a relaxed, appreciative atmosphere where children enjoy attending.[1][6] No specific test results or recent outcomes are detailed in available data.[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Enrollment for first-graders occurs **October 6-17, 2025**, for Berlin children born **October 1, 2019, to September 30, 2020**, starting school obligation in August 2026; parents must register at the local catchment Grundschule.[5] Contact: sekretariat@konkordia.schule.berlin.de or 030/33096560.[1][5]

# Konrad-Agahd-Schule (Grundschule) - School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Konrad-Agahd-Schule is a **public primary school** located in the heart of Neukölln, Berlin, with a strong commitment to integrating **digital media into everyday instruction**[9]. The school emphasizes a collaborative and inclusive approach, welcoming students, families, and educators to participate in its educational community.

The school operates under a **"BuddY" framework**, prioritizing peaceful and cooperative interaction among students[4]. This philosophy is reinforced through conflict resolution programs, including peer mediators (Konfliktlotsen) and door buddies (TürbuddYs), with all students learning and applying the "Stoppregel" (stop rule) to practice fair cooperation[4].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Historical Background**

The Konrad-Agahd-Grundschule was **founded in 1894** as one of two buildings constructed simultaneously with the Peter-Petersen-Grundschule on Jonasstraße[3]. Originally designated as the 11th and 12th municipal schools, the buildings initially operated under gender-separated instruction, with girls taught at the Thomasstraße location and boys at the Jonasstraße site[3]. The school was renamed in 1958 to honor **Konrad Agahd (1867–1922)**, a teacher in Rixdorf/Neukölln for 23 years who was known for his advocacy against child labor[6].

**Community Recognition**

The Konrad-Agahd-Grundschule is **among the most popular primary schools in Neukölln**, as evidenced by high enrollment applications[6]. The school has earned recognition as an **"Intel-(Teaching for the Future) Support Point School"** and an **"Accredited Model School for PC and Online Competence"**[1][5]. Since the 2019-20 school year, the school has also held the designation of **"Begabtenschule" (gifted school)**, supporting selected gifted children across all grade levels[4].

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school operates a **chalk-free learning environment**[5] and features "**Konrads Insel**" (Konrad's Island), a dedicated community space that integrates multiple functions[4][6]. This facility includes:

- A student library (Schülerbibliothek)
- A parent café (Elterncafé)
- A small kitchen
- The "brotZeit" breakfast program, where students receive free breakfast from 7:20 AM accompanied by volunteer seniors[4]

The renovation and construction of Konrads Insel cost approximately **€140,732** (funded by the Neukölln district), with furnishing and operational costs of approximately **€30,000** (funded through Social City funds)[6].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Core Academic Structure**

The school offers **English as the first foreign language**[1]. The curriculum is structured around several key pillars, including media literacy, language development, violence prevention, and all-day care programs[9].

**Specialized Programs**

- **Lernwerkstatt (Learning Workshop)**: Gifted students participate in special mathematically and scientifically-focused courses[4]
- **Familienklasse (Family Class)**: Now in its 9th year of operation, this program supports children with parental involvement in learning, guided by a teacher and family therapist[4]
- **Early Cycling Program**: Students begin learning to ride bicycles in grade 2[1]

**Project-Based Learning**

The school conducts two major school-wide project weeks annually:
- A one-week reading-focused project week in November[4]
- A one-week interdisciplinary project week in March[4]

**Support Services**

The school employs **four school social workers** from tandem BTL, providing families with counseling and support[4]. Additional support includes a school station (Schulstation) and open all-day care (Offene Ganztagbetreuung - OGB)[1].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Digital Integration**

Digital media use is embedded throughout instruction, reflecting the school's status as a model school for PC and online competence[1][9]. The school participates in the **eEducation Master Plan**, positioning it as a digitally advanced institution[1].

**Enrichment Opportunities**

Students benefit from:
- A **student parliament** (Schülerparlament) providing voice in school governance[9]
- **Conflict resolution training** through peer mediation programs[4]
- **Free breakfast program** ("brotZeit") supported by the brotZeit e.V. association and Lidl supermarket[4]
- Access to a school library and parent café within Konrads Insel[4]

**Class Organization**

The school features a **school entry phase (Schulanfangsphase) with grade-based learning groups**, allowing for developmentally appropriate instruction[1].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The school has achieved significant recognition within Berlin's educational landscape:

- **Accredited Model School status** for PC and online competence[1][5]
- **Gifted school designation** (Begabtenschule) since 2019-20[4]
- **Intel Teaching for the Future Support Point School** status[1]
- **eEducation Master Plan participation**[1]
- **High enrollment demand**, indicating strong community reputation[6]

The school's comprehensive approach to violence prevention, social-emotional learning, and academic excellence has established it as a leading institution in the Neukölln district.

## 6. ADMISSIONS

The search results provided do not contain specific information regarding admissions procedures, application deadlines, or enrollment requirements. For detailed admissions information, prospective families should contact the school directly at:

**Phone:** +49 30 632250680 (Sekretariat)[1]
**Email:** sekretariat@agahd.schule.berlin.de[1]
**Website:** www.agahd.net[1]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The **KreativitätsGrundschule Berlin Friedrichshain** operates as a state-recognized open all-day primary school emphasizing **creativity pedagogy** to foster problem-solving skills, potential development, and lifelong learning competencies for future challenges.[1][2][7] It views school as a full-day life and learning environment promoting creativity, individual talents, and holistic child development through small classes, team teaching, and open spaces for exploration while adhering to Berlin's framework curricula as minimum standards.[1][2] Core values include sustainability, social engagement (e.g., support for Ukraine refugees), non-violent communication, and preparation for unknown future professions by prioritizing creative thinking over rote knowledge.[1][7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded on **August 1, 2008**, the school is part of the **Kreativitätsschulzentrum Berlin gGmbH**, which has engaged in Friedrichshain, Lichtenberg, Marzahn, and Treptow for over 20 years, operating over 20 facilities including five kindergartens, three primary schools with after-school care (Hort/eFöB), school social stations, and youth housing.[1][7] Located at **Strausberger Str. 38, 10243 Berlin-Friedrichshain** (near Platz der Vereinten Nationen and Strausberger Platz, 2.4 km from Alexanderplatz), it serves grades 1-6 with about 320 students and integrates with co-located kindergarten and Hort for seamless transitions.[1][2][3][4] Facilities include generous school grounds, libraries, school social stations, Smartboards, iPads, computers from grade 1, high-quality meals accommodating intolerances, and on-site storage for school bags.[1][2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **state-recognized Ersatzschule (substitute school)** and open all-day Grundschule (grades 1-6), it follows **Berlin framework curricula** with enhancements like small classes (max. 24 students), team teaching (two educators for 10 weekly hours in German/math), English from grade 1, additional languages (French, Spanish, Chinese), computer lessons from grade 1, gentle grading from grade 1, high individualization, gifted education, remedial support, and differentiated learning.[1][2] Programs include creative extras, projects, homework integration, no class cancellations, and all-day care with Betreuungskernzeit.[1][2][4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students benefit from **creative add-ons** like project work, excursions, class trips, holiday programs (Ferienspiele, Ferienhort with activities like swimming badges, obstacle courses, inline skating), working groups (AGs) in choir, chess, badminton, traffic education, instrumental music, and events (e.g., family flea markets, company runs, enrollment ceremonies, Bildungsmarkt hosting).[1][2][4][7] Emphasis on arts, music, MINT (math/informatics/science/technology), sustainability (e.g., 1000kg potatoes initiative), nature/wald days, and social projects; integrated Hort offers diverse afternoon activities, festivals (Fasching, Easter, Christmas), and outings.[3][4][7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school's **Krokodil class (4a)** won a prize at the 4th Global Learning Conference (2019) for a project on UN Sustainability Goal "No Poverty," including research on child poverty, chocolate production, a Federal Ministry visit, and a published book with 15 other classes; prize included a school trip to Bockwindmühle Vehlefanz and vouchers.[scraped content] Certified by the **Dachverband Kreativitätspädagogik**; active in community events like hosting Berlin Free Schools Bildungsmarkt and Ukraine support initiatives.[1][2][7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Admissions occur via an **online form** for non-binding enrollment, with detailed procedures available on the site; limited spots available across year groups.[scraped content] **Fees are income-based monthly Schulgeld contributions** (effective from 01.08.2024 for KreativitätsGrundschulen including Friedrichshain), scaled by annual gross family income and number of children:

# KreativitätsGrundschule Berlin Lichtenberg – School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The **KreativitätsGrundschule Berlin Lichtenberg** operates as a **state-recognized private primary school** with a core mission centered on **creativity-based pedagogy and holistic child development**[1]. The school understands itself as "a full-day living and learning space for potential development and competency building," where all children can discover, unfold, and experience their abilities and competencies[1].

The school's pedagogical approach emphasizes **"learning to think differently"** through creative problem-solving processes[1]. Rather than transmitting predetermined knowledge, the school aims to equip students with lifelong competencies for creative thinking and action, preparing them for unknown future professions and challenges[1]. The institution is certified by the **Dachverband Kreativitätspädagogik** (Creative Pedagogy Association)[1].

Core values include **inclusive education** ("a school for all children"), **environmental sustainability** (designated as an "Umweltschule in Europa"), and **social responsibility**, with the organization actively supporting Ukrainian refugees across all its facilities[4].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

The **KreativitätsGrundschule Berlin Karlshorst** is located at **Ehrlichstraße 63, 10318 Berlin** in the Karlshorst district of Lichtenberg[5][6]. The school is situated directly at the edge of Seepark (lake park) in this neighborhood[1].

The school is part of the larger **Kreativitätsschulzentrum Berlin gGmbH**, a non-profit organization that has operated for **more than 20 years** across Berlin's districts of Friedrichshain, Lichtenberg, Marzahn, and Treptow[4]. The organization comprises **more than 20 facilities** including five kindergartens, three primary schools with integrated after-school care (Hort), school social stations, supervised youth residential communities, and supported independent living arrangements[4].

The school operates as an **open all-day facility** (Offener Ganztagsbetrieb) with integrated after-school care (ergänzende Förderung und Betreuung – eFöB)[1]. The school features **spacious grounds**, school libraries, and school social stations[1].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Levels:** Classes 1–6 (primary school)[5][6]

**Class Size:** 20–22 students per class, with **half-class sizes in creative subjects**[1]

**Core Academic Features:**
- Berlin state curriculum (Rahmenlehrpläne) as minimum standards[1]
- **Two educators per class** for five weekly hours each in German and Mathematics[1]
- **No class cancellations** (kein Unterrichtsausfall)[1]
- High individualization of instruction with differentiated learning[1]
- Careful grading system beginning in Grade 1[1]

**Languages Offered:**
- **English from Grade 1**[6]
- **Arabic from Grade 1**[6]
- **Chinese from Grade 1**[6]
- **French** (additional)[6]

**Technology Integration:**
- Smartboards and iPads in classrooms[1]
- Computer instruction from Grade 1[1]

**Special Programs and Support:**
- **Gifted student support** (Hochbegabtenförderung)[6]
- **Learning groups for reading/writing difficulties** (Lerngruppen für Lese-Rechtschreibschwierigkeiten)[6]
- **School social stations** (Schulstationen)[6]
- Remedial instruction and differentiated learning pathways[1]

**Creative Curriculum Areas:**
Beyond the standard curriculum, students participate in **creative subject areas** where they develop strengths and experience in diverse fields[1]. The school offers **high numbers of working groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften)** and project-based learning[1].

**Additional Offerings:**
- Holiday care and holiday trips (Ferienbetreuung, Ferienfahrten)[1]
- Lunch with choice options and accommodation for food intolerances[1]
- Backpacks remain at school (reducing student burden)[1]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Extracurricular Activities:**
The school offers a **high number of working groups** (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) and extensive creative offerings[1]. Recent activities documented include:
- Traffic education programs[1]
- Holiday play programs (Ferienspiele)[1]
- Family flea markets (Familientrödelmarkt)[1]
- Sustainability projects (e.g., 1,000 kg potato distribution to the neighborhood)[1]

**Project-Based Learning:**
Students engage in **intensive project work** aligned with UN Sustainable Development Goals. For example, Grade 4 students investigated global poverty and chocolate production, visited the Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development, and presented findings at the Global Learning Conference[1].

**Physical Activity:**
The school emphasizes movement and sports through obstacle courses, swimming programs, and participation in community running events[1].

**Social-Emotional Development:**
The school implements a **"virtue project"** that teaches social key competencies such as perseverance, kindness, patience, and mutual respect, recognized by the United Nations as an exemplary program[3].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Certifications and Recognition:**
- **State-recognized private school** (Staatlich anerkannte Ersatzschule)[1][6]
- **Certified by the Dachverband Kreativitätspädagogik** (Creative Pedagogy Association)[1]
- **"Umweltschule in Europa"** (Environmental School in Europe) designation[1]
- **UN recognition** for the virtue project promoting social competencies[3]

**Student Achievements:**
- Grade 4 class won recognition at the **4th Global Learning Conference** for their project on UN Sustainable Development Goal "No Poverty," with their work published in a book presented to politicians and media representatives[1]

**Community Engagement:**
The school actively participates in Berlin's educational marketplace and hosts events for free schools in Berlin[1].

## 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES

**Enrollment Process:**
The school accepts applications through an **online registration form** on its website[1]. The organization notes that there are **occasionally free places in all grade levels** across its KreativitätsGrundschulen[4].

**Tuition Structure:**
Tuition is **income-based** and calculated according to family gross annual income[1]. The school uses a **sliding scale model** with reduced fees for families with lower incomes and additional children[1].

**Monthly Tuition by Income Level (as of August 2024):**

| Annual Gross Income | 1st Child | 2nd Child | 3rd Child | 4th Child | 5th Child |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Under €15,401 | €76.75 | €57.50 | €38.25 | €19.00 | €0.00 |
| €15,401–€29,420 | €153.75 | €138.25 | €92.25 | €46.00 | €0.00 |
| €29,421–€36,419 | €235.75 | €188.25 | €125.50 | €62.75 | €0.00 |

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
KreativitätsGrundschule Berlin-Treptow emphasizes creativity, holistic learning, and project-based education to foster confident, social, innovative, and solution-oriented personalities. As a state-recognized Ersatzschule, it follows Berlin's Rahmenlehrpläne while integrating **Kreativitätspädagogik** (creativity pedagogy) for comprehensive child development, with creative methods in all subjects, small class sizes (max 22 students), and a focus on individual strengths through differentiation, social learning, and networking of morning lessons with afternoon programs.[1][3][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Treptow-Köpenick (Treptow district, Niederschöneweide area) at **Hartriegelstr. 77, 12439 Berlin**, the school operates in a natural setting next to a Birkenwäldchen (birch grove), with extensive outdoor facilities including a schoolyard, football field, nature stage, playgrounds, climbing area, and developing Schulgarten for sustainability education. Indoor facilities include a turnhalle (gym), school library, Lernwerkstatt, and eFöB rooms such as Kreativraum, digital media room, Spieleraum, Konstruktionsraum, Holzwerkstatt, Kunstlatelier, Tanz- und Bewegungsraum, and Gemeinschaftsraum. It serves ~160-180 students from grades 1-6 with ~19 educators (including 5 for integration), led by Jürgen Fischer (Schulleiter) and Friedemann Fehr (stellv. Schulleiter/eFöB Leitung); part of KreativitätsSchulzentrum gGmbH.[1][2][3][4][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMICS PROGRAMS
This **Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6) delivers all Berlin Rahmenlehrplan subjects at maximum weekly hours: Deutsch, Mathematik, Englisch (from grade 1 or 3), Sachunterricht/Naturwissenschaften/Gesellschaftswissenschaften, Kunst, Musik, Sport (in gym, yard, or woods), plus Schwimmen (grade 3) and Radfahrprüfung (grade 4). Special features include no homework until grade 5, digital tools (tablets, smartboards), creative methods (Stationenlernen, Lernwerkstatt, Lego Serious Play, outdoor learning), bewertungsfreie Räume (FreiDay grades 1-4, Werkstatt der Künste grades 5-6), second teachers in Deutsch/Mathematik, and pathways for gifted students to transfer to Gymnasium after grade 4. Textbooks include Jojo (Deutsch/Mathematik), Sally/Lighthouse (Englisch), Pusteblume (Sachunterricht).[1][3][4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in vibrant, hands-on activities blending academics with creativity: Stop-Motion videos, water-themed Lernwerkstätten (Regentänze, African masks, Natur experiments), Antarktis live links, Breakdance workshops with world champion Sebastian Jaeger, Artenvielfalt/Naturschutz outings, frozen soap bubble experiments, Zauneidechsen patenschaften with NABU, snow sledding, art explosions, Pixelart, Berufsmesse, Sommerfest art stands, Monsterwerkstatt. Afternoon eFöB (06:00-18:00, Mon-Fri) offers 6-week rotating projects in Natur & Umwelt, Bewegung & Gesundheit, Literatur, Ausdruck & kreatives Spiel, Kunst & Gestalten, Handwerk, Kultur und Kommunikation, Zukunftskompetenzen (3D printing, robots, Minecraft Education); free play in themed rooms; AGs for math/tech/handcraft/language/arts; instrumental music (gitarre, flöte, etc.).[1][3][4][7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific awards, rankings, or test results (e.g., Prüfungsergebnisse) are detailed in the content. Participation noted in competitions like Känguru, Heureka, Mathematikolympiade, Big Challenge.[3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Admissions involve contacting the school office (schule-treptow@krea-schulzentrum.de, 030 225027733, Friedemann Fehr for school, Bettina Rudolph for eFöB); process uses EduPage for login/anmeldung, with Beratungsgespräche for gifted transfers. No specific enrollment requirements listed. **Fee/tuition information**: No exact EUR amounts extracted from text or PDFs (content shows PDF "Schulkostenbeitragstabelle_2024_08.pdf" as fee-related, but no parsed figures; other PDFs like Datenblatt_Krea_Treptow_24_25.pdf are non-fee). Fees likely detailed in unextracted PDFs; contact school for current Schulkostenbeiträge (school contributions), potentially income-based or for eFöB/materials/meals.[scraped content]
