The search results do not provide explicit information about admissions procedures, application deadlines, or enrollment requirements. As a public school in Berlin, the Rixdorfer Schule operates under standard Berlin public school enrollment policies, though specific details are not available in the provided sources.

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Robert-Reinick-Grundschule emphasizes a child-centered approach where every child is recognized and taken seriously in their individuality, promoting respect, understanding, tolerance, non-violence, and democratic values to create a welcoming school environment.[1][7] Key goals include fostering methods, subject-specific, self, and social competencies through diverse teaching methods to spark joy and motivation in learning, viewing mistakes as opportunities for development.[1][7] The school supports positive conflict culture, democracy education, and diversity as a strength, with social-pedagogical support for students, parents, and teachers in personal development, conflict resolution, and transitions.[3][7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1907-1909 as the 11th Spandauer Gemeindeschule (initially "Waldschule") with Siemens support in what is now Jugendweg, the school expanded with a northern wing and gym; renamed Robert-Reinick-Grundschule in 1957 after the painter and poet Robert Reinick (1805-1852), and incorporated buildings from Hermann-Löns-Grundschule in 2006.[4] Located in Berlin-Spandau (Siemensstadt) at Jungfernheideweg 33-49 (or 32-48 per some sources), it serves 548 students (as of 27.09.2024) in a public, tuition-free setting with barrier-free access including parking for disabled, wheelchair elevator, and accessible WC.[1][2][5] Facilities include a library, computer room, media islands in classrooms, interactive whiteboards, projectors, multipurpose room, art room, developing school garden with beehives, leisure rooms, and cafeteria (Mensa).[2] The community features a music profile (guitar in logo), sports profile (basketball), and science focus (microscope); leadership includes Schulleiter Herr Michael Nordgerling (until 11.04.2025, then N.N.), with deputies Frau Sophie Darko and Frau Martina Melster.[1][4][5] After-school care (Hortbetreuung/eFöB) is provided by CJD Berlin e.V. on-site.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a reliable half-day primary school (Verlässliche Halbtagsgrundschule/VHG) with open all-day options (eFöB/OGB), it uses year-group learning (Jahrgangsbezogenes Lernen/JabL) in early phases, mandatory English from grade 3, and learning groups for new arrivals without German proficiency.[1][2] Focus areas include artistic, musical, sports, and natural sciences; extracurricular activities (AGs) cover Liedertheater, Leseprofis (reading promotion for 4th graders), Theater (grades 3-5), Schach, international cooking (grades 5-6), and Ninja/Parkour (grades 2-5) via Sportkinder Berlin e.V.[2][4][6] Events like project week "Ich bin fit" (14.-18.07.2025), "Zu Fuß zur Schule" action days (15.-26.09.2025), and open day for new students (01.10.2025, 10:15-11:45) enhance programs.[8]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in hands-on, creative learning with profiles in music, sports, and sciences, supported by modern tech like whiteboards and computers, plus outdoor elements like the school garden.[1][2][4] Extracurriculars promote skills in reading, theater, strategy (chess), cooking, and physical agility (parkour), fostering creativity, teamwork, and cultural awareness.[6] Social support includes conflict mediation, democracy education, and transition assistance from kindergarten to secondary school, with school social work for personal growth.[3] All-day care ensures extended supervision; recent initiatives emphasize fitness, walking to school for safety/movement, and community events.[1][8]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific test results, awards, or outcome metrics are detailed in available sources; the school reports steady enrollment (548 students) and is expanding, currently hiring qualified teachers.[1][8] Strengths highlighted include barrier-free access, diverse profiles, and comprehensive facilities, with positive community feedback implied through active events and partnerships.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public Grundschule, admission follows Berlin's standard enrollment process via Schulanmeldung; an open day for new starters and parents is scheduled for 01.10.2025 (10:15-11:45, Altbau tour).[8] Support for non-German speakers via lerngruppen; contact Sekretariat at (030) 36702460 or Sekretariat@05G11.schule.berlin.de for inquiries.[1][2][8]

It promotes individual student development by addressing diverse learning prerequisites, encouraging recognition of strengths, error management, high performance motivation, independence, responsibility, social competence, and non-violent conflict resolution.[1] The school values a motivating learning environment where students feel comfortable, supported by well-equipped spaces and a focus on holistic, competency-oriented education.[1][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Lichtenberg (Ortsteil Rummelsburg) at Wönnichstr. 7, 10317 Berlin, this **public Grundschule (primary school, school number 11G08)** serves around 350 students, primarily from the local area of renovated old buildings and multi-family houses.[2][4][6] It operates as a **gebundene Ganztagsschule** (bound all-day school) since at least 2003/2004, with a large, clean building, expansive schoolyard featuring old chestnut trees, adventure playground, and football pitch.[1][4][6] Facilities include 17 classrooms, specialized rooms for music, art, and natural sciences, computer lab, library, and a full floor of themed rooms for supplementary support and care (e.g., Kniffel/Ramba-Zamba room, Forscherinsel).[1][4] Contact: (030) 522 80 89 811, info@robinsonschule-berlin.de.[1]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum extends beyond standard frameworks with offerings in **computer, environment, life skills/religion, and psychomotorics**.[1][6] It features a **Schulanfangsphase (SAPh)** with six year-group-spanning learning groups, health-focused and media-focused profiles, English as first foreign language, and all-day operation.[4][6] Since 2024/25, the school participates in the **"Flex Ganztag" pilot**, enhancing daily variety through modules in research, sports, music, art, and exploration.[1][9] Detailed curricula exist for SAPh, German, and music; instruction is competency-based, differentiated by levels, with needs for further individualization and digital media in upper grades.[4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in a structured all-day rhythm with versatile after-school activities (AGs) like keyboard, judo, crafting, computer, dance, choir, musical, cooking, psychomotorics, football, and ball sports.[1][6] **FlexModules** (e.g., football on Tuesdays) require registration and club membership (e.g., FSV Berolina).[1] Extracurricular highlights include holiday programs (winter/herbstferien), open days (e.g., 17.11.2025, 09:00-14:00 with tours and presentations), sports (football, athletics, swimming, judo), projects, excursions, and events like Robinsonfest and Christmas celebrations.[1][4] The schoolyard supports active breaks; indoor spaces promote play and supplementary care.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school maintains stable enrollment (~350 students) and demonstrates strong conceptual work in all-day rhythm and school development.[4] It features positive inspection outcomes in management, with 23% non-German native speakers and consistent local intake.[4] Categories indicate robust programs in sports (76 posts, e.g., football 35, athletics 19), school events (63), and supplementary education (EFöB, 48).[1] Ongoing developments target individualized upper-grade learning and digital integration.[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free Grundschule**, admission follows Berlin's standard primary school allocation, with annual **Einschulung (enrollment ceremonies)**.[1][4] Open days (e.g., Tag der offenen Tür 17.11.2025 or 08.10.2024) allow tours, program overviews, and presentations.[1][9] FlexModule participation (e.g., football) requires binding registration post-trials and school-AG membership.[1]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Rosa-Parks-Grundschule emphasizes a welcoming environment for diversity, where every child regardless of ethnic, cultural, religious, or social background enriches the school community.[1][2] Its **Leitbild** (school guiding principles) focuses on four pillars: embracing diversity ("Anders sein dürfen"), fostering a safe and trusting climate through tolerance and respect with social learning as a core emphasis, promoting community through open doors for children, parents, staff teamwork, and local partnerships, and supporting joyful learning via project-based, differentiated instruction to build motivation and self-confidence.[1][2][3] Theater and yoga are integral to the school's profile.[1][2][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded on August 1, 2012, through the merger of Niederlausitz-Grundschule and Paul-Dohrmann-Schule (formerly a special education center), the school marked its 10th anniversary in 2022 with **Vision 2032** outlining future development.[3][4][5] Located in Berlin-Kreuzberg (Reichenberger Kiez) at Reichenberger Str. 65, 10999 Berlin, it serves a diverse urban neighborhood with strong community ties, including parent involvement, local partnerships, and a broad educational network.[1][2][6] As a four-track all-day school with ~650 students, 50 teachers, and 26 educators, facilities support structured daily rhythms alternating instruction, care, relaxation, and play, with staggered yard breaks by grade clusters (1/2, 3/4, 5/6) and a schoolyard for optimal use.[2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
This **vierzügige gebundene Ganztagsschule** (four-track mandatory all-day school) runs Monday-Thursday 08:15-15:50 and Fridays until after lunch, with free care from 07:30 and optional paid modules from 06:00-18:00 including holidays.[2] Organized by double grade levels (1/2, 3/4, 5/6) with dedicated teams and classroom transitions at grades 3 and 5, the curriculum prioritizes: **Unterrichtsentwicklung** (training basic competencies, weekly Frei-Day projects on UN's 17 SDGs since 2024/25, screening tools like Laube/Matinko, temporary learning groups, yoga in early years, inclusive instruction, craft groups, reading mentors); **Demokratische Bildung** (social learning curriculum, biweekly class councils, student parliament, assemblies, anti-discrimination focus); and **Theaterpädagogik/Kulturelle Bildung** (theater profile, cultural agents, HofFestSpiele, UdK cooperation).[2][7] All-day integration includes ~1-hour staggered lunch breaks.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in project-oriented learning, such as Frei-Day small-group SDG projects and 5th-grade films on goals like health, education, innovation, biodiversity, and partnerships.[1][7] Theater productions like "Game on" (June-July 2025) explore gaming culture, with performances from June 27 premiere to July 9 HofFestSpiele.[1] Daily rhythms balance tension/relaxation; social learning addresses emotions, conflicts, empathy; yoga and theater build skills; democratic elements include student parliaments and co-design of spaces.[1][2][4] Inklusion, individual support, and parent involvement enhance experiences in a tolerant, participatory environment.[2][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Celebrated 10-year milestone in 2022 with Vision 2032; strong identification with namesake Rosa Parks' civil rights legacy.[1][4][5] Recognized strengths include clear leadership, social competency programs, tailored partner offers, theater implementation, parent integration, and student co-governance.[5] Unique features like theater/yoga distinguish it locally; ongoing projects like SDG films and theater shows demonstrate cultural engagement.[1][2][7] Partizipation emphasized, with students shaping spaces and learning conditions.[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public Grundschule** (tuition-free), admissions follow Berlin's standard public school assignment based on residence in the catchment area (Einzugsbereich) in Kreuzberg.[2][6][8] No specific application details provided; contact via mail@rosa-parks-grundschule.de or +49 30 22502511.[6]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Rothenburg-Grundschule operates as an **offene Gesamttagsschule** (open all-day school) with a strong emphasis on **Montessoripädagogik**, promoting self-determination, eigenverantwortung (personal responsibility), and inklusive (inclusive) education across learning, socio-emotional development, and language.[1][5][6] It integrates multiprofessional teams, including educators supporting classes for about six hours weekly, and fosters year-group mixed teaching (Jahrgangsmischung) for grades 1-3 and 4-6 to encourage autonomous play and interest-driven activities in specialized rooms like kreativraum, bewegungsraum, and ruheraum.[5][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Built in **1910-1911** by Hans Heinrich Müller as a Finanzamt (tax office), the school was renovated and expanded into a Grundschule from 2006-2011 (completed 2013), forming a protected architectural ensemble with the neighboring Fichtenberg-Oberschule.[1][2][3] Located in a verkehrsberuhigter Bereich (traffic-calmed area) at the foot of Fichtenberg near the Botanischer Garten, it serves ~**280 students** in 12 classes (max 23 per class, zweizügig), with over **50 staff** including teachers, quereinsteiger, schulhelfer, erzieher, sekretärin, verwaltungsleiterin, and hausmeister.[1][5][8] Facilities include PC and internet in every classroom, 16-PC computer room, smartboards, whiteboards, Montessori materials, and a new **Bücherinsel** library built in 2024 with student involvement using Deutscher Schulpreis funds.[1][4][5] Community ties include cooperation with Johann-August-Zeune-Schule für Blinde and Nachbarschaftsheim Schöneberg e.V. for ganztagsbetreuung since 2009.[1][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum features **jahrgangsübergreifend (JüL)** teaching for grades 1-3 and 4-6, English as first foreign language from grade 3 (in homogeneous groups, up to three for grade 3), and regular lesefähigkeiten (reading skills) testing with diagnostic tools.[5][7] Grades 1-4 use lernentwicklung Berichte (learning development reports) instead of numerical Zeugnisse, with lernentwicklungsgespräche (development discussions) in grades 3-4; one educator per early-phase (123) and 456 class supports instruction.[1][5] Inclusive practices and Montessori materials are standard.[5][6][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students enjoy **ganztagsbetreuung** from 6:00 AM in Hort, pre-lessons from 7:30 AM, post-lessons until after 13:40 (halbtagsgrundschule), and midday meals (grades 456 in second break, 123 after lessons).[1][6] Open work allows free pursuit of interests in functional rooms across floors, with student-led projects like the 2024 Bücherinsel construction involving planning, modeling, building, and opening.[4][6] Inklusion is emphasized through diagnostics, Fördermaterialien, and multiprofessional support.[6][7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school won the **Deutscher Schulpreis** (German School Prize), using **30,000€** prize money for the student-designed Bücherinsel library opened March 18, 2024, demonstrating effective inklusionspädagogik with proven reading improvements via testdiagnostik.[4][7] Its Denkmal status highlights successful heritage-preserving renovations.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free Grundschule**, admissions follow standard Berlin public school processes for local residents; contact sekretariat@rothenburg.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 90299-2314 for einschulung details.[1][9]

# Rudolf-Hildebrand-Grundschule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Rudolf-Hildebrand-Grundschule welcomes children from diverse backgrounds with a commitment to fostering individual development. The school's core mission emphasizes supporting all students to recognize and develop their individual strengths while promoting inclusive learning.[1] The school operates under the motto "Wir leben Gemeinschaft" (We live community), reflecting its dedication to creating a cohesive learning environment where students from approximately 40 different nations learn together.[1][4]

The school has established **seven golden rules** that guide behavior for all who visit or work at the institution, demonstrating a structured approach to community values and conduct.[1]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Historical Background**

The Rudolf-Hildebrand-Grundschule, formerly known as Gemeindeschule Mariendorf, is housed in a **designated architectural landmark** (Baudenkmal) in Berlin.[4] The current building was constructed in 1907/1908 according to plans by architect Otto Kerwien, designed to accommodate the rapidly growing population of Mariendorf around 1900.[4][6] The five-story brick structure exemplifies Reform architecture, featuring a distinctive clock tower and is architecturally influenced by earlier Berlin school buildings designed by city architect Ludwig Hoffmann.[4][6] Between 1952 and 1954, a gymnasium was added to the complex.[4] The school has been named after the German philologist Rudolf Hildebrand since 1956.[4]

**Location and Accessibility**

The school is located at Friedenstraße 23-25, 12107 Berlin, in the Mariendorf district (Bezirk Tempelhof-Schöneberg).[2][3] It is situated directly adjacent to the U6 subway station, providing excellent public transportation access.[1]

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school features comprehensive facilities including:[1][2]
- An auditorium (Aula) with approximately 200 seats, maintained in its historical style with modern event technology
- A computer room with 26 workstations
- A gymnasium and large gymnastics hall for physical education
- Two additional PC rooms used for computer courses
- A pavilion building housing additional classroom and activity spaces, wheelchair-accessible at ground level

The school also hosts a partnership with the local adult education center (Volkshochschule), which uses school facilities for community courses in art, languages, movement, and dance.[6]

**Student Demographics**

The school serves a highly diverse student population, with children from approximately 40 different nations attending.[1][4] Many students have migration backgrounds, and the school includes **welcome classes** (Willkommensklassen) where children of refugees and newly arrived students focus on learning German language skills and prepare for transition into regular classes.[1]

**Partnerships and Support**

The school participates in the Berlin Senate's bonus program (Bonusprogramm) since 2014, which provides additional support for schools serving disadvantaged populations.[1] External partners include the district central library (Bezirkszentralbibliothek Tempelhof-Schöneberg) and the local police precinct (Polizeiabschnitt 44).[2] Youth social work services are available on-site, led by Boris Lagerstein, with operating hours Monday to Friday from 9:00 to 13:30.[7]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Language Education**

English is the primary foreign language offered at the school.[2] The school provides specialized language instruction through welcome classes for students without German language proficiency, supporting their integration into mainstream classes.[1][2]

**Extracurricular Activities and Working Groups**

The school offers diverse **working groups** (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) including:[2]
- Visual arts (Bildende Kunst)
- Choir (Chor)
- Instrumental groups (Instrumentalgruppe)
- Conflict resolution/peer mediation (Konfliktlotsen)
- Theater

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**School Structure and Schedule**

The Rudolf-Hildebrand-Grundschule is an **open all-day school** (offene Ganztagsschule) serving grades 1-6, following the standard Berlin and Brandenburg educational structure.[4] Regular instruction runs from 8:15 to 14:40.[1]

**Care and Support Services**

The school provides multiple care options to accommodate working families:[1]
- **Reliable half-day care** (Verlässliche Halbtagsgrundschule - VHG): 7:30 to 13:30
- **All-day care** (Hort): 6:00 to 18:00 (available upon registration)
- Remedial instruction (Förderunterricht) typically scheduled before regular classes

The school provides midday meals for students participating in all-day programs.[6]

**Class Organization**

The school operates as a three- to four-stream school (drei- bis vierzügig), meaning three to four classes per grade level.[4] The teaching staff consists of approximately 30 teachers and 15 educators (Erzieher).[4]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The search results provided do not contain specific information about standardized test results, academic achievements, or measurable learning outcomes for this school.

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Contact Information**

**Address:** Friedenstraße 23-25, 12107 Berlin
**Phone:** +49 30 902777456
**Fax:** +49 30 902777457
**Email:** sekretariat@rhg.schule.berlin.de
**Website:** rhg.schule
**School Number:** 07G27

**Secretary Office Hours**

Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, and Friday: 7:30 to 11:30 and 12:00 to 15:00
Thursday: 7:30 to 13:02

**Application Process**

School placement inquiries should be submitted exclusively by email to the school's secretariat.[1] As a public Berlin primary school, admission follows the standard Berlin school enrollment procedures.

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Rudolf-Wissell-Grundschule emphasizes collaborative growth, learning, and creativity under the motto "Gemeinsam wachsen, lernen und gestalten" (Together we grow, learn, and shape).[1] As an open all-day school (offene Ganztagsschule), it offers a rich array of afternoon activities to support holistic development in a green, vibrant neighborhood setting.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in the lively Soldiner Kiez neighborhood of Gesundbrunnen (Berlin-Mitte district), the school benefits from its position at the end of a side street surrounded by greenery.[1] The main historic building, a listed monument designed by Ludwig Hoffmann and built 1913-1914, houses administration, classrooms for grades 1-6, two welcome classes (Willkommensklassen), school social work, two canteens (Mensen), an auditorium (Aula), a school library, and one of two gymnasiums.[1][2][3] A newer building accommodates the extended full-day care (EFöB/Nachmittagsbereich) for after-school groups, homework, and educator-led activities.[1] Adjacent facilities include a large sports field for PE, breaks, and annual sports project days; two playgrounds ("Rollerhof" with toy rentals organized by student patio sponsors, and "Schaukelhof" for climbing and swinging).[1] The ensemble, including a former teachers' residence, features ornate architecture with pilasters, cornices, and baroque motifs, and was originally part of a planned residential block.[2][3] Historically, the site opened in 1915 as the 43rd and 311th Gemeindeschulen, evolved through name changes (e.g., 1st and 2nd Volksschulen, 21st Grundschule), was renamed in 1962 after SPD politician Rudolf Wissell (1869-1962), served as a lazaret during WWII, and has been the 4th Grundschule of Wedding since 1967.[1][2][3][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Limited details are available from the sources, but the school serves grades 1-6 as a public primary school (Grundschule) with welcome classes for language support and integration.[1] It integrates sports programming, including project days on the sports field.[1] As a standard Berlin public Grundschule, it follows the statewide curriculum, though specifics on subjects or special programs are not detailed.[3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in a full-day schedule with afternoon care for those with hort contracts, including group activities, homework support, and educator offerings in the new building.[1] Outdoor learning occurs on the sports field, Rollerhof (with rollers, balls via toy library), and Schaukelhof during breaks and PE.[1] The school fosters community through patio sponsors and welcomes visits for prospective families.[1]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific achievements, test scores, or outcomes are mentioned in the sources. The school's historic listed status and long community presence (over 100 years) highlight its enduring role.[2][3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public tuition-free Grundschule, admission follows Berlin's standard catchment-area process for local residents, with open invitations for on-site visits.[1] It includes Willkommensklassen for newcomers.[1]

# Ruppin-Grundschule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Ruppin-Grundschule operates as a **"verlässliche Grundschule"** (reliable primary school), emphasizing extended care and support for students[5]. The school's pedagogical approach centers on the **Compartment or Cluster School concept**, which organizes classrooms by grade level on single floors and transforms hallways into "learning landscapes" with decentralized workspaces for educators[1]. This design supports intensive promotion of learning and living communities. The school also adheres to Berlin's "Design for all" initiative, ensuring that room acoustics and accessibility are planned for all people[1][2].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Historical Background**

The Ruppin-Grundschule occupies a **protected heritage building** originally constructed in 1913-1914 as the "III. Gemeindeschule Friedenau für Knaben und Mädchen" (Third Municipal School Friedenau for Boys and Girls) according to plans by architect Hans Altmann[1][3][4]. The building is architecturally distinctive for its **clinker brick construction with glazed ceramics**, a rare feature in Berlin at the time[3]. The ceramic work on the facades was handcrafted by sculptors Kuhl and Butzke, including a notable ceramic arch pillar at the main entrance[3]. Following its completion in 1914, the building served as a reserve hospital for wounded soldiers during World War I before becoming a school in March 1919[3].

**Current Facilities and Renovations**

The school currently serves **approximately 600 students across 24 classes**[5]. The campus consists of two three-story buildings arranged at angles to each other and connected by a bridge structure[4]. The complex includes multiple gymnasiums, an auditorium with original decorative elements, and specialized spaces.

Since 2016, the school has undergone comprehensive renovation addressing structural deterioration, including corroded steel beams in the masonry, damaged tiles, and repairs to valuable ceramics[1][2]. A major **generalsanierung** (general renovation) was completed in 2024, with **approximately 28 million euros** invested in the project[7]. The renovation includes:

- **Attic expansion** of the side wing to address significant space constraints[1][2]
- **Barrier-free connectivity** across all levels with a new elevator in the side wing[1][2]
- **Connecting structure** linking both building sections across all floors[1][2]
- **Enlarged cafeteria** with direct access to the schoolyard[1][2]
- **New central main entrance** and completely renovated bathrooms and showers[5]
- **Music rooms** relocated to the third floor of the front building[5]
- **Improved room acoustics** throughout the facility[1][2]

The total building area is approximately **12,800 square meters**[1][5].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

The search results do not contain detailed information about the specific curriculum, subject offerings, or academic programs at Ruppin-Grundschule. The available sources focus primarily on the school's facilities and renovation rather than educational content.

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

The school provides **extended care services from 6:00 AM to 6:00 PM** as part of its "verlässliche Grundschule" designation[5]. The cluster school concept creates conditions where students of the same grade level are located on a single floor, facilitating peer interaction and collaborative learning. The redesigned learning landscapes in hallways provide additional spaces for student work and exploration beyond traditional classrooms[1].

The school has engaged students in educational initiatives beyond the classroom, including participation in university-level programs such as the "Nachhaltigkeit+" (Sustainability+) student university program at Free University Berlin[8].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The search results do not contain information about specific academic achievements, test scores, awards, or measurable student outcomes for Ruppin-Grundschule.

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The **Sabine-Ball-Grundschule** operates under the Christburg Campus, a Christian organization emphasizing biblical values, professional education, and personal development. Its core mission is to enrich Berlin's educational offerings by helping children grow into strong personalities who discover their vocations, with Jesus at the center; it creates safe learning environments where Christian values like respect, trust, and God's love are lived and taught alongside state curricula.[3][5] The philosophy integrates faith-based values—such as wertschätzung (appreciation), individual support, and a culture of mindfulness—with academic competence, viewing children as uniquely valuable creations of God entrusted by parents.[1][PDF Content]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded as part of Christburg Campus, which began with the Elisabeth-Abegg-Grundschule in 1988 as Berlin's smallest school (20 students), the network expanded to include multiple Grundschulen like Sabine-Ball-Grundschule; by 1999, schools moved to the historic Wilhelminian ensemble at Christburger Straße 14-14B in Prenzlauer Berg (now owned by the carrier), with further sites in Spandau and Hellersdorf.[2][3] The community fosters a **familial atmosphere** among parents, teachers, and children, praised for personal greetings, events like school festivals and charity runs, and a small, supportive scale where everyone knows each other.[5] Facilities include bright, friendly rooms in historic buildings, digital blackboards in classes, and open all-day care (OGB).[4][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **state-recognized private Grundschule** (grades 1-6), it fulfills all state learning goals with a Christian perspective, including daily devotions, biblical instruction, and year-group homogeneous classes from grade 1 for targeted support via small groups or individualized learning.[1][4][7] English is taught as the first foreign language; programs feature reading promotion, media literacy (Medienführerschein), and integration of social-emotional learning rooted in Christian values.[6] The broader Christburg Campus offers pathways to Abitur via the Corrie-ten-Boom-Schule (integrated secondary school, grades 7-13).[2][3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in **extracurriculars** like swimming lessons (e.g., Seepferdchen certification for grade 3), snow adventures, field trips (e.g., gardening school, self-defense courses), arts performances, school festivals, charity runs, and movement breaks with sports partnerships.[5][6] Social learning emphasizes competence-building, conflict resolution, and faith integration; after-school care includes OGB and Schulstation; events like pedagogical days and prayer gatherings enhance community.[PDF Content][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Approximately **277 students** at the related Elisabeth-Abegg-Grundschule (part of the same campus model); Christburg Campus schools are state-recognized Ersatzschulen with a child protection concept ("Christburg Campus – ein sicherer Ort") developed since 2023, including training, risk analysis, and intervention plans advised by Deutsche Liga für das Kind.[1][PDF Content] Positive parent testimonials highlight patience, family-like support, and child thriving; first Abitur cohort planned from 2020 in upper school.[2][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
No specific admissions process detailed for Sabine-Ball-Grundschule; general Christburg Campus enrollment is open to all children regardless of background, with a focus on Christian values.[1] **No tuition or fee amounts** (e.g., Schulgeld, monatlich) found in scraped content, PDFs, or school-specific pages; related Immanuel-Grundschule mentions a one-time Verwaltungsgebühr of 70 EUR per first/second child (35 EUR for third), waived for fourth+ simultaneous siblings, but no monthly/annual tuition specified.[7] Zero fee-related pages identified.[Fee/Tuition Pages Found]

description:
# Sachsenwald-Grundschule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Sachsenwald-Grundschule operates with a **participatory approach** where children, teachers, and parents work together to take responsibility and actively shape school life[4]. The school emphasizes **inclusive education**, having integrated students with disabilities or those at risk of disability since 1991, and participating in an inclusion school trial program since 2010[1][4].

A defining characteristic is the school's **sport-focused profile**. Physical education holds high priority, with students receiving four weekly sports lessons instead of the standard three, plus additional hours for those enrolled in the sports emphasis program[4]. Third-grade students participate in year-round swimming instruction[4].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Infrastructure**

The school is located in Berlin's Steglitz district (Bismarck neighborhood) at Sachsenwaldstr. 20-21, 12157 Berlin[1][2]. The main building was constructed in 1958, with a modular pavilion added around 1998 and an expansion building for cafeteria and supplementary care completed in 2005[2].

**Physical Facilities**

The school features extensive modern amenities[1][2]:
- Two computer labs and two internet-connected computers in each classroom
- Smartboards and three iPad cases for flexible classroom use
- A library and accessible facilities including wheelchair-accessible restrooms and parking for people with disabilities
- A spacious, well-designed outdoor area with attractive play equipment, including a treehouse with extra-wide slide, two teepee tents, and a climbing tower (renovated in 2020)[2]
- A school garden and sports field with rubber surface[3]

**Community Partnerships**

The school collaborates with external partners and offers after-school care (Offene Ganztagbetreuung - OGB) through the Nachbarschaftsheim organization[1][3]. Care is available Monday to Friday from 6:00 to 18:00[3], with fixed groups for grades 1-3 and open-concept care for grades 4-6[3].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Language and Core Subjects**

English is the primary foreign language[1]. The school implements a **media education concept** integrating digital media across the curriculum, with the competency goal "Information through digital media" embedded in the school's internal curriculum[4].

**Special Programs and Offerings**

The school offers a **school start phase with grade-level learning groups** and maintains a **school station** for student support[1]. A diverse range of working groups (AGs) operate in sports, art, and music, led by both external partners and school staff[4].

**Sports and Physical Education**

Beyond the four weekly PE lessons, the school offers numerous sports AGs including aikido, basketball, fencing, fitness training, football, handball, hockey, inline skating, line dancing, swimming, dancing, and trampolining[1]. A boys' club and girls' club also operate[1].

**Arts and Music**

The school maintains a choir and children's choir (Zwergenchor)[1].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Participatory Learning**

Students engage in democratic processes through the student parliament (Schüler*innenparlament) and student budget (Schüler*innen Haushalt), where they learn to express opinions, participate in decisions, and develop solutions collaboratively[5].

**School Culture and Community Events**

The school year includes diverse activities and celebrations[5]:
- Sports tournaments and competitions (Zweifelderball tournament, ALBA elementary school league)
- School disco with music and dancing
- School cinema with film screenings
- Project days and garden work
- Traditional Christmas celebrations with singing and crafts
- School festivals and sponsor runs

**Behavioral Framework**

The school developed nine "Sachsenwald Rules" through collaborative input from students, staff, and parents, implemented through a weekly "Rule of the Week" (Mottoregel der Woche) starting April 22, 2024[5][6].

**Support Services**

The school provides school social work (Schulsozialarbeit) and prevention programs addressing violence and emergencies[1].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The school was inspected in June 2018, with an inspection report documenting the school's performance[8]. The school participates in the student budget project as a 2025 project school[5].

The school demonstrates commitment to sustainability and environmental education through its newly developed school garden created during project days in 2025, designed as a sustainable learning and experience space[5].

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Required Documentation**

For school enrollment, families must provide[5]:
- Child's birth certificate and other personal documents
- Parent's identification card or passport
- For unmarried couples: a custody declaration (Sorgerechtserklärung)

**Enrollment Process**

Children are encouraged to attend enrollment appointments accompanied by their parents to experience their new learning environment[5]. Families needing support (such as translation assistance) are welcome to bring support persons[5].

**Contact Information**

- **School Leader:** Annika Waldschmidt[1]
- **Phone:** +49 30 79742910[1]
- **Fax:** +49 30 79742919[1]
- **Email:** sekretariat@sachsenwald.schule.berlin.de[1]
- **Website:** www.sachsenwald-grundschule.de[1]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Scharmützelsee-Grundschule is an **open all-day public primary school** (Grundschule) emphasizing a **sport-focused profile** ("sportbetont") and cultural enthusiasm ("kulturbegeistert"), with key pillars in sports, music, modern foreign languages, social learning, and community partnerships.[1][2][3] It promotes holistic development through Jahrgangsbezogenes Lernen (year-group oriented learning) from grades 1-2, inclusive education, social competency training like "Faustlos" (non-violent training) for grades 1-3, and after-school activities fostering individual needs, talent development, movement, and community.[1][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located at **Hohenstaufenstr. 49, 10779 Berlin (Schöneberg)**, the school serves **460 students** across grades 1-6 with three classes per year group, plus two welcome classes for newly arrived students without German proficiency from grade 3.[1][2][5][7] Staff includes **41 teachers and 23 educators**, led by principal Helke Wattenberg and deputy Matthias Schneider.[2][6][8] As a public school owned by Bezirksamt Tempelhof-Schöneberg, it partners with Nachbarschaftsheim Schöneberg e.V. for all-day care (supporting ~330 students afternoons), Leo-Kestenberg Musikschule for music, and neighbors like Georg-von-Giesche-Schule for language exchanges.[1][2][6][9] Facilities feature a large sports hall (shared), school kitchen, art-decorated rooms, PC rooms, class gardens, and green courtyard projects with monthly Grüngruppen meetings involving staff, parents, and students.[1][2][7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum integrates a **sportbetontes Profil** with extra sports hours from grade 4 (mandatory AG), Pausensport, and events like Bundesjugendspiele, Scharmützler, and handball tournaments.[1][2][7] Music emphasis includes kindergarten continuations, choir partnerships, music evenings, and certification as a musical primary school via Leo-Kestenberg courses.[2] Foreign languages offer **English or French choice from grade 3**, with activities like French baking (galette des rois), cinema trips (e.g., Cinéma Paris), and inter-school collaborations.[2][9] Additional programs: 27 after-school AGs (sport, music, theater), mandatory bike AG in grade 2, media focus (PC rooms, class media corners), inclusion for ~23 students with special needs via Übergangsklasse and welcome classes, and social training ("Buddys," student parliament).[1][2][7] A 2024/2025 school inspection focused on teaching processes under Berlin's Schulqualität framework.[4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in diverse, hands-on activities blending academics, sports, culture, and social growth: French cultural events (baking Dreikönigskuchen, film viewings on Ku'damm), gymnastics and handball competitions (e.g., Jugend trainiert für Olympia wins), Christmas singing, Fasching, reading days in multiple languages, and projects like schoolyard flea markets and gardening.[1][2][10] All-day structure includes supervised lunches (11:45-14:30), homework support, free play, small groups, and themed weeks emphasizing sport, music, nature, creativity, and social learning for grades 1-6.[1] Upcoming events (Feb-Mar 2026) include French/Kunst AGs, Fasching, table tennis tournaments, and parent meetings.[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Sport successes include Bundesjugendspiele gymnastics (grades 3-6) with team cooperative exercises and handball team victories (12:11, 9:5) advancing to Berlin-wide rounds.[2] Cultural highlights feature full cinema bookings for French films, multi-class galette baking traditions, and community events like Weihnachtssingen and Schulhof flea markets.[2][10] The school maintains strong inclusion (23 special needs students supported) and integration via welcome classes since at least 2021, with positive inspection feedback on development.[1][4][5] Participation in regional events (Speedy Cup, Krumme Lanke Lauf) and SPI program underscores ongoing quality.[3][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public Grundschule** (school number 07G12), admission follows Berlin public school guidelines for local residents, with open all-day care via Nachbarschaftsheim partnership.[1][2][6][7] Welcome classes support non-German speakers from grade 3; contact sekretariat@scharmuetzelsee.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 902777162 for enrollment.[6][7]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Schätzelberg-Grundschule emphasizes a supportive, inclusive environment where children feel comfortable, develop social competencies, and grow into independent personalities through fair interactions and meaningful leisure activities.[2] The school's leitbild prioritizes well-being, respectful conduct, responsible behavior, and integration of after-school care into the open all-day school structure, with specific focuses on music education, inclusion, and student buddy programs for peer support.[2][1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Opened in 1965, the school is located in Berlin's Tempelhof-Schöneberg district, Mariendorf neighborhood, near Neukölln, on a 13,000 m² campus including a school building, sports hall, sports grounds, playground, and a 2006/07 extension for cafeteria, kitchen, educator room, and group rooms with large windows to outdoor areas.[1][6] It serves approximately 320-390 students in a three-track (dreizügig) structure, open to children from the district and beyond, with two school social workers supporting the community.[1][4] Facilities support open all-day care (OGB), music emphasis, and inclusion for 18 students with special needs in language, learning, emotional-social, physical-motor, or intellectual development.[2][3]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMICS PROGRAMS
As a music-emphasized primary school (Grundschule), it offers standard subjects like German, math, English (first foreign language), and music, plus specialized music programs starting in grade 1 with musical early education (singing-playing-dancing) and grade 2 instrument trials.[1][3][8] Instruments include violin, viola, cello (string class), guitar, recorder, flute, keyboard, and percussion/drums, provided for a small fee; additional small-group lessons and ensemble play occur beyond regular class music.[1] Programs include open all-day care, year-crossing learning groups in early phases, temporary support groups for reading/writing/math challenges, school library, and inclusion support.[2][3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in music performances, ensemble playing, and events like Fasching (carnival on Feb 17, 2026) and report card day (Jan 30, 2026, end of first half-year).[1][scraped content] Buddy programs pair older students (e.g., 5th graders) with 1st graders for German support, plant care, and homework help; all-day offerings integrate leisure, sports, and cultural activities in a calm, value-appreciating atmosphere.[2] Inclusion provides individualized aid; the school fosters social skills, responsibility, and diverse leisure through varied programs.[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school maintains a music profile since 1997 with regular own performances and external music events participation; it hosts 35 archived events, including ongoing calendars for community engagement.[1][scraped content] It supports ~320-390 students with specialized inclusion for 18, social work, and all-day care, but specific academic outcomes or awards are not detailed in available content.[1][2][4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public Grundschule, admission is open to district children and others; English begins as first foreign language, with music/inclusion programs available based on needs.[1][3] Open house days are listed in the school calendar; contact via phone (+49 30 902777462), email (07G23@07G23.schule.berlin.de), or website.[3][7] No wheelchair access noted.[5]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Schele-Schule positions itself as a "Haus des Lebens und Lernens" (House of Life and Learning), emphasizing individualized education, personal development, and a holistic approach that integrates upbringing and learning in a familial environment without regard to confession or nationality.[1][2][4] It focuses on competent, disciplined, student-oriented instruction with small class sizes to address individual needs, promoting success and joy in learning through the Berlin state curriculum supplemented by social, emotional, practical, aesthetic, and motor skills development.[6][7] The school aims to prepare students for life via an open, participatory structure, social competencies, responsibility, critical thinking, and cooperation with parents and external partners.[3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1900 by Privatlehrer August Sodemann as a familial Tagesheimschule, the Schele-Schule celebrated its 125th anniversary in 2025 and has operated as a private full-day school since 1985, relocating to its current site near the Olympiastadion after WWII destruction.[1][4][7] Located at Olympische Straße 23, 14052 Berlin (Westend, Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf), it serves around 200 students in a supportive community; associated facilities include a kindergarten at Teufelsberg (from age 2) and Kloster, with classes split across Olympische Straße (grades 1-4) and Bayernallee 31 (grades 5-10).[Admissions Page][1][2] Excellent sports facilities are highlighted, including proximity to Olympiapark (e.g., August-Bier-Platz for trials).[PDF: Flyer_Sichtung_Schele-Schule-2025.pdf]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a state-recognized private **Gemeinschaftsschule** (community school) with bound full-day operation, it follows Berlin's state curriculum and school laws, currently offering grades 1-6 (1-4 two-stream, 5-6 single-stream), with plans to expand to Sekundarstufe I/II (grades 7-10 and 11-13) for longer student retention and heterogeneous learning groups.[1][2][3][10] Key foci include **mathematics** (extra logic hours, Känguru competitions, internal contests), **languages** (English from grade 1, French from grade 3/5, Latin in grade 6), and **sport** (swimming grades 1-4, football as subject from grade 5 twice weekly).[1][2][8][9] Football integration via partnerships like KICK ID Academy emphasizes technical/tactical skills, teamwork, and analysis.[8][PDF: Flyer_Sichtung_Schele-Schule-2025.pdf]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
The full-day schedule (8-16:30) enables individualized support and extracurriculars like AGs in hockey, tennis, riding, sailing, chess, ice skating, modern pentathlon, rhythmic gymnastics, football, baking/cooking, and music (guitar, drums, percussion).[1][2][9] Football is prominent: twice-weekly lessons from grade 5, trials at Olympiapark, and partnerships for talent development; no in-house holiday care, but referrals to external programs like LSB Berlin swimming or bewegter-sommer.de.[Ferienbetreuung Page][8][PDF: Flyer_Sichtung_Schele-Schule-2025.pdf] Emphasis on social skills, fair play, and community events fosters a supportive environment.[3][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school excels in sports, winning the **DFB-Schul-Cup** (German School Football Cup) in 2023 and 2024 for boys, plus 5x Berlin state field champions and 3x Drumbo Cup winners; football trialed as a curriculum subject with DFB recognition.[About Page][PDF: Flyer_Sichtung_Schele-Schule-2025.pdf][PDF: DFB-SCHUL-CUP_2023_FINALMAGAZIN.pdf] It participates in national competitions like Känguru math and maintains a strong profile in languages and sports per state recognition.[1][2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Admissions for school (grades 1-4 at Olympische Straße, 5-10 at Bayernallee 31) and kindergarten (Teufelsberg from age 2, Kloster) use a unified process: online form or printable PDF (Schulanmeldung_230106.pdf) sent to Olympische Straße 23, requiring child's birth certificate, passport photo, and latest kindergarten report/card; parental details and captcha required; data used solely for applications.[Admissions Page][PDF: Anmeldeformular] **No specific EUR fee/tuition amounts** (e.g., Schulgeld, Monatsbeitrag, Gebühr) are mentioned across main pages, fee pages, admissions, about, or PDFs; all fee-related flags are False. Holiday care is not offered in-house (e.g., no Herbstferien 2025), with external alternatives suggested.[Ferienbetreuung Page]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Schinkel-Grundschule operates under the guiding principle **"Gemeinsam wachsen"** (Growing Together), emphasizing learning, creativity, community, and individual development in a diverse, colorful, and musical environment.[1][2] It focuses on holistic education through a **musikbetonte** (music-emphasized) profile, promoting social learning, language development, values education, and personalized support to foster strong personalities and intercultural competence.[1][2][3][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in the 1990s as a music-focused school of special pedagogical emphasis, it merged with the Eosander-Grundschule in 1997 and became the first musikbetonte Grundschule in Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf in 2017/18.[3][7] Located in Berlin-Charlottenburg (Bezirk Charlottenburg-Wilmersdorf) at Nithackstr. 8, 10585 Berlin, the building is a listed monument constructed in 1913-1914 by architects Paul Weingärtner and Heinrich Seeling, featuring early modern design with elfenbeinfarben facade, red sandstone, ceramic plates, and a 17-column pergola.[3][7] The community includes approximately **740 students**, **34 pedagogical staff**, and **60 teachers**, supported by partnerships like Pestalozzi-Fröbel-Haus for the open all-day program (OGB).[5][6]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **halbtagsgebundene Grundschule** (half-day bound primary school) with open all-day care (OGB), it offers a music-emphasized curriculum with extra music hours, instrumental lessons from grade 2 (12 instruments like piano, guitar, violin, drums), elective courses, school choir, and band.[1][3][6] Additional focuses include English from grade 3, optional French in upper grades, social learning, language and values education, jahrgangsübergreifende Lerngruppen (cross-grade learning groups) in early phases, and diverse projects/AGs like programming, physics experiments, and therapeutic riding.[1][3][4][6] Individualized support uses diagnostics for tailored plans, modern methods, and digital tools.[4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in hands-on, creative activities such as programming robots, dissecting sheep eyes, Harry Potter storytelling, football tournaments, art projects like polar lights with pastels, and class trips (e.g., to Phaeno in Wolfsburg).[1] Testimonials highlight positive atmosphere, respect, individual support for strengths/weaknesses, and musical integration.[1] The OGB provides structured after-school care with sports, arts, and social activities to balance academics and personal growth.[1][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school promotes long-term learning strategies, talent development, and preparation for further education through continuous diagnostics and holistic offers.[4] Parental feedback praises individualized fostering and positive community; student quotes emphasize excitement in interdisciplinary, experiential learning.[1] It holds official recognition as a musikbetontes Profil school with OGB and cross-grade early education.[6]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public (öffentlich) Grundschule**, admission follows standard Berlin public school procedures based on local catchment area, with no tuition fees.[1][6] Online tools include illness reporting and catering access; parents are encouraged via dedicated pages and OGB office hours (e.g., 14:00-16:00).[1][5]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Schlaufuchs-Grundschule emphasizes movement-oriented education, supplementary support, and holistic development. It offers diverse all-day programs alongside regular instruction to strengthen fine motor skills, creativity, self-confidence, and boundary-testing abilities for students.[1][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Lichtenberg (Fennpfuhl district), the school features newly renovated indoor spaces and a redesigned outdoor area for various activities, including supplementary support and care. Proximity to Fennpfuhl park supports its movement-oriented approach. The school is publicly owned, led by Fabian Tänzer, with contact details including phone (+49 30 36417613), fax (+49 30 26392350), and email (sekretariat@11G38.schule.berlin.de). An associated organization, Schlaufuchs Berlin e.V., promotes educational equity through support for schools, individual fostering, extracurricular projects, and talent development in creative, sports, and craft areas.[1][2][4][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Grundschule** (primary school, school number 11G38), it provides standard primary education with **English** or **French** as the first foreign language. All-day offerings include supplementary instruction support and diverse activities.[1][2][5]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students benefit from all-day programs with movement-focused activities on the outdoor grounds and nearby park, fostering motor skills, creativity, and social competencies. No on-site after-school care is available, but forms for care contracts and pick-up authorizations are provided.[1][4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

Reliable half-day school (7:30-13:30 Uhr) with all-day care option; open Ganztagsbetrieb promotes social interaction in a small-scale setting ("kurze Wege" - short paths).[2][3][8] Hands-on activities via gardens, kitchen, workshops; events like Adventsbasteln (Advent crafting, e.g., Nov 29, 2024), Frühlingsfest (spring festival), Lerninsel-Zeitung (learning island newspaper), Tag der offenen Tür (open day).[Main Page][About Page] SPI program participant since Aug 2021 (GSJ gGmbH).[5]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Schule am Berg is a public primary school (Grundschule) emphasizing a **mathematisch-naturwissenschaftliches Profil** (mathematics and natural sciences focus) to nurture children's natural curiosity and researcher spirit from ages 4-7.[3][4] The school aims to foster independent learning through high methodological competence, broad general education with a strong math-science emphasis, and development of self-responsibility, self-confidence, and creativity, ensuring students feel comfortable and identify with the school community.[4] It promotes scientific thinking in biology, chemistry, and physics via exploration, experiments, projects, excursions, and partnerships with external learning sites.[2][3][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located on a small hill in Altglienicke's town center (Treptow-Köpenick district), the school serves one of four primary schools in the area and was formerly named "Rudolf-Ehrlich-Schule" until 1990, linked to local Holocaust remembrance events like Stolperstein placements for former student families.[1][5] Facilities include a large playground with equipment under trees, a soccer field (Bolzplatz), an old building for grades 4-6 with specialized rooms (science/Nawi, computer, music, library), a modern new building for grades 1-3 and after-school care (Hort), a multi-purpose hall (Speisesaal for meals/events), and a gym; additional features are Smartboards, four breakout rooms, two computer rooms with internet, and a library.[2][4][6] Leadership includes principal Frau Sokolowski, coordinating educator Frau Mann, secretary Frau Janz, administrative head Frau Selin, and janitor Herr Geßner; class sizes range from 24-28 students.[2][3][6] The school fosters a supportive atmosphere, with inspections noting strong development structures, diverse offerings, and a learning-friendly environment.[4]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum follows Berlin's framework with a math-science profile, integrating experiments, projects, and exploration in subjects like Sachunterricht (general studies) and later specialized science classes to build observation, concentration, language, and retention skills.[2][3][4] English as a foreign language starts in grade 3, with optional paid language courses from grade 1; religious education options include evangelical, Alevite, or ethics (Lebenskunde).[2] Extracurriculars encompass open all-day care (OGB), school entry phase with year-group or cross-grade learning groups, theater focus, working groups (AGs) like choir, English children's literature, badminton, soccer, volleyball, theater, karate, ceramics, and collaborations with nearby schools.[2][6] Technology integration begins in grade 1 via computer room; excursions, field trips, and class trips are regular.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience reliable half-day care (VHG) from 7:30-13:30 for grades 1-6 at no cost, full-day options from 6:00-18:00, and holiday care for grades 1-4; initiatives like the waste-free Pausenbrot-Challenge promote environmental awareness.[2][5] Daily life includes reading competitions (e.g., school winner from grade 6), class representatives, substitute plans, online illness reporting, and events like Fasching (carnival), parent evenings (GEV), and open days.[5] Community engagement features local history projects and versatile spaces for performances and sports.[4][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school hosts successful internal reading competitions with class winners advancing to school-level contests, producing a grade 6 Schulsiegerin (school champion).[5] Inspections highlight strengths in school development structures involving staff, diverse in-class and extracurricular offerings, and a conducive learning atmosphere; classroom observations compare favorably.[4] It maintains a theater-oriented profile, varied AGs, and positive community ties, such as Stolperstein events honoring local history.[5][6]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public tuition-free Grundschule (school number 09G10), enrollment is open to local children; key dates include open house (Tag der offenen Tür) on September 29 and official enrollment (Schulanmeldung) October 6-17.[1][5][6] Secretariat hours are Monday-Thursday 8:00-14:30, Friday 8:00-12:30 for inquiries; contact via phone (030-6728350), email (09G10@09G10.schule.berlin.de or grundschuleamberg@t-online.de), or website forms for absences/substitutes.[5][6][7]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Schule am Birkenhof operates as an **integrative campus** in Berlin-Pankow with the guiding principle **"Alle unter einem Dach" (All under one roof)**, emphasizing inclusive education for all students across its Grundschule (primary school) and Sonderpädagogisches Förderzentrum „Lernen“ (special education center focused on learning).[1][3][6] The philosophy prioritizes fostering individual competencies, sustainable thinking, sport orientation, and berufsorientierte (career-oriented) preparation, particularly in the Förderzentrum for grades 3-10.[1][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located at **Arnouxstr. 18, 13127 Berlin (Französisch Buchholz)** in the green district of Pankow, this public school serves over **550 students** with more than **70 qualified staff**, including ~55 teachers, 18 educators (including 4 integration educators), 2 school social workers, and 2 school assistants.[1][3] Facilities include a **green campus**, modern sports hall, two small-field sports fields, athletic areas, well-equipped classrooms, computer room, library, workshops, teaching kitchen, playgrounds, and accessibility features like wheelchair ramps, elevators, and restrooms.[1][2][3] It offers healthy organic lunches and is centrally located with good public transport.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The **Grundschule** is an open all-day school (Ganztagsschule) with **cross-grade teaching in grades 1-2** and homogeneous classes from grade 3, featuring a **sport-focused concept** from grade 3, English as the first foreign language, and inclusive education.[1][2][3] The **Förderzentrum** serves grades 3-10 (Sekundarstufe I with max 13 students per class in 7-10), emphasizing career-oriented content, vocational qualifications like Berufsbildungsreife, internships, and student companies.[1][5][6] Balanced curriculum includes projects, electives (AGs), sustainability, and support for learning disorders like dyslexia and dyscalculia.[1][2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in **diverse activities** such as sports AGs (archery to volleyball), musical/artistic projects, excursions, moved breaks, library use, gifted education in grades 5-6 (competitions in English/math/reading), student companies in crafts/media/sustainability, and practical internships.[1][2][5] The environment promotes sensory learning, nachhaltigkeit (sustainability), innovative concepts, and inclusive support tailored to individual needs on a green campus with play areas.[1][2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The Förderzentrum enables successful transitions to vocational training or direct apprenticeships with school-leaving certificates after grade 10.[1][5] Over 25 years, the school has evolved concepts for effective learning, with small classes and evaluations supporting all students' strengths.[5] Participation in competitions and berufsorientierte projects highlights practical outcomes, though specific metrics like graduation rates are not detailed.[2][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free school** owned by Bezirksamt Pankow, admissions follow Berlin public school processes for Grundschule and special needs Förderzentrum „Lernen“; contact sekretariat@birkenhof.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 474029100 for enrollment.[3][4] It supports inclusive intake with small special education classes per §4 SopädVO.[3][7]

# Schule am Falkplatz - Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

**Schule am Falkplatz** is a **public elementary school** (Grundschule) located in the Prenzlauer Berg district of Berlin that has established itself as an **environmental education leader**[2]. Since the 2007/2008 school year, the school has operated as an "Umweltschule" (Environmental School) in Prenzlauer Berg, embedding ecological principles throughout its educational approach[2][8].

The school's educational philosophy emphasizes **individualized learning** and **child-centered pedagogy**. The school operates as a **reliable half-day school with open all-day care** (verlässliche Halbtagsgrundschule mit offenem Ganztagsbetrieb)[3], providing flexible support for working families while maintaining structured academic instruction.

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Historical Background**

The building housing Schule am Falkplatz has significant architectural heritage. Originally constructed between 1913 and 1916 as the Luisenstädtisches Gymnasium according to designs by Ludwig Hoffmann, it is a **listed architectural monument**[2]. After restructuring of East Berlin's school system following reunification, the building was renovated from 1991 to 1994 and became the 11th primary school of Prenzlauer Berg. The school received its current name in 2004 when a new school and leisure courtyard was inaugurated[2].

**Current Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school comprises two main building sections[7]:

- **SAPH Building**: The newer, comprehensively renovated structure housing the early learning phase (Schulanfangsphase) classes, accessed from Kopenhagener Straße
- **Historic Building**: A four-story structure containing classrooms for grades 3-6, specialized subject rooms, and group spaces connected by spacious corridors and staircases

**Environmental and Educational Facilities**[6][7]:
- Ecological garden (ökologischer Garten)
- Animal care station (Tierstation)
- Ecological workshop (Ökologische Werkstatt) - equipped and used by other district schools
- School library with environmental focus
- Two computer rooms with 15 student computers
- Laptop availability for instruction
- Student club (Schülerclub)
- School garden

The school underwent comprehensive renovation beginning in 2014, with upgrades to windows, façade, assembly hall, and kitchen to accommodate growing enrollment[2][3].

**School Size and Community**

The school serves approximately **674-695 students** across 27 classes[3][7], making it one of the largest primary schools in Pankow district. The school is led by **Sabine Bachmann** (Schulleitung)[1][2].

**Contact Information**:

- Phone: +49 30 443875010

- School Number: 03G11

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Language Instruction**

English is the primary foreign language (1. Fremdsprache)[1]. The school offers additional language learning opportunities through working groups in Russian, English, and Spanish[1].

**Core Academic Structure**

The school follows Berlin's framework curriculum (Rahmenplan) with quality instruction across all subject areas designed to optimally prepare students for secondary education[5]. Teaching is characterized by **clear structure, step-by-step guidance, and explicit behavioral expectations**, with visible rituals and feedback systems (such as smiley faces on the board) to support student learning[5].

**Early Learning Phase (SAPH)**

Students in grades 1-2 learn in **mixed-age learning groups** (jahrgangsgemischte Lerngruppen) within the SAPH structure[1][3]. From grade 3 onward, instruction is organized by grade level, currently operating 4-5 parallel classes per grade[3]. Learning spaces are individually and child-appropriately furnished[5][7].

**Specialized Programs and Electives**

**Schwerpunktunterricht** (Emphasis Instruction) for grades 5-6 includes[4]:
- Cross-grade project instruction focused on environmental and ecological themes
- Social learning instruction

**Working Groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften)** offered include[1]:
- Computer
- School choir
- Pottery workshop
- Theater
- Newspaper club
- Language clubs (Russian, English, Spanish)
- Library club
- Animal care
- Chess
- Sports (basketball, soccer, judo, karate, dance, table tennis, apparatus gymnastics)
- School garden

**Environmental Education Focus**

The school's environmental curriculum includes[6]:
- Students serving as "energy detectives" and "climate agents"
- Collaboration with Humboldt University Berlin on ecological learning workshops
- Student-led environmental awareness initiatives for other schools

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Pedagogical Approach**

The school emphasizes **rhythmized daily structure** balancing instruction and breaks, particularly in the early learning phase[3]. Students are encouraged to **freely explore and experiment** with a consistent contact person supporting them through all situations while fostering independence[3]. The school attends to overlapping developmental needs of children in their individual learning phases[3].

**Learning Spaces and Support**

Group rooms are used for structured games, diverse projects (including science-focused topics), and individualized learning[3]. Supplementary support and care areas are located immediately adjacent to classrooms on each floor, accessible during morning instruction for integrated support[5][7].

**Participation and Responsibility**

The school emphasizes **student involvement in various responsibility areas of school life** and **project-oriented instruction**[7]. Students participate in environmental stewardship through the school garden, animal care station, and ecological workshop.

**Swimming Instruction**

Swimming instruction is offered as part of the physical education curriculum[4].

**Library and Reading Support**

The school maintains an environmental-focused library and actively promotes reading through programs such as "Tolles Buch" (Great Book) reading promotion events and grade 6 reading competitions[8].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Environmental Recognition**

The school has earned distinction as an environmental education leader:
- Designated "Umweltschule im Prenzlauer Berg" since 2007/2008[2][8]
- Titled "Berliner Klima Schule 2010" (Berlin Climate School 2010)[1]
- Regular applicant for the Lenné Prize (environmental education award)[7]

**Cultural and Educational Partnerships**

- Participation in "Theater and School" project (TUSCH - Theater Schulen Kooperation)[1]
- Collaboration with Humboldt University Berlin on ecological learning initiatives[6]

**Inspection Findings**

According to school inspection reports, strengths include[7]:
- Clear classroom structure and organization
- Individual, child-appropriate learning space design
- Student involvement in school life and responsibility areas
- Project-oriented instructional offerings
- Measures to anchor the school's environmental profile

**Development Areas**

The school identified priorities for continued improvement[7]:
- Development of competency-oriented work plans linked to interdisciplinary focus areas
- Consistent implementation of the school profile throughout instruction and school life
- Task formats promoting problem-solving learning

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's mission emphasizes creating a welcoming environment where every child and staff member feels comfortable and eager to attend, serving as a place of encounter with a strong focus on polite interactions, performance willingness, creativity, and skill development.[1][2] It promotes partnership behavior, teamwork, personal responsibility, solidarity, participation, and environmentally conscious actions among students.[1] The affiliated all-day care follows the **Early Excellence approach**, prioritizing individual learning and development processes based on each child's strengths and needs.[3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded around 1974, the Schule am Fliederbusch celebrated its 50th anniversary in recent years with a cultural program featuring student performances, dance, song, and an international buffet by parents.[6] Located in Berlin's Neukölln district (Rudow neighborhood) at Kornradenstr. 2, 12357 Berlin, near Gropiusstadt, it serves approximately 385 students from diverse family backgrounds, many facing precarious conditions including high rates of transfer benefits, single-parent households, violence, mobbing, racism, and special needs like autism.[3][5] The school has about 28 teachers, 18 educators, two social pedagogues, and other specialists, though only a minority are fully trained pedagogues, leading to resource challenges.[3][5] Facilities include open, transparent spaces for movement, play, experimentation, a modernized gym, two computer rooms, laptops, projectors, library, school garden, accessible parking for disabled persons, wheelchair-accessible WC, and ramp access.[3][4] It partners with Pestalozzi-Fröbel-Haus (PFH) for all-day care since August 2014 and external groups like Seniorpartner in School (SiS) e.V. and Trialog e.V.[3][4][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a public **Grundschule** (primary school, school number 08G33), it offers English as the first foreign language, open all-day care (OGB), and year-group learning groups in the introductory phase.[1][4] Extracurricular activities (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) include football, gymnastics, jazz dance, cooking, painting, young naturalists, graffiti, theater, computers, school garden, baking, Arabic and Turkish classes, crafting, play group, belly dance, ball school, judo, girls' football, and film club.[1][4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in diverse, hands-on activities in varied spaces designed for movement, relaxation, play, experimentation, nature exploration, social interaction, and responsibility-building, supported by the all-day care's focus on social competencies, independence, and accountability.[3] The curriculum emphasizes language learning, creativity, and inclusivity for a multicultural student body, with additional support for special needs amid challenges like violence and behavioral issues often linked to home environments.[1][4][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific academic achievements, test results, or outcomes are detailed in available sources, though the school maintains operations in a challenging socioeconomic context and has sustained long-term partnerships like the 10-year collaboration with PFH.[4][5][6] It receives targeted funding from the Berlin Senate, recently reduced from €80,000 to €30,000 annually due to administrative requirements.[5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public tuition-free Grundschule, admissions follow standard Berlin public school procedures based on local residency in the Neukölln/Rudow catchment area; no specific application details or fees are mentioned.[2][4]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Schule am Ginkobaum emphasizes a respectful, peaceful environment fostering relationships based on respect, justice, and tolerance, with conflict resolution through programs like Konfliktlotsen, Schülerparlament, and Klassenrat.[1] It prioritizes individualized learning processes, social learning, inclusion via a Leitbild focusing on team work and school community strengthening, and music as a core medium for holistic development, personality growth, sensory expression, well-being, learning joy, and social cohesion under the "Musikalische Grundschule" certification since 2017 (renewed 2020).[1][2][3][4][5] The school promotes active music engagement across subjects ("4 Ms": more music by more participants in more subjects at more opportunities) to enhance participation and equity.[4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Johannisthal, Treptow-Köpenick district, this public primary school (Grundschule) celebrated its 60th jubilee and operates as a reliable half-day school (VHG) with open all-day care from 6:00-18:00, supported by 24 educators in 2022/2023.[1][2][5][9] Enrollment varies: 460 students (recent),[1] 470 (2019),[3] 525 (2020/21),[5] 595 (2022/23).[2] It features three streams (dreizügig), year-group-spanning SaPh classes for grades 1-2 (shifted to year-specific since 2020/21), and inclusion support like "Kleinklasse" for up to 6 students with special needs, run by JAO gGmbH since 2017/18 and SPI program since 08.2021.[1][2][5][6] Facilities include a sports hall, Lebenskunderaum, 6 SaPh splitting rooms (used as after-school spaces), 2 VHG rooms, 2 small rooms for flexible projects, computer room, 2 computers per classroom, teaching kitchen, and children's library.[5][7] Community involvement includes the "Die kleinen Jo-thaler" parents' association and parent-co-designed website.[3][9] Leadership: Rektorin Martina Graße and Konrektorin Ramm.[7][8]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Core focus on reading competency development, individualized instruction (above Berlin average except problem-based learning per 2019 inspection), English as first foreign language, instrumental music, and "Musikalische Grundschule" integrating music into all areas for holistic growth.[1][3][4][7] SaPh structure supports paced learning without overload/underload, mutual help, and independence; includes social-pedagogical offers and flexible "Kleinklasse" for reintegration.[1][5] Offers open all-day care (OGB), school station, and computer-equipped classrooms.[7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in music/sports events (e.g., Weihnachtskonzert, Crosslauf, Flugkistenrennen), cultural activities (Leseabend, Laternenfest, Zu Fuß zur Schule, Weihnachtsbasteln), and community projects; all-day care extends learning with 24 educators.[9] Emphasis on varied, needs-based support via inspections-derived measures, inclusive settings, and music-driven creativity.[3][4] Recent events (as of early 2026): strikes (Jan 27/29, 2026), Leseabend (Mar 13, 2026), Elterncafé (Oct 15, 2025).[9]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
2019 inspection praised improved instruction (individualized processes above Berlin average), inclusive leadership, parent involvement, and learning climate; school meets diverse needs through varied offers and support.[3] Certified "Musikalische Grundschule" since June 2017, extended October 2020.[1][4][9] Strong on social learning and reintegration via "Kleinklasse."[1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public tuition-free Grundschule, admission follows Berlin public school processes; new parents invited to events like Elterncafé (e.g., Oct 15, 2025 for grades 1-3).[2][5][9] Contact: Springbornstr. 250, 12487 Berlin; (030) 636 33 17; Sekretariat@ginkobaum.schule.berlin.de.[1][7][8][9]
