The Schule am grünen Stadtrand emphasizes holistic and individual child development through modern, inclusive pedagogical approaches tailored to diverse needs and abilities.[1] It fosters a familial atmosphere in a nature-adjacent location, promoting free personal growth, learning beyond the classroom via excursions, hiking days, and class trips, and strong community collaboration among parents, teachers, educators, and students.[1][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located at Geraer Ring 54, 12689 Berlin (Marzahn-Hellersdorf district, Marzahn locality), this public primary school (Grundschule, school number 10G34) serves approximately 260 students in grades 1-6 as a reliable half-day school with open all-day operations.[1][2][3] Formerly operated as a special needs school (10S10) focused on intellectual development, it now functions as a school association including mainstream and specialized classes for intellectual development.[2][4] Community ties include partnerships with Kita Knirpsenstadt am Glitzerbach for kindergarten transitions, lehrreich Wilmersdorf for tutoring, Füchse Berlin (handball AG), Zirkus Cabuwazi (annual grade 4 project), and StayCool (resilience and anti-bullying training).[1] One of Berlin's first family-school centers opened here in late 2023, operated by Kiek in gGmbH, offering consultation, family support, reading/play cafes, and events in dedicated spaces (Room A1.5).[1][5][9] Facilities include 2 PC rooms, workshops, teaching kitchen, English and music rooms, Snoezelraum, ETEP room, and open all-day care (Hort) from 06:00-18:00.[7] School closing times: Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri after 12:00, Wed after 10:00; after-school appointments by arrangement.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6), it offers standard Berlin primary curriculum with first foreign languages in **English** or **French**, multilingual parent resources (e.g., Arabic, Turkish, Polish, Ukrainian), sports/swimming instruction, and specialized support for intellectual development.[1][4][7] Extracurriculars (AGs) include handball (Füchse Berlin), circus projects, resilience training, tutoring, football, self-defense, PC courses, woodworking, and sports/games for special classes.[1][7] Open all-day care (OGB) supports extended learning.[7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students benefit from a nature-rich setting near Brandenburg landscapes with quick S-Bahn access to Berlin center, fostering outdoor learning via excursions and trips.[1] Inclusive practices address individual needs, with after-school programs enhancing skills in sports, arts, resilience, and academics; family events, festivals, and projects build community.[1][5] Specialized rooms support sensory and educational therapy; multilingual support aids diverse families.[1][7]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school highlights opening one of Berlin's first family-school centers in late 2023 as a key milestone, strengthening family-school-Kita ties and preventive support services.[1][5] It maintains a collaborative ecosystem with external partners for tutoring, sports, and social training, though specific academic outcomes or test results are not detailed in available content.[1][7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Admissions information points to a dedicated "Schulanmeldung" section on the website; the school hosts open house days (Tag der offenen Tür) listed in its calendar.[1][7] As a public tuition-free Grundschule, enrollment follows Berlin's standard primary school registration process, with support for transitions from local Kitas.[1]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's motto, **"Unsere Schule hat den TRICK raus"**, stands for **Toleranz (Tolerance), Respekt (Respect), Information, Chancengleichheit (Equal Opportunities), and Kompetenz (Competence)**, emphasizing a supportive environment where students feel welcome, learn respectfully, and develop key skills.[1][3][5][7][10] It promotes a lernförderliche Atmosphäre (learning-promoting atmosphere) with friendly interactions, regular self-evaluation, and goals like media competency and individualized learning to ensure all children acquire necessary competencies.[5][7][9][10]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1991 as the 1st Grundschule Weißensee, renamed Schule am Hamburger Platz in 2005, the public primary school (Grundschule, school number 03G01) serves 510 students in 20 classes taught by 30 teachers in Berlin-Pankow, Weißensee district.[1][2][5] It supports a diverse community, with 14% non-German native speakers and special needs students (learning, language, emotional-social development, autism).[5] Facilities include student-designed classrooms, music room, art room, science room for grades 4-6, and computer access from grade 1; it operates as an open all-day school (6:00-18:00) with supplementary care.[1][5][7]

### 3. CURRICURICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Core curriculum focuses on reading, writing, math, and digital media/internet skills for independent learning, with English as the first foreign language from grade 1.[3][4][7][9] The Schulanfangsphase (early primary phase) since 2008 uses mixed-age groups (ages 5-8) for 1-3 years, combining grade-specific and cross-grade learning; includes project weeks, special support hours with digital media, and facultative religious/ethics classes.[1][4][5][7][9] Media profile emphasizes "Computerlotsen" and methods competency.[9]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in open Ganztag (all-day) structure with supplementary care, diverse projects, and extracurricular activities (AGs) like soccer, badminton, crafting, nature/environment, chess, dance, ceramics, gardening, cooking, aerobics, and storytelling.[1][3][4][9] Experiences promote creativity, relaxation, sports, and exploration in a respectful, tolerant setting with lunch provision and after-school options.[1][4][10]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
School inspections highlight strengths in learning atmosphere, collegial development, media integration, and living the TRICK principles through targeted measures.[10] It maintains broad-based school development, identifies strengths/weaknesses via inspections, and offers parking for disabled persons.[7][9][10]

# Schule am Heidekampgraben - Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

**Schule am Heidekampgraben** is a **public primary school** (Grundschule) dedicated to preserving students' joy in learning.[2] The school's core mission emphasizes creating a dynamic school environment where students can discover and develop their abilities while maintaining enthusiasm for education.[2]

The school is an active participant in the **"Musikalische Grundschule" (Musical Primary School)** development project,[2] integrating music across the curriculum as a central pedagogical approach. This initiative reflects the school's commitment to holistic, arts-integrated education.

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Setting**

The school is located in the **Treptow-Köpenick district**, specifically in the Baumschulenweg neighborhood, in a park-like green setting near the Heidekampgraben.[2][5] The school was newly established on August 1, 2013.[2]

**Building and Infrastructure**

The school building was originally constructed in 1967 as a DDR-type building ("Berlin SK").[8] The entire building envelope has undergone energy-efficient renovation, including roof and structural improvements.[8]

**Facilities and Services**

The school offers **Mietra lockers** for students to securely store school materials, sports clothing, art supplies, and personal valuables, reducing physical strain and enabling students to move more freely throughout the school.[2] Meal services are provided through **Z-Catering**, with ordering information available on the school website.[2] The school also offers **open all-day care (OGB - Offene Ganztagbetreuung)** for students in grades 1-6.[3]

**Community Engagement**

The school maintains an active **support association (Förderverein Kinderkram e.V.)** that organizes community activities and fundraising initiatives.[2] The school also provides **parent accompaniment (Elternbegleitung)** services to support family engagement in the educational process.[2]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Structure and Class Organization**

The school serves grades 1-6 with the following structure:[9]
- **School entry phase (SAPH):** 8 learning groups
- **Grades 3-6:** Three to four classes per grade level

**Subject Areas**

The curriculum encompasses the following subject areas:[2]
- German (Deutsch)
- Mathematics (Mathematik)
- English (Englisch) - first foreign language
- Natural Sciences and Social Studies (NaWi und SU)
- Social Studies (Gewi)
- Art (Kunst)
- Music (Musik)
- Physical Education (Sport)
- Life Skills/Ethics (Lebenskunde)
- Artistic Design (Künstlerische Gestaltung)
- Welcome Classes (Willkommensklassen) for newly arrived students

**Special Programs**

The school offers **welcome classes** to support students new to the German education system.[2] **School social work (Schulsozialarbeit)** is available to support student wellbeing and development.[2]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Daily School Activities**

The school operates as a **reliable half-day primary school** with structured morning and afternoon programs.[2]

**Morning Program** includes core academic instruction across all subject areas, with particular emphasis on the Musical Primary School project integration.[2]

**Afternoon Program** includes:[2]
- **Hort (after-school care)** for students in extended care
- **Student club (Schülerclub)** activities
- **Working groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften)** including specialized clubs such as:
  - Nature AG (environmental education)
  - Choir (Chorkids)
  - Film workshop (Trixmix-Filmwerkstatt)

**Student Leadership and Development**

The school develops student agency through programs including:[2]
- **Conflict mediators (Konfliktlotsen)** - peer mediation program
- **Student media work** - student journalism and media literacy
- **Collaborative learning** - peer support and cooperative learning structures

**Enrichment Activities**

The school organizes regular competitions and events including the **Känguru Mathematics Competition**, **The Big Challenge** (English language competition), and **Federal Youth Games (Bundesjugendspiele)**.[2]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Schule am Mohnweg emphasizes a friendly learning environment that supports each child's individual development through varied, practical instruction providing a solid foundation for further education and life.[4][5] It operates as an inclusive, sport-focused primary school (Grundschule) promoting education equity, particularly for children in challenging situations via social work, conflict management, and targeted support programs.[1][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Built in 1996-1997 and inaugurated in 1997, the school is located in the heart of Altglienicke, Berlin (Treptow-Köpenick district), serving approximately 560-640 students in 25 classes plus two welcome classes (Willkommensklassen).[2][3][5] Facilities include modern classrooms, a well-equipped three-part sports hall used by local clubs, and a large schoolyard with diverse play and outdoor sports equipment.[1][2][5] Community partnerships feature sports clubs (e.g., Judo with PSV Olympia Berlin, football with Köpenicker Sport Club, handball with VSG Altglienicke), circus projects (CABUWAZI), music schools, language programs (English/French), and social services like JAO gGmbH for flexible schooling and youth support.[1][2][6] A parent-led Förderverein supports projects, materials, and events.[7] Leadership: Nadine Büthner (principal), Nicole Haueis (deputy).[1][8] Accessibility includes parking for disabled, wheelchair elevator, and adapted WC.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a nearly four-track half-day school (Halbtagsgrundschule) with open all-day care (offene Ganztagsbetreuung), it offers a sports profile (sportbetontes Profil) from grades 1-6, English as first foreign language, gifted education (Hochbegabtenförderung), reading/writing support groups, year-group learning groups in early phases, school station (Schulstation), and flexible projects for socio-emotional challenges.[1][2][3] Extracurriculars include judo, football, athletics, gymnastics, ball games, handball, guitar, choir, dance/samba, ceramics, computers, young explorers, and languages via partners.[1] School hours: 08:00-14:50, with 7 periods (e.g., 1st: 08:00-08:45; 7th: 14:05-14:50).[Scraped Curriculum Page]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in practical, inclusive learning with sports emphasis, afternoon play activities ("Miteinander spielerisch durch den Nachhalt"), enrollment events, project weeks, summer festivals, and holiday care.[4][5][9] Social work provides class/group/individual support, conflict mediation, and child protection, targeting disadvantaged students.[2] After-school care (Hort/OGB) runs optionally 06:00-07:30 and 13:30-16:00 (fee-based), with late care until 18:00.[3]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific academic outcomes or awards detailed; recent activities highlight community events like enrollment ("Hase-Hopp ins neue Schuljahr"), summer holidays with relaxed care, project weeks, summer fest 2024, and sports cups (e.g., "Union macht Schule").[4][9] The school maintains reliable operations as a public inclusive institution with strong local partnerships.[2][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public tuition-free Grundschule (ownership: öffentlich), admissions follow Berlin's local catchment system for primary school (grades 1-6); contact sekretariat@mohnweg.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 6797490 for enrollment details like Einschulung.[1][School Information] Two Willkommensklassen support integration.[5] After-school care is fee-based but core schooling free.[3]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Schule am Pegasuseck is a music-focused public primary school (Grundschule) emphasizing musical education alongside standard curriculum, with a strong commitment to supporting socially disadvantaged students, including those from single-parent families, refugees, and children with social-emotional challenges.[1][2] It integrates special programs like flexible small-group classes for students in grades 1-4 facing learning, performance, and behavioral issues, aiming for reintegration into regular classes through pedagogical and social support in cooperation with teachers, parents, therapy centers, youth welfare offices, and school psychology.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin's Kosmosviertel, a known social hotspot with many disadvantaged families and a refugee accommodation nearby since 2017, the school has served students with migration backgrounds via welcome classes since 2015.[1] It has 386 students from grades 1 to 6, led by principal Ines Rick (appointed around summer prior to recent updates), with ongoing modernization including a planned extension building and courtyard renovation.[1][5][6] Facilities include the main school building, a separate after-school care (Hort) building, gym with outdoor sports areas, library, computer room, computers in classrooms, and a barrier-free addition; a branch exists at Uranusstr. 17.[1][2] Community ties involve partnerships with kindergartens, sports clubs (e.g., PSV Olympia, VSG Altglienicke, Grünauer Ballspiel Club), 1. FC Union Berlin for "degewo am Ball" soccer training since around 2023, a family center (FZGS) operated by JAO gGmbH since 2023, and school social work.[2][4][7][8] Office hours: Monday 8-15, Tuesday 10-17, Wednesday 8-14, Thursday 10-17, Friday 8-14.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a public Grundschule, it offers standard primary education (grades 1-6) with a **musikbetontes Profil** ("Musical Primary School"), English as the first foreign language, learning groups for reading/writing difficulties and new arrivals without German knowledge, year-group-based early-phase groups, special education small classes per §4 SopädVO, and open all-day care (OGB).[2] Extracurricular activities (AGs) include choir, drum group, computer club, handball, soccer, step aerobic, theater, flute, and unplugged games.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in music-emphasized learning, sports partnerships promoting fair play and teamwork (e.g., soccer with professional trainers), small-group support for emotional and behavioral needs, and integration programs for refugees.[1][2][4] All-day options via OGB and Hort provide extended care, with social-pedagogical accompaniment and family involvement.[1][2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific test results, awards, or outcome metrics are detailed in available sources, though the school maintains active programs for integration and sports, with ongoing facility upgrades signaling investment in improvement.[1][2][5][6]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public tuition-free Grundschule in Berlin's Treptow-Köpenick district (Altglienicke), admission follows local catchment area rules for grades 1-6, with welcome classes for non-German speakers and special support groups available.[1][2] Contact: Pegasuseck 5, 12524 Berlin; Tel. +49 30 6733032; Email 09G11@09G11.schule.berlin.de; School number 09G11.[2][3]

# Schule am Regenweiher - Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Schule am Regenweiher is guided by three core principles: **"Wir sind beweglich" (We are flexible), "Wir sind neugierig" (We are curious), and "Wir sind sozial" (We are social).**[5] The school distinguishes itself through **continuous language development, emphasis on social learning, democratic education, and media literacy.**[5]

The school's educational mission centers on developing students' pedagogical competencies through a structured school program that reflects the institution's unique identity and educational values.[5] The school is actively engaged in ongoing development and refinement of its educational approach, having recently established several key conceptual frameworks including a Social Learning Curriculum, Media Education Concept, Inclusion Concept, and School Code of Conduct.[5]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Setting:** Schule am Regenweiher is located in Gropiusstadt in southern Neukölln, Berlin, at Johannisthaler Chaussee 328-340, 12351 Berlin.[1][2][6] The school is situated in a green environment with a family-oriented atmosphere.[5]

**Student and Staff Population:** The school serves **375 students taught by 44 educators** and operates as a **public elementary school with open all-day care (offene Ganztagsbetreuung).**[8]

**Contact Information:** The school's secretariat is available Monday through Thursday from 7:30 AM to 12:30 PM and 1:00 PM to 3:00 PM, and Friday from 7:30 AM to 12:30 PM. The contact person is Frau Reißig, reachable at sekretariat@regenweiher.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 6044012.[6]

**School Leadership:** The Extended School Leadership (ESL) in the 2022/23 school year included Nicola Ringelhan, Kathrin Kriegel, Franziska Hampe, and Svenja Schneising.[9]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Elective Compulsory Instruction (WUV):** Students in grades 5 and 6 participate in two hours per week of **interdisciplinary elective compulsory instruction based on student ideas and interests.**[3] Current offerings include:

- **Grade 5a:** Human rights, cyberbullying prevention, and sustainability/environmental topics
- **Grade 5b:** Social learning, team building, de-escalation techniques, conflict resolution, and methodology competencies, including regular class councils and police-led prevention modules
- **Grade 6b:** Student-selected topics with emphasis on community building through cooperation games, self-organized field trips, and collaborative film production[3]

**Religious Education:** The school offers **open religious instruction for all children, emphasizing respect for diverse backgrounds and perspectives.**[7]

**Special Programs and Initiatives:**
- **Social Learning Support:** Integrated across grades 2-6 with dedicated support staff[4]
- **Media Literacy Week:** Annual programming focused on media competency development[4]
- **Student Parliament and Budget:** Student-led democratic participation structures[4]
- **Transition Support:** Dedicated assistance for students transitioning from kindergarten to primary school and from primary to secondary school[4]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Project-Based Learning:** The school emphasizes experiential learning through projects such as:
- Robot construction using geometric shapes and recycled materials[5]
- Climate-friendly nutrition education with hands-on cooking experiences[5]
- Multicultural fairy tale experiences and cultural exploration[5]
- Family diversity and human uniqueness projects[5]

**Social and Emotional Development:** 
- **School Social Work:** Available daily from 8:00 AM to 3:00 PM, offering open office hours and scheduled appointments[4]
- **Team Members:** Bahar Aslan (B.A. Social Work & M.A. Social Management) and Franziska Drabner (B.A. Social Work) provide family counseling, student support, conflict mediation, and individual enrichment across grade levels[4]
- **Conflict Resolution:** Peer mediation and conflict resolution services are core offerings[4]

**Community Engagement:**
- Parent-child afternoons and parent cafés[4]
- Student parliament and student budget initiatives[4]
- Donation festivals and community events[4]
- Theater visits and cultural outings[5]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The school is recognized for its **engagement in climate and environmental protection initiatives** at the district level.[8] Recent accomplishments include successful implementation of its Social Learning Curriculum, Media Education Concept, and Inclusion Concept, demonstrating institutional commitment to comprehensive student development.[5]

The school maintains active student participation in democratic processes through functioning student parliaments and student budget programs, indicating strong development of civic competencies.[4]

## 6. ADMISSIONS

As a **public elementary school (Grundschule) in Berlin, admission follows standard Berlin public school enrollment procedures.** No specific admissions information is provided in the available sources. Prospective families should contact the school secretariat at sekretariat@regenweiher.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 6044012 for enrollment details and procedures.

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's mission centers on providing comprehensive primary education as an **offene Ganztagsschule** (open all-day school) with integrated early morning and after-school care, emphasizing holistic child development through play, learning, and extracurricular activities.[3][5][6] It supports around 285 students, most of whom attend the Hort (after-school program), fostering independence, creativity, and social skills via structured routines, homework assistance, and events like festivals and excursions.[2][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin's **Lichtenberg** district (Fennpfuhl neighborhood), the public Grundschule am Roederplatz (school number 11G02) serves the local community with modern facilities upgraded in 2013-2014, including 8,400 m² of outdoor space featuring sports tracks, jumping pits, soccer/volleyball fields, climbing nets, playground equipment, trampolines, a green classroom, and relaxation areas funded by €853,000 from the Stadtumbau Ost program.[1][2] The school building operates from 6:00-18:00, accommodating ~260-285 students and 18 state-recognized educators in its Hort program.[2][3][6] Community ties include events like discounted ISTAF indoor athletics tickets (€15 per ticket) and ALBA Berlin height contests.[1][8]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a standard Berlin **Grundschule** (primary school for grades 1-4/6), the curriculum follows state guidelines with subject-specific conferences (e.g., English/GeWi/NaWi on March 3, 2026; Math on March 5; Art on March 12; German on March 19; Sports on March 26).[1][6] Daily schedule includes 7 lessons from 08:00-15:00 (regular) or shorter variants, with two yard breaks; homework support is integrated into after-school care.[3][6] Extracurriculars encompass clubs for karate, dance, and soccer, plus creative activities like cooking, crafting, and reading.[3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience a full-day structure from 6:00 (early drop-off) to 18:00, blending academics with play-based afternoons in Hort rooms for group activities, relaxation, and homework help.[3][5][6] Annual highlights include Schulfest, Herbstfest/Halloween, Christmas crafting, Fasching, and Easter; vacations feature outings to theaters, zoos, museums (e.g., Science Museum, Tierpark), libraries, playgrounds, and "Gärten der Welt."[3] Facilities promote motor skills via sports areas and balanced play in green spaces; rules emphasize safety, no running/pushing, and cleanliness.[2][6] Most students (~260) participate in state-contracted Hort, free for grades 1-2 core time (13:30-16:00).[3][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Limited public data on achievements; a school inspection report exists from Berlin's Senatsverwaltung für Bildung, but details are unavailable.[7] The school participates in community sports events like ISTAF indoor (2026) and ALBA Berlin contests, indicating active extracurricular engagement.[8] Facilities modernization (2014) meets Berlin education standards, supporting physical development for 285 students.[2]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Enrollment for first-graders (Schulanfänger) requires the child's presence during the period October 6-17, 2025, at the secretariat (specific hours: Mon/Wed 08:30-12:30; Tue/Thu 10:00-13:00/14:00-17:00).[5] Forms include school medical exam, deferral, early enrollment, and transfers (including to Brandenburg or private schools).[5] As an open all-day school, Hort contracts via Jugendamt Lichtenberg are needed for pre-school (6:00-7:30) or post-school care (13:30-16:00/18:00), available from August 1 if applied timely; core Hort (13:30-16:00) is free for grades 1-2 without needs assessment.[5]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Schule am Sandsteinweg operates as an **offene Ganztagsschule** (open all-day school), emphasizing a "Schule als Lebensraum" (school as a living space) concept developed by former principal Petra Balzer in 1989, fostering a welcoming environment with strong community involvement and animal-assisted learning to reduce conflicts and promote well-being[1][2][6]. Current principal Heike Hertha prioritizes a "Wir-Gefühl" (we-feeling), integrating classroom teachers into after-school care, parental engagement via the Förderverein, and facilities like a school zoo to create an inclusive "Ort zum Wohlfühlen" (place to feel good)[1][3].

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded on October 7, 1957, as the Christoph-Ruden-Schule, it expanded with additional buildings in 1968 (Haus 2), 1972 (sports hall), and 1995 (Haus 3 and 4), serving grades 1-6 on a 40,000 m² campus in Berlin-Neukölln's Buckow district[2][3]. The school houses over 100 animals including goats, sheep, rabbits, Hängebauchschweine, ponies, horses, ducks, pigeons, and bees, with a school zoo established in 1990, stables, riding arena, circus tent, tierfarm, schulgarten with pond, and separate Lernhäuser for grades 1-3[1][2][3][6]. Community features include active parents (even alumni) building animal enclosures, a weekly Zirkus-Café for parent interactions, Förderverein support via Amazon donations, and Schulsozialarbeit since 2021/22[1][4][8]. It serves ~758 students (2024/2025) with 51.8% migration background, supported by 50 teachers and 26 educators, making it Neukölln's largest primary school[2].

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a state Grundschule (primary school, grades 1-6), it follows Berlin's Rahmenlehrpläne with English as the first foreign language, Schulanfangsphase with year-group learning groups, and offerings like Projektunterricht, Zirkusprojekt, and working groups (AGs) in Reiten, Tennis, Internet-Cafe, Fußball, Chor, Basketball[3][8]. Unique programs integrate animals into classrooms (2 hours/week per class), schulinterner Reitunterricht with riding badge exams, and offene Ganztagbetreuung (OGB) up to 18:00 with three modules; class teachers provide 6 hours/week in-class support[1][2][3][6]. Facilities support digital learning and Schulstation[3].

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students benefit from detailed **Unterrichtszeiten** (lesson times) starting at 07:15 (pre-school hour) up to 15:00, with A/B-Wochen scheduling, Arbeitsgemeinschaften, Speiseplan, and Ferienbetreuung for ~100 students[8][Curriculum Page]. Experiences include hands-on animal care (e.g., ponies at enrollment, goats in class), riding lessons, circus activities, sports, and environmental initiatives like Schulwegpaten for safe commuting and klimaschutz efforts[1][2][3][6][10]. Animal interactions have reduced accidents by one-third and fights, enhancing social skills[6].

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school reports fewer accidents (down one-third) and rare conflicts due to animal programs, with high parental demand from beyond the catchment area despite the socially challenging neighborhood; gymnasiums eagerly accept its graduates[2][6]. It transitioned successfully to full Ganztagsteam cooperation, maintains ~758 enrollment as Neukölln's largest Grundschule, and engages in SPI-Programm since 08.2021 with tandem BTL support[1][2][4][5].

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public Grundschule, admission is via local Einzugsgebiet with Schulanmeldung forms requiring both guardians' signatures for offener Ganztag (EfoeB); info evening for 2026/2027 starters on Do 25.09.2025 at 18:00 in Hort/Muschelkalkweg, plus Buchungskalender and Senatsseite overview[8]. Contact: Hornblendeweg 2, 12349 Berlin; Tel. (030) 762860; info@sams.gs or sekretariat@sams.schule.berlin.de[3][8].

# Schule am Teltowkanal – Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Schule am Teltowkanal identifies itself as **"Die grüne Grundschule im Herzen von Neukölln"** (The green primary school in the heart of Neukölln)[3], reflecting its commitment to environmental education and sustainability. The school's philosophy emphasizes holistic development through diverse learning experiences, including cultural, artistic, and athletic programs alongside traditional academics.

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Setting**

The school is located in the Britz district of Neukölln, Berlin, at Rungiusstraße 46-52, 12347 Berlin[2][3]. It is situated in close proximity to the Teltowkanal, a natural waterway that influences the school's environmental character[1].

**Student Population**

Approximately 360 students attend the school across grades 1-6[1]. The school serves a diverse community and offers specialized support for newcomers, including **learning groups for students without German language skills**[1][4].

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school features comprehensive facilities including[4]:
- Computerfachräume (computer labs)
- Schulgarten (school garden)
- Schulteich (school pond)
- Schülerbücherei (student library)
- Spielhof (play area)

The naturalistic garden and proximity to the canal support the school's environmental education mission[1].

**School Administration**

The school is led by **Nancy Pokall** (Schulleitung)[4][9]. The school office is located in House J, with secretarial staff including Fr. Scheffner available during office hours[3]. The school operates under the Bezirksamt Neukölln (Neukölln District Office)[9].

**Contact Information**

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Schule am Wäldchen operates as a **"Schule des sozialen Miteinanders"** (school of social togetherness), emphasizing social learning, violence prevention, health promotion, and reading proficiency.[1][4] Key focuses include a healthy school environment through programs like Gesundheitswoche (health week), Klasse 2000 from grade 1, a school library, reading mentors, and a grade 6 reading competition, alongside social initiatives such as conflict mediators from grade 4 and cooperation with local kindergarten "Sonnenschein" via projects like "Tanz in die Schule."[1][4] The school prioritizes an inclusive, supportive atmosphere with recognition culture, violence prevention support from the Schulstation, and inter-cultural parent-child cafes.[1][4]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin's Lichtenberg district (Neu-Hohenschönhausen), this public primary school (Grundschule, school number 11G25) at Wustrower Str. 28, 13051 Berlin, serves students aged 5.5 to 13 in a reliable half-day structure with open all-day care (OGB).[1][3] Facilities include 25 general classrooms (each for max 25 students with interactive Promethean whiteboards), specialized rooms for early grades, movement spaces, a library, computer room, student club, science/music/gym rooms, early/late care areas, and a multi-purpose foyer; all classrooms feature interactive whiteboards.[1][2] Community ties involve the Schulstation for social support, partnerships with Jugendamt Lichtenberg, SPIK e.V., Verein Klasse 2000 e.V., and kindergarten collaborations; it offers interpreter services and parent events.[1][2][4] A 2015 inspection report and 2020 school program highlight strong leadership and equipment.[2]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum follows Berlin's standards with English as the first foreign language from grade 1, health-focused profile, open all-day care, flexible learning groups in early phases, and a school station.[1] Mandatory elective courses (Wahlpflichtunterricht/WUV) for grades 5-6 (Tues-Thurs, 2 hours) include Sportliche Spiele, Badminton, Creative Writing/School Newspaper, Computer, Library, Theater, History, Table Tennis, Creative Design, Ethics, Rhythm/Song/Dance, Handicrafts, and student newspaper "Waldlicht" (sold at 0.25 EUR self-cost).[1] Differentiated support covers math/German remediation, reading/writing difficulties, German as second language, and emotional-social needs; portfolios, class councils, and peer mediation are used.[1][2][6] Extracurriculars (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) feature karate, dance, silk painting, theater, and sports club access.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in social-emotional learning via violence prevention, conflict mediators (from grade 4), class councils, and Gewaltfreie Kommunikation (non-violent communication).[4][6] Reading and health initiatives include mentors, competitions, and Klasse 2000; sports involve competitions and AGs with presentations of achievements.[1][4] Creative projects like the "Waldlicht" newspaper foster real-world skills in research, writing, and publishing.[1] Inclusivity supports diverse backgrounds with remediation, portfolios, and competitions (e.g., Big Challenge, Känguru); inter-cultural cafes and kindergarten transitions enhance community.[1][2][4][6] All-day care and movement rooms support holistic development.[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school earns praise in inspections for leadership, equipment, teacher satisfaction, and use of special education expertise; a 2015 report notes positive development plans.[2] Strengths include diagnostic strategies, remediation in core subjects, social training, and student presentations at competitions and assemblies (grades 4-6).[1][2][6] Sports successes are showcased school-wide, with conflict mediators awarded; parent involvement thrives at events like school festivals.[1][4] The 2020 program highlights high social load support via bonuses and partnerships.[2] Prüfungsergebnisse (exam results) are available via official portraits, though specifics are not detailed here.[1]

As a public tuition-free Grundschule, admission is open to local children in the catchment area, with transitions from kindergarten supported via info evenings, "Tanz in die Schule" project, and trial hours for preschoolers.[1][4] Contact: Tel. +49 30 92403080, email 11G25@11g25.schule.berlin.de, leadership Frank Thoenelt.[1] No specific application process detailed beyond standard Berlin public school enrollment.[1][3]

# Schule am Wildgarten - School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Schule am Wildgarten is a **public special education school** (Förderschule) serving students with learning support needs and intellectual development support in Berlin's Treptow-Köpenick district.[5] The school operates as both a primary school (Grundschule) and secondary level I institution (Sekundarstufe I) with two distinct focus areas: "Lernen" (Learning) and "Geistige Entwicklung" (Intellectual Development).[5]

The school's educational approach emphasizes **individualized instruction in small classes** with **sonderpädagogische Prinzipien** (special education principles).[1][2] Beyond academic content, the school prioritizes **lebenspraktische Inhalte** (life skills and practical knowledge), integrating real-world competencies into daily instruction.[2] A distinctive feature is the school's commitment to **"duales Lernen"** (dual learning), which combines classroom instruction with practical, work-oriented experiences to bridge the gap between school and employment.[2]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Accessibility**

The school is located at Köpenicker Landstraße 185a in the Baumschulenweg district of Treptow-Köpenick, Berlin.[5] It is accessible via S-Bahn Baumschulenweg (approximately 10 minutes on foot) or by buses 166, 167, and 165.[5]

**Enrollment and Community**

The school serves approximately 170-300 students across both primary and secondary levels.[4][6] The school community includes teachers, educators (Erzieher*innen), social workers (Sozialarbeiter*innen), and dedicated support staff working collaboratively to serve students and families.[2]

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school underwent significant renovation and modernization completed in April 2021.[6] The 4,150 m² facility features a distinctive architectural design with colorful façade elements on each building side, creating an inviting and respectful environment for students.[6] Interior spaces include color-coded corridors—each hallway painted in its own color with subtle variations in classroom areas—to help students identify with their classroom "Vorgarten" (front garden) and foster a sense of belonging.[6] The school incorporates nature-based learning elements, with two nesting boxes attached to each classroom that students can observe and integrate into their curriculum.[6]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Academic Structure**

Students transition into the school from primary education beginning in Grade 3, with progression to secondary level I (Sekundarstufe I) at Grade 7.[1] The curriculum is delivered through **individualized tasks with adapted requirements** that match each student's learning level and pace.[1]

**Vocational Preparation and Career Orientation**

A cornerstone of the school's program is **intensive vocational preparation** beginning in Grade 8, featuring:

- Structured internships (Praktika) starting in Grade 8[1]
- Work experience in workshops and practical settings[1]
- Collaboration with career entry mentors (Berufseinstiegsbegleiter)[1]
- Practical, work-oriented learning experiences integrated throughout the curriculum[2]

The dual learning model connects classroom content with real-world professional contexts, beginning in primary school and continuing through secondary level.[2]

**Certifications and Outcomes**

Students can earn two types of school-leaving certificates: **BO** (Berufsorientierungsabschluss - Career Orientation Certificate) and **BBR** (Berufsreife - Basic Vocational Readiness).[1]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Support Services**

The school provides comprehensive support through:

- **Social work services** (Schulsozialarbeit) offering counseling and support to students, teachers, and parents[4][8]
- **Individual promotion and differentiated instruction** tailored to each student's needs[1]
- **Prevention work and conflict resolution** programs integrated into school culture[1]
- **Extensive partnerships with external organizations** including Cabuwazi, Wildwasser, Berliner Jungs, and Waldschule Plänterwald[8]

**Enrichment and Wellbeing Activities**

The school emphasizes holistic student development through:

- **Numerous sports activities** and physical education programs[1]
- **Social learning and team-building activities** (e.g., Fairplayer program)[8]
- **Arts and creative offerings** including open art workshops (Offener Kunsttreff)[8]
- **Nature-based learning** through forest school programs and outdoor activities[8]
- **Student leadership development** through student parliament (Schülerparlament) and class councils (Klassenräte)[8]
- **Specialized support groups** including girls' programs (Mädchen-Treff) and boys' programs (Jungstreff)[8]

**Behavioral and Emotional Support**

The school implements:

- **Violence prevention and conflict management** programs[1][8]
- **School distancing counseling** for students at risk of disengagement[8]
- **Child protection collaboration** through school-based advisory teams[8]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The search results do not provide specific data on standardized test scores, graduation rates, or quantified academic achievement metrics. However, the school's success is reflected in its comprehensive support infrastructure and partnerships with external organizations dedicated to student success in both academic and vocational domains.[1][2][8]

The school's recent facility renovation (completed April 2021) demonstrates institutional investment in creating a welcoming, high-quality learning environment that respects and supports students with diverse learning needs.[6]

# Schule am Wilhelmsberg (Grundschule) - School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Schule am Wilhelmsberg is committed to offering **a diverse spectrum across all areas of school life**: in instruction, care within the reliable half-day primary school framework, in open all-day operations, and in other leisure activities[2]. The school emphasizes **transparent communication systems and collaborative teamwork** among teachers, educators, and social pedagogues[4].

The school prioritizes **democracy education, anti-racism initiatives, and school health** as core values within its educational approach[5].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Infrastructure**

Schule am Wilhelmsberg is located in the **Lichtenberg district** in the **Alt-Hohenschönhausen neighborhood** of Berlin[1]. The school occupies **two buildings** and has undergone extensive renovations over the past 15 years, including work on the main building, gymnasium, and schoolyard[2].

A **branch location** is available at Mittelstr. 16, 13055 Berlin, with accessible parking for people with disabilities and wheelchair-accessible restrooms[3].

**School Community**

The school serves approximately **740 students organized in 32 classes**[2]. The pedagogical team consists of **90 teachers and educators**, supplemented by three teachers for civic education (Lebenskunde), two social workers from the school station, one secretary, an administrative director, one school health professional, and two custodians[2].

**Leadership**

- **Headmistress:** Frau Reiter
- **Deputy Headmistress:** Frau Maierhofer
- **Coordinating Educator:** Herr Schütz[7]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Language Instruction**

**English** is offered as the first foreign language[3].

**Special Programs and Offerings**

The school provides:
- **Schulanfangsphase** (school entry phase) with both grade-level and mixed-age learning groups[3]
- **Computer instruction** beginning in Grade 3[3]
- **Conflict resolution program** with student mediators (Konfliktlotsen)[3]
- **Partnerships** with music schools and sports clubs[3]

**After-School Care**

The school offers **open all-day care (Offene Ganztagbetreuung - OGB)** and extended care (eFöB)[2][3]. The after-school care program can be reached at 030 97 99 68 36[7].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Support Services**

The **Schulstation Chamäleon** (school station) supports students in navigating social and interpersonal challenges within the school community[8].

**School Events**

Recent activities include:

As of July 7, 2025, Schule am Wilhelmsberg was officially recognized as the **36th primary school** in a specific initiative or program[6].

The school's educational philosophy emphasizes individualized learning, including Montessori methods with year-group-spanning classes for grades 1-3, and supports the JÜL 1-3 model with 60-minute periods and English from grade 2.[2][7] It promotes diverse learning opportunities through its school program, focusing on open all-day care (Offene Ganztagbetreuung - OGB), language education, cultural and political education, and individual learning approaches.[2][7]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Established before 1938 (celebrating 70 years in 1999, implying founding around 1929), the school was renamed "Grundschule an den Püttbergen" in 1992 after previously being the "12. Grundschule – Köpenick" in 1991.[7] It serves the Rahnsdorf community in Berlin's Treptow-Köpenick district, with ongoing facility improvements including roof renovation, fire safety measures, extension construction, hallway redesigns, new flooring, metal lockers, interactive whiteboards in 16 classrooms, a school museum, and a school library.[2][6][7] Green spaces expanded post-1969 with over 100 sqm of new greenery and 600 trees/shrubs.[7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum follows Berlin's framework plans with a Montessori branch introduced in 2001, expanding to year-group-spanning classes (grades 1-3) by 2003, alongside three age-homogeneous classes and one mixed-age class as of 2019.[2][7] Key programs include English as the first foreign language from grade 2, JÜL 1-3 with 60-minute models, open all-day care, year-specific and cross-year learning groups in early phases, gifted student afternoon courses via Schulverbund Nord under the Senate's high-ability promotion concept, and working groups in choir, artistic design, computers, and chess.[2][3][7] Additional focus areas: individual learning, language/cultural/political education, school quality support, and prevention programs.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school has sustained a Montessori program since 2001, developed a comprehensive school program (2006-2011) with ongoing updates, and participates in Berlin's gifted education initiatives.[3][7] Facility modernizations support improved learning environments, including exam results tracking and professional management noted in official portraits, though specific outcome metrics are not detailed.[2]

The Schule an der Victoriastadt positions itself as an open, community-oriented primary school that listens to the needs of its school community and promotes students based on their individual strengths, with a strong emphasis on **musisch-künstlerischer** (arts and music) focus, scientific experimentation, and polite interpersonal conduct.[1][3][4] It fosters an inclusive environment where ideas from teachers, students, parents, and partners enrich school life, and it commits to reliable cooperation with external partners.[3][4] Core principles include responsible development of each student, support for non-native German speakers through targeted groups, clear communication of curriculum and assessments, and a positive learning atmosphere.[3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin-Lichtenberg (Rummelsburg), this **public Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6, school number 11G16) at Nöldnerstr. 44, 10317 Berlin, serves an catchment area including Rummelsburg and parts of Neu-Lichtenberg.[1][2][7] Originally a special needs school (11S01) with a learning support center, it transitioned to a full primary school in 2014/15 after closing the special center in 2013/14, leading to increased enrollment; renovations occurred in 2020.[2][5] The historic listed building currently hosts approximately **660-706 students** in **30-32 classes**.[3][4] Facilities include two computer rooms, laptop sets, a teaching kitchen, workshop, and open all-day care (OGB); it offers school-related social work via a Schulstation for prevention and early intervention.[2][7] Community ties include partnerships like Berliner Lesepaten for reading support and the "Musikalische Grundschule" project since 2015.[1][6]

As a **reliable half-day primary school**, core instruction runs 7:45-13:30, with optional early care (6:00-7:45) and after-school (16:00-18:00); students enter from 7:30, with a detailed hourly schedule up to 15:40 for extended days.[3] The profile emphasizes **musisch-ästhetische Bildung** (arts-aesthetic education), integrating music across subjects via the Senate-supported "Musikalische Grundschule" project to enhance learning, social skills, and enjoyment.[6] Subjects include GeWi, Kunst, Mathematik, Musik, Sport, NaWi, Deutsch, Englisch; concepts cover Hausaufgaben (homework), Bibliothek (library), Demokratie, Medien, Mobilität, Sprachbildung, and more, with tailored support for language learners and special needs integration.[3][7] English is introduced as the first foreign language; working groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) and year-group learning clusters in early phases are available.[7]

Students engage in hands-on naturwissenschaftliches experimenting, artistic pursuits, and music-infused daily life to create relaxed, joyful learning; polite conduct and mutual respect are prioritized.[4][6] Activities include book drives, sports like Sparki-Cup hall football, "Post mit Herz" charity, Christmas singing, and star-making crafts, alongside reading patronage and library initiatives.[1] The school promotes democratic values, media literacy, child protection, and mobility concepts, with flexible homework support and behavior codes; all-day options extend experiences beyond half-days.[3][7] A school agreement ensures teacher accountability for fostering development, informing parents, and collaborating closely.[3]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
A school inspection on November 13 and 15, 2023, identified strengths and development areas, with the full report publicly available.[1] Participation in the certified "Musikalische Grundschule" project since 2015 demonstrates sustained profile development in arts education.[6] Recent community events like the 2023 book collection and 2025/2026 activities (e.g., Christmas events, sports) highlight active engagement; statistics and evaluations are documented internally.[1][2] No specific exam results or quantitative outcomes are detailed in available content.[7]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public tuition-free Grundschule**, enrollment follows Berlin's standard public school processes via Schulanmeldung; resources include a school path portal for safe routes and danger spots.[1][7] Contact the secretariat (Tel: 030 5107047, Email: 11g16@11g16.schule.berlin.de or sekretariat@11g16.schule.berlin.de) Monday-Thursday 7:30-12:15/13:00-14:00, Friday 7:30-12:00 (note temporary closures, e.g., until March 7 due to illness).[1][8] Post-primary options include Lichtenberg Oberschulen open days and directories.[1]

# Schule an der Wuhlheide - Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Schule an der Wuhlheide operates with a clear **mission centered on holistic child development** and collaborative partnership between school and family[1]. The school's core values emphasize:

- Developing a sense of community and fostering intellectual curiosity
- Creating joyful, engaging instruction
- Promoting healthy living and personal responsibility
- Cultivating politeness and helpfulness
- Supporting diverse interests and talents
- Encouraging independent thinking and initiative

The school explicitly states that these goals are achievable only through a **strong, trusting partnership between home and school**, where all stakeholders work together to help students develop according to their individual potential and talents[1].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Basic Information**
Schule an der Wuhlheide is located at Kottmeierstraße 2, 12459 Berlin in the Oberschöneweide district of Treptow-Köpenick[2]. It operates as a **public (öffentlich) state school** with school number 09G22[5].

**School Structure and Enrollment**
The school currently serves approximately **470 students with rising enrollment trends**[3]. The school operates as a **bound all-day school (gebundener Ganztag)**, meaning students attend structured programming throughout the day[2][8].

**Staffing and Support**
The school employs a **multiprofessional team** including 33 teachers and nearly as many additional pedagogical staff members—educators, integration assistants, and a school social worker[3]. Teachers and educators work in fixed teams through grade 4, with closer coordination continuing in upper grades[3].

**Facilities and Resources**
The school provides access to an **aula (assembly hall), library, computer room, and school garden**[5]. A second location is available at Firlstraße 25, 12459 Berlin, with accessible parking for people with disabilities[5]. The school operates a cafeteria with lunch service provided by a catering company, with meal times accommodating different class schedules from 11:35 to 13:45[3].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Musical Emphasis**
Schule an der Wuhlheide is designated as a **music-focused school (musikbetonte Grundschule)**[4]. The school maintains **three brass classes (Bläserklassen)** as a curricular focus[3]. In December 2014, these brass classes performed with the Berlin Philharmonic Orchestra at the Berlin Philharmonie[3]. The school cooperates with **TUSCH Berlin**, a theater-school partnership program[3].

**Language Instruction**
English is offered as the **first foreign language**[5].

**Extracurricular Activities and Working Groups**
The school offers diverse **working groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften)** including brass classes, football, woodworking, student newspaper, Comenius program, choir, sports, judo, crafts, and school garden activities[5].

**Additional Support Services**
The school provides **school social work services** and operates under a **school entry phase with grade-level learning groups**[5]. Reading mentors (Lesepaten) support literacy development[5].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Daily Schedule**
The school day operates with an **open beginning at 7:30 AM**, with formal instruction beginning at 8:00 AM and continuing until 4:00 PM (16:00)[3]. This extended day structure allows for integrated academic and enrichment programming.

**Collaborative Teaching Model**
Through grade 4, **fixed teams of one teacher and one educator work together** with the same group of students, ensuring consistent relationships and coordinated support[3]. Teachers and educators participate in regular coordination meetings and grade-level team conferences to align their work[3].

**Interdisciplinary Approach**
The school emphasizes **close coordination between all professional staff**, ensuring that educators understand academic expectations and can reinforce learning during non-instructional time[3].

The school's motto, "Einfach gute Schule machen" (Simply making a good school), emphasizes creating a welcoming environment where everyday experiences contribute to education, as reflected in the Goethe quote: "Alles, was uns begegnet, lässt Spuren zurück. Alles trägt unmerklich zu unserer Bildung bei."[8] The website invites visitors to follow "Auf unseren Spuren" (On our traces) to understand what defines the school and its priorities, focusing on organization, teaching, and community engagement.[1][8]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Schule im Gutspark is a public primary school (Grundschule) in Berlin-Lichtenberg, located at Josef-Orlopp-Straße 20, 10367 Berlin, led by Astrid Blaß since at least 2012.[1][2][9] It serves the Lichtenberg district (Schulnummer 11G03) and collaborates with local kindergartens (e.g., Kita "Spatzennest," Kita "Phantasialand") and organizations like SV Tora Berlin e.V. for sports and JULI Jugendhilfe for after-school support.[1] Facilities include a library, computer room, teaching kitchen, wood workshop, bicycle workshop, two music rooms, outdoor classrooms, flower garden, accessible parking, and wheelchair-accessible restrooms.[1]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum features English as the first foreign language, a mathematics-natural sciences profile, year-group-specific learning groups in the early school phase, and open all-day care (Offene Ganztagbetreuung, OGB).[1][6] Extracurricular activities (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) include handball, theater, cooking & baking, computers, library, keyboard, karate, dancing, judo, ball school, guitar lessons, math enrichment, and ceramics, often in partnerships with local clubs.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students benefit from open all-day programs, school station support, and hands-on facilities like workshops and gardens promoting practical learning.[1][6] After-school offerings and cooperatives with nearby kindergartens ease the transition to school, while activities foster skills in sports, arts, music, and STEM.[1]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Schule im Ostseekarree operates as a **Ganztagsgrundschule in offener Form** (all-day primary school in open form), emphasizing "Schule in Bewegung" (school in motion) and "Zukunft in Hohenschönhausen" (future in Hohenschönhausen). Its philosophy promotes inclusive education through the Startchancen-Programm, Berliner Leseband for reading promotion, MITsprache for integration of diverse backgrounds, and efforts in inclusion to address varied student needs.[2][3] The school fosters social and digital competencies in a praxis-oriented manner, ensuring every student feels supported, as articulated by principal Torsten Leschke with a Stephen Hawking quote on creating a hopeful future.[1][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in **Bezirk Lichtenberg, Ortsteil Neu-Hohenschönhausen**, the school serves over 580 students from grades 1 to primary transition, with modern facilities including a Modularen Ergänzungsbau (MEB) from 2020, a renovated sports hall (2017), interactive displays in classrooms (kreidefrei), and access to sports and leisure facilities.[1][2][3] Community involvement includes a newly founded Förderverein (2024) for funding projects, Pausen-Buddies for conflict resolution, and Schulstation social workers; partnerships feature Verein für ambulante Versorgung Hohenschönhausen e.V., BALL e.V., and sports clubs like Tora Berlin e.V. (Karate) and JUDO-CLUB '03.[2][3][6] The school is public (öffentlich), tuition-free, with school number 11G10.[1][3]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum focuses on **targeted reading promotion (Berliner Leseband)**, digital integration via interactive displays, and year-spanning learning groups in the Schulanfangsphase. It includes English as the first foreign language, offene Ganztagbetreuung (OGB), and programs like Startchancen for equity and MITsprache for language/integration support.[2][3] Praxis-oriented learning occurs in classrooms and external sites, with inclusive practices for diverse needs.[2]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in a broad **AG- and project offerings** including Karate, Line-Dance, Fußball, Gitarre, Rhythmus, Judo, circus project weeks, and class trips. All-day structure (Montag-Freitag, outside holidays) features: 6:00-7:30 Frühhort (fee-based), 7:30-13:30 free learning support, 13:30-16:00 offene Hortarbeit (fee-based), 16:00-18:00 Späthort (fee-based). Additional supports include Schulstation (open Mon-Thu 7:00-14:30, Fri 7:00-13:00 or by appointment), Pausen-Buddies, and wheelchair-accessible facilities.[2][3]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Recognized as one of the first schools in the **Startchancen-Programm** by federal and state governments, it is described as a popular institution in Lichtenberg due to modern infrastructure, wide extracurriculars, and reading initiatives. Over 580 students transition successfully; specific Prüfungsergebnisse (exam results) and outcomes are referenced but not detailed in available data.[2][3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public Grundschule**, admission follows Berlin's standard public primary school enrollment processes, serving the local catchment area in Neu-Hohenschönhausen. Contact the Sekretariat at 030 9240 3000 or Sekretariat@11G10.schule.berlin.de (open Mon-Fri 7:30-8:30, Mon-Thu 13:00-13:30) for inquiries; leadership includes Rektor Torsten Leschke and Stellvertretender Dr. Tony Bewersdorff.[1][2][3]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The school's mission emphasizes creating a safe, supportive environment where children learn, play, discover talents, and build friendships according to their individual needs, strengths, and interests. It focuses on holistic development through a multiprofessional team addressing diverse needs, rhythmic full-day programs alternating learning and relaxation, social learning, team spirit, and meaningful leisure activities with reliable supervision. Core values include inclusivity, diversity (with 13 languages spoken), differentiated learning, language development, and anti-racism initiatives like "Schule ohne Rassismus - Schule mit Courage". The philosophy integrates school and social-pedagogical aspects, promoting self-reliance, conflict resolution, and harmonious coexistence in a "learning oasis" amid urban Berlin-Neukölln.[1][3][4][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Established as a primary school (Grundschule) in Berlin-Neukölln (Bezirk Neukölln, school number 08G35), it originated in the 1970s as a child center including kindergarten and health services; the intercultural parent center opened in 2009, and school social work by FiPP e.V. began that year, expanding in 2014. The community is diverse with 616-654 students (about two-thirds local), supported by 51 teachers, 41 educators, 4 non-pedagogical staff, 3 school social workers, parent representatives, student council, and trust educators. Facilities span 2.3 hectares including a large schoolyard, separate leisure house, sports field, competition-standard hall, two playgrounds, adjacent park, digital tools (beamer-equipped classrooms, tablet cases), and ongoing toilet renovations since 2019.[1][2][3][4][5][8][9]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a public **Grundschule** (grades 0-6), the curriculum features differentiated learning, supplementary support, 30 extracurricular activities (AGs), digital offerings, language promotion (including heritages language in Arabic from 2022/23), and rhythmic full-day structure: learning phases alternate with relaxation; two days split morning/afternoon lessons; 90-minute blocks for longer breaks; afternoon blocks (14:30-16:00) cover non-core subjects; 2-3 weekly pre-lessons for homework. Entry phase links preschool and grade 1 over two years with continuous teacher-educator teams; unguided midday time from grade 3 for independent planning; preventive programs like social learning and mediators.[3][4][5]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience a full-day "rhythmic" program with structured learning, relaxation, independent leisure planning, and diverse activities in a spacious, inclusive setting. Support includes individual emotional/fachlich guidance, school social work for conflicts, self-esteem, family issues, and bullying; open hours, class interventions, and parent involvement via intercultural center and GEV. Events like Christmas celebrations, reading competitions (e.g., 2025 winner Asli Zengin), and projects foster engagement. Lower grades (0-2) have dedicated floors with leisure/sanitary rooms; musisch/sport focus in grades 1-4.[1][3][4][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school holds "Schule ohne Rassismus - Schule mit Courage" certification and received a 2019 inspection report praising **herausragende** (outstanding) work in educating, supporting, and caring for its heterogeneous students. It maintains strong social work integration since 2009 and conflict mediation training amid challenges. Enrollment stability (616-654 students) and multiprofessional success in diversity management are noted.[1][5][8][9]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a tuition-free public **Grundschule**, admissions follow Berlin's standard process via local school enrollment (Schulanmeldung), with info on safe school routes and forms available on the website. Contact sekretariat@grundheide.schule.berlin.de or (030) 682 960 13 for details; intercultural parent center supports families.[1][2]

The school emphasizes a collaborative, trusting partnership among children, parents, and educators in a vibrant, multifaceted environment mirroring Berlin's Treptow-Köpenick district. Pedagogues address individual student needs by providing ample time and autonomy in learning processes, fostering versatile school activities and diverse after-school offerings.[5][8]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located at Rudower Straße 201, 12557 Berlin in the Köpenick area of **Treptow-Köpenick** district, this public **Grundschule** (primary school, school number 09G18) serves over **500 students** in **23 classes**.[1][6][8] The campus includes bright classrooms, a large music room with instruments, modern science lab for experiments, teaching kitchen, PC room, gymnastics room for yoga and active breaks, extensive playground with sandbox, table tennis, and funcourt.[8] A separate after-school care building (Hort) features lounges, craft workshop, Snoozelen relaxation room, and library with over 2,000 books and audio materials.[8] Facilities shared with Schule an der Dahme include a recently renovated two-court gym; a mobile extension building (MEB) with 21 new classrooms is under construction.[8] The **team** comprises **47 teachers**, **33 educators**, and **2 school social workers**.[8] Community ties include resilience training by Andreas Langer and partnerships like tjfbg gGmbH since 2009.[4][5]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Standard Grundschule curriculum integrates English as the first foreign language, open all-day care (OGB), and transitional phase with cross-grade learning groups.[6] Specialized spaces support music, science experiments, computing, cooking, and nature studies via working groups.[6][8] The school library is embedded in German classes to promote reading competency amid digital trends, with storytelling by reading sponsors.[Curriculum Page] Daily schedule: Periods 1-2 (8:00-9:40 incl. breakfast), 3-4 (10:00-11:30), up to 8 (14:30-15:15).[5][Main Page] Care options: Reliable half-day (07:30-13:30), open full-day (06:00-18:00), and holiday programs.[8]

Students engage in hands-on activities like resilience training, police visits, wild herb foraging, waffle-making at outdoor labs, Christmas crafting, and water-themed holidays.[Main Page] Facilities enable play, relaxation (Snoozelen), reading, yoga, ball games, and experimentation; blog highlights vibrant events like mural painting and group celebrations.[Main Page][8] After-school (Hort) and holiday care extend learning with crafts and library access.[8]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific academic outcomes, test results, or awards detailed in available content; focus is on facilities, care programs, and community activities like strike notices and ferien programs.[Main Page][6] School portrait notes equipment and offerings but lacks performance data.[6]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Contact hours: Mon 10-12/13-14, Tue/Wed/Fri 7-9/10-12/13-14 (Thu 10-12/13-14); phone (030) 6432 6715/6798, email sekretariat@09g18.schule.berlin.de.[Main Page][6] Calendar events include enrollment period (10.02.2026), trial lessons (20.02.2026), and studientag (09.02.2026); parents receive letters on strikes and closures.[Main Page] As a public school, admission follows Berlin Grundschule catchment (Köllnische Vorstadt area).[1][7]

# SchuleEins School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

SchuleEins's motto is **"Open the gates – let life come in!"**[1] The school is built on the philosophy of integrated care, blending learning and support throughout the day through its **Soziales Leben (Social Life) program**, which connects morning classes with afternoon activities in a meaningful, cohesive way[1]. Within this community, students learn to take responsibility for themselves and others, develop skills to understand conflicts and problems, and actively work together on solutions[1].

The school emphasizes a **Life Worlds (Lebenswelten)** subject that supports interdisciplinary and cross-grade learning experiences tailored to each age group[1].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**SchuleEins** was **founded in 2007** as an independent community school (Gemeinschaftsschule in freier Trägerschaft)[1]. The school operates across multiple locations in Berlin's Pankow district:

- **Speicher** (elementary school, grades 1–4): Hadlichstraße 44, 13187 Berlin
- **Waisenhaus** (middle school, grades 5–8): Berliner Straße 120/121, 13187 Berlin[1]

The Speicher building is a historic ancient tobacco storage facility, while the Waisenhaus is a former Jewish orphanage[1].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

SchuleEins serves **grades 1 through 13** with teaching aligned to the official Berlin state curriculum[1]. The school offers:

- **All standard Berlin school-leaving qualifications**, including the university entrance qualification (Abitur, comparable to A-levels or the International Baccalaureate)[1]
- **Inclusive, mixed-ability learning** from grade 1 through graduation[1]
- **Mandatory foreign languages**: English and Spanish[1]
- **Personalized learning paths** through targeted support and enrichment programs, the da Vinci program, lectures, and in-class differentiation[1]
- **Project-based learning**, including open afternoon workshops, themed project weeks, and subject-related excursions[1]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

The school provides **full-day care and holiday care**, with closure only between Christmas and New Year[1]. Students engage in project-based learning through open afternoon workshops, themed project weeks, and subject-related excursions[1].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

No specific awards, rankings, or certifications are mentioned in the available content.

## 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES

The school conducts information evenings for families interested in transitioning to secondary level (grade 6 to 7), where they can meet school leadership, students, and staff to learn about the school's profile and admission procedures[4].

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Schulen Christburg Campus Prenzlauer Berg operates as a private, state-recognized Christian primary school (Grundschule) under the Christburg Campus gGmbH, emphasizing biblical foundations, professional competence, and holistic development of children into strong personalities who discover their vocations.[1][6] Core values include authenticity, servanthood, progress, grace, respect, and team spirit, with education integrating state curricula alongside Christian perspectives centered on Jesus, fostering trust, responsibility, and awareness of God's love.[2][5] The philosophy views children as entrusted by God and parents, prioritizing a culture of mindfulness, child protection, and value-based learning from grades 1 to Abitur.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1988 as part of the Christburg Campus (initially Christburg Campus Berlin e.V.), the campus has grown to include Kitas, three primary schools, and secondary education, with the Prenzlauer Berg site established in 1999 in a historic, listed Wilhelminian school ensemble at Christburger Str. 14, 10405 Berlin, owned since 2005.[1][6] It serves around 900 students across sites in Prenzlauer Berg, Spandau, and Hellersdorf, promoting a familial atmosphere praised by parents for personal greetings, events like school festivals, and community bonds among parents, teachers, and children.[2] Facilities feature bright rooms, digital whiteboards in every class, workshops, and after-school care; the campus emphasizes safety via a comprehensive child protection concept ("Christburg Campus – ein sicherer Ort") updated September 2025, including prevention, intervention plans, and staff training.[0 PDF]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As the **Elisabeth-Abegg-Grundschule**, it offers state-recognized primary education (grades 1-6? typical for Grundschule) with open all-day care (OGB), English as first foreign language, reading promotion, media literacy badge, and differentiated learning like year-group learning groups in early phases.[3][4] Part of the broader Christburg Campus, it connects to the **Corrie-ten-Boom-Schule** (integrated secondary school, grades 7-13, with gymnasiale Oberstufe for Abitur in three years and high MSA pass rates).[1][5] Programs include state goals plus Christian values, digital tools, part-time English instruction, math/German support, and vocational prep like job training and internships.[3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in movement breaks, diverse sports via clubs and partners, music with school bands and external teachers, excursions (e.g., swimming for grade 3, snow adventures, garden school visits), self-defense courses (grade 6), presentations, and events like summer festivals and prayer gatherings.[4] Extracurriculars cover football, school newspaper, sewing, and workshops; after-school care (Hort) emphasizes consistent discipline and trusting relationships.[4][7] Activities build social-emotional skills, with parent testimonials highlighting patience, emotional support, and community events like performances and charity runs.

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The campus received the **Fair-Family-Gütesiegel** in 2014 for family support; Corrie-ten-Boom-Schule boasts near-100% MSA success rates and first Abitur cohort in 2020.[1][5] Positive parent feedback notes emotional well-being, integration, and strong results versus Berlin averages; child protection concept developed 2023-2024 with Deutsche Liga für das Kind support.[0 PDF][1]

### 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES
Admissions involve applications via the website (e.g., Corrie-ten-Boom-Schule details pedagogical concept and enrollment); requirements include alignment with Christian values, with processes like info sessions implied.[5] As a private Ersatzschule, state funding covers ~2/3 costs, with parents/spenders covering the rest—no specific EUR amounts found on the site or PDFs (0 fee pages).[1] Inquire directly for details, as scraped content lacks tuition data.
