# Schweizerhof-Grundschule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Schweizerhof-Grundschule operates according to the motto: **"Gemeinsam wachsen… Jeder wie er kann… Alle miteinander…"** (Growing together… Each in their own way… All together…)[2]. The school's educational approach emphasizes community living and mutual support while simultaneously encouraging individual development and self-directed learning. Students engage with **Freiarbeitsmaterialien** (free work materials) in every classroom and participate in **FreiDays**, where students independently work on projects[2]. For grades 3-6, the school offers an individualized learning plan on two days per week, allowing children to learn autonomously[2].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Development**

The Schweizerhof-Grundschule was **established in 1962** as a three-form primary school (dreizügige Grundschule) using the Schuster system, including an administrative building and sports hall[1]. The school underwent significant expansion in 1976-1977 with the addition of a two-story building for specialized classes and a second sports hall[1]. Most recently, a new building housing a cafeteria and after-school care facilities was constructed between 2013-2015[1].

**Current Size and Staffing**

The school serves **438 students** with a staff of **36 teachers** and **15 educators (Erzieher*innen)**[2].

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school provides comprehensive facilities including[2]:
- Differentiation rooms (Differenzierungsräume)
- Science room (Nawi-Raum)
- Music room
- Workshop room (Werkraum)
- Auditorium (Aula)
- Media library (Mediothek)
- Computer rooms
- Gymnasium
- Green classroom (grünes Klassenzimmer)

The school also features a **school dog named Otto** as part of the teaching staff[2]. A **support association (Förderverein)** provides additional support to the school[2].

**Recent Construction**

The 2013-2015 expansion replaced a former preschool building with a new structure containing 6 group rooms and auxiliary spaces on the ground floor, plus a cafeteria, after-school care administration, and multipurpose rooms on the upper floor[1]. The new building employs solid construction methods with red clinker brick facades and wood-glass elements, architecturally aligned with existing school buildings[1].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**School Form**

The Schweizerhof-Grundschule operates as a **full-day school in open form** (Ganztagsschule in offener Form)[2].

**Support for Gifted Students**

Two teachers are dedicated to supporting students with special talents and high ability (Förderung von besonderer Begabung und Hochbegabung)[2].

**Learning Support Services**

The school offers **Lernförderung** (learning support), where students can register for additional learning assistance occurring up to four times per week within the school[2].

**Sustainability Focus**

The Schweizerhof-Grundschule is a **certified environmental school in Europe and international sustainability school** (zertifizierte Umweltschule in Europa/internationale Nachhaltigkeitsschule)[2].

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

Students engage in innovative learning formats that emphasize active participation and project-based work. The **FreiDays program** allows students to work independently on projects, with approximately 250 children engaging in project work each Thursday at 10:30 AM[4]. These projects have produced impressive outcomes and include various activities such as cooking groups and collaborative initiatives[4].

The school emphasizes experiential learning through its green classroom and outdoor spaces, supporting environmental education and sustainability practices[2].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The school has achieved **certification as an environmental school in Europe and international sustainability school**, demonstrating commitment to sustainability education[2]. The implementation of innovative learning formats like FreiDays has generated notable student projects and engagement[4].

## 6. ADMISSIONS

No specific admissions information is provided in the available sources. As a public primary school (Grundschule) in Berlin, admissions typically follow standard Berlin public school enrollment procedures, though specific details are not available in the search results.

# Seepark-Grundschule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The **Seepark-Grundschule** operates under the guiding principle: **"Das Kind steht im Mittelpunkt"** (The child is at the center).[1] The school's overarching motto is **"Gesunde Kinder – gesundes Schulklima – gesunde Umwelt"** (Healthy children – healthy school climate – healthy environment).[3]

The pedagogical concept is collaboratively shaped by the entire school community, with students and their guardians actively involved in school development processes.[1] The school emphasizes that students learn not only with and from one another but also support each other, with the focus on each child's individual strengths and diverse talents.[3]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Development**

The Seepark-Grundschule was founded in August 2021 in the Karlshorst district of Berlin-Lichtenberg.[3] The school was officially opened on October 11, 2024, after a construction period of approximately three years.[2][3] The new building is part of the larger Parkstadt Karlshorst development project on a 12-hectare site at Blockdammweg.[1]

**Location and Accessibility**

The school is located at **Blockdammweg 60, 10318 Berlin-Karlshorst**.[1][2] It is accessible via tram at the "Blockdammweg" stop.[10] The building features elevators for barrier-free access.[4]

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school is a **three-track (dreizügige) modular Compartment school** designed to accommodate up to 432 students.[2][4] Key facilities include:

- A three-field sports hall (Dreifelderhalle) adjacent to the building[2][4]
- A cafeteria with kitchen on the ground floor[4]
- WLAN throughout the entire building[4]
- Interactive whiteboards in all classrooms[4]
- Green outdoor spaces (to be completed by end of 2024)[5]
- Photovoltaic panels and rainwater management systems for sustainability[5]

The building employs the **Compartment principle**, which creates flexible learning environments organized as spatial units with smaller, adaptable teaching areas that function like mini-schools.[2] Common facilities and specialized subject rooms are located on the ground floor, while general classrooms and additional subject rooms occupy the three upper floors.[2]

**School Community**

The school currently has 381 students in the 2025/26 school year, with 43.6% having a non-German home language.[2] The teaching staff consists of 20 colleagues working with enthusiasm on building the new school.[1]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Languages**

The school offers **English and French as foreign languages**.[2]

**Elective Programs**

The school offers elective instruction in **Nature and Sustainability** (Wahlpflichtunterricht Natur und Nachhaltigkeit), where students engage in environmental projects and contribute to school displays.[User website content]

**Grade Structure**

The school currently serves grades 1-5, with the following class distribution:

- Three first-grade classes
- Three second-grade classes
- Three third-grade classes
- Two fourth-grade classes
- Two fifth-grade classes[1]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

The school emphasizes inclusive education and flexible learning forms enabled by the Compartment design.[5] Students are actively involved in school development processes and participate in diverse activities including:

- Sports competitions (e.g., Sparki Cup tournament)[User website content]
- Environmental and sustainability projects[User website content]
- Cultural events and performances[3]
- Movement and play opportunities through the sports hall and outdoor facilities[5]

The school's design supports all-day programs (Ganztagsangebote) and inclusive education.[4]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The school was recognized as part of Berlin's School Building Initiative (Berliner Schulbauoffensive) for creating much-needed school capacity in the district.[3] The opening ceremony in October 2024 featured performances by the school choir and student presentations, demonstrating active community engagement.[3][6]

The school's sustainable building practices, including photovoltaic systems and rainwater management, exemplify commitment to environmental responsibility.[5]

## 6. ADMISSIONS

School registrations for the 2026/27 school year occur from October 6-17, 2025.[User website content] The school serves children particularly from the Karlshorster Prinzenviertel and Parkstadt neighborhoods.[User website content]

As a public school in Berlin, the Seepark-Grundschule is tuition-free and operates under public administration.

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Selma-Lagerlöf-Grundschule emphasizes creating a welcoming environment where learning is joyful, fostering respect, trust, tolerance, and individual support for all students.[1][3][5] As a reliable half-day primary school with open all-day operations, it prioritizes a positive learning atmosphere, innovative pedagogy, creative and organized instruction, equal opportunities, and individualized learning guidance, particularly as a new **Startchancenschule** providing targeted resources to develop talents and inclusive pathways.[1][3][4][5]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in northern Marzahn (Berlin-Marzahn-Hellersdorf), a quieter area with many panel buildings and socially disadvantaged families, the school serves over 400 students (approximately 425), with 32% from non-German speaking homes.[1][3][4][5] It features a school building, gym, large schoolyard used for recreation and movement, and a school garden with raised beds, shrubs, and trees; it is one of Berlin's first chalk-free schools with focuses on media and environmental education.[1][3] The school has evolved into an inclusive primary since 2012, starting integration from grade 3, supported by 32 teachers (25 female, 7 male), 17 educators in open all-day care (OGB/hort), and partnerships like the "Schulstation Selma" (open 7:30-16:00, financed by the district).[1][3][4][5] Leadership includes Schulleiterin Heike Kaden.[4][5][6]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
This three-track primary school (grades 1-6) offers solid learning in a rhythmized half-day schedule (7:30-13:30), with optional all-day OGB/hort program for grades 1-4 (195 students enrolled as of 2018), English as the first foreign language, a welcome class for new arrivals without German skills, year-spanning learning groups in early phases, and inclusive education.[3][4][5] Special emphases include media and environmental education, with 18 regular classes plus one welcome class; it participates in inclusion trials and provides individualized support.[1][3][4]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience a child-centered, self-determined learning environment with geborgenheit (security), modern and creative approaches, and versatile after-school programs including hort activities.[1][3][5][6] The school promotes curiosity through innovative methods, afternoon care, parental cooperation, and events like open days (e.g., October 2), Christmas markets, autumn festivals, and music boxes, alongside a school newspaper (Selma Post) and gallery impressions.[1][5][6] Open all-day care includes AGs (activity groups) and eFöB weekly plans.[5][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school is recognized as an early adopter of chalk-free teaching and has transitioned to inclusive operations since 2012, now designated as a **Startchancenschule** for enhanced support in a socially challenging area.[1][3][4] It maintains a young, dedicated team fostering a welcoming climate, with additional district resources via Schulstation Selma since 2002.[1][3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
School enrollment requires scheduling an appointment by phone or email with the secretariat and bringing a completed form from the Downloads section; Kitagruppen (daycare groups) must pre-register for open day events.[5][6] It offers lerngruppen for newcomers without German knowledge.[4]

# Siegerland-Grundschule: Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Siegerland-Grundschule is committed to creating a **welcoming learning and living environment** where children feel secure and are supported according to their individual strengths.[2] The school places children's diverse needs, prerequisites, and abilities at the center of its work, employing differentiated methods and tailored offerings to ensure equitable education.[2]

A cornerstone of the school's philosophy is **social development and intercultural understanding**. Every class participates in weekly social learning sessions designed to strengthen children's interpersonal skills and foster acceptance of the diverse cultures represented in the school community.[1][2] The school emphasizes non-violent learning practices and views itself as both a place of learning and living where children can thrive holistically.[2]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Growth**

The Siegerland-Grundschule was **established in 1965** in the Falkenhagener Feld development area in Berlin's Spandau district.[1] The school has grown to serve approximately **560 students** as of 2024, supported by 40 teachers, 32 educators, and three social workers.[1][2]

**Facilities and Infrastructure**

The school offers comprehensive facilities designed to support all-day learning:[1]

- Cafeteria with distribution kitchen
- All-day and leisure spaces
- Schoolyard landscape
- Two computer rooms and media corners
- Smartboards in all classrooms
- Workshop and technology room
- Pedagogical teaching kitchen
- Gymnasium
- Green classroom and school garden
- Streetball court
- Accessible facilities including wheelchair-accessible elevator, accessible restrooms, and parking for people with disabilities

**Community Partnerships**

The primary cooperation partner is **Fipp e.V.**, which develops conceptual priorities in coordination with school leadership to enable meaningful all-day care.[1] The school also maintains close partnerships with the Klubhaus and Jugendtheaterwerkstatt Spandau (Youth Theater Workshop).[1] Additional external partners include Siemens AG, the Association of Berlin Merchants and Industrialists (VBKI), and a sister school partnership in Siegen.[5]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Language and Digital Education**

English is the primary foreign language offered.[5] The school emphasizes **digital competency development**, with IT hours integrated into the curriculum for grades 5 and 6 to prepare students for secondary education.[2] All classrooms are equipped with smartboards, and the school maintains two well-equipped computer rooms used regularly by all classes.[2]

**Interdisciplinary Learning**

The school integrates various subjects through project-based and thematic approaches. A **green classroom and school garden** provide hands-on learning opportunities, while the pedagogical teaching kitchen supports practical education.[1]

**Special Programs**

- **Theater and Dance**: Grades 5 and 6 collaborate with the Jugendtheaterwerkstatt annually to create theatrical productions or dance theater pieces.[1]
- **eEducation and eTwinning**: The school participates in eEducation partnership programs and eTwinning projects for international collaboration.[5]
- **Early Support Program (Früh-Förderband)**: Specialized early intervention support is available.[5]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**All-Day Structure**

The Siegerland-Grundschule operates as a **bound all-day school (gebundene Ganztagsgrundschule)**, with instruction running from 8:00 AM to 4:00 PM.[1] The daily schedule is rhythmicized, alternating between formal instruction and extracurricular activities, projects, and integrated lunch periods.[1]

**Sports and Physical Activity**

The school prioritizes athletic engagement through:[1]

- Annual sponsor run (Sponsoren-Lauf)
- Annual volleyball tournament (Völkerball-Turnier)
- Traditional Kiesteich run

**Student Voice and Participation**

The school operates a **student parliament (Schülerparlament)** that gives students a voice in school governance and decision-making.[5]

**Professional Development Culture**

Teachers engage in continuous professional development through school-wide training concepts, internal professional learning sessions, and dedicated study days where the entire pedagogical team addresses common themes.[1]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The school celebrated its **60th anniversary in September 2025** and marked **20 years of partnership with Fipp e.V.**[9] The school has been recognized as an **eEducation partner school**, indicating commitment to digital innovation in education.[5]

The school's focus on social-emotional learning and inclusive practices reflects its commitment to developing well-rounded citizens capable of navigating diverse communities. Recent activities, such as hosting notable community figures for reading events (including the district mayor and Olympic athlete Ronald Rauhe), demonstrate the school's integration into the broader Berlin community.[1]

## 6. ADMISSIONS

The search results provided do not contain specific information about admissions procedures, enrollment timelines, or application requirements. For admissions details, prospective families should contact the school directly at **030/3789110** or **sekretariat@siegerland-schule.de**.[3][4]

**Contact Information**

|--------|-------------|
| Address | Hermann-Schmidt-Weg 4, 13589 Berlin |
| Phone | +49 30 3789110 |
| Fax | +49 30 378911111 |
| Email | sekretariat@siegerland-schule.de |
| School Principal | Sandra Schweigert |
| Deputy Principal | Herr Zacholowsky |
| Website | www.siegerland-schule.de |

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Silberstein-Schule emphasizes a **künstlerisch-sprachliches Profil** (artistic-linguistic profile), with additional sportlich-kreatives elements, fostering a partnerschaftlich, gewaltfrei, and respektvoll atmosphere.[1][2][8] Key focuses include Lesekompetenz (reading competence), Gewaltprävention (violence prevention) via Konfliktlotsenausbildung and Mediation, and social learning in a multikulturelle setting.[1][2][3][5][6] The school's Leitbild promotes positive interactions among all persons.[2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded on January 5, 1891, as the third school in Rixdorf, it was renamed in 1967 after Dr. Raphael Silberstein, a socially engaged physician advancing child health and school medical care.[1] Located in Berlin-Neukölln (a challenging area with diverse languages like Arabic, Turkish, Serbian, Russian), it serves a multikulturelle community as a gebundene Ganztagsschule with care from 7:30-16:00 (extendable 6:00-7:30 and 16:00-18:00, including holidays).[1][3][4][7] Facilities include a computerraum, Fahrradwerkstatt, and partnerships with Kleiner Fratz e.V., Vielfalt e.V. (Schulstation), VBKI, and VHS for extended care and support.[1][2][3][6] Leadership: Schulleiterin Frau I. Evdokimova; Stellvertretende tba.[9] Contact: Tel. 030 62008922, E-Mail sekretariat@silberstein.schule.berlin.de or info@silberstein-schule.de.[1][3][8]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a public Grundschule (08G21), it offers Englisch from 3. Klasse, herkunftssprachlicher Unterricht (Turkish, Arabic), Schulanfangsphase with jahrgangsübergreifenden Lerngruppen and 3 hours musisch-ästhetischem Unterricht (music, visual arts, drama), Lesepaten, and interne Lernförderung.[1][2][3] Profiles: kunstbetontes and musikbetontes, with offene Ganztagbetreuung (OGB) and Schulstation.[3] Gewaltprävention program since 08.2023 via OPTIMUS gGmbH/Vielfalt e.V.[5][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students access 70+ Arbeitsgemeinschaften (AGs) like Keramik, Kunst, Musical, Fußball, Medien, Tanz, Tischtennis, Rollschuh, Fahrrad-Werkstatt, Back-AG, Film-AG, Chor, Taekwondo, Mädchen/Jungengruppen, Schmuck, Basteln, Hausaufgabenhilfe, and Spiel & Spaß.[1][2][3][4][6] Betreuung by Erzieher*innen, Integrationserzieher*innen, Schulsozialarbeiter*innen follows Ganztagsschulkonzept; includes sozialpädagogische Gruppen, Beratung, Krisenintervention, interkulturelle Elternarbeit.[1][6] Turnaround-Programm addresses past issues like Unterrichtsausfall and high Abbruchquoten with new rules.[4]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Participating in Turnaround-Programm for three years (as of ~2020), showing improvements like renovated facilities and structured activities amid challenges in a Brennpunkt area.[4] No specific Prüfungsergebnisse or outcome metrics detailed; management listed as N.N.[3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a tuition-free public Grundschule, admissions follow Berlin public school procedures (not detailed in sources).[3] Extended care (e.g., 6:00-18:00, holidays) available upon parental request.[1]

# SIS Swiss International School Berlin - Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

**Core Mission:** SIS Berlin operates as a **bilingual private school** offering continuous education from kindergarten through secondary school with an international orientation.[1] The school's educational concept centers on developing students into **cosmopolitan, independent, and respectful individuals** who learn with joy and confidence.[1]

**Key Educational Principles:**
- **Bilingual Foundation:** English and German serve as **equal roles in school communication**—both in the classroom and throughout daily school life, enabling children to express themselves naturally and confidently in both languages.[3]
- **Student-Centered Learning:** Students are placed at the center of all educational efforts, with teachers dedicated to making learning a positive and rewarding experience.[1]
- **International Perspective with Local Anchoring:** The school combines international educational standards with local German curriculum requirements.[3]
- **Technology-Enhanced Learning:** Technology is integrated across all subjects with a pedagogical framework supporting digital literacy, programming, media communication, and e-safety from kindergarten onwards.[4]
- **Values-Based Education:** The school emphasizes independence, respect, and ethical orientation in its pedagogical approach.[4]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Development:**
SIS Berlin was established in **2016** when SIS took over Internationale Schule Villa Amalienhof, which continues operating as SIS Berlin.[4] The school is part of the **SIS Swiss International Schools Group**, a network operating since 1999 in Switzerland, Germany, Brazil, and Italy.[5]

**Location and Contact:**
- **Address:** Heerstraße 465, 13593 Berlin, Germany (school entrance at Reimerweg 11)[1]
- **Phone:** +49 30 3643 9820[1]
- **Email:** info.berlin@swissinternationalschool.de[1]
- **Office Hours:** Monday to Friday, 8 a.m. to 3 p.m.[1]

**Community Scale:**
- SIS Berlin serves approximately **250 students** across all levels.[5]
- The broader SIS network encompasses **20 schools worldwide** with over **4,844 students** representing **102 nationalities** and employing **1,070 staff members**.[4]

**School Status:**
The primary school and secondary school (Years 7-10) are **state-recognised private schools**, equal to state schools in all aspects.[1] The secondary school (Years 11-13) is currently a **state-approved private school**.[1] Students automatically meet German compulsory school attendance requirements.[1]

**Ownership and Governance:**
SIS is a company of the **Klett Group** (Germany) in joint venture with Kalaidos Education Group (Switzerland).[4] The school is financed by a combination of **school fees and state subsidies**.[1]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Levels and Structure:**
SIS Berlin offers a **unified learning concept spanning kindergarten through secondary school**, enabling seamless transitions across educational stages:[1][4]

- **Kindergarten** (ages 3-5)
- **Primary School/Grundschule** (Years 1-6)
- **Secondary School/Sekundarschule** (Years 7-10)
- **Upper Secondary/Gymnasiale Oberstufe** (Years 11-13)

**Languages of Instruction:**
- **Bilingual curriculum** with English and German as equal working languages[3]
- **German language arts** (Deutsch A LAL - Language and Literature)[2]
- **English language arts** (English A LAL - Language and Literature)[2]
- **Spanish** (Spanish AB - Language B)[2]
- **Hebrew** (Hebrew A LIT - Language and Literature)[2]
- **Filipino** (Filipino A LIT - Language and Literature)[2]

**Graduation Pathways:**
- **German Abitur** (university entrance qualification)[5]
- **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)** - authorized since March 9, 2016[2]

**IB Curriculum Subjects Offered:**
Students are registered for IB subjects including Math Analysis, Biology, Spanish AB, Visual Arts, English A LAL, German A LAL, History, Theory of Knowledge, Hebrew A LIT, and Filipino A LIT.[2]

**All-Day Program:**
The school operates as a **full-day school** (Ganztagsschule) with integrated afternoon offerings.[1][4]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Extracurricular and Enrichment Activities:**
- **English Language Clubs:** Extra training sessions to improve English proficiency[3]
- **Student Exchange Programmes:** International exchange opportunities for students[4]
- **Technology Integration:** Hands-on exploration of digital technology, programming, and media literacy from kindergarten onwards[4]
- **Vibrant School Community:** The school emphasizes a dynamic, inclusive community environment where students feel respected and heard[3]

**Learning Environment:**
Parents report that the school creates an environment where **children are excited to attend school** and maintain happiness even after full days, reflecting strong student engagement and positive school culture.[3]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**Accreditations and Certifications:**
- **IB World School Status:** Authorized to offer the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme since March 9, 2016[2]
- **State Recognition:** Primary and secondary schools (Years 7-10) are state-recognised private schools with full equivalence to state schools[1]
- **International Benchmarking:** All SIS schools periodically participate in the **International Schools' Assessment (ISA)**, an international benchmarking test[4]
- **Quality Assurance:** SIS guarantees high educational and quality standards with continuous evaluation and development of structures and processes[4]

**Sustainability Commitment:**
SIS committed to **climate neutrality by 2029**, with student-led sustainability projects embedded across all school locations.[4]

## 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES

**Enrollment Process:**
The school maintains an active admissions process with regular **open house days and information evenings** (Tage der offenen Tür und Informationsabende).[4] Prospective families can register for these events through the school website.

**Partial Scholarships:**
SIS Berlin offers **partial scholarships** (Teilstipendien) for primary school and secondary school attendance under certain conditions, recognizing the importance of making bilingual education accessible to diverse families.[4]

**Fee Information:**
Unfortunately, the specific tuition fees for SIS Berlin are **not detailed in the available search results**. While the school website includes a "Preisliste" (price list) section for SIS Berlin, the actual fee amounts were not accessible in the provided content. Fee information for other SIS locations (such as Stuttgart-Fellbach) indicates that schools offer tiered pricing based on school level (kindergarten, primary, secondary) and care arrangements (half-day vs. full-day), with income-based adjustments available.[4]

**Financing Structure:**
The school is financed through a combination of **parental contributions (school fees) and state subsidies**, making it a hybrid-funded private institution.[1]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Sonnenblumen-Grundschule aims to foster optimism, joy, and practical knowledge in students, drawing inspiration from sunflowers as symbols of cheerfulness and utility, ensuring school life promotes both emotional well-being and skill acquisition.[6][1] As a **gebundene Ganztagsschule** (bound all-day school), it emphasizes social competence among students, teachers, and parents; professional collaboration between educators like teachers and childcare workers; and partnerships with external entities to develop versatile, reality-oriented competencies for real-life demands.[3] Learning permeates all activities, with a focus on quality through school-specific concepts, a rhythmized daily schedule optimizing instruction, and free-time offers that reinforce or extend competencies beyond formal lessons.[3][6]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located in Berlin's **Treptow-Köpenick** district (Ortsteil Baumschulenweg), the school serves approximately 450 students from a 1930s residential area of single- and multi-family homes, with most drawn from the local catchment area.[4][7][1] It features a **virtual school tour (Rundgang)**, library (recently reopened with volunteer help), school meals (Schulspeisung), and spaces for rhythmized daily routines including early and late after-school care (Früh- und Späthort).[6][2] Community involvement includes a parents' association (Förderverein) funding extras like equipment and activities (e.g., badminton, hikes, game afternoons), school social work since 2021/22, regular prevention programs, and musical initiatives as a **Musikalische Grundschule**.[5][7][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum supports a full-day structure with mandatory attendance from 08:00 to 16:00, including warm communal lunches, performance evaluations across subjects, elective courses (AGs und Kurse), musical programs, and transitions to secondary schools.[6][8][1] Key elements include rhythmization for optimal learning conditions, supplementary support, library access, and concepts like Leitbild (mission statement) emphasizing competence bundling in social, methodological, self, and factual areas.[3][6] Some areas (e.g., detailed programs) are password-protected.[Scraped Content]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students experience an all-day rhythm blending instruction, free time, and activities like music refreshers, prevention workshops, and library reading to promote continuous learning in relaxed yet competency-focused settings.[6][3] Offerings include team-taught environments, social work support, partnerships, parental involvement via Schul-ABC resources, and communal meals, all fostering optimism, cooperation, and practical skills.[6][7][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
No specific achievements, test scores, or outcomes are detailed in available content; focus remains on qualitative goals like high-quality learning environments and community engagement, such as successful library reopening with volunteers.[6] Proximity to nearby schools (e.g., Gymnasium Baumschulenweg at 0.8 km) supports transitions.[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public Grundschule, enrollment is mandatory for catchment-area children, with applications for early admission (births Oct 2020–Mar 2021) requiring forms, power of attorney, and school medical checks via Jugendgesundheitsdienst.[8] Key dates include an info event on 25.09.2025 at 16:15 in the Mensa; registration from 01.08.2026; even for private/ersatz school requests, initial enrollment here is required, with decisions by late May.[8] Safe school route plans are provided.[9]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Sonnen-Grundschule emphasizes an inclusive, community-oriented environment where students live, learn, and work together regardless of gender, disability, skin color, ethnic origin, religion, age, or sexual orientation. Core values include **participation, courage, diversity, and tolerance**, fostering an open and considerate atmosphere that allows every individual to contribute their strengths.[3][4][10] The school's philosophy prioritizes boosting **learning joy**, acquiring essential competencies per the Berlin framework curriculum, cross-disciplinary language support, social education for tolerance and democracy, and independent learning.[3][6][8]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Established with naming on July 7, 1960, in Berlin's Neukölln district (Planetenvierfel), the school was initially 2-zügig with 463 students across 13 classes (about 35 per class); it has grown to support up to 576 students through a recent modular extension building (MEB) inaugurated to address rising enrollment from 355 students.[2][5] Located at **Dammweg 228, 12057 Berlin**, it serves a catchment area between Sonnenallee high-rises, idyllic row houses under the S-Bahn ring, and the former Wall strip at Heidekampgraben, primarily drawing students from the Weiße Siedlung around Diesel- and Aronsstraße.[4] The community is diverse, with 20 languages spoken, many students from migrant or refugee backgrounds, and 33% facing poverty; it features strong neighborhood ties via Kiez-AG and family events.[5][8] Facilities include a new 3-story MEB with **16 classrooms (60m² each), 10 group rooms (25m²), teacher areas, 170m² mensa, and 40m² kitchen**; profiles highlight open all-day care (OGB), health-focused and sports-oriented programs, environmental education, and year-group learning groups in early phases.[2][6]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum follows the **Berlin Rahmenlehrplan**, with targeted support like Deutsch in 2nd grade, Mathematics in 5th grade, IQ-testing as needed (1st-6th), and special needs/inclusive education.[3][6] English starts in 3rd grade; programs emphasize motor skills via crafts, visual arts, play, silence/meditation, and fächerübergreifende language promotion.[3][5] Profiles include **health-oriented, sports-focused, environmental education**, and open Ganztagbetreuung; historical innovations feature integration classes, Montessori training, open teaching, violence prevention via mediation, and ETEP training for strength-based development.[5][6]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in hands-on, thematic projects via the **Koffer Akademie**, such as archaeology digs, animal tracking in snow, museum workshops, seasonal explorations (autumn, spring), fairy tales with Brothers Grimm, Tutanchamun's tomb, ancient Greece (Odysseus), local tours (Kreuzberg, Berlin-Mitte), Stone Age simulations, bee hotels, and library orientations.[4] Recent initiatives include **Schüler*innenHaushalt Berlin 2025** for student budget decisions promoting democracy.[4] Extracurriculars cover project weeks, class trips, school festivals, and all-day options with homework help, playrooms, creative workshops, sports, and AGs like basteln, cooking, music.[2][5][10] School social work fosters a positive atmosphere for social competence.[10]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school expanded to 4-zügig capacity via MEB for growing enrollment, enhancing learning motivation with modern spaces.[2] It integrates democracy projects like student budgets and maintains neighborhood engagement (e.g., traffic lights, family festivals).[4][5] Challenges include low Gymnasium transition rates (25% vs. Berlin average) amid diversity and poverty, yet it prioritizes individualized support and Lernfreude.[8] No specific test scores or awards detailed; focus remains on inclusive growth and community vitality.[3]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **public Grundschule (tuition-free)**, admission is based on the local catchment area (Einzugsgebiet) in Neukölln's southern Sonnenallee/Weiße Siedlung; no fees or application processes mentioned, aligning with Berlin public school enrollment for ages ~6-10 (1st-6th grade).[1][4] Open Ganztagbetreuung available; contact via sekretariat@sonnen.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 689750-90.[6]

# Sonnenuhr-Schule (Grundschule) - Comprehensive School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Sonnenuhr-Schule is a **public primary school** located in Berlin-Lichtenberg that emphasizes creating a **calm, supportive learning environment** through both acoustic improvements and pedagogical innovation[8]. The school prioritizes **language development**, offering English as a contact language from Grade 1, with a language-focused profile that prepares students for bilingual instruction in secondary education[1][2]. The school's approach centers on developing students' emotional and cognitive capabilities through structured support systems, including the "Schulinsel" (school island) social work program that provides confidential counseling and conflict resolution assistance[5].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Development**

The school was **established in 1975** and was officially registered as the 2nd primary school in Berlin-Lichtenberg in 1990/91[2]. In June 2022, all classrooms received new electronic whiteboards[2].

**Current Enrollment and Staffing**

The school serves approximately **390 students** across grades 1-6, supported by **30 teachers** plus two life skills educators and additional support staff[2].

**Facilities and Resources**

The school provides comprehensive facilities including[1][2]:
- Library with connections to the Anton-Saefkow-Platz library and Theater an der Parkaue
- Computer room with internet access
- Teaching kitchen (Schülerküche)
- Wheelchair-accessible restrooms
- Playground in a green setting
- Dedicated spaces: play room, creative workshop, experimentation workshop, gymnasium, construction room, and student horticultural areas

**Notable Infrastructure Project**

Through the "Leise Schule" (Quiet School) project initiated in 2012/2013, the school's parent association successfully completed acoustic renovation of 20 of 28 teaching rooms, improving speech intelligibility and creating healthier learning conditions[8].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Language Education**

English instruction begins in Grade 1 as a contact language, incorporating singing, playing, crafting, and skill development in counting, colors, and motor coordination[2]. The school offers additional language courses including Spanish and Russian through working groups[1].

**Specialized Programs**

- **Language-focused profile** with preparation for bilingual secondary education[1]
- **School starting phase** (Schulanfangsphase) with age-grouped learning classes[1]
- **Open all-day care** (Offene Ganztagbetreuung - OGB)[1]
- **Music education partnership** with Schostakowitsch-Musikschule, providing one hour weekly instruction in grades 1-2, supported by the Lichtenberg district's Cultural Education Initiative[2]
- **Guitar and piano courses** available for grades 2-6[2]

**Extracurricular Activities**

The school offers diverse working groups (Arbeitsgemeinschaften) including[1][2]:
- **Sports**: Football (girls and boys), karate, judo, table tennis, swimming
- **Arts and Culture**: Drawing, pottery, dancing, school musical, circus activities
- **Academic**: English course, young naturalists, computer club, Spanish, Russian, school newspaper ("Sunshine")
- **Special Programs**: Conflict mediation training (HHT - Streitschlichter)

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Afternoon Care and Recreation**

After completing homework, students choose from various activities including use of the play room, creative workshop, experimentation workshop, gymnasium, construction room, library, student kitchen, playground, and hort rooms[2]. Regular working groups in sports, crafting, cooking, music, dancing, and computer skills occur throughout the week, with diverse offerings during school holidays[2].

**Social and Emotional Support**

The "Schulinsel" provides a welcoming space where students can address conflicts, feelings of isolation, anger, or home difficulties. The program emphasizes collaborative problem-solving to strengthen emotional and cognitive development while maintaining confidentiality[5].

**Community Partnerships**

The school maintains partnerships with[2]:
- Local sports clubs (Lichtenberger FFC 09 e.V., Sportverein Lichtenberg 47 e.V., SV Tora Berlin e.V., PSV Olympia Berlin e.V., Schwimmverein Berolina e.V.)
- 1. FC Union Berlin (official partner school)[2]
- Theater an der Parkaue
- Anton-Saefkow-Platz library

**Participation in Competitions and Projects**

Students engage in diverse competitions in sports and arts, participate in "Newspaper in School" (Zeitung in der Schule), the "Berlin Fairy Tale Book" project, and paper collection competitions ("Berlin Seeks the Super Collector")[2].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The school demonstrates commitment to **continuous improvement** through its "Leise Schule" project, which has improved acoustic conditions and created a foundation for better learning outcomes and reduced conflicts[8]. The school's **language-focused profile** and bilingual preparation program position students for advanced language study in secondary education[1][2].

The school's **partnership with 1. FC Union Berlin** reflects recognition of its educational quality and community standing[2]. Participation in regional competitions and cultural projects demonstrates student achievement across multiple domains[2].

## 6. ADMISSIONS

**Registration Process**

New student registrations occur annually from **October 9-20**, with appointment scheduling available by phone beginning September 18[2]. The school follows Berlin's standard primary school enrollment procedures.

**Enrollment Information**

- **School Type**: Public primary school (Grundschule)
- **Grades**: 1-6
- **Current Capacity**: Approximately 390 students
- **Language of Instruction**: German (with English from Grade 1)

**After-School Care**

Extended afternoon care (EFöB) is available and **tuition-free for Grade 3 students** as of the current school year[2]. Contracts must be completed for participation. Meal ordering is available through the Z-Catering platform[2].

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Spartacus-Grundschule is a **sport-focused primary school** (Grundschule) emphasizing lifelong activity in learning, movement, play, and sports, with students taught in year-group homogeneous classes.[1][3][4] The school fosters a joyful daily experience, individual identification with the school community, and collaborative shaping of school life with parents in an atmosphere of politeness and mutual respect, supported by attentive educators addressing student needs.[1][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located at Friedenstr. 40-45, 10249 Berlin in the Friedrichshain-Kreuzberg district, south of Volkspark Friedrichshain, the public school serves around 300 students with 19 educators, plus social pedagogues and workers from Stützrad e.V.[1][4][6] Facilities include a modern gymnasium, nearby parks and playgrounds, integrated sports hall, digital whiteboards, WLAN, and planned outdoor sports area; it collaborates with Berlin sports clubs (e.g., ALBA, EBT), other schools (e.g., Lasker-Oberschule, 02K06, 02Y01), and kindergartens.[1][3][4][6][7] Recent developments feature a new building with barrier-free access, artistic elements, and sports spaces.[7]

### 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
**Year-group homogeneous instruction** includes English from grade 1, sports profile with extra lessons (1 hour/week for grades 1-2, 3 hours/week for grades 3-6 beyond regular PE), and elective courses from grade 5 offering sports and other subjects.[1][3][4] Programs feature open all-day operation (Mo-Fr 6:00-18:00), after-school activities (e.g., judo, ceramics, football, handball, chess, basketball, volleyball, English for aftercare), art education, and schulanfangsphase with year-specific learning groups.[3][4] Schedule from 2024/25: 8 periods from 08:00-15:20, with breaks and lunch.[1]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in active daily routines with extra sports (2 hours/week beyond regular), creative projects, team-building through sports, and personalized support in a reliable half-day structure with open all-day options.[1][3][4] Emphasis on fairness, responsibility, community, creativity, and interest-based electives promotes holistic development, social learning via school social work, and talent nurturing through club partnerships.[3][4][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Strengths include diverse sports offerings, club/Kindergarten cooperations, social learning support, and reliable organization; many sports-profile students advance to Berlin elite sports schools post-grade 4/6.[6] Recent event: Cross-Lauf success on 09.10.2024.[1] Inspection highlights favorable conditions for sports talents.[6]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public Grundschule**, admissions follow Berlin public school processes (e.g., via berlin.de Schulanmeldung); no specific fees or private requirements noted.[1][4]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Spreewald-Grundschule emphasizes a **theater-focused profile** recognized by the Berlin Senate School Administration since August 2002, viewing multilingualism as a resource and opportunity in a diverse community.[1][2][3] The school fosters belief in its students' potential within a welcoming, multicultural environment that welcomes visitors in multiple languages and invites hospitation.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Established as a full-day elementary school (Ganztagsgrundschule) since March 1, 2004, in a modernized building designed by architect Hinrich Baller, featuring a dedicated theater room and the distinctive "Baller-Bau" leisure house with gym, fully operational since September 2020.[1][2][4][5] Located in Berlin-Schöneberg's diverse Winterfeldtplatz neighborhood (a "Kiez" with high migration background, around 90% of students), it has a unique green schoolyard with play and sports equipment; facilities include library, computer room, kitchen, school cafe, garden, pond, pottery room, and wheelchair-accessible features like elevators and ramps.[1][2][3][4][6] The site has faced challenges, including prolonged legal disputes over the gym since 1989, roof issues, and security concerns leading to private guards funded via bonus programs.[5][8]

### 3. CURRICRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
Offers bound full-day operations (gebundener Ganztagsbetrieb) or open full-day care (offener Ganztag), with theater emphasis including regular projects, early English from grade 1, life skills, religious instruction (Catholic/Evangelical, Islamic), bilingual German-Turkish literacy, and learning groups for new arrivals without German skills.[1][2][3] Extracurricular activities (AGs) cover music studio, crafting, dance, yoga, sports (football, basketball, table tennis), arts, upcycling, computers, library, games, and international themes like world travel or Europe.[1][3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in a vibrant, inclusive setting with theater, sports, arts, and multilingual support tailored to a high-needs community (90% migration background, fee exemptions for learning materials).[1][3][8] Experiences include class-specific lunch rooms, unique outdoor play, and cooperative projects with nearby schools; challenges like violence have prompted security measures.[3][5][8]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Recognized as a **theater-emphasized school** by Berlin authorities since 2002, with modern facilities renovated and reopened post-2020; noted for architectural significance and community integration despite socioeconomic challenges.[1][2][3][4][7] No specific academic outcome data available in sources.

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public Grundschule, admission follows Berlin's local enrollment processes; supports newcomers via language learning groups and open full-day options; contact via sekretariat@spreewald.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 902777151.[3]

### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Stechlinsee-Grundschule emphasizes a child-centered approach, fostering democratic values, musical education, and exploratory learning in a supportive ganztag (all-day) environment.[1][3] Key focuses include **Demokratiebildung** with weekly Klassenräte (class councils) and a Schülerparlament (student parliament), participation in the "Demokratie und Diversity" project since 2018/2019, and certification as a "Haus der kleinen Forscher" since 2018 to promote scientific curiosity and independent thinking.[1][2][3]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1906 and named after Brandenburg's deepest lake (Stechlinsee), the school is located in Berlin-Friedenau (Tempelhof-Schöneberg district) and serves as a public ganztagsgrundschule with about **640 students** in **27 classes** (including a Willkommensklasse) for the 2024/25 school year.[3][4] It has been one of 14 **musikbetonte** (music-emphasized) Berlin primary schools since 1995/96, with around 400 children engaging in musical offerings.[3][7] Facilities include specialized rooms for building, media, sciences, movement, reading, theater, music, and sensory activities; a large garden; fresh on-site meals; computer rooms; digital blackboards in all classrooms (kreidefreie Schule); and after-school care via KAH (six Schülerläden) and Pestalozzi-Fröbel-Haus (PFH).[1][2][3] Leadership: Schulleiter Herr Kiefer (currently a.D.), 1. Konrektor Herr Siebert, 2. Konrektorin Frau Pergande.[6] Community involvement includes a Förderverein (parents' support association) with events like charity drives (e.g., €447.70 raised for World Toilet Day 2025) and a student newspaper.[7]

### 3. CURRICULM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **Grundschule** (primary school, grades 1-6), instruction occurs in **jahrgangshomogene** (age-homogeneous) classes with **foreign language options**: English or French starting in grade 3 (switchable upon transition to Sekundarstufe I).[7][4] Core programs feature **musikbetonung** with AGs (working groups) like choir, ensembles (blockflöte, flute, brass), instruments (piano, guitar, violin), pop band, and dance theater; **medienbildung** with internet license courses in grade 3 and computer rooms; **mobilitätsbildung** including police-led traffic education (grades 1-3), bike exams (grade 4), and BUND "Zu Fuß zur Schule" since 2020; plus **Hochbegabtenförderung** (gifted support) and offene Ganztagbetreuung (OGB).[1][2][4] Daily schedule starts at 07:25 (0. Stunde) with lessons until 11:45+ and breaks.[3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in hands-on projects like "Die Forscherwerkstatt" for science exploration, self-directed afternoons in ganztag care with homework support, musical performances (e.g., Weihnachtskonzerte 2025), sports AGs (football, basketball), and democratic participation via councils.[1][7] Ganztag offers meal choices via Z-Catering (separate registration for non-hort students), a student newspaper, and events like excursions to Stechlinsee.[1][3][5][7] Digital tools and traffic safety training enhance practical skills.[2]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Certified as "Haus der kleinen Forscher" (2018) for STEM promotion; ongoing "Demokratie und Diversity" project participant; successful community fundraisers (e.g., €447.70 for World Toilet Day); active student media and events like Christmas concerts and school newspaper editions.[1][2][7] Official portraits note positive management, professionalität, and offerings in music, languages, and OGB, with prüfungsergebnisse available via Berlin.de.[4]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
Public **tuition-free** Grundschule with no open house in the current year due to organizational reasons; standard Berlin primary enrollment processes apply (local district priority).[7][4] Contact: sekretariat@stechlinsee.schule.berlin.de, Tel: +49 30 902777908; online krankmeldung and ausfall-plan available.[4][6] Ganztag/hort via KAH/PFH; meals via Z-Catering.[1][5]

# Stephanus-Grundschule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The **Stephanus-Grundschule is an evangelical Protestant primary school** operated by the Stephanus-Stiftung (Stephanus Foundation), a diaconal organization committed to social responsibility and inclusive community building.[1] The school's mission centers on **diversity, inclusion, and reformist pedagogy**.

**Core Values:**
The school embraces a **holistic learning philosophy** where "head, heart, and hand" serve as the primary teaching tools, enabling each child to grasp, experience, and understand concepts in their own way.[2] The school welcomes families of all faiths, including non-religious families, viewing differences in origin, culture, and religion as enriching the school community.[1] Key values include trust, tolerance, responsibility, openness, respect, and appreciation for each child's unique development.[1]

**Pedagogical Approach:**
The school implements **reformist pedagogy** with emphasis on:
- **Prepared environment and free work** (Freiarbeit) with individual support[2]
- **Class-based learning** where students acquire diverse methods through age-appropriate offerings[2]
- **Cross-grade project instruction and weekly planning work** as reformist elements embedded across all classes[2]
- **Open lesson beginnings** connected to daily free work periods[2]
- **Mixed-age learning opportunities** during free work phases, allowing peer learning[2]

The school creates a **trusting and inspiring environment** where children are encouraged to shape their school community, learn to listen to one another, and resolve conflicts peacefully.[2]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Founding and Growth:**
The evangelical Stephanus-Grundschule was **established in the 2017/18 school year** by the Stephanus-Stiftung as its operator.[1] As of the 2024/25 school year, approximately **273 children from grades 1-6** attend the school.[1]

**Location and Community:**
The school is located at **Parkstraße 22, 13086 Berlin** in the Weißensee district (Lichtenberg borough).[1][5] The school benefits from its position on the **Stephanus-Stiftung campus**, which provides numerous learning opportunities and resources.[2] The school actively engages with the local Weißensee community, maintains partnerships with local churches, and welcomes people of diverse social, cultural, and national backgrounds.[2]

**Accessibility:**
The school is designed as a **barrier-reduced primary school** (barrierearme Grundschule), open to all families seeking a denominational, open all-day school with reformist pedagogy.[1]

**Facilities:**
- Each class has its own classroom, but the entire facility (hallways, stairwells, outdoor grounds) is utilized for learning[3]
- **Learning islands** throughout the school invite students to settle and work[3]
- Multiple **themed rooms** covering all educational areas[3]
- Access to the Stephanus-Stiftung campus facilities

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Grade Levels:**
The school serves **grades 1-6** (primary education).[1]

**Pedagogical Structure:**
- **Age-appropriate learning offerings** designed to optimize learning processes[2]
- **Free work periods** integrated with open lesson beginnings[2]
- **Class-based instruction** combined with cross-grade project work[2]
- **Weekly planning work** as a reformist pedagogical element[2]

**All-Day School Model:**
The school operates as an **open all-day school** (offene Ganztagsschule) with **guaranteed instruction/supervision from 7:30 AM to 2:00 PM**.[3] This core time is provided to all students.

**Extended Care Options:**
For families requiring additional supervision beyond the core hours, **supplementary care and support (Hort)** is available:
- **Before school:** 6:00 AM to 7:30 AM
- **After school:** 2:00 PM to 6:00 PM
- **Holiday care** during school breaks[3]

Students can access this extended care through a needs assessment (Bedarfsbescheid) submitted to the youth welfare office (Jugendamt).[3]

**Afternoon Programming:**
During afternoon care, children pursue individual interests through:
- Multiple **themed rooms** covering all educational areas[3]
- **Supervised homework time**[3]
- **Weekly activity groups (AGs)** led by pedagogical staff[3]
- **Courses offered by cooperation partners** available for enrollment[3]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Learning Environment:**
Students engage in **individualized, social, and creative learning** within a trusting and inspiring environment.[1] The school emphasizes:
- **Self-directed exploration** of individual interests[3]
- **Peer learning and collaboration** during free work phases[2]
- **Peaceful conflict resolution** and mutual respect[2]
- **Active participation** in shaping the school community[2]

**Extracurricular Activities:**
- **Weekly activity groups (AGs)** led by pedagogical staff[3]
- **Courses from cooperation partners** available for student enrollment[3]
- Access to the broader Stephanus-Stiftung campus for additional learning opportunities

**Community Engagement:**
The school actively engages with local churches and community partners in the Weißensee neighborhood, fostering connections beyond the school walls.[2]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The search results do not contain specific information about school rankings, certifications, awards, or documented student outcomes. The school's focus on **inclusive, reformist pedagogy** and its **barrier-reduced design** reflect its commitment to accessibility and individualized development rather than traditional achievement metrics.

## 6. ADMISSIONS AND FEES

**Admissions:**
The school is open to families seeking a **denominational, open all-day school with reformist pedagogy**, including non-religious families.[1] The school is registered as a **private school** (code 03P39) in Berlin's school system.[8]

**Contact Information:**
- **School Principal:** Mathilda Simonett
- **Deputy Principal:** Diana Tallarek
- **Address:** Parkstraße 22, 13086 Berlin
- **Phone:** 030 9624 9231
- **Email:** stephanus-grundschule@stephanus.org[5]

**Fee Information:**
**No tuition fees or specific fee amounts are disclosed in the available website content or PDF documents.** The search results do not contain pricing information for school fees, registration fees, material fees, meal plans, or after-school care costs. Families interested in enrollment should contact the school directly for current fee information.

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
The Sternberg-Grundschule emphasizes a values-based education centered on the **"Sternberger Erklärungen"**, a school manifesto promoting respect, diversity, mindfulness, and constructive dialogue in a multicultural environment with 45 nationalities represented among its 280 students.[1][4][5] Core principles include respecting others, listening attentively, addressing conflicts actively, and valuing diverse worldviews, reinforced through the weekly subject **"Wertebegegnung"** (Values Encounter), which fosters tolerance, ethical reflection, and independent thinking.[2][4] The school's philosophy, aligned with the Early Excellence approach, prioritizes individual strengths, family collaboration, community openness, time for personal worldviews, stimulating learning environments, social support, movement, and healthy nutrition.[1]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Located at Mettestr. 8, 10825 Berlin (Schöneberg), between Innsbrucker Platz and Rudolf-Wilde-Park, the public Grundschule serves approximately 270-280 students with a diverse team speaking 9 languages.[1][3][5][6] Leadership includes Schulleiterin Andrea Beermann and Konrektorin Katharina Legnowska.[3][6] Since 2005, it has partnered with Pestalozzi-Fröbel-Haus for open all-day care (Ganztagsbetreuung), serving over 180 students with 18 pedagogical staff, including five integration specialists.[1][3] The school collaborates with 10 partners, such as Kooperative Kinderturnen KOKITU (football courses), Juxirkus, and a piano teacher, embedding community ties.[1][5] Certified as Berlin's first **Weltethos-Schule** on September 24, 2025, it highlights values integration.[2][4][5]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
The curriculum features a **foreign languages profile** with small classes, offering English or French as the first foreign language since 1987/88 (initially in grades 5-6).[3][5][9] It includes individualized learning paths via competency grids, year-spanning groups in early phases, language emphasis, and the dedicated **Wertebegegnung** subject.[1][3][4] Open all-day care integrates educational support during and outside lessons.[1][3]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in diverse activities like football (Sparki-Cup, Crelle-Cup with FairPlay focus), reading competitions (project "On lit", internal contests with national representation), Christmas celebrations, and circus/sports partnerships.[1][5] Emphasis on integration, social learning, movement, and healthy eating creates a supportive, inclusive environment for 280 children from 45 nationalities.[1][5]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
Achievements include top national placements in the English competition **"The Big Challenge"**, an innovation prize for reading promotion ("On lit"), and certification as the first Berlin **Weltethos-Schule** in 2025.[2][5] Student Amir from 6a won the 2025 internal reading competition, advancing nationally.[5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a public tuition-free Grundschule owned by the State of Berlin (via Bezirksamt Tempelhof-Schöneberg), admissions follow standard Berlin public school processes, with no specific fees or barriers noted; contact Schulleitung@sternberg.schule.berlin.de or +49 30 902777156 for enrollment.[3][6][7]

# Süd-Grundschule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Süd-Grundschule is a public elementary school in Berlin-Zehlendorf dedicated to fostering individual student development within a supportive community environment. The school emphasizes creating a secure and nurturing learning space where children's talents are identified early and systematically developed through targeted support programs and enrichment activities.

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Historical Background**

Süd-Grundschule was **established in 1914** under the name "2. Gemeindeschule Zehlendorf."[8] The school building is an architectural landmark designed by Rudolf Walter and constructed between 1908-1910 using innovative Kalksandstein (artificial limestone sandstone) rather than conventional brick construction.[2] Originally, the building housed separate boys' and girls' schools within its two-winged structure, with the southern wing accommodating the boys' school and the western wing the girls' school.[2]

**Campus and Facilities**

The school is located at Claszeile 56/57 in Berlin-Zehlendorf (postal code 14165).[6][7] The campus features:

- A spacious school building with sound-insulated classrooms equipped with PC workstations and interactive whiteboards[1]
- A **school library** (Schulbücherei)[1]
- A **computer room** (Computerraum)[1]
- A **school café** (Schulcafé)[1]
- A **science room** (NaWi-Raum) with modern equipment[1]
- A renovated teacher's room with computer workstations and interactive whiteboard[1]
- A historic, wood-paneled room in the adjacent care building that serves students[1]
- A **school garden** (Schulgarten)[7]
- A **cafeteria** (Mensa) located in the care building, serving both school and after-school care children[1]

The school courtyard contains tall pine trees that are remnants of the former forest belt that once separated the villa colony from Reichstraße.[2]

**Complementary Care and Support**

The school offers **Ergänzende Förderung und Betreuung (EFöB)** — complementary care and support — housed in a historic building directly across from the main school building.[1] Contact for EFöB: 030-67031126.[7]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

The search results provided do not contain detailed information about the specific curriculum, subject offerings, or academic programs at Süd-Grundschule. The school's website content sections for curriculum details were not included in the available materials.

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**Enrichment Activities and Programs**

The school offers diverse extracurricular activities and projects that extend learning beyond the classroom:

- **Basketball AG** (working group) with competitive participation in school tournaments[7]
- **Volleyball program** — the school achieved Berlin state championship status in school volleyball[7]
- **Relay day participation** (Staffeltag der Berliner Schulen)[7]
- **School garden projects** involving students and families[7]
- **Library programs** with regular student engagement on Tuesdays and Thursdays[7]

**Community and Civic Engagement**

The school integrates historical awareness and civic responsibility into student learning. For example, students participated in a project day commemorating Kristallnacht (November 9, 1938), studying the persecution of Jewish citizens and honoring five boys who lived in a child protection house (Kinderschutzhaus) at Claszeile 57 and were murdered during the Holocaust. Students cleaned and maintained the Stolpersteine (stumbling stones) memorial plaques located on the sidewalk in front of the school.[6]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

The school has demonstrated success in competitive sports:

- **Berlin state champions in school volleyball**[7]
- Strong performance in relay day competitions with multiple teams placing in top positions[7]
- Basketball teams achieving competitive placements (5th and 17th place among 32 participating teams in one competition)[7]

## 6. ADMISSIONS

The most recent enrollment data shows that **57 students were enrolled in first grade** (classes 1a, 1b, and 1c) at the September 2025 enrollment ceremony.[9] As a public school, Süd-Grundschule follows Berlin's standard public school enrollment procedures. Specific admissions requirements and procedures are not detailed in the available search results.

**Contact Information**

- **Address:** Claszeile 56/57, 14165 Berlin
- **Telephone:** 030-84509638
- **Fax:** 030-8152630
- **Email:** info@sued-grundschule.de
- **EFöB Telephone:** 030-67031126[6][7]

# Teltow-Grundschule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

The Teltow-Grundschule is a **creative "Kiezschule"** (neighborhood school) that reflects the diversity of the changing Schöneberg community.[4] The school's core mission centers on creating an inclusive environment where all students feel at home and receive support tailored to their individual needs.[4] The school motto emphasizes: "We meet each other with respect and appreciation while maintaining binding rules—the Teltow-Grundschule is a school where everyone feels comfortable."[1]

The school consciously embraces the increasing heterogeneity of its student body as both a challenge and an opportunity, particularly supporting children with diagnosed special educational needs or those requiring enhanced support in language development and physical-motor skills.[4]

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Location and Contact:**
The Teltow-Grundschule is located in the Tempelhof-Schöneberg district at Feurigstr. 57, 10827 Berlin.[3][6] The school can be reached at (030) 90277-7160 or sekretariat@teltow.schule.berlin.de.[6]

**Leadership:**
The school is led by Schulleiter (Principal) Herr Achten, with Frau Noodt serving as Stellvertretende Schulleiterin (Deputy Principal).[6]

**Turnaround Success:**
The Teltow-Grundschule experienced a significant institutional turnaround. In 2010/11, the school faced closure with only 260 students enrolled. The dedicated faculty responded by implementing comprehensive school development initiatives focused on language education, integrated all-day programming, and inclusive practices, successfully revitalizing the institution.[5]

**Community Partnerships:**
The school collaborates with several community organizations including Nachbarschaftsheim Schöneberg e.V., the Volkshochschule (VHS), the municipal music school, and the city library.[1] The school also partners with the Schulpsychologisches und inklusionspädagogisches Beratungszentrum (SIBUZ) for specialized consultation and support.[5]

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

**Academic Profile:**
The school emphasizes three core academic areas:[1]
- **Kunst** (Art)
- **Sport** (Physical Education)
- **Schwerpunkt Lesen** (Reading Focus)

**Language Education:**
English is taught as the first foreign language beginning in grade 3.[1] The school implements a comprehensive language development concept with the emphasis on "Präsentieren in allen Klassenstufen" (Presentations across all grade levels), enabling students to develop and expand their language skills through presentations in every subject.[5]

**Extracurricular Activities and Working Groups:**
The school offers a diverse range of **Arbeitsgemeinschaften** (working groups and clubs):[1]
- Sports: Football, Ball School, Basketball, Swimming, Table Tennis
- Arts: Painting, Theater, Woodworking
- Media and Environment: School Newspaper, Environmental Detectives, Computer

**Special Programs:**
The school offers **Schulinsel** (School Island), a music-pedagogical and therapeutic program, along with art, music, and theater projects.[3] Recent initiatives include a three-day clay building project at the Gartenarbeitsschule (Garden Work School) and holiday programs featuring educational excursions.[2]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

**All-Day School Structure:**
The Teltow-Grundschule is a **gebundene Ganztagsschule** (bound all-day school), meaning all-day programming is integrated into the regular school day.[2] Each class is accompanied by a **Klassenteam** consisting of a teacher and an educator who share joint responsibility for students throughout the school day.[2][5]

**Core Hours:**
All children receive reliable, **cost-free care and instruction from 07:30 to 16:00 hours.**[2] Extended care options are available on a fee-paying basis: early care begins at 06:00 and late care extends until 18:00.[1][2]

**Individualized Learning:**
The school emphasizes individualized work, particularly in the Schulanfangsphase (school entry phase).[4] The school offers flexible, adapted inclusion concepts for each child based on family background and individual needs.[5] Cooperative support planning involves all stakeholders in developing and implementing individualized education plans.[5]

**School Entry Phase:**
The school offers both jahrgangsbezogene (grade-level) and jahrgangsübergreifende (mixed-age) learning groups in the school entry phase.[3]

**Schulstation (School Station):**
The school operates a Schulstation providing social work services, including holiday programs with excursions and support for student well-being.[1][2]

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

**School Inspection Results:**
The Teltow-Grundschule has been evaluated twice by the school inspection authority (Schulinspektion) with **very good results.**[2]

**Identified Strengths:**
The 2017 school inspection report identified the following strengths:[4]
- Engaged faculty that supports and promotes students according to their individual needs
- Individualized instruction in the school entry phase
- School atmosphere characterized by appreciation and respect
- Broad range of extracurricular offerings
- Conceptually and structurally sound school leadership

**Quality Assurance:**
The school maintains multiple conceptual documents developed by the faculty to ensure school quality, with goals implemented daily in instruction.[2] These include comprehensive school programs, language development concepts, internal evaluation processes, and structured support frameworks.[7]

**Inclusive Education Recognition:**
The school has successfully implemented inclusive practices that serve students with diverse learning needs and backgrounds, supported by structured support teams and consultation with specialized advisory centers.[5]

## 6. ADMISSIONS

The school welcomes prospective families to visit and observe classes. Interested parents are encouraged to contact the school to arrange a visit: sekretariat@teltow.schule.berlin.de or (030) 90277-7160.[6]

The school provides comprehensive information and support for the transition to primary school (Einschulung) and guidance for the transition to secondary school (Übergang zur Oberschule), with teachers offering criteria and counseling to support family decision-making.[2]

description:
### 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY
Tempelherren-Grundschule positions itself as **an inclusive place for learning, creativity, and community**, emphasizing a strong sense of togetherness through sports, music, theater, media literacy, and social skills.[1][7] The school fosters movement, team spirit, artistic expression, digital competence from 4th grade via laptop classes, and conflict resolution through school social work and buddy programs, preparing students for a digital future while building empathy and responsibility.[1][2]

### 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES
Founded in 1958 as the Tempelherren-Grundschule (previously the 6th Grundschule since 1951), the school originated post-WWII in a building that housed multiple institutions from 1945, evolving into a unified elementary school.[8] Located at **Boelckestraße 58-60, 12101 Berlin (Tempelhof-Schöneberg)**, it benefits from community partnerships like JUGI-Hessenring for after-school activities (e.g., VR, sports, cooking), Gartenarbeitsschule for environmental education, and local cycling programs.[6][9] The community includes a parents' council (GEV), booster club (Förderverein), open all-day care (OGB) run by NUSZ UFA-Fabrik e.V., and youth social work.[1][3][7]

### 3. CURRICURUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS
As a **public Grundschule (elementary school, grades 1-6)** with profiles in **sports, media, health, theater, and open all-day operations**, the curriculum integrates English as the first foreign language, year-group learning groups in early grades, and digital tools in 4th-grade laptop classes.[2][1] Extracurriculars emphasize **sport** (e.g., AGs in athletics, basketball with Jugendrotkreuz), **music/theater** (Tempeldrummer band, Small-Band, Kulturabende), and social development via Schulsozialarbeit.[1][7]

### 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES
Students engage in **active, holistic experiences** with moved breaks, schoolyard basketball/football tournaments, competitions (Crosslauf, Staffeltage, Mädchenlauf, Mini-Marathon, Zwergen-Cup, Oldie-Cup), Bundesjugendspiele, and internal events like Schulparklauf.[1][7] OGB offers extended hours (Mon-Fri 06:00-18:00) with homework support, movement, conflict resolution, and creative input; youth social work and partnerships enhance language, self-confidence, and environmental learning.[3][6]

### 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES
The school actively participates in district-wide events like two annual football tournaments (Zwergen-Cup, Oldie-Cup) for Tempelhof-Schöneberg Grundschulen, cross-country runs, relays, and team sports competitions, fostering enthusiasm and skills.[1][7] It maintains profiles in sports, media, and theater, with ongoing programs like SPI since 2020, but specific academic outcomes or test results are not detailed in available data.[2][5]

### 6. ADMISSIONS
As a **tuition-free public Grundschule**, admissions follow Berlin's standard public school enrollment process via eGovernment/Schulanmeldung, with no fees mentioned; contact Sekretariat for illness reports or appointments.[2][7] Open Ganztagsbetreuung (OGB) is available on-site without specified enrollment fees.[3]

# Thalia-Grundschule School Profile

## 1. MISSION AND EDUCATIONAL PHILOSOPHY

Limited information about the school's explicit mission statement is available in the provided sources. However, the school operates as a **public elementary school** (Grundschule) in Berlin with support from a school development association (Schulförderverein)[2].

## 2. HISTORY, COMMUNITY AND FACILITIES

**Historical Background**

The Thalia-Grundschule has deep roots in the Stralau community. The original building was constructed between 1891 and 1894 as a village school (Dorfschule) for the rapidly growing population of the Stralau peninsula[2][4]. The school opened in 1894 and served as an educational institution until the early 1950s[2]. Following incorporation into Berlin in 1920, it became the 35th municipal school with an eight-year curriculum[5].

The building underwent significant architectural modernization between 1928 and 1930, when municipal architect Franz Meurer designed and added an expressionistic gymnasium annex featuring a two-story structure with a gymnasium on the ground floor and an auditorium (Aula) on the upper floor[2][5]. This addition included a rooftop terrace for outdoor exercise[1].

**Current Status and Facilities**

After serving as a youth home (Jugendheim) from the early 1950s through the 1989 fall of the Berlin Wall, the building underwent comprehensive modernization in 1999 and reopened as the Thalia-Grundschule in 2000[5]. Both the school building and gymnasium are protected historical monuments (Denkmalschutz)[2][5].

The school is located at **Alt-Stralau 34, 10245 Berlin** in the Friedrichshain district[2][6]. Current facilities include:

- Accessible gymnasium and auditorium
- Rollstuhlgerechter Aufzug (wheelchair-accessible elevator)
- Wheelchair-accessible restrooms
- Parking for people with disabilities
- Accessible entry (ground level or ramp access)[6]

**Architectural Significance**

The main school building is a three-story clinker brick structure decorated with cornices and stripes of dark glazed tiles and white plaster panels[2]. The gymnasium annex exemplifies expressionist architecture of the 1920s, distinguished by distinctive small pointed gables running along the roof edges[2][5].

## 3. CURRICULUM AND ACADEMIC PROGRAMS

The school offers **English as the first foreign language** (1. Fremdsprache Englisch)[6]. Educational programs include:

- Schulanfangsphase (school entry phase) with grade-level learning groups
- Schulanfangsphase with mixed-age learning groups
- Offene Ganztagbetreuung (open all-day care)[6]

## 4. STUDENT LEARNING EXPERIENCES

The school provides **open all-day care** (Offene Ganztagbetreuung) and meal services through Z-Catering[6]. The school hosts a day of open doors (Tag der offenen Tür) for prospective families[6].

## 5. SCHOOL ACHIEVEMENTS AND OUTCOMES

No specific achievement data, test scores, or outcome metrics are provided in the available sources.

## 6. ADMISSIONS

As a public elementary school in Berlin, the Thalia-Grundschule operates under Berlin's public school system. No specific admissions requirements or procedures are detailed in the provided sources. Interested families are encouraged to contact the school directly or attend the day of open doors event.
